#### Q107: I need to match tiles in a 1995-built house. The original supplier has closed. How do I track down the manufacturer or find matching discontinued tiles?
**Answer:** Several strategies: (1) Check the back of spare tiles for manufacturer marks or country of origin codes — these can identify the factory even if the brand has changed; (2) Contact the Tile Council of South Africa (TCSA) — they maintain historical records of SA tile manufacturers and can help identify the source; (3) Post photos and dimensions on renovation forums and social media groups — someone may recognise the pattern; (4) Visit tile jobbing warehouses in Johannesburg (Kramerville, Randburg) and Cape Town (Montague Gardens, Epping) — they often hold discontinued stock for decades; (5) Contact Italian/Spanish/Portuguese tile manufacturers directly with photos — many keep archive records; (6) Consider tile "tattooing" services that can print matching patterns onto blank tiles. Matching 30-year-old tiles is extremely difficult — in most cases, strategic re-tiling (using close matches in hidden areas, salvaging originals for visible spots) is more practical than a perfect match.

#### Q108: My tile supplier is offering "factory seconds" at 40% off. They say the defects are "minor shade variation only." If I install them and the variation is worse than expected, can I return them?
**Answer:** Factory seconds are sold "as is" — the CPA Section 55 right to quality goods is modified by the disclosure that these are imperfect goods. However, the supplier cannot misrepresent the nature of the defects. If they said "minor shade variation only" and the tiles have major defects (size variation, warping, cracking), they misrepresented the product and you have a CPA claim. Protect yourself: (1) Inspect the entire batch before accepting — don't rely on sample boards; (2) Get the defect description in writing; (3) Photograph the offered tiles before delivery; (4) Calculate whether the savings justify the risk — for a visible area, seconds are rarely worth it. For utility areas (garage, storeroom), the risk is acceptable. Keep in mind: no manufacturer warranty applies to seconds, and the supplier's return policy is likely "no returns" except for misrepresentation.

#### Q109: I specified a tile that my architect sourced from a boutique supplier. The supplier has gone into business rescue and can't deliver. My architect says it's not her problem — I should have used a mainstream supplier. Is she liable?
**Answer:** Your architect's liability depends on your agreement and their professional duty. If the architect was appointed to specify AND procure the tiles (turnkey responsibility), the supplier's failure is their problem. If the architect only specified and you procured directly, the risk is yours. However, even in a specification-only role, architects have a duty to specify products that are reasonably available and from financially stable suppliers — specifying from a boutique supplier with doubtful financials may be professional negligence if a mainstream equivalent was available. Your claim: (1) Check your architect's appointment agreement — does it include procurement responsibility? (2) Was the architect aware of the supplier's financial distress? (3) Is there a readily available mainstream alternative the architect should have specified? If the architect was negligent in supplier selection, the Architectural Professions Council of South Africa (APCSA) accepts complaints about professional conduct. For significant losses, consult a professional negligence attorney.

#### Q110: I'm trying to match tiles for a small repair. A tile "matching service" says they can colour-match any tile for R850 per tile. Is this legitimate, and will the match actually work?
**Answer:** Tile colour-matching services exist and use spectrophotometer analysis to create custom glaze colours that are then applied to blank bisque tiles. The technology works, but results vary: (1) The match is colour-only — texture, gloss level, and dimensional size may differ; (2) The match is under specific lighting conditions — it may look perfect in daylight but different under LED; (3) At R850 per tile, it's only economical for 1-3 visible tiles; (4) The service typically requires a physical sample of your original tile and 2-3 weeks lead time. Reputable services will guarantee a visual match under specified conditions. Before committing: (1) Ask to see examples of previous matches; (2) Confirm the guarantee — will they redo if the match isn't acceptable? (3) Consider whether strategic tile relocation (moving a hidden original tile to the visible spot) is cheaper and more effective.

#### Q111: I bought tiles online from a UK supplier who shipped to South Africa. They arrived cracked due to poor packaging. The supplier says returns are at my cost to the UK (R12,000 shipping). Under SA consumer law, who pays for return shipping of defective goods?
**Answer:** Under CPA Section 55 and 56, if goods are defective, the supplier must remedy the defect at their cost — this includes return shipping if a return is required. However, your situation is complicated by cross-border jurisdiction. The CPA applies to transactions "in the ordinary course of business" in South Africa — a UK supplier shipping to SA may argue UK consumer law applies. Your position: (1) If the supplier marketed to SA consumers (SA website, SA pricing, SA delivery), the CPA likely applies; (2) Demand the supplier arrange collection at their cost or provide a full refund without return; (3) If they refuse, file a chargeback with your credit card company — defective goods are a valid chargeback reason; (4) For large amounts, consult a consumer attorney about cross-border CPA application. The R12,000 return cost makes returning uneconomical — insist on a no-return refund or local disposal with full reimbursement.

#### Q112: My developer installed tiles throughout my new home, and I kept 5m² of spares. Now, 4 years later, I need repairs but the spares have a visibly different shade from the installed tiles. Is this "tile aging" or a batch issue, and can I claim?
**Answer:** This is likely a combination of both. All tiles undergo subtle colour change over time due to UV exposure, cleaning chemicals, and general wear — this is normal and not a defect. However, if the spare tiles were stored in dark, stable conditions and show a dramatically different shade, there may have been a batch mismatch from installation. Your claim options: (1) Against the developer: if the original installation used mixed batches (visible at the time but you didn't notice), you may have a latent defect claim under the NHBRC 5-year warranty — but aesthetic batch variation is rarely classified as a "defect"; (2) The 4-year elapsed time weakens any claim — you should have noticed batch variation at handover; (3) For NHBRC claims, the warranty covers structural defects, not aesthetic issues. Your practical solution: use the spares in the least visible repair spots, or accept that spare tiles stored for years will never perfectly match installed tiles. This is why post-installation sealing and consistent cleaning products matter for long-term colour consistency.

#### Q113: I found discontinued tiles I need on Gumtree. The seller won't let me see them before purchase — "first come first served, no holds." If I buy and they don't match, do I have any CPA rights against a private seller?
**Answer:** The CPA applies to transactions "in the ordinary course of business" — private sales between individuals (one-off transactions) are generally exempt from CPA protections. If the Gumtree seller is a private individual selling leftover renovation materials, the CPA does not apply and the sale is likely voetstoots (as-is). Your only protections: (1) Common law — if the seller made specific fraudulent misrepresentations about the match, you may have a claim for fraud; (2) If the seller is actually a business selling regularly on Gumtree (de facto trader), the CPA may apply despite the platform; (3) For cash-on-collection sales, inspect before paying — walk away if they don't match. The risk of buying tiles sight-unseen from private sellers is entirely yours. For high-value purchases, always inspect first, bring a sample to compare, and pay only after verification.

#### Q114: My architect specified a tile that's been discontinued but the supplier says they'll "make a special batch" for me at 3x the normal price with no quality guarantee. Should I accept this?
**Answer:** Absolutely not. A "special batch" with no quality guarantee is a red flag for multiple reasons: (1) Without standard production line controls, the tiles may have inconsistent water absorption, breaking strength, or dimensional stability; (2) No warranty means no recourse if they fail; (3) At 3x price, you're paying a premium for an inferior product. Your architect should specify an available alternative. If they insist on this tile, they are not acting in your best interests. Demand the architect provide three available alternatives at market prices, or engage another architect/designer. The "special batch" offer is a desperation play by a supplier who can't source the product — it should never be accepted for a residential installation where quality and warranty matter. Even for commercial projects, special batches require enhanced quality control that a small one-off order won't receive.

#### Q115: I have 30m² of tiles left over from my renovation. The supplier won't accept returns (past 30 days). Can I sell them, and do I need to charge VAT if I sell to another private individual?
**Answer:** You can sell leftover tiles as a private individual without VAT implications — the sale is not "in the course of business" and you're not a VAT vendor. Platforms: Gumtree, Facebook Marketplace, local renovation groups. Tips for selling: (1) Provide the exact product code, batch number, and original purchase date; (2) Include photos under natural light showing the true colour; (3) Price at 50-70% of retail for unopened boxes, 30-50% for opened boxes; (4) Offer to let the buyer compare to their existing tiles before purchase. If you sell to a business that will resell, they may want a tax invoice — as a non-vendor, you cannot issue one. If you're doing frequent renovations and regularly selling leftover materials, SARS may consider this a taxable activity — keep individual sale values below R50,000 annually to avoid this scrutiny. The tax risk for occasional private sales is negligible.

### Section 10: Special Rooms, Climate & Unusual Applications

#### Q116: I'm building a wine cellar in my Constantia home that needs to maintain 12-16°C and 70-80% humidity year-round. Can I use standard porcelain tiles, or do I need special tiles for this environment?
**Answer:** Standard porcelain tiles (water absorption <0.5%) are technically suitable for wine cellar conditions — they won't absorb moisture or degrade in high humidity. However, several wine-specific considerations apply: (1) Natural stone tiles (slate, travertine) are traditional for wine cellars and regulate humidity better than porcelain; (2) If using porcelain, ensure the substrate has a vapour barrier — 70-80% humidity will eventually penetrate to the concrete and cause mould if unprotected; (3) Grout selection is critical — use epoxy grout, not cement grout, as cement degrades in sustained humidity; (4) The cellar's cooling system must maintain stable temperature — thermal cycling (even within 12-16°C range) causes expansion/contraction that stresses tiles and grout; (5) Dark-coloured tiles show condensation less visibly. Consult a wine cellar specialist — the tile choice is secondary to the climate control and waterproofing system. Budget R2,500-R5,000/m² for a proper wine cellar installation including climate control, insulation, and suitable finishes.

#### Q117: I want to tile my shower floor with the same large-format 1200x600mm tiles as the walls for a seamless look. My tiler says this is unsafe because of slip resistance. Is he right, and are there large-format tiles with sufficient slip resistance for shower floors?
**Answer:** Your tiler is correct to raise this concern. SANS 10107 (Code of Practice for the Design of Egress Facilities) and SABS 0107 address slip resistance in wet areas. Standard large-format porcelain tiles typically have an R9 or R10 slip rating (DIN 51130 pendulum test), which is inadequate for shower floors where bare feet contact wet surfaces. For shower floors, you need: (1) R11 or R12 slip-rated tiles (textured surface); (2) Tiles with a wet pendulum test value of 35+ (AS 4586 standard); (3) Smaller tiles (300x300mm or less) with more grout lines for grip; (4) Or use the large-format on walls with a matching mosaic or smaller slip-rated tile on the floor. Some manufacturers now produce large-format tiles with R11 ratings, but they are expensive and limited in design. The aesthetic of seamless large-format must be balanced against the safety requirement — a slip injury in your shower exposes you to liability under the Occupiers' Liability Act.

#### Q118: I'm converting my garage into a granny flat and want to install tiles directly on the existing concrete garage floor. The concrete has oil stains and paint marks. Can tiles be installed over this, or must the concrete be ground clean?
**Answer:** Tiles can technically be installed over stained and painted concrete, but the long-term results are poor. Oil stains prevent proper adhesive bonding — the oil migrates through the adhesive over time causing delamination. Paint creates a bond-breaker layer. Proper preparation: (1) Grind the concrete to remove all paint, oil, and contaminants — this is essential; (2) Repair cracks and level uneven areas (garage floors typically have falls for drainage that need correction for habitable use); (3) Apply a suitable primer/sealer if oil penetration is deep; (4) Use a flexible adhesive and allow for movement joints — garage slabs have different expansion characteristics than house floors. The grinding cost (R50-R120/m²) is minor compared to the cost of relaying failed tiles. Also check: garage conversion requires building plan approval and possibly rezoning — ensure compliance before investing in finishes.

#### Q119: My Cape Town home has a basement that floods during winter storms. I want to install tiles that can handle periodic flooding. What type of tile and installation system should I use?
**Answer:** For periodically flooded basements, the key is not the tile but the installation system: (1) Use porcelain tiles (not ceramic) — <0.5% water absorption; (2) Install a full cavity drainage membrane (CDM) system beneath the tiles — this creates an air gap between the tile bed and the slab, allowing floodwater to drain to a sump pump; (3) Use epoxy grout — cement grout will degrade with repeated wet/dry cycles; (4) Install the tiles on a floating system (not bonded to the slab) so the floor can be lifted and the cavity cleaned after flooding; (5) Ensure the substrate has a waterproofing membrane beneath the CDM. Without a cavity system, any tile installation in a flooding basement will eventually fail — the hydrostatic pressure forces water through the weakest points. This is specialised work — engage a waterproofing contractor, not just a tiler. Budget R1,500-R3,000/m² including the cavity system.

#### Q120: I want to install heated tiles on my outdoor patio in Johannesburg. Will the tiles crack due to freeze-thaw cycles when the heating is off in winter?
**Answer:** If you select frost-resistant porcelain tiles (Class BIa under SANS 1449, water absorption <0.5%), freeze-thaw cracking is unlikely. However, several factors matter: (1) The heating system must have frost protection mode — most quality systems will maintain 5°C minimum even when "off" to prevent freezing in the screed; (2) The installation must include expansion joints at perimeter and every 8-10m² — thermal cycling between heated and frozen states causes significant movement; (3) Use a flexible tile adhesive (S1 classification) that accommodates thermal movement; (4) The substrate must be properly cured and dry before heating is activated — trapped moisture + freeze-thaw = spalling. Johannesburg's Highveld winter temperatures (regularly below -5°C) are more severe than coastal climates — don't skimp on frost resistance ratings. Consult a heating specialist who understands Highveld conditions.

#### Q121: I'm tiling a commercial kitchen floor for my restaurant. The health inspector says I need "non-slip, non-porous, coved" flooring. What does "coved" mean, and can a tiler create this with standard tiles?
**Answer:** "Coved" means a curved transition between the floor and wall (no 90-degree corner), which eliminates the junction where dirt, grease, and bacteria accumulate — essential for food safety compliance under Regulation 638 of the Foodstuffs, Cosmetics and Disinfectants Act. Standard tiles cannot create a true cove — you need: (1) Specialist cove tiles (curved base tiles that extend up the wall 100-150mm); (2) Or a seamless polyurethane or epoxy flooring system (Altro, Polyflor, Mapei) that can be coved on-site; (3) Or vinyl sheeting with welded seams and coved skirting (most common in SA commercial kitchens). A tiler can approximate coving with standard tiles by mitre-cutting wall tiles to meet floor tiles at 45 degrees, but this doesn't meet true food safety standards. For restaurant compliance, use an approved commercial kitchen flooring system installed by a certified contractor — not a residential tiler.

#### Q122: I want to tile my swimming pool surround with the same tiles as my patio for visual continuity. The pool chemical supplier says chlorine will damage the tiles and grout. Is this true?
**Answer:** Chlorine and other pool chemicals can damage certain tiles and grout over time: (1) Acid-washed stone tiles (travertine, marble) will etch with chlorine splashes; (2) Cement grout degrades with chemical exposure — use epoxy grout for pool surrounds; (3) Low-quality glazed tiles may experience glaze degradation; (4) Salt chlorinator systems are particularly aggressive — salt crystallisation in grout joints causes spalling. Suitable pool surround tiles: (1) Full-body porcelain with R11/R12 slip rating (wet bare feet); (2) Acid-resistant natural stone (granite, quartzite); (3) Always use epoxy grout; (4) Ensure 1-2% fall away from the pool for drainage. The visual continuity with your patio tiles is achievable if you select a porcelain tile that works for both applications — many manufacturers produce matching ranges with different slip ratings for wet vs. dry areas. Budget 20-30% more for pool-suitable tiles vs. standard patio tiles.

#### Q123: My Durban home's subfloor has active mould growth due to the humidity. Can I tile over it after cleaning, or must the mould be professionally remediated first?
**Answer:** Never tile over active mould. Mould is a living organism that will continue to grow beneath the tiles, feeding on organic material in the adhesive and grout. Within 6-12 months, it will bloom through grout joints and potentially lift tiles as the organic mass expands. Proper remediation: (1) Identify and eliminate the moisture source (rising damp, poor ventilation, plumbing leak); (2) Professionally remediate the mould — for extensive growth (>1m²), use a registered remediation contractor who follows SANS 10222 (Mould Assessment and Remediation); (3) Treat the concrete with a fungicidal solution; (4) Apply a waterproofing membrane before tiling; (5) Ensure the room has adequate ventilation (extractor fans, window ventilation). Durban's coastal humidity (average 75%+) makes mould a constant risk — consider using anti-mould additives in grout and installing a dehumidifier. Tiling over mould doesn't solve the problem — it hides it and makes remediation far more expensive later.

#### Q124: I'm building a steam room in my home. Can I use standard bathroom tiles, or do I need tiles rated for steam room conditions?
**Answer:** Standard bathroom tiles are not suitable for steam room conditions. Steam rooms operate at 40-50°C with 100% humidity — far more aggressive than a typical bathroom. Requirements: (1) Tiles must be frost-resistant and thermal-shock resistant — the rapid temperature change when steam hits cold tiles causes cracking in standard products; (2) Use only porcelain (BIa classification), not ceramic — the steam penetration will degrade ceramic tiles over time; (3) All adhesive and grout must be steam-rated epoxy systems — standard cement-based products will fail within months; (4) The substrate must be fully waterproofed with a steam-rated membrane (not standard shower waterproofing); (5) Install a sloped floor with steam-rated drainage. Steam room tiling is a specialist application — engage a contractor with steam room experience. SANS 10400 Part T doesn't specifically address residential steam rooms, but the general principles of moisture management apply. Budget R4,000-R8,000/m² for a properly built home steam room.

#### Q125: I want to install tiles on the ceiling of my covered outdoor patio in Pretoria. Is this possible, and what special installation methods are needed?
**Answer:** Ceiling tiles are possible but challenging: (1) Tile weight: porcelain tiles at 10-15kg/m² create significant dead load on the ceiling structure — a structural engineer must verify the ceiling can support this (standard ceilings are not designed for this load); (2) Installation method: tiles must be mechanically supported, not just adhesive-bonded — use a suspended grid system with tile clips, or a cement board substrate with heavy-duty adhesive and mechanical fixings; (3) Gravity is working against you — tile adhesive has initial "grab" but can slump before curing; (4) Expansion joints are critical — ceiling tiles experience more thermal movement than floor tiles. Common applications: lightweight acoustic ceiling tiles (not ceramic), or decorative panels designed for ceilings. If you genuinely want porcelain on a ceiling, budget for structural assessment (R5,000-R15,000), reinforced substrate, specialist adhesive, and expect installation costs 3-4x floor tiling rates. Most designers achieve the visual effect with lighter alternatives.

#### Q126: My Limpopo farm has a borehole water supply with high calcium content (hard water). Will this affect my tile installation or cause long-term problems?
**Answer:** Hard water affects tiles primarily through limescale buildup on the surface — white, crusty deposits that are unsightly and difficult to remove. This is an aesthetic issue, not a structural one. However: (1) Limescale is most visible on dark-coloured tiles — choose lighter colours where the deposits will be less conspicuous; (2) Use a water softener for the house supply if the hardness exceeds 200mg/l CaCO3 — this protects all fixtures, not just tiles; (3) Clean tiles regularly with descaling products (not acidic on natural stone — use pH-neutral descalers); (4) For wall tiles in shower areas, squeegee after use to minimise deposits. Hard water does not affect the tile installation itself — the adhesive and grout cure chemically and are not affected by water hardness. The long-term maintenance burden is the consideration, not the installation.

#### Q127: I want to install tiles in my horse stable for easy cleaning. Are there tiles rated for equine use, and will the horses slip?
**Answer:** Equine-rated flooring is a specialised field. Standard tiles are unsuitable for stables: (1) Horses need cushioned, non-slip flooring — hard tiles cause hoof and leg problems (capped hocks, slip injuries); (2) If a horse falls on tile, the injury is severe and you face liability; (3) Urine degrades grout rapidly — ammonia attacks cement-based products; (4) Heavy point loading (horse hooves) can crack tiles. Suitable stable flooring: (1) Rubber stable mats (the industry standard) — 15-25mm thick, non-slip, cushioned, easy to clean; (2) Interlocking rubber pavers over concrete; (3) Sealed concrete with rubber matting in high-wear areas. If you absolutely want a tiled "look," consider rubber tiles designed for equestrian use — they provide the aesthetic without the risks. No responsible equine professional would recommend ceramic or porcelain tiles in a stable environment.

#### Q128: My thatched roof house in the Drakensberg needs a new floor. The building inspector says I need a 30-minute fire-rated floor because the thatch is a fire risk. Do fire-rated floor tiles exist?
**Answer:** Tiles themselves are inherently fire-resistant (porcelain is fired at 1200°C+ and is non-combustible). However, "fire-rated floor" refers to the entire floor assembly, not just the surface finish. For thatched roof buildings, the concern is fire spread from the roof to the structure — a 30-minute fire-rated floor assembly prevents fire from penetrating through the floor to the level below. The tile type is irrelevant to the fire rating — it's about: (1) The substrate (concrete slab achieves 30-60 minutes inherently); (2) If it's a timber floor, fire-rated plasterboard or Fyrchek beneath; (3) Fire stops at penetrations (pipes, conduits); (4) The thatch itself should have fire retardant treatment (required by SANS 10400 Part T and most insurance policies). Your tiles can be any porcelain or ceramic — focus on the floor assembly and thatch treatment. Thatched buildings require specialist insurance — standard homeowner's policies often exclude thatch. Confirm your coverage before proceeding.

#### Q129: I want to install underfloor heating under tiles in my Karoo holiday home that's unoccupied for 6 months of the year. Will the system damage tiles if left off for extended periods, and what about the extreme temperature swings?
**Answer:** Underfloor heating systems are designed for continuous or regular cycling — extended shutdown doesn't damage the system itself. However, the Karoo environment poses specific challenges: (1) Extreme diurnal temperature swings (40°C day, 5°C night in summer) cause significant substrate expansion/contraction — tiles must be installed with generous expansion joints; (2) During 6-month vacancy, the house experiences unregulated temperature extremes that stress tiles and grout regardless of underfloor heating; (3) When activating the system after long shutdown, ramp up temperature gradually (5°C per day) to avoid thermal shock; (4) Frost protection mode must be maintained in winter — Karoo nights regularly drop below freezing. The underfloor heating is not the risk factor — the Karoo climate is. Use only BIa frost-resistant porcelain, S1 flexible adhesive, and proper expansion joint design. Have a caretaker check the property monthly and run the heating system periodically.

#### Q130: I'm tiling my home gym floor. The equipment supplier says I need "vibration-dampening" flooring to protect the slab and reduce noise to the floor below. Can standard tiles provide this, or do I need a special system?
**Answer:** Standard tile-on-screed provides no vibration dampening. For a home gym: (1) Heavy weights dropped on tile will crack the tiles and potentially the slab; (2) Treadmills and exercise equipment transmit vibration through rigid tile floors; (3) If there's a dwelling below, noise transfer will be significant. Solutions: (1) Install a rubber gym mat system over the tiles — interlocking rubber tiles (15-25mm) that protect the floor and provide grip; (2) Or use a specialised sports flooring system (Mondo, Gerflor) designed for gyms; (3) If you want the tile aesthetic, install tiles with a 25mm rubber underlayment beneath — this is expensive but provides the look with dampening; (4) For weightlifting areas, a dedicated platform (wood + rubber) is essential. Most home gyms use rubber flooring throughout with tile-look patterns. Budget R400-R800/m² for quality gym flooring vs. R200-R400/m² for standard tiles that will be damaged by gym use.

### Section 11: High-Rise, Penthouse & Access Challenges

#### Q131: I'm on the 15th floor of a Cape Town apartment building and want 1200x1200mm tiles. The lift can only take 1000mm tiles. The building manager refuses to allow crane access from the balcony. Can I cut tiles before delivery, and will this void warranties?
**Answer:** Cutting tiles before delivery does not void the manufacturer's warranty — the warranty covers manufacturing defects, not how you handle the tiles post-purchase. However, pre-cutting creates problems: (1) Factory-cut edges are precision-cut and rectified; site-cut edges are inferior and visible; (2) If tiles need to be cut to fit the lift (e.g., 1000mm), the remaining 200mm strips are waste — you'll need to order significantly more; (3) Large-format tiles cut before installation are more fragile during handling — breakage risk increases. Better alternatives: (1) Source 1000x1000mm or 900x900mm tiles that fit the lift — many manufacturers produce these standard sizes; (2) Negotiate with the building manager for a one-time crane permit with full insurance and professional rigging; (3) Use a freight elevator if the building has one (often larger capacity). The building manager's refusal of crane access is defensible under safety regulations — balcony hoisting in Cape Town wind requires specialist rigging and wind assessment.

#### Q132: My penthouse renovation requires removing old tiles and installing new ones. The body corporate is worried about weight — are large-format porcelain tiles significantly heavier than the ceramic tiles being removed, and could this affect the slab's structural capacity?
**Answer:** Large-format porcelain tiles are actually lighter per square metre than smaller ceramic tiles for equivalent coverage, because there are fewer grout lines (grout weighs more than you think). However, the total dead load comparison depends on: (1) Tile thickness — 1200x600mm porcelain is typically 9-12mm thick vs. old ceramic at 8-10mm; (2) The screed/adhesive bed — new installation may add 15-25mm of screed + adhesive; (3) The removed tiles and screed — if fully removed, the net weight change may be minimal or even negative. For a structural assessment: (1) Residential slabs are designed for 1.5-2.0 kN/m² live load plus dead load; (2) A 25mm screed + 12mm tile adds approximately 0.7 kN/m² — well within capacity; (3) If adding underfloor heating screed, the total increases to 1.2-1.5 kN/m² — still typically within capacity but worth verifying. For peace of mind, a structural engineer can confirm slab capacity for R3,000-R8,000 — cheap insurance for a penthouse renovation.

#### Q133: I live on the 8th floor and want to tile my balcony. The body corporate says balconies are "common property" and I need their approval. But I have exclusive use. Who is right?
**Answer:** Check your sectional plan at the Deeds Office — this is definitive. Many apartment balconies are registered as exclusive use common property under Section 27 of the ST Act, not as part of the section. If your balcony is exclusive use common property: (1) You need body corporate approval for any alteration, including re-tiling; (2) They can impose conditions (tile type, colour, slip rating, installation method); (3) You must maintain the balcony in good condition but cannot alter it without consent. If the balcony is part of your section (less common in high-rises), you don't need approval for like-for-like replacement, but may for material changes if visible from common property. Balcony tiling also has safety implications — the body corporate can require R11+ slip-rated tiles and proper falls for drainage. Obtain your sectional plan to confirm the legal status before applying.

#### Q134: I'm renovating a penthouse in Umhlanga. The tiles need to be delivered by crane to the roof and then lowered to the unit. The crane operator says he needs a "lift plan" signed off by an engineer. What is this, and who arranges it?
**Answer:** A lift plan (rigging plan) is a formal document required under the Driven Machinery Regulations (OHSA) for any crane lifting operation. It specifies: (1) The crane's position, outrigger setup, and radius; (2) Load weight and centre of gravity; (3) Lift path and clearance from structures; (4) Wind speed limits and weather restrictions; (5) Personnel roles and communication methods; (6) Emergency procedures. For penthouse tile delivery: (1) The crane hire company typically prepares the lift plan — it's part of their service; (2) For complex lifts (high-rise, restricted space, high-value loads), a professional engineer must certify the lift plan — the crane company arranges this; (3) Building management must approve the lift plan — they may require their own engineer's review; (4) You pay for all of this — budget R8,000-R25,000 for the lift plan, engineering sign-off, and crane mobilisation, plus R3,000-R8,000 per hour for the crane. For large-format tiles on high floors, the delivery logistics often cost more than the tiles themselves.

#### Q135: My high-rise contractor says he can't tile on windy days because the wind affects his ability to cut tiles on the balcony. Is this a real concern, or is he finding excuses not to work?
**Answer:** Wind affecting tile cutting is a genuine safety concern, not an excuse. In high-rise buildings: (1) Wind speeds increase significantly with height — a 20km/h ground wind can be 40-50km/h on the 15th floor; (2) Tile cutting on a balcony in high wind creates dust that blows into neighbouring units — body corporate complaints are likely; (3) Wet cutting requires water that wind blows across the facade — water ingress into units below is a liability; (4) Small tile fragments can become wind-borne projectiles at height — serious safety risk. Best practice: (1) Cut tiles inside the unit (messier but contained); (2) Use a portable wet-cutting station with full water containment; (3) Schedule cutting for calm mornings; (4) Install temporary windbreaks on the balcony. Your contractor's caution is professionally responsible. However, he should have alternative work plans for windy days (grouting, cleaning, preparation) rather than stopping entirely.

#### Q136: The body corporate in my Cape Town high-rise requires all renovation waste to be removed through the service elevator, but the service elevator is out of order for 3 months. Can I use the passenger lift for tile removal waste, and who pays if the lift is damaged?
**Answer:** Without the service elevator, you have a practical problem that requires negotiation. The body corporate cannot require you to use a non-functional elevator and simultaneously refuse alternatives. Your options: (1) Request the body corporate arrange alternative waste removal — stairwell carry-down with protection, temporary hoist, or delayed removal when the service elevator is repaired; (2) If you use the passenger lift with body corporate consent and proper protection (padded walls, floor protection), you are liable for any damage caused by your contractor; (3) If the body corporate unreasonably refuses all alternatives while their elevator is broken, you may have a claim for the delay costs through CSOS. The key is getting consent for whatever alternative is used — unauthorised use of the passenger lift for construction waste is a breach of conduct rules and exposes you to fines plus damage liability. The body corporate should also claim from their elevator maintenance contractor for the 3-month outage — that's unreasonably long.

#### Q137: I want to install tiles on the external walls of my 10th-floor apartment balcony (not the floor — the walls). The body corporate says external appearance changes need approval, but these are "my" walls. Do they have authority over what I do on my balcony walls?
**Answer:** If your balcony is exclusive use common property (the most common arrangement), the body corporate absolutely has authority over external appearance changes — the walls form part of the building's external facade, which affects the scheme's overall appearance and property values. Even if the balcony is part of your section, if the balcony walls are visible from outside the building, PMR 68(1)(v) allows conduct rules to regulate changes visible from common property. You need body corporate approval for external balcony wall tiling, and they can prescribe colour, tile type, and finish. Additionally, external wall tiling at height requires consideration of: (1) Wind load on the tile adhesion — standard floor adhesive is insufficient for vertical external applications; (2) Waterproofing behind the tiles — water ingress at height causes significant damage; (3) Building plan approval may be required for external cladding changes. Submit a formal application with full specifications and samples.

#### Q138: My high-rise contractor needs to use a generator during load shedding. The generator exhaust on the balcony is causing fumes to enter the unit above. The neighbour is complaining. What are my obligations?
**Answer:** You are creating a nuisance that interferes with your neighbour's use and enjoyment of their unit. Under ST Act Section 44 and common law, you must not use your section in a way that causes nuisance to others. Generator exhaust fumes entering another unit is a clear nuisance. Your obligations: (1) Relocate the generator to a position where exhaust does not affect neighbouring units — this may require a longer power cable or a different balcony; (2) Use an inverter/battery system instead for power tools — zero emissions; (3) If generator is essential, use it only during hours when the neighbour is not home (with their agreement); (4) Install an exhaust extension pipe directing fumes away from the building. If the neighbour escalates, the body corporate can issue a compliance notice and ultimately apply for a court interdict. Don't let a generator dispute derail your renovation — find an alternative power solution.

#### Q139: I'm on the 20th floor of a Sandton building. The body corporate says no "wet trades" (tiling, screeding) on weekends because water leaks could affect multiple units below before anyone notices. Is this a valid rule?
**Answer:** This rule has a rational basis and is likely enforceable. In high-rise buildings, water leaks from wet trades can cascade through multiple units before being detected, causing extensive damage. Restricting wet trades to weekdays when building management and contractors are available to respond quickly is a reasonable risk management measure. However: (1) The rule must be in the conduct rules (properly adopted) to be enforceable — an informal management decision is not sufficient; (2) "Dry" work (tile cutting, layout, grouting cured surfaces) could reasonably be allowed on weekends; (3) If your project timeline is critically affected, request a variance with enhanced precautions (water leak detection system, standby plumber, building management on call). The body corporate's concern is legitimate — a burst pipe or failed waterproofing on the 20th floor can cause millions in damage to units below. Weekend wet trade bans in high-rises are standard practice globally and will be upheld by CSOS.

#### Q140: I want to install a tile feature wall in my high-rise apartment. The tiles weigh 35kg/m². The partition wall is drywall on steel studs. Can this support the weight?
**Answer:** Standard drywall on 64mm steel studs supports approximately 20-25kg/m² — your 35kg/m² tiles exceed this capacity. Solutions: (1) Replace the drywall with 15mm cement fibre board (Jackboard, Tilebacker) on the steel studs — this supports 40-50kg/m²; (2) Install additional vertical studs at 400mm centres (vs. standard 600mm) to increase support; (3) Use lightweight tiles for the feature wall — porcelain tiles at 35kg/m² are overkill for a wall; consider 6-8mm thin porcelain (14-18kg/m²) which achieves the same look at less than half the weight; (4) For heavy natural stone tiles, mechanical fixings (tile brackets, anchor systems) are required in addition to adhesive. A structural engineer or competent builder must assess the wall structure before installation. Do not rely on adhesive alone to support heavy tiles on a partition wall — failure causes injury and extensive damage. Budget R400-R800/m² for the cement board substrate preparation plus tile installation.

### Section 12: Rental Properties & Tenant Rights

#### Q141: I want to retile my rental property in Randburg while the tenant is in the second year of a 2-year lease. The tenant says I can't access the property for "non-essential" renovations without his consent. Is he right?
**Answer:** Under the Rental Housing Act and common law, a landlord has the right to access the property for repairs and maintenance with reasonable notice, but not for discretionary improvements that interfere with the tenant's peaceful enjoyment. "Essential" vs. "non-essential" is the key distinction: (1) If the existing flooring is damaged, unsafe, or causing damp issues, replacement is maintenance and you have a right of access; (2) If the flooring is functional and you're replacing it for aesthetic upgrade or value enhancement, this is an improvement and the tenant can refuse access; (3) Even for maintenance, you must give reasonable notice (typically 48-72 hours) and minimise disruption. Your options: (1) Wait until the lease ends in year 2; (2) Negotiate with the tenant — offer rent reduction during the work, temporary alternative accommodation, or a mutually convenient schedule; (3) If the tenant unreasonably refuses essential maintenance, apply to the Rental Housing Tribunal for an access order.

#### Q142: My tenant in a Durban apartment wants to install his own tiles at his expense because he "doesn't like the existing flooring." He says he'll do it during the weekend without disturbing anyone. Can I stop him, and who owns the tiles when he leaves?
**Answer:** You can and should stop him. Under the lease agreement and ST Act, tenants cannot make alterations to the property without landlord consent. Unauthorised alterations constitute a breach of lease and the tenant is liable for restoration costs. Your response: (1) Issue a written notice prohibiting the installation and reminding the tenant of the lease terms; (2) If tiles are installed without consent, the tenant must remove them and restore the original flooring at their cost, or forfeit their deposit to cover restoration; (3) Under common law, improvements made without the landlord's request become the landlord's property (accesio principle) — if the tenant installs tiles, you may acquire ownership of them, but the tenant has no right to compensation unless you agreed to the improvement. The tenant's preference for different flooring does not create a right to alter your property. Document your prohibition in writing — this protects your deposit claim if the tenant proceeds anyway.

#### Q143: I'm retiling my rental property between tenants. The outgoing tenant's deposit is being used for damage repairs unrelated to flooring. Can I also claim a portion of the deposit for tile replacement that was necessitated by the tenant damaging the original tiles?
**Answer:** Yes, provided the tile damage exceeds fair wear and tear. Under the Rental Housing Act and standard lease terms: (1) Fair wear and tear (gradual deterioration from normal use) is the landlord's cost — you cannot claim from the deposit for tiles that simply reached end of reasonable life; (2) Damage caused by tenant negligence (dropping heavy objects, water damage from unreported leaks, improper cleaning with harsh chemicals) can be claimed from the deposit; (3) You must provide written evidence: photos of damage, quotes for repair/replacement, and an assessment distinguishing damage from wear and tear; (4) The tenant has the right to be present during the exit inspection. Deposit claims must be finalised within 14 days of lease termination. If the deposit is insufficient for all claims, you can pursue the tenant for the balance — but for amounts below R20,000, the cost of recovery often exceeds the claim. Focus on clear documentation of tenant-caused vs. wear-and-tear damage.

#### Q144: My rental property tenant has been in place for 5 years. The tiles are worn but functional. The tenant claims this is "fair wear and tear" and I must replace them at my cost. Who decides what constitutes fair wear and tear for floor tiles?
**Answer:** Fair wear and tear is determined by an objective standard — what deterioration would reasonably occur over the specific period with normal use by a reasonable tenant. For floor tiles over 5 years: (1) Grout discolouration, minor surface scratches, and slight gloss reduction on polished tiles = fair wear and tear (landlord's cost); (2) Cracked tiles from dropped objects, chipped edges, stains from unreported spills, or lifting due to tenant-caused water damage = tenant liability; (3) Worn traffic patterns in high-use areas (kitchen, hallway) = fair wear and tear; (4) Damage from tenant's pets or unauthorised modifications = tenant liability. If you dispute the tenant's classification, the Rental Housing Tribunal can adjudicate. For a 5-year residential lease, significant tile replacement is typically landlord's cost unless specific tenant negligence is proven. Budget for flooring replacement every 7-10 years in rental properties as a standard capital expense.

#### Q145: I'm a landlord renovating between tenants. The lease says the tenant must allow access for "showings to prospective tenants." Can I show the property while tiles are being installed — dust, noise, and half-finished floors?
**Answer:** You can schedule showings during renovation, but practically, it's counterproductive. Prospective tenants seeing a construction site will either be deterred or demand rent reductions. Legally: (1) The outgoing tenant (if still in occupation) must allow reasonable access for showings, but "reasonable" doesn't include during active construction — the tenant can argue this interferes with their quiet enjoyment; (2) For vacant properties, there is no tenant to restrict access — show as you wish; (3) If the property is managed by an agent, the agent should advise on optimal showing timing. Best practice: complete the renovation, have the property professionally cleaned, then show it. The rental income lost from a 2-week delay is usually less than the rent reduction you'll need to offer for a property shown mid-construction. First impressions are everything in rental marketing.

#### Q146: My tenant installed adhesive vinyl tiles over my ceramic floor tiles without permission. When she vacates, the vinyl adhesive has left a residue that's impossible to remove without damaging the ceramic tiles underneath. Can I claim the full tile replacement cost from her deposit?
**Answer:** Yes. Unauthorised installation of vinyl tiles over your property is a lease breach, and the resulting damage (adhesive residue that damages the original tiles during removal) is tenant-caused damage, not fair wear and tear. Your claim: (1) Cost of professional adhesive removal attempt; (2) If removal damages the ceramic tiles, full replacement cost; (3) Or, if the vinyl tiles can be left in place and the original tiles are undamaged, cost of professional removal and cleaning only. Document the condition before and after with photos. The tenant may argue the vinyl tiles "protected" your tiles — this argument fails if the adhesive residue causes damage. Professional adhesive removal from ceramic tiles costs R100-R250/m² and doesn't always work. Include a specific "no adhesives or overlays" clause in future leases.

#### Q147: I'm selling a tenanted property. The buyer wants to retile before taking occupation, but the tenant has 8 months remaining on the lease. Can the buyer do this while the tenant is still in place?
**Answer:** The buyer inherits the existing lease (huur gaat voor koop — lease takes precedence over sale). The buyer cannot retile while the tenant is in occupation without the tenant's consent. The tenant has the right to quiet enjoyment for the lease term. Options: (1) The buyer must wait until lease termination; (2) Negotiate with the tenant for early termination — offer compensation (moving costs, rent refund) for the inconvenience; (3) The buyer purchases subject to the lease and renovates after vacancy; (4) If the existing tiles are genuinely damaged and require replacement for habitability, this is maintenance that the landlord (now the buyer) can undertake with reasonable notice — but the tenant can still object to timing and disruption. Most sale agreements for tenanted properties include a clause acknowledging the lease terms. If the buyer didn't realise this, they have no recourse against you unless you misrepresented the lease terms.

#### Q148: My tenant wants to renew the lease for another 2 years, but I was planning to renovate (including retiling) and increase the rent significantly. Do I have to renew the lease, and can I non-renew to renovate?
**Answer:** Unless your lease has an automatic renewal clause, you are not obligated to renew. Under the Rental Housing Act: (1) Either party can terminate the lease at its natural expiry by giving appropriate notice (typically 1-2 months as specified in the lease); (2) You do not need a reason to non-renew — "not renewing for renovation" is a legitimate business decision; (3) You cannot terminate specifically to discriminate against a tenant (e.g., racial discrimination) — this would be an unfair practice; (4) Provide proper written notice of non-renewal within the timeframe specified in the lease. The tenant may be upset, especially if they've been reliable, but you have no legal obligation to renew. Once the tenant vacates, you can renovate and re-let at market rates. Just ensure your non-renewal notice is in writing, within the lease timeframe, and doesn't reference prohibited grounds (race, family status, disability).

#### Q149: I'm renovating a rental property and the contractor damaged the tenant's personal property (furniture scratched by tile cutting dust). Who is liable — me as landlord, or the contractor?
**Answer:** Both you and the contractor may be liable. As landlord, you have a contractual duty to the tenant to ensure their property is not damaged by your agents (contractors). The contractor has a direct duty of care to the tenant under delict. The tenant can sue either or both of you. Your best course: (1) The tenant claims against your contractor's public liability insurance (if they have it) — this is the most direct route; (2) If the contractor is uninsured, you may need to compensate the tenant and claim indemnification from the contractor; (3) Your landlord insurance may cover this if you have tenant liability cover. Prevention: (1) Always require the tenant to remove or cover valuables before renovation; (2) The contractor must provide full dust and damage protection; (3) Photograph the tenant's belongings before work starts. The tenant's household insurance may also cover this — they should claim from their insurer who then subrogates.

#### Q150: The Rental Housing Tribunal has ruled that I must retile my rental property's bathroom because the existing tiles present a "health hazard" due to mould. Can I appeal this ruling, and how long do I have to comply?
**Answer:** Tribunal rulings can be appealed to the High Court within 30 days of the ruling, but appeals are expensive (R50,000+) and rarely succeed unless there was a clear legal error or procedural unfairness. If the mould hazard is genuine (visible black mould, failed waterproofing), the Tribunal's ruling will likely stand. Compliance timelines: (1) The Tribunal's order will specify a deadline — typically 30-60 days; (2) If you fail to comply, the tenant can apply for a writ of execution; (3) The Tribunal can also impose administrative fines for non-compliance. Your options: (1) Comply with the order — it's usually cheaper than appealing; (2) If the mould is tenant-caused (poor ventilation, unreported leaks), gather evidence and argue this in your appeal — but the appeal timeline is short and costly; (3) Negotiate a phased compliance plan with the tenant through the Tribunal. The lesson: address maintenance complaints promptly before they escalate to Tribunal rulings. A Tribunal ruling also becomes public record and may affect your reputation as a landlord.

### Section 13: Personal Crisis Mid-Renovation

#### Q151: My husband and I are divorcing mid-renovation. We've paid R380,000 for tiles and materials that are stored in the garage. Who owns these materials — the person who paid for them, or is it split as part of the joint estate?
**Answer:** Under SA matrimonial property law, the answer depends on your marital regime: (1) In community of property: all assets acquired during marriage form part of the joint estate, regardless of who paid. The tiles are joint property and must be divided as part of the divorce settlement. One spouse can buy out the other's half-share, or the materials can be sold and proceeds split; (2) Out of community of property: the spouse who paid owns the materials, but if the property itself is jointly owned, the materials are likely considered improvements to joint property; (3) With accrual: the value of the materials affects the accrual calculation. In practice: (1) If one spouse will retain the house, they typically take the materials and adjust the property division accordingly; (2) If the house is being sold, the materials can be sold with the property (adding value) or sold separately; (3) If neither wants the materials, sell them and split proceeds. Do not let the materials deteriorate during divorce proceedings — you have a duty to preserve marital assets. Document the inventory and value for the settlement.

#### Q152: My renovation partner (we co-own the property as joint tenants) has died. The renovation is half-complete. His heirs want the renovation stopped and the property sold as-is. I want to complete it. Who decides?
**Answer:** This is complex. Joint tenancy includes the right of survivorship — upon death, the deceased's share automatically passes to the surviving joint tenant (you), not to his heirs. However: (1) Verify the ownership structure at the Deeds Office — if you're tenants in common (not joint tenants), the deceased's share forms part of his estate and passes to his heirs; (2) If joint tenants: you now own the property solely and can complete the renovation without the heirs' consent; (3) If tenants in common: the heirs become co-owners with you, and decisions about the property require agreement. If the heirs (now co-owners) want to sell and you want to continue, you may need to: (a) Buy out their share; (b) Apply for a partition order (court-ordered sale); (c) Reach a negotiated compromise. The renovation costs already paid by the deceased may be claimable against his estate. Engage an estate attorney immediately — the ownership structure determines everything.

#### Q153: I lost my job two weeks into a R600,000 renovation. I've already paid the 50% deposit. Can I legally pause the contract, and what are my rights regarding materials already delivered?
**Answer:** Standard construction contracts do not include a "job loss" termination right. Your position: (1) Review the contract for termination clauses — most allow termination by the owner with notice, but you forfeit the deposit or pay a cancellation fee (typically 10-25% of contract value); (2) You can request the contractor pause work, but they are not obligated to agree — the contract remains enforceable; (3) Materials already delivered and cut to size are typically yours (paid for from the deposit); (4) Materials ordered but not yet delivered may be cancellable with supplier restocking fees. Practical steps: (1) Negotiate a payment plan with the contractor — many will accommodate genuine hardship rather than pursue a client with no assets; (2) Pause non-essential phases and complete the renovation in stages as funds become available; (3) Consider a personal loan to complete the renovation — a half-finished renovation typically reduces property value more than the cost of completion; (4) If you must terminate, understand the financial consequences before acting — a signed contract is legally binding regardless of your employment status.

#### Q154: My elderly mother was living in the granny flat we're renovating. She has moved to a care facility and may not return. Should we complete the renovation for rental purposes, or stop now? The tiles have already been delivered.
**Answer:** This is a financial decision with legal considerations: (1) If you've already paid deposits and materials have been delivered, stopping now means sunk costs with no return — the materials will deteriorate in storage and lose value; (2) Completing the renovation for rental purposes generates income that may help fund your mother's care costs — this is a legitimate use of the property; (3) If your mother is mentally competent, she should consent to the change of use; (4) If she lacks capacity, the consent of her appointed curator or holder of power of attorney is required; (5) If the granny flat was specifically bequeathed to her or held in a trust for her benefit, the trustees must approve the change of use. The delivered tiles are a sunk cost — completing the renovation is almost always the better financial decision. Even if you don't rent it out immediately, a completed flat adds property value; a half-renovated flat detracts from it. Consult your mother's attorney or the Master's Office if capacity issues exist.

#### Q155: My partner and I are splitting up. We jointly own the property but the renovation contract is only in my name. Is my ex-partner liable for the remaining contractor payments even though they're not on the contract?
**Answer:** Contractual liability and property ownership are separate. If the renovation contract is solely in your name, you are solely liable to the contractor for payment — the contractor cannot sue your ex-partner under the contract. However: (1) If your ex-partner co-owns the property, they have benefited from the renovation (increased property value) and you may have a claim for unjust enrichment against them for their share of the value increase; (2) If you were married in community of property, the contractor can claim against the joint estate regardless of whose name is on the contract; (3) If unmarried co-owners, the financial settlement between you should account for renovation expenditure — this is typically resolved in the co-ownership dissolution agreement. Do not stop paying the contractor because of the relationship breakdown — your contractual obligations continue regardless. Address the interpersonal financial settlement separately from the contractor payments.

#### Q156: I've been diagnosed with a terminal illness and want to ensure my half-finished renovation is completed for my family's benefit. Can I write a clause in my will directing my executor to complete the renovation using estate funds?
**Answer:** Yes, you can include a specific direction in your will for the executor to complete the renovation using estate assets. However: (1) The executor has discretion — they must act in the best interests of the estate and beneficiaries, not blindly follow instructions that may be imprudent; (2) If completing the renovation increases the estate's value (net of costs), the executor will likely comply; (3) If the renovation cost exceeds the value added, the executor may decline as it would disadvantage beneficiaries; (4) Provide specific instructions: contractor details, estimated completion cost, payment schedule, and your assessment of the value increase; (5) Ensure your executor has the contact details for the contractor and suppliers; (6) Consider a living trust or specific bequest of renovation funds rather than a will instruction — this gives more certainty. Discuss this with your estate planning attorney to structure it effectively. The key principle: executor discretion can override will instructions if they're not in the estate's best interests.

#### Q157: My renovation was halted when I was hospitalised unexpectedly for 6 weeks. The contractor says he's moving to another job and will return "when convenient." Can he do this, and what about my materials sitting on site?
**Answer:** This depends on your contract. Most contracts specify: (1) A project timeline with start and completion dates; (2) Provisions for delay — who bears the risk of owner-caused delays; (3) Your hospitalisation is an "owner-caused delay" — the contractor is not obligated to hold his crew indefinitely. Your rights: (1) The contractor must secure the site and protect materials before demobilising; (2) Materials on site remain your property — the contractor has a duty of care as bailee; (3) The contractor cannot abandon the project entirely — he must return to complete within a reasonable time or formally terminate the contract; (4) If the contractor refuses to return, you can engage another contractor and claim damages (additional costs) from the original contractor if their refusal was unreasonable. Practical approach: negotiate a restart date, offer to cover reasonable standby costs (R2,000-R5,000 per week) to hold the slot, or accept demobilisation with a guaranteed restart date. Materials should be stored securely — discuss with the contractor who has site access during the delay.

#### Q158: I'm the executor of my late brother's estate. He was mid-renovation with no contract — just verbal agreements and WhatsApp messages with the contractor. The contractor claims R180,000 for "work done" but has no invoices or timesheets. What do I pay?
**Answer:** As executor, you must pay legitimate estate debts but are not obligated to pay unsubstantiated claims. The contractor's verbal/Whatsapp agreement is legally binding (oral contracts are valid), but the burden of proof is on the contractor to demonstrate the work done and its value. Your process: (1) Request detailed substantiation: itemised work description, dates, hours, materials used, rates agreed; (2) Verify the work was actually done — inspect the property, photograph everything; (3) Engage a quantity surveyor or independent contractor to value the work (R3,000-R8,000) — this provides an objective benchmark; (4) If the contractor's claim exceeds the independent valuation, offer the independently assessed amount; (5) If no agreement is reached, the contractor must sue the estate — but without documentation, their claim is weak. WhatsApp messages can serve as evidence of the agreement and some work scope, but without timesheets or material receipts, quantifying the claim is difficult. Pay only what can be reasonably substantiated. The Master's Office can provide guidance on estate debt verification.

#### Q159: I'm pregnant and the tile adhesive fumes from the bathroom renovation are making me sick. The contractor says low-VOC adhesive is "too expensive" and refuses to use it. Can I force this change, and who pays the difference?
**Answer:** You cannot "force" a change unless your contract specifies low-VOC products. However: (1) Standard tile adhesives do emit VOCs (volatile organic compounds) that can cause nausea, headaches, and respiratory irritation — pregnant women are more sensitive; (2) Under OHSA and common law, the contractor must provide a safe working environment, and by extension, should not create health hazards for occupants; (3) If you vacate during installation, the health concern is mitigated, but the contractor should still use appropriate products; (4) Low-VOC adhesives cost 20-40% more but are widely available (Mapei Keraflex, Lanko). Your options: (1) Offer to pay the difference — it's typically R20-R50 per m², a small cost for health peace of mind; (2) Vacate during the work and for 48 hours after; (3) If the contractor absolutely refuses, engage another contractor who will comply. The health of you and your pregnancy overrides cost considerations — the price difference is negligible in the context of a full renovation. Do not expose yourself to VOCs during pregnancy if avoidable.

#### Q160: My therapist has recommended that I pause my renovation because the stress is severely affecting my mental health. Can I use this as grounds to terminate the renovation contract without penalty?
**Answer:** Unfortunately, mental health stress from renovation is not a recognised legal ground for contract termination without consequences. A renovation contract is a binding commercial agreement, and personal stress does not create a right of cancellation. Your options: (1) Review the contract for termination clauses — most allow termination with a cancellation fee (10-25% of contract value); (2) Negotiate a pause rather than termination — most contractors will accommodate a reasonable pause, especially if you offer a holding fee; (3) If the stress is genuinely clinical, consult your attorney about whether you lacked contractual capacity when signing — but this is a high bar requiring medical evidence that you couldn't understand the contract's nature; (4) Consider assigning the contract to a family member or friend who can oversee completion. Your mental health is important — discuss a pause with your contractor and focus on self-care during the project. A good contractor will work with you on scheduling to minimise disruption.

### Section 14: Contractor Disputes, Bankruptcy & Legal Recourse

#### Q161: I paid a 50% deposit (R185,000) to a tiling contractor who has now gone into business rescue. The work hasn't started. Where do I stand in the business rescue proceedings — am I a secured or unsecured creditor?
**Answer:** As a prepaying customer, you are an unsecured creditor in the business rescue proceedings. This is the worst position to be in — secured creditors (banks, bondholders) and employees are paid first, and unsecured creditors typically receive cents on the rand, if anything. Your position: (1) File a claim with the business rescue practitioner immediately — you have a limited time to lodge claims (typically 30 days of the business rescue notice); (2) You are not a "preferent creditor" — deposits for work not done rank equally with other trade creditors; (3) If the contractor was an NHBRC-registered home builder, you may have limited recourse through the NHBRC's warranty fund, but this typically covers structural defects, not deposit losses; (4) If the business rescue fails and liquidation follows, you become a concurrent creditor in the liquidation — expect minimal recovery. Prevention: never pay more than 10-15% deposit unless the contract is backed by a bank guarantee or the contractor is a large, established company. For R185,000, always use an escrow account or bank-guaranteed payment structure.

#### Q162: My contractor completed the tiling but the finish is unacceptable — lippage, uneven grout lines, cracked tiles. He's demanding final payment and threatening to lien the property. Can he do this, and what should I pay?
**Answer:** Under common law, a contractor has a builder's lien (ius retentionis) over the property for work done until paid. However: (1) The lien applies only to the value of work properly completed — defective work does not attract a lien; (2) You can withhold payment proportionate to the defect severity — a reasonable amount reflecting the cost of rectification; (3) The contractor can register a lien against the property title, which prevents sale until resolved; (4) You should obtain an independent assessment of the defects and rectification cost (R3,000-R8,000 from a forensic tiler or quantity surveyor). Your process: (1) Document all defects with photos and video; (2) Get an independent rectification quote; (3) Offer to pay the contract balance less the rectification cost, held in escrow until the contractor rectifies or you engage another contractor; (4) If the contractor refuses and registers a lien, you can apply to court for its removal or pay the undisputed portion into court. Do not pay 100% for defective work — you lose all leverage for rectification.

#### Q163: My contractor was not registered with the CIDB but advertised himself as a "professional tiler." The work is defective. Does his lack of CIDB registration affect my legal claim against him?
**Answer:** CIDB registration is required for contractors working on construction projects above specified thresholds (currently R200,000 for Grade 1). For residential tiling below this threshold, CIDB registration is not legally required, so lack of registration doesn't automatically strengthen your claim. However: (1) Advertising as "professional" without the competence to back it up may constitute misrepresentation under the CPA; (2) Regardless of registration, the contractor is liable for negligent or defective work under common law (contractual and delictual claims); (3) If the contractor claimed NHBRC registration but isn't registered, this is a more serious misrepresentation with potential criminal elements (fraud). Your claim for defective work is based on: breach of contract (failure to perform to standard), negligence (falling below reasonable professional standard), and CPA rights (services must be performed in a proper manner). CIDB status is irrelevant to these claims. Focus on the quality of work, not the registration status, unless he made false claims about it.

#### Q164: My contractor sub-contracted the tiling to another tiler without my knowledge or consent. The sub-contractor's work is defective. Who is liable — the main contractor or the sub-contractor?
**Answer:** The main contractor is liable to you. Under the common law of contract, the main contractor cannot delegate their contractual obligations without your consent — "delegatus non potest delegare" (a delegate cannot delegate). The sub-contractor's defective work is the main contractor's responsibility. Your rights: (1) Demand rectification from the main contractor — they must fix the defects or engage someone who can; (2) The main contractor then claims against the sub-contractor for their negligence — this is not your concern; (3) If the main contractor refuses, withhold payment, obtain independent rectification quotes, and pursue the main contractor for the cost. The sub-contractor has no direct contractual relationship with you (unless they were novated into the contract, which requires your consent). Your legal claim is solely against the main contractor. This is why vetting the main contractor matters — you have no direct recourse against their sub-contractor choices.

#### Q165: I discovered my contractor is using undocumented foreign workers. He says they are "sub-contractors" not employees. I'm worried about liability if they're injured on my property. Should I be concerned?
**Answer:** Yes. The distinction between employees and sub-contractors is legally significant: (1) If they are employees, the contractor must register them for UIF, PAYE, and COIDA — failure is a criminal offence; (2) If they are genuine independent sub-contractors, they should have their own tax registration and insurance; (3) If injured on your property: under COIDA, employees of a registered employer cannot sue the employer but claim from the Compensation Fund — if the employer didn't register them, they can sue both the employer and potentially you as the "occupier" who facilitated the employment; (4) Under common law, you as property owner owe a duty of care to all persons lawfully on your property. Your risk is highest if the workers are actually employees misclassified as sub-contractors — the Department of Labour actively prosecutes this. Protect yourself: (1) Ask your contractor for proof of UIF and COIDA registration for all workers; (2) If he refuses, this is a red flag — consider terminating the contract; (3) Your homeowner's public liability insurance may not cover incidents involving illegal workers.

#### Q166: My contractor disappeared with my R90,000 deposit. SAPS says it's a "civil matter" and won't open a case. The contractor isn't responding to lawyers' letters. Is there anything else I can do?
**Answer:** SAPS often incorrectly classifies contractor fraud as civil. If the contractor took your deposit with no intention of performing the work (never ordered materials, never scheduled crew, has done this to other clients), this is fraud (criminal) under the Prevention of Organised Crime Act if part of a pattern, or common law theft by false pretences. Your escalation route: (1) File a complaint with the NHBRC — they investigate fraudulent builders; (2) Report to the CIDB if the contractor claimed registration; (3) File a complaint with the Consumer Tribunal under CPA Section 41 for false representations; (4) If SAPS refuses to open a case, escalate to the SAPS Provincial Commissioner or the Independent Police Investigative Directorate (IPID) for dereliction of duty; (5) For significant amounts (R90,000 qualifies), open a private criminal prosecution — engage an attorney; (6) Check if other victims exist — multiple complaints trigger P priority. Also: search the contractor on the High Court judgments website — if they have multiple judgments against them, this supports a fraud argument. Act quickly — fraudulent contractors often dissipate assets.

#### Q167: My contractor and I had a verbal agreement only. Now he's claiming extras I never agreed to. He says "industry standard" justifies the charges. Without a written contract, how do I dispute this?
**Answer:** Verbal contracts are legally valid but difficult to prove. Your position: (1) Document everything you can recall about the original agreement — scope, price, inclusions/exclusions; (2) Gather any corroborating evidence — WhatsApp messages, emails, witness statements, bank transfer descriptions; (3) "Industry standard" charges must be reasonable and customary — engage a quantity surveyor to benchmark the claimed extras against market rates (R3,000-R8,000); (4) Under common law, extras require express or implied agreement — the contractor cannot unilaterally add charges; (5) If the extras were genuinely necessary but you weren't consulted, the contractor should have stopped work and requested instruction — proceeding without instruction weakens their claim. Your defence: offer to pay only the originally agreed amount plus extras that were reasonably necessary and properly notified. If the contractor disputes, the onus is on them to prove the extras were agreed — without written evidence, their claim is weak. For future: always use written contracts — the NHBRC provides standard home building contracts free of charge.

#### Q168: My contractor's surety bond (performance guarantee) has expired mid-project, and the bank won't renew it because of a credit issue with the contractor. Should I halt the project until a new bond is in place?
**Answer:** Yes. A performance guarantee protects you if the contractor defaults — without it, your deposit and progress payments are at risk. If the contractor cannot maintain a performance guarantee, this signals financial distress. Your options: (1) Halt payments until a replacement guarantee is provided — this is a material breach of contract if the guarantee was a condition; (2) Request the contractor provide an alternative form of security (bank guarantee, parent company guarantee, retention holdback); (3) If no replacement is forthcoming, consider terminating the contract for breach and engaging another contractor — but understand the financial implications; (4) Reduce payment terms to weekly with verification of work completed before each payment, minimising your exposure. A contractor who cannot maintain a surety bond is a contractor in financial trouble — proceed with extreme caution. The expired bond is a warning sign that should not be ignored.

#### Q169: My contractor installed tiles over a weekend while I was away. When I returned, I found he'd used a different, cheaper tile than specified. He says the specified tiles were "out of stock" and he "did me a favour" with a "similar" alternative. What are my rights?
**Answer:** The contractor had no authority to substitute materials without your consent. This is a breach of contract and potentially a CPA violation (Section 41: false representations if he invoices for the specified tile while installing a cheaper one). Your rights: (1) Demand removal of the incorrect tiles and installation of the specified tiles at the contractor's cost; (2) If the alternative is genuinely acceptable to you, negotiate a price reduction reflecting the cost difference plus compensation for the unauthorised substitution; (3) If the contractor refuses, withhold payment, obtain independent quotes for removal and reinstallation, and claim these costs plus damages from the contractor; (4) Report to the NHBRC if the contractor is registered — unauthorised material substitution is a serious professional misconduct. The "out of stock" excuse does not justify unilateral substitution — the contractor should have contacted you for instruction. Document the installed tiles (photos, product codes) and compare to the specification. If the contractor charged you for the specified tile but installed a cheaper one, this is fraud — consider criminal charges.

#### Q170: I'm in a dispute with my contractor and have stopped paying. He's now threatened to remove the tiles he's already installed if I don't pay. Can he legally do this?
**Answer:** No. Once tiles are affixed to your property, they become part of the immovable property by accession (accesio) — they are no longer the contractor's movable property and cannot be "repossessed." The contractor's only remedy for non-payment is: (1) A builder's lien over the property for the value of work done; (2) A court action for payment; (3) Arbitration if the contract provides for it. Removing installed tiles would constitute: (1) Malicious damage to property (criminal); (2) Trespass; (3) Breach of contract. If the contractor attempts removal, contact SAPS immediately and seek an urgent interdict preventing further damage. However: (1) Materials delivered but not yet installed remain the contractor's property until paid for — they can remove uninstalled materials; (2) Tools and equipment on site remain the contractor's property. Your best course: hold the disputed amount in an escrow account or pay it into the trust account of your attorney pending resolution — this demonstrates good faith while protecting your position.

### Section 15: Coastal Climate, Damp & Mould

#### Q171: I'm renovating a 1980s Durban beachfront apartment. When we removed the old tiles, the entire subfloor was covered in black mould. The body corporate says the waterproofing membrane failed. Who pays for the remediation — me, the body corporate, or both?
**Answer:** This depends on what failed and where. In a sectional title: (1) If the waterproofing failure was in the original building membrane (part of the structure), the body corporate is responsible for repair under PMR 68(1)(iii) and Section 37(1)(b) of the ST Act — waterproofing of common property is a body corporate maintenance obligation; (2) If the waterproofing failure resulted from your alterations, previous owner's modifications, or a failed bathroom seal, you are responsible; (3) The mould remediation itself typically falls on whoever caused the conditions that allowed mould growth.
