#### Q: 44 My labourer has been with me for 6 months, coming in 3-4 days a week. He now says he's a permanent employee and demands paid annual leave. Is he right?
**Answer:** Under the Basic Conditions of Employment Act (BCEA), an employee is entitled to 21 consecutive days of annual leave (or 1 day for every 17 days worked) after completing 12 months of continuous service. However, "continuous service" is broadly interpreted — if there was a reasonable expectation of ongoing work, the periods link together. More importantly, if he works regularly and has a reasonable expectation of continued employment, he may argue he is a permanent employee under the Labour Relations Act, entitled to all BCEA benefits including sick leave, family responsibility leave, and notice pay. At 6 months, he does not yet qualify for annual leave under the 12-month rule, but he does qualify for sick leave (1 day for every 26 days worked in the first 6-month cycle) and family responsibility leave (3 days per year).

#### Q: 45 I fired a casual labourer because he kept arriving drunk. He's now taken me to the CCMA for unfair dismissal. But he's casual — can he even do that?
**Answer:** Yes. Any employee — permanent, temporary, or casual — who works more than 24 hours per month and has been employed for more than 4 months has the right to refer an unfair dismissal dispute to the CCMA under the Labour Relations Act. Even employees below the 24-hour/4-month thresholds can challenge dismissal if it was automatically unfair (e.g., based on race, gender, pregnancy, or trade union activity). For drunkenness, you must follow a fair procedure: investigate the allegations, give the employee a chance to respond, consider alternatives to dismissal (warning, suspension), and then decide. Summary dismissal for a first offence is generally unfair unless the misconduct is gross (e.g., endangering safety). At the CCMA, you'll need to prove both substantive fairness (the misconduct occurred) and procedural fairness (proper process was followed).

#### Q: 46 I don't give payslips to my casual workers because they're paid cash. A labour inspector visited my site and said this is illegal. Is it?
**Answer:** Yes. Section 33 of the BCEA requires every employer to provide a written payslip to every employee at each pay interval, regardless of payment method or employment status. The payslip must include: employer name and address, employee name and job, period of payment, total money earned, deductions (with reasons), actual amount paid, and UIF contribution details. Failure to provide payslips is an offence punishable by a fine (Schedule 1 of the BCEA — up to R1,500 per employee for a first offence, escalating for repeat offences). The cash payment method is legal, but the payslip requirement is not waived. Implement a simple handwritten or printed payslip system immediately.

#### Q: 47 My tiler is an independent contractor who invoices me monthly. Do I need to deduct PAYE from his payments, or is he responsible for his own tax?
**Answer:** If he is genuinely an independent contractor (uses his own tools, works for multiple clients, controls how and when he works, bears his own financial risk), you do not deduct PAYE. He is responsible for his own tax (provisional tax if applicable) and should invoice you with his tax number. However, if SARS determines he is actually your employee (the "deemed employee" provision under the Fourth Schedule), you become liable for PAYE, UIF, and SDL on all payments made to him. The key is the substance of the relationship. To protect yourself, ensure he provides invoices, uses his own equipment, works for other contractors too, and is registered for tax. Keep copies of his tax clearance and SARS correspondence.

#### Q: 48 One of my workers is HIV-positive and is frequently sick. He's missing 2-3 days per month. Can I dismiss him for poor attendance, or is that automatically unfair discrimination?
**Answer:** Dismissing someone because of their HIV status is automatically unfair under the Labour Relations Act (Section 187) and constitutes unfair discrimination under the Employment Equity Act and the Code of Good Practice on Key Aspects of HIV/AIDS. However, dismissal for incapacity due to genuine poor performance or attendance is possible if you follow the incapacity procedure: (a) investigate the reasons for absence, (b) give the employee a chance to respond, (c) consider reasonable accommodation (e.g., lighter duties, adjusted hours), (d) obtain medical reports with the employee's consent, (e) explore alternatives to dismissal. If, after reasonable accommodation, the employee cannot perform the essential functions of the job, dismissal may be fair — but this is a high threshold. Consult the CCMA code of good practice on dismissal for incapacity. Never dismiss without following this procedure.

#### Q: 49 I employ 3 permanent staff. Am I required to have a written employment contract with each of them, or is a verbal agreement enough?
**Answer:** Section 29 of the BCEA requires every employer to provide written particulars of employment to every employee within the first day of employment if they work more than 24 hours per month. These particulars must include: full name and address of employer, employee's name and occupation, place of work, date of employment, hours and days of work, remuneration and payment frequency, leave entitlements, notice period, and description of any council or sectoral determination. A formal written contract is not legally required (verbal contracts are valid), but the written particulars are mandatory. In practice, a comprehensive written employment contract protects both parties and prevents disputes. There is no cost to drafting a basic contract — Labour Department templates are available online.

#### Q: 50 A labourer walked off my site with R4,200 worth of my tools. Can I deduct the value from his final wages?
**Answer:** No. Section 34 of the BCEA prohibits an employer from making any deduction from an employee's remuneration without the employee's written consent, except for statutory deductions (PAYE, UIF, garnishee orders). Deduction for loss or damage is only permitted if: (a) the loss or damage occurred in the course of employment and was due to the employee's fault, (b) the employer has followed a fair enquiry, (c) the deduction does not exceed 25% of the employee's remuneration, and (d) the employee has given written consent. Unilateral deduction constitutes an unlawful deduction, and the employee can report you to the labour inspector or CCMA. Your remedy is to lay criminal charges (theft) and/or sue for damages in the Magistrate's Court — not to withhold wages.

#### Q: 51 I'm a small contractor. Do I need to register for COIDA if I have no employees and only use subcontractors?
**Answer:** If you genuinely have no employees and all workers are independent subcontractors, COIDA registration is not required for employee coverage — but the Compensation Commissioner may still require registration if you engage "deemed employees." However, your subcontractors should have their own COIDA registration. More importantly, if a subcontractor's employee is injured on your site, the question of who is the "employer" for COIDA purposes can be complex. The safest approach is to: (a) verify each subcontractor's COIDA letter of good standing before engagement, (b) include an indemnity clause in your subcontractor agreement, (c) consider public liability insurance that covers subcontractor-related incidents. If you directly control and supervise the subcontractor's workers, you may be deemed their employer for COIDA purposes.

#### Q: 52 My employee was injured on site. I took him to a private hospital because the public hospital was too far. Can I claim these medical costs back from the Compensation Commissioner?
**Answer:** Under COIDA, the Compensation Commissioner determines which medical practitioner the employee may consult (usually a public hospital or designated doctor). If you unilaterally took him to a private hospital, the Commissioner may not reimburse those costs unless the private treatment was necessary due to an emergency and no public facility was accessible. You must report the injury to the Commissioner within 7 days (Form W.CL.2) and the employee must submit a claim (Form W.CL.1). Authorised medical costs are then paid directly by the Commissioner. Your upfront payment does not guarantee reimbursement. Always report injuries immediately and follow the Commissioner's procedures.

#### Q: 53 I want to employ a Zimbabwean national who has a valid Zimbabwe Exemption Permit (ZEP). Do I need to do anything special regarding labour law and UIF?
**Answer:** Zimbabwean nationals with valid ZEPs (or its successor permits) are legally authorised to work in South Africa. You must: (a) verify the permit is current and valid for the employment period, (b) register them for UIF (they are entitled to UIF benefits), (c) register for COIDA (they are covered), (d) comply with all BCEA requirements. ZEP holders are not "illegal foreigners" under the Immigration Act. However, if their permit expires during employment, you must ensure renewal or terminate employment — continuing to employ someone whose permit has expired becomes a criminal offence. Keep copies of their permit on file.

#### Q: 54 A female employee told me she's pregnant and asked for lighter duties. I'm a small contractor — I can't afford to carry someone doing half the work. What are my obligations?
**Answer:** Under the BCEA and the Code of Good Practice on the Protection of Employees During Pregnancy and After Childbirth, you must reasonably accommodate a pregnant employee's needs. This may include: lighter duties, adjusted hours, or temporary transfer to less physically demanding work. If no suitable alternative work exists, she may be entitled to leave (though maternity leave officially starts 4 weeks before birth). You cannot dismiss her because of pregnancy — this is automatically unfair under Section 187 of the LRA. However, the Code recognises that smaller employers may have limited options for accommodation. Document your efforts to find suitable alternative duties. If accommodation is genuinely impossible, consult the Department of Labour for guidance before taking any adverse action.

#### Q: 55 I work with my brother. We split profits 50/50. Is he my employee, my partner, or a self-employed contractor? How does SARS see this?
**Answer:** SARS looks at substance, not labels. If you make all business decisions, provide all tools and materials, set the work schedule, and your brother simply works under your direction for a share of profits, he is likely your employee or partner in a partnership (not a company). If you are a sole proprietor and he contributes labour for a profit share, this may constitute a partnership under common law — meaning you are jointly liable for all business debts and he is taxed on his share of partnership profits (not as a salaried employee). If there is no partnership agreement and he simply helps out, he may be an employee with PAYE, UIF, and COIDA obligations. The cleanest structure is to formalise: either register a partnership, or employ him formally, or form a Pty Ltd with shareholders' agreement.

#### Q: 56 A labour inspector arrived at my site unannounced. Can I refuse to let him in? What powers does he have?
**Answer:** No, you cannot refuse entry. Section 65 of the BCEA and Section 29 of the OHSA empower labour inspectors to: enter any workplace at any reasonable time without prior notice, inspect records (employment contracts, payslips, UIF records, COIDA registration), interview employees and employers, take photographs and samples, and issue compliance orders. Refusing entry or obstructing an inspector is an offence punishable by a fine or imprisonment. The inspector must produce his or her identity card upon request. Cooperate fully, answer questions honestly, and do not attempt to hide violations — if violations are found, ask for the correction period and comply promptly. Appeals against compliance orders go to the Labour Court.

#### Q: 57 I want to give my employee a company phone for work purposes. Can I make him pay for personal calls, and can I take the phone back if he leaves?
**Answer:** You can require the employee to pay for personal calls provided this is agreed in writing (Section 34 BCEA — written consent for deductions). The phone remains company property and must be returned upon termination of employment — include this in the employment contract or a separate company property agreement. If the employee refuses to return the phone, you can deduct its reasonable value from his final pay (with written consent) or pursue civil recovery. Do not withhold his entire final pay — only the phone's depreciated value, and only with consent or a court order. Best practice: keep an asset register, have employees sign for all company property, and clarify acceptable use policies in writing.

#### Q: 58 My employee asked me for a loan of R5,000. If I give it, can I deduct repayments from his wages?
**Answer:** Yes, but only with his written consent under Section 34 of the BCEA. The deduction for loan repayment must not reduce his remuneration below the minimum wage (if applicable) or below a reasonable living amount. The written consent must specify the amount, frequency, and duration of deductions. A loan agreement should be separate from the employment contract to avoid it being construed as part of remuneration. Do not charge interest unless you are registered as a credit provider under the National Credit Act — informal money lending can trigger NCA compliance requirements. Best practice: formalise the loan with a simple written agreement signed by both parties.

#### Q: 59 I subcontracted a tiler who brought his own assistant. The assistant was injured. Who is responsible — me, the tiler, or both of us?
**Answer:** The primary employer for COIDA purposes is the person who pays the assistant's wages and has direct control over his work — likely the tiler. However, if you exercised significant control over the assistant (gave him instructions, set his hours, provided tools), you may be deemed a "deemed employer" under the OHSA and COIDA. The courts apply a "dual employer" doctrine in some cases. Your protection: (a) verify the tiler's COIDA letter of good standing before engagement, (b) include an indemnity clause in the subcontract, (c) avoid direct supervision of the subcontractor's employees, (d) ensure the subcontractor provides his own PPE. If the assistant sues, both you and the tiler may be joined as respondents.

#### Q: 60 I discovered my employee is working for my competitor on weekends using skills I trained him in. Can I stop this? Do I own the skills I trained?
**Answer:** You cannot own "skills" — once trained, an employee can use those skills anywhere. However, you can restrict competing activities if: (a) the employee's contract includes a restraint of trade clause (limited in time and geographic scope), or (b) the employee is disclosing confidential information (client lists, pricing, techniques). Under common law, an employee owes a duty of good faith — working for a direct competitor using insider knowledge may breach this duty. But moonlighting itself is not unlawful unless it affects performance or involves a conflict of interest. If his weekend work is not affecting his weekday performance and he's not stealing clients, you have limited recourse. Include a reasonable restraint of trade in future employment contracts.
## 4. PAYMENT DISPUTES, BUILDER'S LIEN & SMALL CLAIMS

#### Q: 61 I finished a tiling job and the client is refusing to pay the last R18,000, claiming "the finish isn't perfect." She can't specify any actual defects. What's my fastest legal remedy?
**Answer:** For R18,000 (below the R20,000 Small Claims Court limit), file a claim in the Small Claims Court — no lawyers required, low cost (R100–R200), and typically resolved within 6–8 weeks. Serve a formal Letter of Demand first (required by the Magistrates' Courts Act), giving her 14 days to pay. If she still refuses, file your summons at the Clerk of the Small Claims Court in your jurisdiction. Bring: your quote/contract, proof of completion, photos of the completed work, your invoices, and any correspondence. The commissioner will hear both sides and issue a judgment immediately. If she cannot identify specific defects, her defence will fail. A judgment in your favour is enforceable like any court order.

#### Q: 62 I have a builder's lien over a property where I did R95,000 worth of tiling work and wasn't paid. The client has sold the property to a new buyer. Does my lien survive the sale, or do I lose it?
**Answer:** A builder's lien (ius retentionis) is a real right that attaches to the improved property and binds subsequent purchasers IF it was registered or the purchaser had notice of it. In South Africa, a builder's lien does not need registration to be valid against the original owner, but an unregistered lien is vulnerable to a bona fide purchaser for value without notice. If the sale has already been registered in the Deeds Office and the purchaser had no knowledge of your claim, your lien is likely extinguished — you become an unsecured creditor of the original owner. To prevent this, you should have registered a caveat against the property (a "pending litigation" caveat) at the Deeds Office as soon as payment was withheld. In future, act fast — register a caveat within days of non-payment.

#### Q: 63 The client owes me R32,000. Can I take her straight to the Small Claims Court, or is it too much?
**Answer:** The Small Claims Court has a jurisdictional limit of R20,000. For R32,000, you have three options: (a) abandon the amount above R20,000 and claim only R20,000 in the SCC (you cannot split the claim), (b) issue a summons in the Magistrate's Court (where you can represent yourself for claims under R100,000, or use an attorney), (c) negotiate a settlement for R20,000 to use the SCC route. The Magistrate's Court is more formal but still accessible — you'll need to prepare a summons, serve it via the sheriff, and attend a pre-trial conference. For R32,000, the Magistrate's Court is your proper forum. You can represent yourself (no attorney required for claims under R100,000 in the Regional Court).

#### Q: 64 A client paid me with a cheque that bounced. She then claimed her bank made an error. Two months later, I still don't have my money. What can I do?
**Answer:** A bounced cheque is not a payment — the debt remains outstanding. Write a formal letter of demand including a request for the original amount plus any bank charges you incurred. If she doesn't pay, sue in the Small Claims Court (if under R20,000) or Magistrate's Court. The fact that the cheque bounced is evidence of her inability or unwillingness to pay. You can also consider laying a charge of fraud if you believe she knew the cheque would bounce (Section 26 of the National Payment Systems Act prohibits knowingly drawing cheques without funds). However, criminal proceedings don't get you paid faster — civil action is your primary remedy. Going forward, require EFT payment or cash — avoid cheques from unreliable clients.

#### Q: 65 I agreed to a 10% retention on a R85,000 job. The client is holding R8,500 for "12 months just to be safe." The contract didn't specify a retention period. What's the legal position?
**Answer:** Retention money should be governed by the contract. If no period is specified, the common law implies a "reasonable time" — for tiling work, this is typically the defects liability period specified in the agreement, or 3 months for minor defects. A 12-month retention for tiling work is excessive unless specifically agreed. Demand the release of the retention, giving the client a reasonable opportunity (14 days) to identify any defects. If no defects are reported, the retention must be released. For residential work with no formal contract, a retention clause is unusual — most residential contracts are "pay on completion." If the client refuses, sue for the retention amount. Note: under the JBCC (Joint Building Contracts Committee) agreements, standard retention is 5% with release at practical completion and final completion.

#### Q: 66 A client is offering to pay me in cryptocurrency (Bitcoin) because she says her bank account is frozen. Should I accept this?
**Answer:** While cryptocurrency is not illegal in South Africa, accepting it as payment carries significant risks: (a) SARS treats crypto as an asset, not currency — you must declare the Rand equivalent at the time of receipt as taxable income, (b) the value can fluctuate wildly between receipt and conversion to Rand, (c) if the client's bank account is frozen due to legal proceedings, you may be drawn into those proceedings, (d) there is no chargeback protection if the transaction fails. The SA Reserve Bank has issued warnings about crypto payments. SARB is moving toward regulation but has not yet prohibited crypto transactions. If you accept, document the Rand value at the time of transaction, convert to Rand immediately, and declare it in your tax return. For a small contractor, the administrative complexity and risk likely outweigh any benefit.

#### Q: 67 My client went into debt review under the National Credit Act. I'm owed R22,000. Does debt review mean I can't sue her anymore?
**Answer:** Once a consumer is under debt review, all legal action by credit providers is automatically stayed under Section 88 of the National Credit Act — this includes summonses, judgments, and enforcement. However, the stay only applies if your debt qualifies as a "credit agreement" under the NCA. If your tiling contract was a simple service contract with payment terms (not a credit facility, not a lay-by, not an instalment sale), you may not be a "credit provider" under the NCA and the stay may not apply to you. If the debt review court order has been granted, all creditors (including you) are bound by the restructured payment plan. If no court order has been granted yet, you may be able to apply to the magistrate's court to have your debt excluded from the debt review if it is not an NCA-regulated credit agreement. Consult an attorney — this is complex.

#### Q: 68 I was tiling a flat in a sectional title scheme. The body corporate is now claiming I damaged the common property lift and wants me to pay R15,000. But I never used the lift — my labourers carried tiles up the stairs. Do I owe anything?
**Answer:** If you genuinely did not use the lift and have evidence (witness statements, photos showing tiles carried via stairs), you have no liability. However, the body corporate may claim you caused damage to the stairwell, entrance, or other common areas. Under Section 44 of the Sectional Titles Schemes Management Act, owners (and their invitees, including contractors) are liable for damage caused to common property. If other contractors were working in the building simultaneously, the body corporate must prove you caused the damage. Do not admit liability. Demand specifics: photographs of the damage, repair quotes, and evidence linking the damage to your work. If they cannot prove causation, their claim fails.

#### Q: 69 I quoted a job based on a floor plan the architect provided. On site, the actual area was 40% larger. The client says I must honour the quote. Do I have to?
**Answer:** This depends on the wording of your quote. If your quote stated "as per architect's drawings" and the drawings were incorrect, you have a claim for a variation order — the contract was based on a fundamental misrepresentation (whether innocent or negligent) of the scope. Under the law of contract, if the basis of the contract (the floor area) was materially different from what was represented, you may be entitled to rescind the contract or claim the difference as a quantum meruit (reasonable value of the extra work). However, if your quote was a lump sum for the entire job regardless of area, you may be bound. Always quote per square metre with a clause stating: "Quantities based on provided plans. Variations in actual quantities will be priced at the agreed rate per m²."

#### Q: 70 A client is threatening to post negative reviews about me on social media and HelloPeter unless I give her a R10,000 discount. Is this blackmail, and can I stop her?
**Answer:** This constitutes extortion under common law and potentially criminal intimidation under the Intimidation Act 72 of 1982 — threatening to publish damaging information to extract a financial benefit. If the reviews are false and published, you may also have a civil claim for defamation. Keep all threatening messages (screenshot WhatsApp, save emails) as evidence. Respond in writing stating you will not be extorted and any false reviews will be met with legal action. If she publishes false reviews, you can: (a) report the review platform requesting removal (most platforms have policies against extortion-based reviews), (b) issue a cease-and-desist letter, (c) sue for defamation if actual financial loss results. Do not give the discount — it legitimises the behaviour and encourages repeat demands.

#### Q: 71 I'm owed R14,000 by a client who has now emigrated to Australia. Can I still sue him, and from where?
**Answer:** Yes. You can sue in a South African court and serve the summons outside South Africa under High Court Rule 4 (for the High Court) or Magistrates' Courts Act provisions for service outside the jurisdiction. You need the court's leave to serve outside the Republic, supported by an affidavit explaining why the South African court has jurisdiction (the contract was concluded here, the work was done here, the property is here). Service is effected through the South African diplomatic mission in Australia or via the Hague Service Convention. If you obtain judgment in South Africa, you can enforce it in Australia under reciprocal enforcement agreements. However, the legal costs may exceed R14,000 — consider whether the debt is worth pursuing. If he has assets in South Africa (the property you tiled), pursue those instead.

#### Q: 72 A client wants to pay me in instalments over 6 months. I'm desperate for work and agreed verbally. He paid month 1 and 2, then stopped. What's my position?
**Answer:** A verbal instalment agreement is legally binding if the essential terms (amount, timing, number of instalments) were agreed. His failure to pay subsequent instalments is a breach of contract. You can sue for the full outstanding balance immediately — an instalment agreement typically contains an acceleration clause (if one instalment is missed, the full balance becomes due), or this is implied at common law. Issue a letter of demand for the full balance. If he doesn't pay, proceed to the Small Claims Court (if under R20,000). His partial payment (months 1 and 2) is evidence that an agreement existed and that he acknowledged the debt — this strengthens your case. In future, always put instalment agreements in writing.

#### Q: 73 I signed a contract that says "payment within 30 days of invoice." It's been 60 days. Does the contract mean I have to wait indefinitely, or can I take action now?
**Answer:** The 30-day payment term is a contractual obligation. Once 30 days have passed without payment, the client is in breach of contract. You are not required to wait indefinitely. After the 30-day period expires: (a) issue a letter of demand giving 7 additional days, (b) if still unpaid, charge interest at the agreed rate (or at the SARS prescribed rate if no rate is specified), (c) file in the Small Claims Court or Magistrate's Court. Under the Prescribed Rate of Interest Act, you are entitled to interest on the debt from the date of breach. The National Credit Act may also apply if this is a credit transaction. Do not continue working for a client who has breached payment terms — suspend work until payment is brought current.

#### Q: 74 A client's son — who is a lawyer — is demanding I reduce my invoice by 30% or he'll "make my life difficult with regulatory bodies." I did nothing wrong. How do I handle this?
**Answer:** This is intimidation and potentially extortion. A lawyer making threats to abuse regulatory processes for personal gain can be reported to the Legal Practice Council for professional misconduct. Document the threat in writing (save all messages). Respond professionally: state that your invoice reflects the agreed scope of work, that you stand by the quality of your work, and that you will not be intimidated into reducing a legitimate invoice. If he files a complaint with the CIDB, NHBRC, or SARS, cooperate fully — if you have done nothing wrong, the complaint will be dismissed, and you may have a claim for malicious prosecution or abuse of process. Issue your letter of demand and proceed to court if payment is not forthcoming.

#### Q: 75 I worked on a house that was being renovated for resale. The developer says he'll pay me when the house sells. It's been 8 months. Do I have any rights?
**Answer:** "Pay when paid" clauses are generally unenforceable in South African construction law unless explicitly agreed in writing. You performed the work — you are entitled to payment according to the agreed terms. If no specific terms were agreed, payment is due on completion (or within a reasonable time). The developer's failure to sell the house is his business risk, not yours. Demand payment immediately. If he refuses, you may have a builder's lien over the property if the work improved its value — this could allow you to secure your claim against the property itself. File a caveat at the Deeds Office to protect your claim, then sue. Do not accept "pay when paid" terms without written agreement and a clear time limit.

#### Q: 76 A client paid a R5,000 deposit but cancelled the job before I started because she "changed her mind." I've already ordered materials specifically for her job. Can I keep the deposit?
**Answer:** This depends on your contract terms. If your quote stated the deposit is non-refundable and that cancellation fees apply, you can retain the deposit to cover your losses (materials ordered, administrative costs, lost opportunity). If there was no cancellation clause, you can only retain the deposit to the extent of your actual losses — the cost of the ordered materials (if they cannot be used elsewhere) plus any direct costs incurred. You must refund any excess. Under the CPA, if the client is a consumer, an unreasonably high cancellation fee may be challenged as an unfair term. Calculate your actual loss and offer a refund of the balance. Materials that can be used on other jobs do not count as a loss.

#### Q: 77 I'm a sub-contractor. The main contractor went into liquidation owing me R67,000. I heard the client (developer) still holds retention money. Can I claim directly from the developer?
**Answer:** Possibly. Under the common law of delegation, if the developer holds retention money that was deducted from the main contractor's payments, that money is trust property — it does not form part of the main contractor's insolvent estate. You may have a direct claim against the developer for the retention money if you can prove you performed the work for which retention was held. However, this requires legal action — an interdict or application to the liquidator to release the retention to you. The liquidator will assess all claims. You should also file a claim as a concurrent creditor in the liquidation. If the developer has not yet paid the main contractor's final account, you may have a direct claim against the developer under the "pay-direct" principle. Consult an attorney — this is complex insolvency law.

#### Q: 78 A client says she overpaid me by R3,500 because she miscalculated the square metres. She's demanding a refund. I believe my measurements are correct. Must I refund?
**Answer:** No, not unless she can prove your measurements were incorrect. Your invoice is based on your professional measurement, which is part of the service. If there is a genuine dispute about the area tiled, offer to jointly remeasure with an independent witness present. If your measurement is correct, no refund is due. If her overpayment was genuinely accidental (e.g., she paid R25,000 instead of R21,500 because she added a zero), that is a different matter — payment by mistake may be recoverable under the condictio indebiti. But if she simply disputes your measurement, the burden is on her to prove you overcharged. Do not refund without verifying the claim. If she paid by EFT into your account, the money is presumed to be payment of a debt — she must prove it was paid in error.

#### Q: 79 I quoted a job and the client accepted. Before starting, material prices increased by 18% due to a factory strike. Can I increase my price, or am I locked in?
**Answer:** Under South African contract law, a fixed-price quote is binding even if your costs increase — this is your commercial risk unless the contract contains a price escalation clause or a material adverse change clause. However, you may have two defences: (a) if the strike was unforeseeable when you quoted and makes performance radically more onerous (not just more expensive), the doctrine of supervening impossibility or the principle of "just and equitable" adjustment under the Consumer Protection Act may apply, or (b) if you can prove there was a mutual mistake about the basis of pricing. Neither defence is guaranteed to succeed. In future, include a clause: "Prices based on current material costs. Increases exceeding 10% will be passed on with supporting documentation."

#### Q: 80 A client wants to pay me by doing barter — she'll give me her old car worth R20,000 in exchange for tiling work. Are there tax implications?
**Answer:** Yes. SARS taxes barter transactions at fair market value. The value of the car you receive is taxable income in your hands — you must declare R20,000 as income and pay tax on it (income tax and possibly VAT if you are registered). Similarly, the client must account for the disposal of the car. From a practical perspective, barter also creates complications: (a) you have no cash to pay suppliers or labourers, (b) transfer of ownership requires paperwork (NATIS documents for the vehicle), (c) the car's actual value may differ from the agreed value, (d) if the car has outstanding finance or is stolen, you receive nothing. SARS requires barter transactions to be recorded at fair market value in your books. It is almost always better to insist on cash payment.
## 5. LOAD SHEDDING, WEATHER & FORCE MAJEURE

#### Q: 81 Stage 6 load shedding hit during a job. My adhesive skinned over and I had to throw away R4,800 worth of materials. The client says it's not her problem. Who pays?
**Answer:** Load shedding is a foreseeable risk in South Africa — Eskom has been implementing load shedding since 2007. A "force majeure" defence (supervening impossibility) is unlikely to succeed because load shedding is no longer an unforeseeable event. Unless your contract specifically allocates load shedding risk to the client, the cost typically falls on the contractor as a business risk. However, you can argue: (a) if the client caused the delay that pushed the work into load shedding hours, she may bear some responsibility, (b) if the adhesive failure was due to product defect rather than load shedding, the supplier may be liable. Your best protection going forward: use extended-open-time adhesives designed for hot climates, keep a generator on site for critical mixing and curing periods, and include a load shedding clause in your quotes.

#### Q: 82 I included a "force majeure" clause in my contract covering load shedding. The client says load shedding is not force majeure because it's normal in South Africa. Is she right?
**Answer:** Force majeure clauses are interpreted strictly — they only cover events that make performance objectively impossible, not merely more difficult or expensive. Load shedding, while disruptive, rarely makes tiling impossible (generators exist, work can be rescheduled). South African courts have held that foreseeable events generally do not qualify as force majeure unless the contract explicitly defines them as such. If your contract specifically lists "Eskom load shedding of Stage 4 or higher" as force majeure with defined consequences (time extension, cost sharing), it may be enforceable as a contractual term. But a generic force majeure clause without specific reference to load shedding is unlikely to protect you. Be precise in your contracts: specify exactly what happens during load shedding (delays, generator costs, wasted materials).

#### Q: 83 I'm tiling in Durban. A tropical storm flooded the site overnight and the screed I laid the day before is now waterlogged. The client wants me to continue. I say the screed needs to be redone. Who's right?
**Answer:** You are right. SANS 10400-J requires screeds to achieve specified strength and moisture content before tiling. A waterlogged screed has compromised integrity — tiling over it will result in adhesive failure, efflorescence, and potential tile debonding. Document the flooding with photographs and moisture readings. If the client insists on proceeding against your advice, provide written confirmation that you are proceeding under protest and that the client assumes all risk for future failures. Better yet, refuse to proceed — your professional reputation and potential liability outweigh the client's impatience. The cost of redoing the screed should be borne by the party responsible for site protection. If the client was responsible for securing the building and failed to do so, she bears the cost. If it was your responsibility, it is your cost.

#### Q: 84 A KZN hailstorm broke the windows of the house I was tiling. Water damaged the substrate. The client says I should have protected the site. Am I responsible for weather damage to a house I'm working in?
**Answer:** Your responsibility for site protection depends on your contract. If you agreed to take possession of the site and protect it during construction, you may be liable for damage caused by your failure to secure the property (e.g., not boarding up openings). However, hailstorm damage is an act of God — unless your negligence exacerbated it (e.g., you removed protective covering and didn't replace it), you are not liable for the hail damage itself. The damaged substrate is a variation to your scope — charge for remediation as an extra. Check your contract's risk allocation clause. Standard JBCC agreements place the risk of loss or damage to the works on the contractor only after "practical completion" — before that, the employer carries the risk. If you have no formal contract, argue that weather damage is the owner's risk.

#### Q: 85 I was tiling an outdoor patio in Cape Town. Winter rain started and I couldn't complete the grouting. The client says I should have checked the weather forecast and planned better. Is it my fault?
**Answer:** Checking weather forecasts is part of professional site planning. Cape Town's winter rainfall pattern (May–September) is predictable. Tiling outdoor areas during the rainy season without contingency planning is negligent planning. However, if an unseasonal storm occurred outside the normal rainfall period, that may be a genuine weather delay. Your obligation is to plan for foreseeable weather — if you proceeded with outdoor tiling in July without rain protection, the delay and any resulting damage are your responsibility. If the rain was unforeseeable, it may constitute a justifiable delay entitling you to a time extension. Always check 7-day forecasts before scheduling outdoor work, have tarpaulin or temporary roofing on standby, and include a weather delay clause in your quote.

#### Q: 86 Load shedding destroyed my electronic laser level worth R18,000 when the power surge hit when it came back on. Can I claim this from Eskom?
**Answer:** Claims against Eskom for load-shedding-related damage are theoretically possible under the Eskom Act and the law of delict (negligence), but practically almost impossible to pursue for small amounts. Eskom has sovereign immunity protections and the cost of litigation would exceed your claim. Your remedy is through insurance — check whether your tools and equipment insurance covers power surge damage. If not, use surge protectors on all electronic equipment going forward. Alternatively, you may have a claim against your electricity provider if the surge was caused by their infrastructure failure. For a small contractor, the practical answer is: insure your equipment and use surge protection — do not waste time suing Eskom.

#### Q: 87 The client wants me to work through load shedding using my generator. I don't have a generator. She's threatening to withhold payment for delays. Can she do this?
**Answer:** She can only withhold payment for delays if the contract required you to provide power continuity or if you guaranteed specific completion dates without load shedding exemptions. In the absence of such terms, load shedding delays are at the employer's risk (unless the contract says otherwise). You are not required to own a generator — it is a business investment, not a legal obligation. However, if load shedding causes delay beyond the contractual completion date, the client may have a claim for late completion damages (liquidated damages) if the contract provides for them. Without a liquidated damages clause, her claim is limited to actual proven losses. Do not allow a client to unilaterally impose new requirements (like generator provision) not in the contract.

#### Q: 88 I quoted a 5-day job. KZN floods closed the roads and I couldn't access the site for 3 days. The client says I must absorb the delay costs because "floods are normal in KZN." Are they?
**Answer:** KZN floods are increasingly common but each event's severity varies. If the roads were officially closed by authorities, the delay is due to supervening impossibility — you could not lawfully access the site. This entitles you to a time extension without penalty. However, the cost of the delay (accommodation, idle labour, equipment hire) depends on your contract. If the contract is silent, each party bears its own delay costs — you absorb your costs, the client absorbs theirs. If the client claims floods are "normal," this undermines a force majeure defence but does not negate your right to a time extension for events outside your control. Municipal road closures are official events — request the closure notice from the municipality as evidence.

#### Q: 89 I'm tiling in the Karoo where temperatures regularly exceed 40°C. The adhesive is skinning before I can bed the tiles. The supplier says I'm applying it too slowly. I say the product isn't suitable for these conditions. Who's right?
**Answer:** Both factors may be at play. SANS 10400-J and adhesive manufacturer specifications define application temperature ranges (typically 5°C to 35°C for most cementitious adhesives). Above 35°C, adhesives cure too quickly due to rapid moisture evaporation. The supplier should specify temperature limitations on the technical data sheet. If the product is rated only to 35°C and you're working at 40°C, the product is not suitable — the supplier should recommend a high-temperature or extended-open-time formulation. However, you also have a duty to adjust your technique: smaller sections, dampening the substrate, working in morning/evening hours. If the product is technically rated for the temperature and you're working too slowly, the fault is yours. Always check the TDS before specifying products for extreme climates.

#### Q: 90 My generator was stolen from site during load shedding. The security guard the client hired was asleep. Who bears the loss?
**Answer:** The allocation of risk for theft depends on your contract. If the client provided site security as part of her obligations, and the theft occurred due to the guard's negligence, the client may be liable for your loss — but only if the contract specifically made her responsible for site security and your equipment. If there was no such agreement, tools and equipment are typically the contractor's risk. Your remedy is through insurance (tools-in-transit or all-risk contractor's insurance) or a claim against the security company (if you can prove negligence). The sleeping guard is evidence of negligence — obtain an incident report and consider a civil claim against the security company. Going forward, include a clause: "Contractor's tools and equipment remain contractor's risk unless client provides secure storage facility."
## 6. MATERIAL ISSUES, SUPPLIER DISPUTES & CLIENT-SUPPLIED TILES

#### Q: 91 I installed 400m² of tiles from a well-known supplier. After installation, visible shade variation appeared across the floor — some tiles are noticeably darker. The client has rejected the entire installation. The supplier says it's a "batch variation" and not their fault. Who's liable?
**Answer:** Under SANS 1449, tiles are classified by shade variation rating: V1 (uniform), V2 (slight variation), V3 (moderate variation), and V4 (substantial variation). The box must indicate the variation rating. If the tiles were sold as V1 or V2 and the actual variation exceeds that rating, the supplier is liable under the CPA Section 56 (implied warranty of quality). If the tiles were correctly labelled V3 or V4, and you installed them without mixing batches or randomising the layout, the installation fault may be yours — high-variation tiles must be blended during installation to avoid clustering. Demand the batch numbers and variation rating from the supplier. If the supplier sold mismatched batches as one batch, they are liable. If the variation is within the stated rating, the liability depends on installation technique.

#### Q: 92 A client bought tiles from a clearance sale — end-of-range stock at 60% off. I installed them. Six months later, 30% of the tiles have cracked. The supplier says "clearance stock, no warranty." The client blames me. What's my legal position?
**Answer:** Clearance sale goods are still subject to the CPA Section 56 implied warranty unless the consumer was explicitly informed of the specific defect and agreed to purchase despite it. "No warranty" signage does not override the CPA for defects that were not disclosed at the time of sale. If the tiles have a latent manufacturing defect (e.g., improper firing causing internal stresses), the supplier remains liable. If the tiles were sold as "seconds" with visible defects clearly marked, and the cracking is related to those known defects, the client assumed the risk. Your liability is limited to installation defects — improper adhesive, no expansion joints, substrate movement. Document your installation method (adhesive type, trowel size, expansion joints) and request independent assessment. If installation was correct, the supplier is liable even for clearance stock.

#### Q: 93 I bought tiles on my trade account for a job, but the client cancelled. I can't return the tiles because the supplier has a "special order, no returns" policy. Can I use these tiles for my own home renovation without causing problems?
**Answer:** Using job-purchased materials for personal use is ethically questionable and potentially creates tax and accounting issues. From a legal perspective, once you purchase the tiles, they are your property — you can use them as you wish. However: (a) if you claimed the cost as a business expense and then used them personally, SARS may consider this a taxable fringe benefit or disallow the deduction, (b) if the supplier gave you a trade discount based on business volume and discovers you used stock personally, they may revoke your trade account, (c) if you originally quoted the client for these specific tiles and she paid a deposit, you may have a duty to refund the deposit or use the tiles for her job if she reinstates. The safest approach: return the tiles if possible (negotiate with the supplier), or sell them to another client at cost, or declare personal use in your tax records.

#### Q: 94 I specified and supplied imported Italian tiles for a luxury job. Due to the Rand/Euro exchange rate crash, my cost went up 35% between quote and delivery. The client won't pay the increase. Can I cancel the contract?
**Answer:** Unless your contract contains a price escalation or currency fluctuation clause, you are bound by your quoted price. Exchange rate fluctuations are a normal commercial risk for contractors who import materials. Cancellation would be a breach of contract, exposing you to damages. Your options: (a) absorb the loss and complete the job (commercial decision), (b) negotiate a price increase with the client (voluntary), (c) if the increase makes performance genuinely impossible (not just unprofitable), argue supervening impossibility — but this is a high threshold. Going forward: quote imported materials at "prevailing exchange rate" with a specific rate locked in for acceptance within a limited period, or add a currency fluctuation clause covering movements exceeding a specified percentage.

#### Q: 95 The client supplied her own tiles and adhesive. I installed them using my normal technique. The tiles are now debonding. She's blaming my installation. The adhesive she bought was the cheapest available and clearly unsuitable for the tile type. Am I liable?
**Answer:** Your liability depends on whether you had a duty to advise against the unsuitable adhesive. As a professional tiler, you have a duty of care that includes advising the client if the materials supplied are clearly unsuitable for the application. If you installed the tiles knowing the adhesive was wrong without warning the client, you share liability. If you advised against the adhesive in writing and the client insisted, your liability is minimised — but you may still bear some responsibility for proceeding with known unsuitable materials. The best practice: always provide a written memo specifying required adhesive type and state: "Installation warranty is void if client-supplied materials do not meet these specifications." If you advised against the adhesive, produce the written advice as your defence.

#### Q: 96 I used 25kg adhesive bags that had been stored in my open trailer for 3 weeks. Rain got into some bags. I used them anyway because they looked fine. Now the tiles are lifting. Is this my fault or the supplier's?
**Answer:** This is your fault. Cementitious adhesives are hygroscopic — moisture ingress starts the curing process prematurely. Even if the bags looked fine externally, internal clumping indicates moisture absorption. Manufacturer specifications universally require storage in a dry, covered area. Using water-damaged adhesive constitutes negligence, and any resulting failure is your liability. You cannot claim against the supplier for your failure to store the product correctly. The adhesive technical data sheet (TDS) specifies storage conditions — ignorance is not a defence. Going forward: store all cementitious products in a dry, weatherproof area, and discard any bag that shows signs of moisture exposure. The cost of replacement adhesive is far less than the cost of retiling.

#### Q: 97 A supplier sold me tiles as "first grade" but the batch number indicates they are "commercial grade" (second grade with more lenient tolerances). I only discovered this after installation. Do I have a claim against the supplier for the difference in value?
**Answer:** Yes. Selling commercial grade tiles as first grade is misrepresentation under the CPA Section 41 (false, misleading, or deceptive representations). You have a claim for the difference in value between first grade and commercial grade tiles, plus any consequential damages (removal and replacement costs if the grade difference caused defects). First grade and commercial grade tiles have different tolerances under SANS 1449 — commercial grade allows greater size variation, warping, and surface defects. If these tolerances caused visible defects in the installation, the supplier is liable for all remediation costs. Preserve the batch records, packaging, and your invoice as evidence. Report the supplier to the National Consumer Commission if they refuse to rectify.

#### Q: 98 I ordered 200m² of tiles. The supplier delivered 180m² and said the balance is "on back order" with no confirmed delivery date. The client wants me to start immediately. Can I cancel the order and buy from another supplier, charging the original supplier for the price difference?
**Answer:** If the supplier failed to deliver the full quantity within the agreed timeframe (or a reasonable time if no timeframe was specified), they are in breach of contract. You are entitled to: (a) cancel the entire order and claim damages (the price difference from an alternative supplier), or (b) accept the 180m² and claim damages for the shortfall. Under the CPA Section 47, if the supplier cannot deliver within a reasonable time, you can cancel and receive a full refund. Document the original delivery promise, the shortfall, and your costs of cover (purchasing elsewhere). If the alternative supplier charges more, the original supplier is liable for the difference plus any direct costs (additional delivery charges, delay costs).

#### Q: 99 The client wants me to install tiles he bought from a bankrupt supplier's auction. The tiles have no box markings, no batch numbers, and no SANS 1449 compliance label. Should I install them?
**Answer:** No. SANS 1449 requires tile packaging to bear the manufacturer's mark, grade, size, and batch number. Tiles without these markings may be defective, non-compliant, counterfeit, or recalled stock. Installing non-compliant tiles exposes you to liability under the NBR (National Building Regulations) and the CPA. If the tiles fail, you cannot trace the batch or claim against a manufacturer. Refuse the installation and document your refusal in writing, citing SANS 1449 compliance requirements. If the client insists, require him to sign a waiver acknowledging: "I accept full responsibility for tile quality and release the contractor from all liability for tile failure." Even with a waiver, your professional reputation is at risk — consider whether the job is worth it.

#### Q: 100 I used a rapid-set adhesive on a large format tile (600x600mm) job because the client was in a rush. The tiles are now showing lippage and hollow spots. The adhesive manufacturer says their product is not suitable for large format tiles. Am I liable, or is the supplier who sold me the adhesive liable?
**Answer:** You are primarily liable. As the tiling professional, you have a duty to select the correct adhesive for the tile format and application. Rapid-set adhesives have shorter open times and are generally not recommended for large format tiles because they don't allow sufficient time for proper bedding and adjustment. The supplier's liability depends on whether they knew your application and recommended the wrong product. If you simply bought the adhesive without asking for advice, the supplier has no liability. If you told them you were tiling 600x600mm tiles and they recommended rapid-set, they share liability for negligent advice. Under the CPA, suppliers who give advice have a duty of care to ensure the advice is appropriate. Document what you told the supplier at the time of purchase.
## 7. ASBESTOS, SAFETY & SITE HAZARDS

#### Q: 101 I was ripping out old floor tiles in a 1970s building and discovered they contain asbestos. I immediately stopped work. The client is furious and says I'm being dramatic. What are my legal obligations under the Asbestos Regulations?
**Answer:** You did exactly the right thing. The Asbestos Regulations of 2020 (under the OHSA) are strict: any work that may disturb asbestos-containing materials (ACMs) must cease immediately. You are legally required to: (a) notify the client in writing of the discovery, (b) ensure no further disturbance of the ACMs, (c) secure the area to prevent exposure, (d) advise the client that only a registered asbestos removal contractor (accredited by the Department of Employment and Labour) may remove asbestos-containing materials. Removing asbestos yourself is a criminal offence — Section 24 of the Regulations prohibits unlicensed asbestos work. The client must engage an approved asbestos contractor at their cost. Do not resume work until the asbestos has been properly removed and clearance certificates issued. Your refusal to continue is a legal obligation, not a choice.

#### Q: 102 I disturbed asbestos tiles before realising what they were. I've now been exposed. What are my rights, and what should I do immediately?
**Answer:** Immediate steps: (1) Stop work and leave the area, (2) Do not brush off or vacuum dust from your clothing — wet-wipe or shower and change, (3) Report the exposure to the client and your COIDA administrator within the required timeframe, (4) Seek medical assessment and request documentation of the exposure, (5) Ensure the area is properly decontaminated by a licensed asbestos contractor. Under COIDA, you may have a claim for occupational disease if you develop an asbestos-related condition in future — document the exposure date, duration, concentration (if known), and type of asbestos. Asbestos-related diseases have long latency periods (10–40 years). Keep this documentation for life. If the client knew or should have known about the asbestos and failed to warn you, you may also have a civil claim for negligence.

#### Q: 103 A client wants me to remove vinyl asbestos tiles myself to "save money" and promises not to tell anyone. She'll pay me double my normal rate. Can I do this if I wear a mask?
**Answer:** Absolutely not. This is a criminal offence under the Asbestos Regulations 2020. Only contractors holding an asbestos removal permit from the Chief Inspector of the Department of Employment and Labour may remove asbestos-containing materials. Individual penalties include fines and imprisonment. A dust mask (P1/P2) is completely inadequate for asbestos — removal requires full PPE including respiratory protective equipment (RPE), negative pressure enclosures, and HEPA-filtered vacuums. The client's promise of secrecy is meaningless — if you or a future occupant develops mesothelioma, the prosecution will trace the removal to you. The double payment is not worth a criminal record or your life. Refuse the job and report the request to the Department of Employment and Labour.

#### Q: 104 I discovered asbestos sheeting used as an underlay beneath floor tiles in a commercial building. The building owner says it was "grandfathered in" because it predates the asbestos ban. Does that mean I can work around it?
**Answer:** No. There is no "grandfathering" exception that allows unsafe work with asbestos. The Asbestos Regulations 2020 apply to all asbestos-containing materials regardless of installation date. If your work will disturb the asbestos sheeting (e.g., removing tiles adhered to it, drilling into it, cutting it), you must follow the full regulatory framework: notification, risk assessment, licensed removal, and disposal at an approved hazardous waste facility. The fact that the asbestos was legally installed in the 1960s does not exempt it from current safety requirements. The building owner is responsible for asbestos management and must engage a licensed assessor to identify all ACMs before any renovation work begins. Do not proceed until a licensed asbestos professional has assessed and managed the risk.

#### Q: 105 A client asked me to install tiles over old asbestos floor tiles rather than removing them. Is encapsulation (tiling over asbestos) legal and safe?
**Answer:** Encapsulation (covering asbestos-containing materials without disturbing them) is a recognised asbestos management strategy under the Asbestos Regulations, provided: (a) the ACMs are in good condition (not friable, not damaged), (b) the encapsulation method is approved (e.g., sealing with a penetrating encapsulant before covering), (c) the covering material will not require penetration of the ACM layer in future, (d) an asbestos register is maintained for the building recording the location of the covered asbestos. However, you must not disturb the asbestos tiles during preparation — no sanding, grinding, or aggressive surface preparation. If the existing tiles are loose or damaged, encapsulation is not appropriate. You are not legally required to be a licensed asbestos professional to encapsulate, but you should document the encapsulation and advise the client to update the building's asbestos register.

#### Q: 106 My labourer cut his hand deeply with a tile cutter. I don't have a first aid kit on site. Am I legally required to have one, and what are the consequences of not having it?
**Answer:** Yes. Section 8 of the OHSA and General Safety Regulation 3 require every employer to provide first aid equipment (a first aid box) and ensure a trained first aider is available. For construction sites, Regulation 3 specifies the contents of the first aid box based on the number of employees. Failure to provide first aid equipment is an offence under the OHSA — the Department of Employment and Labour can issue a contravention notice and fine. More seriously, if the lack of first aid exacerbated the injury (e.g., infection, greater blood loss), you face increased COIDA liability and potential civil claims for negligence. Immediate action: buy a regulation-compliant first aid kit today, and send at least one employee on a first aid course (St John or Red Cross). The cost is minimal compared to the liability.

#### Q: 107 I was working on a site where the client refused to provide water for mixing adhesive because "she's on a water restriction." I had to use collected rainwater. The adhesive didn't perform correctly. Who's at fault?
**Answer:** Adhesive manufacturers specify potable (clean) water for mixing. Rainwater may contain contaminants (dust, organic matter, acidic pH) that affect adhesive performance. The client has a duty under the OHSA Section 8 to provide adequate welfare facilities including clean water for construction purposes. If she refused, she breached this duty and contributed to the defective work. However, you also had a duty to ensure the water was suitable — using visibly contaminated water without testing was negligent. The liability is likely shared. Going forward: specify in your contract that the client must provide clean, potable water for construction use, or bring your own water and price it into the job. Do not compromise on water quality — it affects adhesive performance, screed curing, and grout integrity.

#### Q: 108 A client has a rooftop terrace that needs tiling. There's no edge protection (railing) and a 4-storey drop. I refused to work without scaffolding and guardrails. The client says I'm "being difficult." What does the law say?
**Answer:** You are absolutely correct, and the client is endangering your life. The Construction Regulations 2014 (under OHSA) require edge protection (guardrails, toe boards, or safety nets) for any work at height where a fall could occur. Regulation 8 specifically addresses fall protection — working on an unguarded 4-storey edge without fall protection is a criminal offence by the employer (you, if self-employed, or your client as the site controller). You have a legal right to refuse unsafe work under Section 14 of the OHSA, and it is unlawful for any person to discriminate against you for exercising this right. Do not work on that roof without proper edge protection, scaffolding with guardrails, or a harness and lifeline system. Report the client to the Department of Employment and Labour if they pressure you to work unsafely.

#### Q: 109 I discovered a gas leak while preparing a floor for tiling. The client knew about it and "thought it wasn't serious." Can I refuse to work until it's fixed?
**Answer:** Yes. A gas leak is a serious hazard that creates risk of explosion, fire, and asphyxiation. Under Section 14 of the OHSA, every employee (including self-employed contractors) has the right to leave any workplace where they have reasonable grounds to believe there is an imminent danger to their health or safety. You are legally protected from any adverse action for exercising this right. Document the hazard, notify the client in writing that work is suspended until the leak is repaired by a registered gas practitioner (per PER regulations), and request a gas compliance certificate before resuming. Do not attempt to fix the leak yourself — gas work must be done by a registered gas installer. If the client refuses, report to the local gas supplier and the Department of Employment and Labour.

#### Q: 110 I don't provide hard hats to my 2-person team because we're "just tilers" and not on a construction site with falling objects. Is this legally acceptable?
**Answer:** It depends on the site-specific risk assessment. The OHSA requires PPE (Personal Protective Equipment) to be provided where hazards exist. On a standard residential tiling job with no overhead work, falling object risk is minimal, and hard hats may not be required. However, on construction sites with multiple contractors, scaffolding, or overhead work, hard hats are mandatory under the Construction Regulations. Other PPE is non-negotiable for tilers: safety boots (steel toe — tile shards are sharp), safety glasses (tile cutting creates flying fragments), knee pads, and dust masks (silica dust from cutting is a serious respiratory hazard). Silica dust exposure is regulated under the OHSA Hazardous Chemical Substances Regulations — cutting tiles dry without extraction or respiratory protection is a violation.

#### Q: 111 I was tiling a bathroom and hit an electrical cable embedded in the screed. There was no electrical plan showing the cable. The client is blaming me. Am I liable for the repair?
**Answer:** If there was no electrical plan or indication of the cable's presence, you are not liable — you had no way of knowing the cable was there. The client (or previous contractor) is responsible for providing accurate information about embedded services before tiling work begins. However, if the cable was visibly marked (e.g., warning tape above it in the screed) or you drilled deeper than necessary for the tiling application, you may share liability. Under standard contract terms, hidden services are the employer's risk unless the contractor was negligent in failing to detect obvious signs. Document the absence of plans and warning markers. The repair cost should be borne by the client or their insurer.

#### Q: 112 A client wants me to remove lead paint from walls before tiling the bathroom skirting. Is this safe, and do I need special training?
**Answer:** Lead paint removal is hazardous and regulated. Inhaling or ingesting lead dust causes serious neurological damage. The Hazardous Chemical Substances Regulations under OHSA require: risk assessment, air monitoring if disturbance is significant, respiratory protection (P3 filter minimum), containment, and disposal as hazardous waste. For small areas (like skirting boards), wet scraping with HEPA vacuum cleanup may be acceptable with proper PPE. For large areas, a licensed hazardous materials contractor is required. You should not undertake lead paint removal without understanding the risks and proper procedures. The paint's lead content can be tested with a simple swab kit (available at hardware stores). If lead is present and the area is extensive, refer the client to a specialist.

#### Q: 113 My labourer is refusing to work in a basement with visible mould on the walls. He says it's a health hazard. Is he right, and can he legally refuse?
**Answer:** Yes, he is right, and yes, he can legally refuse. Mould produces mycotoxins and spores that cause respiratory illness, allergic reactions, and in severe cases, neurological effects. Under Section 14 of the OHSA, an employee may refuse to work in conditions they reasonably believe pose an imminent danger to their health. Mould remediation should be conducted by a professional with appropriate PPE (respirators, gloves, containment) before any other work proceeds. You, as the employer, also have a duty of care under Section 8 of the OHSA to provide a safe working environment. Do not force employees to work in mould-contaminated areas. The client must engage a mould remediation specialist. Document your refusal and the health basis for it.

#### Q: 114 I was asked to tile a floor in a room that had recently been fumigated for termites. The fumigator said it's "safe after 24 hours." It's been 36 hours. Is it actually safe for me and my labourers?
**Answer:** Fumigation safety depends on the chemical used, the concentration, ventilation, and the fumigator's certification. "Safe after 24 hours" is a generalisation — some fumigants (like sulfuryl fluoride) require aeration periods of 6–24 hours plus ventilation, while others may require longer. The fumigator should provide a clearance certificate confirming safe re-entry levels measured with appropriate detection equipment. Do not take their word for it — request the written clearance certificate with gas concentration readings. Under the OHSA Hazardous Chemical Substances Regulations, you have a right to information about chemical hazards in the workplace (Section 41). If no clearance certificate is available, refuse entry and demand independent air quality testing. Your health is not worth risking for a day's work.

#### Q: 115 I don't have contractor's all-risk insurance. I keep telling myself I'll get it "next month." What's the real risk if something goes wrong on a job?
**Answer:** The risk is potentially business-ending. Without insurance, you are personally liable for: (a) damage to the client's property (e.g., flooding an apartment below), (b) injury to third parties (e.g., a tile falling and hitting someone), (c) theft or damage to your tools, (d) defective work claims that require complete removal and redoing at your cost. A single liability claim can exceed R500,000 — enough to bankrupt a small contractor. Contractor's all-risk insurance typically costs R500–R2,000 per month depending on turnover and coverage limits. Public liability insurance (covering third-party injury and property damage) is the minimum you should have. Tool insurance and professional indemnity insurance are also advisable. Operating without insurance is gambling your entire livelihood on nothing going wrong — eventually, something will.

#### Q: 116 I'm tiling a restaurant kitchen. The client wants the work done during business hours while the kitchen is operational. Is this even legal from a health and safety perspective?
**Answer:** Tiling in an operational commercial kitchen creates multiple hazards: wet floors (slip risk), dust contamination of food (health violation), noise disruption, and potential chemical contamination from adhesives and sealers. The Foodstuffs, Cosmetics and Disinfectants Act and municipal health bylaws prohibit conditions that may contaminate food. Working during operational hours likely violates these regulations. The Occupational Health and Safety Act also requires a safe working environment — an active kitchen with hot oil, sharp knives, and wet floors is not safe for tiling work. Advise the client that work must be done after hours or during a scheduled closure. If they insist, require written confirmation that they accept all liability for health code violations and safety incidents.

#### Q: 117 I found a bee hive inside a wall cavity while preparing for tiling. The client wants me to just "seal it in" with the tiles. Is this safe and legal?
**Answer:** No. Sealing a live bee hive into a wall is inhumane, creates a future hazard (if the bees find another exit into the building), and may violate municipal bylaws regarding pest control. African honey bees can be defensive and dangerous in large colonies. The correct procedure is to engage a registered bee removal specialist who will either relocate the colony (preferred) or exterminate it if relocation is not possible. Do not attempt DIY bee removal — multiple stings can be fatal, especially to those with allergies. The cost of professional bee removal is the client's responsibility — it is a pre-existing condition unrelated to your tiling scope. Document the discovery and suspend work until the hive is safely removed.

#### Q: 118 I was tiling near a swimming pool. My labourer can't swim and nearly fell in. Am I required to provide water safety training or life jackets for poolside work?
**Answer:** Under Section 8 of the OHSA, you must assess risks and take reasonable measures to protect employees. For poolside work, this includes: warning signs, temporary barriers around the pool edge, non-slip footwear, and ensuring workers are aware of the hazard. Life jackets are not typically required for poolside tiling work but may be reasonable if the pool is deep and workers carry heavy materials near the edge. The most practical protection is a temporary fence or barrier keeping workers at a safe distance from the pool edge. If a worker cannot swim, do not assign them to work within fall distance of the pool without additional safeguards. Document your risk assessment and safety measures.

#### Q: 119 I accidentally drilled into a water pipe while preparing a substrate. Water flooded the apartment below. The body corporate says I'm liable for all damage — the flooded apartment, the common area ceiling, and their legal costs. Could this really cost me hundreds of thousands?
**Answer:** Yes. Water damage in multi-unit buildings cascades rapidly — the apartment below, electrical systems, common areas, and mould remediation can easily exceed R200,000–R500,000. If you don't have public liability insurance, you are personally liable for all reasonable repair costs. Your defence depends on whether the pipe was marked on available plans (if not, the body corporate or owner may share liability) and whether you took reasonable precautions (stud finders, service detection equipment). However, as the contractor on site, the primary liability falls on you for damage caused by your actions. The body corporate's claim for legal costs is only valid if they were reasonable and necessary — you can challenge excessive costs. This is exactly why contractor's liability insurance is essential.

#### Q: 120 I was offered a job tiling a pharmaceutical cleanroom. The client says I need to wear a full bunny suit and go through decontamination. Do I charge extra for this, and is it my responsibility to provide the suit?
**Answer:** Yes, charge extra — cleanroom tiling requires significantly more time, specialised materials (low-VOC adhesives and grouts approved for cleanroom environments), and PPE that reduces worker efficiency. The bunny suit and decontamination protocol are the client's obligation to provide as part of site-specific safety and contamination control requirements — they know their cleanroom classification and protocols. Your responsibility is to comply with the protocols once provided. The additional costs include: extended preparation time, specialised materials (often 2-3x standard material cost), reduced labour productivity (suiting up/de-suiting), and potentially background checks or medical clearances required for pharmaceutical facility access. Quote this as a separate line item: "Cleanroom protocol compliance and specialised materials."
## 8. TOOLS, THEFT & INSURANCE NIGHTMARES

#### Q: 121 My R85,000 trailer full of tools was stolen from outside a client's property overnight. I had no insurance. The client says it's not her responsibility because I chose to park there. Is she right?
**Answer:** Generally, yes. Unless your contract specifically required the client to provide secure parking or storage for your tools, the risk of theft is yours as the contractor. Your tools and equipment are your business assets — protecting them is your responsibility. The client's duty of care does not extend to safeguarding your property unless she agreed to it or her negligence created the risk (e.g., she told you to park in a known high-crime area when secure parking was available). Without insurance, you bear the full loss. Your only potential recourse is: (a) claiming from the client's household insurance if her policy covers contractors' tools (unlikely), (b) SAPS case number for possible recovery (statistically unlikely). This is a hard lesson: tools and equipment insurance is non-negotiable for contractors.

#### Q: 122 I have tools insurance, but the insurer is refusing my claim because the tools were stolen from an "unattended vehicle" and the policy requires tools to be in a "locked, alarmed vehicle." My vehicle was locked but not alarmed. Do I have any recourse?
**Answer:** Read your policy carefully — insurance policies are interpreted strictly, and if the policy requires an alarmed vehicle, you must comply. However, you can argue: (a) the term "alarmed" is ambiguous — does it mean a factory-fitted alarm or any alarm? (b) the insurer did not draw this exclusion to your attention clearly when you took out the policy (Duty of Fair Treatment under the Financial Sector Regulation Act), (c) a locked vehicle without an alarm is still "secure" and the insurer's requirement is unreasonable. Lodge a complaint with the insurer's internal complaints department first, then escalate to the Ombud for Short-Term Insurance if unresolved. In future, read every exclusion in your policy and comply with all security requirements — insurers deny claims on technicalities.

#### Q: 123 A client's domestic worker stole my tile cutter while I was on lunch break. The client caught her and she confessed, but she has no money to repay. Can I claim from the client's insurance?
**Answer:** The domestic worker's theft creates a complex liability question. If she was the client's employee acting within the scope of her employment (even wrongfully), the client may be vicariously liable — but theft is generally outside the scope of employment. The client's household insurance typically covers the client's own contents, not contractors' tools. Your claim is against the thief (who has no assets) or potentially the client if she was negligent (e.g., she knew the worker had a history of theft and gave her access to your tools). Your best remedy is your own tools insurance. If you have none, a civil claim against the domestic worker will likely be unenforceable. Report the theft to SAPS — a criminal conviction may support a future civil claim if she acquires assets.

#### Q: 124 I bought a second-hand tile cutter from another contractor. It turned out to be stolen property. The police seized it. Can I claim the purchase price back from the seller?
**Answer:** If the seller knew or should have known the item was stolen, you can claim the purchase price back on the grounds of fraud or breach of implied warranty of title. If the seller was also an innocent purchaser, your claim is more difficult — you both suffered from the theft. The general rule is that stolen property remains the property of the original owner, and no title passes, even to an innocent purchaser (nemo dat quod non habet — no one gives what they do not have). Your remedy is against the thief (if caught) or the seller (if they warranted clear title).
