Going forward: always request proof of purchase or ownership when buying second-hand equipment, buy from reputable sources, and keep receipts. Buying stolen goods, even innocently, results in total loss.

#### Q: 125 I'm storing tools at a friend's workshop. His premises were burgled and my tools were stolen. His insurance says they only cover his own tools, not mine stored there. Is this correct?
**Answer:** Unless your friend specifically listed your tools on his policy or has a "goods in trust" extension, his insurance will not cover your tools. Most business contents policies cover only the policyholder's property. Your tools are your responsibility to insure — "goods in trust" or "tools anywhere" coverage on your own policy is what you need. Some insurers offer "tools in transit and storage" extensions that cover tools while at temporary locations. Without this, you have no insurance claim. You may have a claim against your friend for negligence if he failed to take reasonable security measures (e.g., didn't lock the door, left the alarm off) — but suing a friend is rarely worth the relationship damage. The lesson: never assume someone else's insurance covers your property.

#### Q: 126 My truck with all my tools was hijacked in Johannesburg. The insurer says they'll only pay "market value" which is 40% less than replacement cost. Can I challenge this?
**Answer:** Insurance policies typically pay the lesser of market value or sum insured. "Market value" means the value of the tools at the time of loss (depreciated), not the cost of new replacements. You can challenge the insurer's valuation by: (a) obtaining independent valuations from tool suppliers showing current replacement prices, (b) providing maintenance records showing the tools were in excellent condition, (c) checking if your policy has a "new for old" or "replacement cost" extension. If the policy clearly states "market value" and you agreed to it, your challenge is limited to disputing the insurer's depreciation calculation. When renewing your policy, consider upgrading to replacement cost coverage — it costs more but eliminates the depreciation shortfall.

#### Q: 127 I hired equipment (a floor grinder) from a hire company. It was stolen from site. Their contract says I'm liable for the full replacement value regardless of fault. Is this enforceable?
**Answer:** Hire contracts typically include strict liability clauses making the hirer responsible for loss or damage during the hire period. These clauses are generally enforceable under South African contract law because: (a) they are clearly stated in the contract, (b) the hire company has no control over the equipment once it leaves their premises, (c) insurance options are usually offered at hire. However, if the theft was due to the hire company's negligence (e.g., they delivered to the wrong address, failed to provide security instructions), you may have a counterclaim. If you were forced to sign the contract without opportunity to read it (contract of adhesion), you may challenge the term as unfair under the CPA. Going forward: always take the insurance option offered by hire companies — it is cheaper than replacing stolen equipment.

#### Q: 128 My employee was using my company vehicle and crashed it while under the influence. The vehicle is a write-off and my tools inside were destroyed. My insurance won't pay because he was drunk. What now?
**Answer:** Standard vehicle and tools insurance policies exclude loss caused by driving under the influence — this exclusion is absolute and non-negotiable. Your claim will be denied. Your remedy is against the employee: you can claim damages from him for the vehicle and tools (suing an employee is legally permissible but practically difficult if he has no assets). You can also report him to SAPS for drunk driving. From an employment law perspective, this is gross misconduct warranting immediate dismissal (after a fair disciplinary hearing). Going forward: implement a zero-tolerance alcohol policy for company vehicle use, conduct random breathalyser tests, and ensure your insurance policy is fully understood by all employees. Consider whether the employee's state of intoxication was obvious and you should have prevented him from driving.

#### Q: 129 I have public liability insurance but not "works in progress" insurance. A fire in an adjacent apartment spread and damaged tiles I'd just installed. The client wants me to replace them at my cost. Am I covered?
**Answer:** Public liability insurance covers damage you cause to third parties — it does not cover damage to your own work or materials caused by external events. The fire in the adjacent apartment is not your fault, so public liability does not respond. "Works in progress" or "contract works" insurance would cover this — it protects the work and materials on site against fire, theft, storm, and other perils. Without it, you are not insured for this loss. The client's building insurance may cover the fire damage — he should claim from his insurer. If the fire was caused by a third party's negligence, that third party is liable. Do not absorb the cost of damage you did not cause — advise the client to claim from his building insurer or the negligent party.

#### Q: 130 I bought a "lifetime warranty" tile cutter online from an international supplier. It broke after 18 months. The supplier has disappeared. Is there any way to enforce the warranty?
**Answer:** Enforcing a warranty against a foreign supplier who has disappeared is practically impossible for a small contractor. If you paid by credit card, you may have a chargeback claim with your bank (time limits apply — usually 120 days from discovery). If the supplier had a South African distributor or agent, they may honour the warranty under the CPA (implied warranty applies to all goods sold in South Africa, even imported). If you imported it yourself directly, the CPA still technically applies (the supplier is the "importer" under Section 1), but enforcement against a defunct foreign entity requires international litigation — not viable for a tool worth a few thousand Rand. The lesson: buy tools with local warranty support from established suppliers. "Lifetime warranties" from unknown online sellers are worthless if the seller vanishes.
## 9. SANS STANDARDS, TECHNICAL SPECIFICATIONS & INSTALLATION FAILURES

#### Q: 131 A client wants tiles laid on a screed that was poured 5 days ago. The architect says it's fine. I know SANS 10400-J requires 28 days. If I refuse and lose the job, can I be penalised? If I agree and it fails, am I liable?
**Answer:** You are 100% correct to refuse. SANS 10400-J (Table 2) requires a minimum 28-day curing period for concrete screeds before tiling, with moisture content below a specified percentage (typically tested with a CM meter or calcium carbide test). Tiling on a 5-day-old screed will result in adhesive failure, efflorescence, and tile debonding because the screed has not achieved sufficient strength and is still releasing moisture. If you proceed against your professional judgment, you are fully liable for the failure — the architect's approval does not transfer liability to him. His approval was negligent (potentially exposing him to liability too), but your liability as the installer is primary. Stand your ground. Provide the client with the SANS reference and offer to perform a moisture test as proof. Losing one job is better than being sued for a R100,000 redo.

#### Q: 132 I tiled a shower floor with 600x600mm tiles and didn't use a waterproofing membrane because the client said "the screed guy already waterproofed." Six months later, the ceiling below is water-damaged. Who's liable?
**Answer:** SANS 10400-P (Drainage) and SANS 10400-J require waterproofing in wet areas (showers, baths) regardless of what the screed contractor did. As the tiling contractor, you have a professional duty to verify that adequate waterproofing exists before tiling. If you tiled over an unverified or inadequate waterproofing layer, you share liability for the water damage. The waterproofing is part of the tiling scope in wet areas — passing responsibility to the screed contractor is not a valid defence unless you verified his work and have written confirmation. Going forward: always specify waterproofing as part of your quote for wet areas, conduct a flood test (24-hour ponding test) before tiling, and obtain written confirmation of waterproofing from the previous contractor or do it yourself.

#### Q: 133 I installed tiles with 2mm grout joints as specified by the client. The tiles have expanded slightly and the joints are now virtually non-existent, causing chipping. The client blames my installation. Is 2mm too narrow?
**Answer:** SANS 1449 and good tiling practice recommend minimum grout joint widths based on tile type, size, and substrate stability. For ceramic tiles, 2mm is generally acceptable for walls but may be too narrow for floors subject to foot traffic and thermal movement. For large format tiles (400mm+), wider joints (3–5mm) are recommended to accommodate substrate movement and tile expansion. If the tiles were installed with no expansion joints perimeters (SANS 10400-J requires expansion joints at perimeters and at intervals not exceeding 4.5m in each direction for interior floors), the failure is a design/installation issue, not just the joint width. Your liability depends on whether you advised the client that 2mm joints on floors carry a risk. If you simply followed her instruction without professional advice, you share liability.

#### Q: 134 A tile supplier's technical rep recommended a specific adhesive for a glass mosaic pool installation. The adhesive failed after 3 months. The supplier says "we only recommended it — the choice was yours." Is the supplier liable for bad technical advice?
**Answer:** Under the CPA Section 54, a supplier who provides advice or recommendations must exercise reasonable care and skill. If the technical rep gave negligent advice (recommending an adhesive unsuitable for glass mosaic in a submerged pool environment), the supplier is liable for the consequential damage. Glass mosaic in pools requires specialised epoxy or polymer-modified adhesive rated for permanent immersion — standard cementitious adhesive is not suitable. Document the recommendation: what was said, by whom, when, and in what context. If the supplier's technical data sheet confirms the product is not rated for submerged glass mosaic, the advice was clearly negligent. Your claim is against the supplier for the cost of removal, replacement, and remediation. Preserve any written or email recommendations as evidence.

#### Q: 135 I tiled over an existing tiled floor because the client didn't want to pay for removal. I roughened the old tiles and used a bonding agent. Now the new tiles are debonding. Is this my fault?
**Answer:** Tiling over existing tiles is generally not recommended by manufacturers or SANS standards, but it can work if done correctly: the existing tiles must be firmly bonded (no hollow sounds), thoroughly cleaned (no grease, wax, or soap residue), mechanically roughened or primed with a suitable bonding primer, and the new adhesive must be compatible. If you followed best practice and the debonding is due to the old tiles coming loose (indicating they were not as well-bonded as they appeared), the failure is a latent defect in the substrate, not your installation. However, if you failed to properly prepare the surface or used an unsuitable adhesive, the liability is yours. Going forward: always document the substrate condition before tiling over existing tiles, test adhesion of old tiles with a hammer tap test, and include a written disclaimer for tiling over existing finishes.

#### Q: 136 I used a silicone grout in a shower because the client wanted "the best." After 4 months, the grout is mouldy and discoloured. The client is furious because "the best" shouldn't mould. Is this my fault?
**Answer:** All grout in wet areas is susceptible to mould growth if not properly maintained — even "mould-resistant" grouts will eventually mould if not cleaned and ventilated. Silicone grout is actually more resistant to mould than cementitious grout, but no grout is mould-proof. Mould growth is a maintenance issue, not an installation defect. Under the CPA, goods must be fit for purpose — silicone grout is fit for wet area application. The mould is caused by: inadequate ventilation (no extractor fan or window), failure to clean grout regularly, or soap/shampoo residue accumulation. Explain this to the client and offer to clean and reseal the grout (a maintenance service, not a warranty claim). If she expected a maintenance-free solution, she had unrealistic expectations — no such product exists for shower grout.

#### Q: 137 I installed underfloor heating under tiles. The heating cable failed after 11 months. The electrician who installed the cable blames my tile adhesive for overheating the cable. The adhesive manufacturer says their product is heat-rated to 80°C. Who's actually liable?
**Answer:** This requires expert forensic investigation. Underfloor heating cables should not exceed 27-29°C surface temperature for comfort — if the cable is reaching 80°C, the heating system is either incorrectly specified (wattage too high for the area), incorrectly installed (insulation issues), or incorrectly controlled (thermostat fault). Tile adhesive rated to 80°C is more than adequate for normal underfloor heating. The electrician's claim that your adhesive caused overheating is scientifically dubious — adhesive does not generate heat. Independent testing should determine: the actual cable temperature, the adhesive's heat resistance certification, and whether the heating system was installed per manufacturer specifications. If the cable temperature exceeded design limits due to electrical fault, the electrician is liable. Your liability only arises if you covered the heating system without verifying it was tested and operational first.

#### Q: 138 I used an uncoupling membrane on a wooden floor substrate as recommended. The tiles still cracked. The membrane manufacturer says I installed it incorrectly. I followed their video tutorial exactly. How do I prove my installation was correct?
**Answer:** Document everything: photographs of each installation step, the batch number of the membrane, the specific video tutorial you followed (with date accessed), the adhesive batch and mixing ratio, ambient temperature records, and the substrate condition before installation. Wooden floors are inherently unstable (expansion/contraction with humidity) — even with an uncoupling membrane, large format tiles or rigid tiles may crack. SANS 10400-J requires timber floors to be structurally stable, adequately ventilated below, and properly prepared before tiling. If the floor had excessive deflection (bounce), no membrane will prevent cracking — this is a structural issue, not a tiling issue. Commission an independent inspection by a registered professional engineer or building inspector to determine the root cause. If the wooden floor exceeded allowable deflection limits, the builder is liable, not you.

#### Q: 139 A client wants me to install natural stone tiles (travertine) in a bathroom using the same method I use for ceramic tiles. I know stone requires different adhesive, sealing, and maintenance. If I explain this and she still insists on ceramic tile method, can I just do what she asks?
**Answer:** No. Natural stone is porous, moisture-sensitive, and requires: (a) a polymer-modified adhesive (white adhesive to prevent staining), (b) pre-sealing before installation to prevent staining from grout, (c) flexible grout (not standard cementitious), (d) post-installation sealing, and (e) awareness that stone will patina and change over time. Installing stone like ceramic will result in staining, efflorescence, and premature degradation. If you proceed knowing the method is wrong, you are professionally negligent regardless of the client's instructions. Refuse the job unless the client agrees to the correct stone installation method. Provide her with written technical information from the stone supplier. Your professional duty overrides a client's uninformed preference.

#### Q: 140 I tiled a balcony with no fall to the drain — it was perfectly level because the client wanted "a flat look." Now water pools and the tiles are lifting. She blames me. But she specifically asked for level!
**Answer:** A professional tiler has a duty not to install tiles in a manner that contravenes building regulations or manufacturer specifications, even if the client requests it. SANS 10400-P requires external balconies to have a minimum fall of 1:50 to drains. Installing a level balcony creates a water trap that will inevitably fail — the client may have asked for it, but you should have refused. This is professional negligence. Your only defence is if you advised against it in writing and the client signed a waiver acknowledging the risk — but even a waiver may not fully protect you if the installation contravenes SANS. Going forward: never compromise on falls in wet areas. Explain that SANS requires a minimum fall and that a flat balcony is not possible for a professional installation.

#### Q: 141 I installed tiles in a house built on expansive clay soil in Centurion. After the first rainy season, the floor heaved and the tiles cracked in a wave pattern. The client says my tiles are defective. Is this my problem?
**Answer:** Wave-pattern cracking following the soil's expansion and contraction pattern is a substrate movement issue, not a tile defect. Expansive clay (smectite clay) swells when wet and shrinks when dry, causing significant floor movement. SANS 10400-H addresses foundations on expansive soils — the house should have been designed with specific foundation depth, drainage, and soil treatment. If the house was properly designed for expansive soils and still moved excessively, this may be a design or construction defect (the builder's liability). If the house was not designed for expansive soils at all, the builder and architect are negligent. Your tiles are not defective — they cracked because the floor moved beyond reasonable limits. An independent geotechnical assessment will confirm the cause. Document the crack pattern — it will match the soil heave pattern, proving substrate failure.

#### Q: 142 I used a premixed (bucket) adhesive from a reputable brand for a wall tiling job. After 6 weeks, tiles started falling off. The manufacturer says premixed adhesive is only suitable for dry areas and I used it in a bathroom with occasional shower spray. Is this my fault?
**Answer:** Premixed (ready-to-use) adhesives are typically organic/polymer-based and have different performance characteristics from cementitious adhesives. Many premixed adhesives are rated only for dry to intermittently wet areas, not for direct water exposure. As the installer, you have a duty to verify that the adhesive is suitable for the application — "reputable brand" does not mean "suitable for all applications." The technical data sheet (TDS) specifies the adhesive's classification (e.g., Type D1, D2 under EN 12004) and its water resistance rating. If you used a D1 adhesive (no water resistance) in a wet area, the fault is yours. However, if the product was mislabelled or the salesperson recommended it for bathroom use, the supplier shares liability. Always check the TDS suitability classification before specifying any adhesive.

#### Q: 143 I grouted a floor with an epoxy grout because the client wanted "the strongest possible grout." The grout has stained the porous tiles because I didn't seal them first. The client wants the tiles replaced. Is epoxy grout staining my liability?
**Answer:** Yes. Epoxy grout is extremely strong and stain-resistant, but it will stain porous tiles (natural stone, unglazed ceramics, some porcelain) during installation because the epoxy resin penetrates the tile body before curing. The standard procedure for epoxy grout with porous tiles is to pre-seal the tiles before grouting. If you failed to pre-seal, the staining is your fault. The TDS for the epoxy grout almost certainly warns about this. The tiles may need replacement if the staining cannot be removed chemically (some epoxy stains can be partially removed with specialised cleaners, but full removal is rare). Going forward: always test epoxy grout on a sample tile first, and pre-seal any porous tile before epoxy grouting. Include this step in your quote.

#### Q: 144 I installed tiles in an industrial freezer room (-18°C). The tiles and adhesive both failed within 2 months. The client says I used the wrong products. Were there "right" products for this application?
**Answer:** Standard ceramic tiles and cementitious adhesives are not rated for temperatures below 0°C, especially with freeze-thaw cycling. Freezer room flooring requires: (a) tiles rated for freeze-thaw conditions (porcelain with low water absorption, typically <0.5%), (b) frost-resistant adhesive (specialised polymer-modified adhesive rated for sub-zero application), (c) flexible grout with freeze-thaw resistance, (d) appropriate expansion joints to accommodate thermal cycling. If you used standard products without verifying their low-temperature rating, you are liable. However, if the client specified standard products knowing the application, liability may be shared. Freezer room flooring is a specialist application — if you are not experienced in this area, you should have declined the job or engaged a specialist to specify the correct system.

#### Q: 145 I tiled a concrete floor that had been painted with epoxy paint. I scabbled the surface and used a bonding agent, but the tiles lifted after 4 months. Did I do enough preparation?
**Answer:** Epoxy paint creates a non-porous, chemically resistant barrier that is extremely difficult to tile over. Mechanical scabbling alone often does not remove all paint residue from the concrete pores, and bonding agents may not adhere to epoxy. The correct preparation is: complete removal of all paint by shot-blasting or grinding to bare concrete (not just scabbling), followed by a suitable primer compatible with the adhesive system. If the epoxy paint was not fully removed, the adhesive bond is compromised at the paint layer. Your liability depends on whether your preparation met industry standards. If you simply scabbled without removing all paint, your preparation was likely inadequate. For epoxy-coated floors, shot-blasting is the industry standard preparation method before tiling.

#### Q: 146 I used a 12mm trowel notch for 600x600mm tiles as recommended by the adhesive manufacturer. The client had her own "tile expert" inspect the work and he says I should have used a 20mm trowel. Now she's demanding removal and redo. Who's right?
**Answer:** Trowel notch size is determined by: tile size, tile flatness (lippage tolerance), substrate flatness, and adhesive manufacturer recommendations. A 12mm trowel is generally suitable for 600x600mm tiles if the substrate is flat and the tiles have good dimensional consistency. A 20mm trowel is more commonly used for very large format tiles (800mm+) or uneven substrates. If the adhesive manufacturer's TDS specifies 12mm for this tile size, you followed the correct specification. The client's "expert" is entitled to his opinion, but the manufacturer's published TDS carries more weight in a dispute. If the tiles are fully bedded (verified by lift-off tests showing >90% coverage), your installation is correct. Do not accept removal demands without independent technical assessment. The client's expert should provide his assessment in writing with reference to specific standards.

#### Q: 147 I installed tiles with a "rectified" edge (precision-cut for minimal joints). The client complains about visible lippage. Rectified tiles are supposed to be perfectly flat — is this a tile defect or an installation issue?
**Answer:** Rectified tiles have machined edges for consistent sizing, but they are not automatically perfectly flat — "rectified" refers to the edges, not the body flatness. Lippage in rectified tile installations is usually caused by: (a) substrate unevenness (rectified tiles magnify any substrate irregularity), (b) improper tile levelling system use (or failure to use one), (c) tile warpage (even rectified tiles can have slight warpage per SANS 1449 tolerances), (d) adhesive bedding technique. SANS 10107 (code of practice for tiling) specifies maximum allowable lippage. If the lippage exceeds these limits, it may be an installation issue. If the tiles themselves exceed flatness tolerances under SANS 1449, it is a tile defect. An independent tiling inspector can measure both the installation quality and the tile flatness to determine the cause.

#### Q: 148 A client wants me to tile a staircase with 600x600mm polished porcelain tiles. I know this is a slip hazard and the treads are too small for the tile size. She insists. What do I do?
**Answer:** Refuse. Staircase tiling with large format polished porcelain is a serious safety hazard. SANS 10107 and SANS 10400-T (fire protection) address stair safety. Polished porcelain has an R9 or lower slip rating — inadequate for stair treads where SANS 10107 recommends R10–R11 for dry internal stairs and R11–R12 for external or potentially wet stairs. A 600mm tile on a standard 250–280mm tread will require excessive cutting and create narrow, fragile edges. As a professional, you have a duty of care to refuse work that creates a foreseeable safety risk. If you install it and someone falls, you face delictual liability for negligence. Provide the client with safer alternatives: smaller format anti-slip tiles, stair-nosing profiles, or tiles with appropriate R-rating. Document your refusal and safety concerns in writing.

#### Q: 149 I installed tiles over a Damp Proof Course (DPC) membrane in a basement. The tiles lifted because moisture built up under the DPC and couldn't evaporate. Is this a design flaw or my installation error?
**Answer:** This is a design flaw. A DPC is designed to prevent moisture ingress from below — but if it is completely sealed under tiles with impermeable adhesive, any residual moisture or trapped vapour has nowhere to go and will eventually cause adhesive failure. Basement floors with active moisture should use a ventilated system or a moisture-tolerant installation method: (a) a vapour-permeable adhesive system, (b) a ventilated cavity system (raised floor), (c) or active moisture management (sump pump, drainage). If the architect or builder specified tiling directly over a DPC without addressing vapour management, the design is at fault. As the tiler, you should have flagged the moisture risk before installation — but the primary liability lies with whoever designed the system. An independent building inspector can confirm moisture levels and system design adequacy.

#### Q: 150 I tiled a residential floor and the client wanted the tiles laid "diagonally" at 45 degrees. Now she's complaining that the room looks "crooked" because the walls aren't perfectly square. She wants me to redo it for free. Do I have to?
**Answer:** Diagonal tile layouts accentuate any wall irregularity because the tile edges create strong diagonal lines that the eye follows. If the walls are not perfectly square (which is common in residential construction), diagonal laying will make the skewness visible — this is not an installation error but a geometric reality. When the client chose diagonal layout, she implicitly accepted that wall irregularities would be more visible. You should have warned her of this risk before starting — a good practice is to snap chalk lines showing the diagonal layout before tile installation so the client can see how the layout follows the room geometry. If she approved the layout after seeing the lines, you have no liability. If you did not warn her, offer a goodwill adjustment (perhaps a border tile to disguise the skew) but do not accept a full redo.
## 10. TOWNSHIP POLITICS, NEIGHBOUR DISPUTES & UNUSUAL SITE CONDITIONS

#### Q: 151 I'm a white contractor working in a township. The local business forum says I need their "permission" to work there and must pay a 10% levy. Is this legal?
**Answer:** No. No business forum, community organisation, or informal group has a legal right to demand payment for the privilege of working in any area of South Africa. Such demands constitute extortion under the Prevention of Organised Crime Act (POCA). However, this is a complex reality — local business forums in some townships have significant influence and can disrupt work through protest action. The practical approach: engage respectfully with local business forums, explain your employment of local labour, and seek cooperation rather than confrontation. Report extortion demands to SAPS. The Gauteng Township Economy Act and similar provincial legislation aim to protect all businesses operating in townships. If you experience threats or violence, obtain a protection order from the Magistrate's Court under the Protection from Harassment Act. Always employ local labour where possible — this builds community support.

#### Q: 152 I was tiling in a rural Eastern Cape village. The traditional council (inkosi) says I need to pay "umngeno" (a fee for working in his area) before I can start. Is this a legitimate requirement?
**Answer:** Traditional councils have authority over communal land under the Traditional Leadership and Governance Framework Act 41 of 2003. On communal land (not privately owned land), the traditional council can regulate economic activities and impose reasonable fees for development. However, on privately owned land with a registered title deed, the traditional council has no legal authority to levy fees — the owner has the right to engage any contractor. If the property is on communal land, some form of community engagement or fee may be customary and practically necessary. If it is privately owned, the fee is not legally enforceable — though refusing to pay may create practical difficulties. Clarify the land tenure status before quoting. If privately owned, politely decline and proceed with the owner's authorisation. If communal land, negotiate the fee as a project cost.

#### Q: 153 My tiling work in a complex caused vibration that cracked the neighbour's wall. The neighbour is threatening me with a lawyer. Am I liable, or is the client who hired me?
**Answer:** Under the neighbour law principle (sic utere tuo ut alienum non laedas — use your property so as not to harm another's), the person who caused the nuisance is primarily liable. As the contractor who created the vibration, you are the direct cause. However, the client (property owner) is also liable as the person who engaged you to work on their property. The neighbour's claim is against both of you jointly and severally. Your defence: (a) the vibration was reasonable for tiling work (tile cutting and scabbling produce unavoidable vibration), (b) the neighbour's wall was structurally unsound and would have cracked from any minor vibration, (c) you used standard equipment and techniques. If the wall was already compromised (latent defect), the neighbour's claim may fail or be reduced. Notify your public liability insurer immediately.

#### Q: 154 I was working in a complex where the neighbour complained to the body corporate about "excessive noise" at 10am on a Tuesday. The body corporate issued a warning letter threatening fines. Can they actually fine me, or only the unit owner?
**Answer:** The body corporate can only enforce its conduct rules against owners (or occupiers) of units within the scheme — not against third-party contractors. The owner who engaged you is the person liable for any breach of conduct rules, including noise restrictions. The body corporate can fine the owner (unit holder) for noise violations. The owner may then claim indemnification from you if the noise was excessive or outside permitted hours — but 10am Tuesday is within standard working hours (07:00–18:00 per most bylaws and conduct rules). The body corporate's fine is likely unenforceable if the noise occurred during permitted hours. Advise the client to challenge the fine and point out that tiling work during daytime hours is reasonable and lawful. The body corporate must specify what bylaw or conduct rule was violated.

#### Q: 155 I'm tiling a house in a heritage area in the Western Cape. The client didn't tell me it's a heritage property. The neighbour reported the work to Heritage Western Cape and they've issued a stop-work order. Can I claim my standing time costs from the client?
**Answer:** Yes. The National Heritage Resources Act 25 of 1999 requires any alteration to a heritage-protected property to have a heritage permit from the relevant heritage authority (Heritage Western Cape for the Western Cape). Working without a permit is an offence, and the owner has a duty to inform contractors of heritage restrictions. If the client failed to disclose the heritage status and obtain the required permit, she is liable for all costs arising from the stop-work order, including your standing time, remobilisation costs, and any demurrage on hired equipment. This should be treated as a variation or delay caused by the employer. Going forward: always ask whether a property has heritage protection before quoting, and include a clause requiring the client to obtain all necessary permits before work commences.

#### Q: 156 I was tiling in a house and the neighbour's domestic worker accused me of stealing her cellphone. I didn't. The neighbour now won't let me park near her house and is badmouthing me to other residents. What can I do?
**Answer:** False accusations of theft are defamatory if published to a third party (the other residents). You can: (a) demand a retraction and apology in writing, (b) if the accusation was made to SAPS and no theft charge was laid, the false report may constitute crimen iniuria (unlawful impairment of dignity), (c) if reputational damage has resulted in lost business, you may sue for defamation in the Magistrate's Court. The neighbour's parking ban is only enforceable if it is on her property — public roads cannot be restricted. For the theft accusation: if you are confident of your innocence, offer to cooperate with any investigation (though SAPS is unlikely to investigate a missing cellphone without evidence). Document all defamatory statements and their witnesses.

#### Q: 157 I need to tile a flat in a Sandton high-rise. The body corporate won't give me access to the service lift because I don't have "their" contractor insurance. Can they legally block me?
**Answer:** Body corporates under the Sectional Titles Schemes Management Act can impose reasonable rules for the use of common property, including requiring contractors to provide proof of insurance before using common facilities. This is generally lawful provided the rule is applied uniformly and is reasonable. If their rule requires all contractors to have public liability insurance and show proof, that is reasonable. If they are blocking you while allowing other uninsured contractors access, that is discriminatory and unlawful. Provide your public liability insurance certificate — if you don't have insurance, this is a wake-up call to get it. The body corporate cannot impose arbitrary restrictions but can enforce reasonable safety and insurance requirements for common property access.

#### Q: 158 A client in a security estate won't give me the access code for the estate gate because "it's confidential." She says I must wait at the gate for her to let me in every morning. This wastes 30 minutes daily. Can I charge for standing time?
**Answer:** Yes. Access to the site is the client's responsibility under standard contract terms. If she fails to provide reasonable access, causing delay to your work, the delay is an employer-caused delay entitling you to: (a) a time extension, (b) standing time costs (wages for idle labour, vehicle waiting time), and (c) remobilisation costs if the delay exceeds half a day. Document each day's delay with arrival time and access time. Request the access code in writing, or request that the client arrange a contractor access pass with the estate security. If she refuses all reasonable alternatives, include the standing time in your variation claim. This is a standard contractual entitlement — the client cannot unreasonably withhold access and expect you to absorb the cost.

#### Q: 159 I was tiling a house and the neighbour's tree roots had lifted the concrete floor by nearly 80mm. The client wants me to tile over it anyway with a "thick bed" of adhesive. I know this will fail. What do I tell her?
**Answer:** Tiling over a floor lifted 80mm by tree roots is a temporary fix that will fail — the roots will continue growing and lift the tiles. SANS 10400-J requires substrates to be structurally sound and stable. The correct solution is tree root removal or barrier installation by an arborist, followed by floor repair or replacement. As a professional, you must refuse to install tiles over an obviously unstable substrate — doing so would be negligent and you would be liable when it fails. Provide the client with a written explanation: tree roots will continue to expand, the lifting force will crack tiles and debond adhesive, and the correct remediation is arboricultural work before tiling. If she insists, require a signed waiver acknowledging that you have no warranty on the installation — but even a waiver may not fully protect you from negligence claims.

#### Q: 160 I tiled a house in a mining town (Welkom). After 6 months, the tiles started showing orange staining. The client's borehole water has high iron content. Is this my problem or the water's?
**Answer:** Iron-rich borehole water causes orange/brown staining (iron oxidation) on porous tiles and grout. This is a water quality issue, not an installation defect. However, your liability depends on whether you should have anticipated the problem: (a) if you used unglazed or porous tiles in an area where iron staining is known (common in mining towns with borehole water), you may share liability for material selection, (b) if you used impervious porcelain tiles and the staining is on the grout only, this is purely a maintenance issue — grout should be sealed to reduce staining. Advise the client to: test borehole water quality, install iron filtration, seal grout annually, and use appropriate cleaning products (acid-based cleaners remove iron stains). The borehole water quality is not your responsibility unless you guaranteed the installation against all water conditions.

#### Q: 161 I was tiling in a complex where the security guard refused to let my delivery truck in because the driver didn't have an ID document. The load was time-sensitive (wet screed). The screed set in the truck. Who pays for the wasted screed?
**Answer:** The client is responsible for ensuring site access for deliveries. If the security guard was enforcing complex rules and the client failed to arrange access or pre-register the delivery, the wasted screed is a client-caused delay. The same applies if the client knew about the ID requirement and didn't inform you. Document: the delivery time, the security guard's refusal and reason, your notification to the client, and the screed's condition. Charge the client for the wasted screed and re-delivery. Going forward: include in your contract that "the client shall ensure unimpeded site access for all deliveries and personnel. Costs arising from access restrictions shall be for the client's account."

#### Q: 162 I'm a small contractor. A big developer wants me to sign a contract that says I must carry R5 million public liability insurance. My current policy is only R1 million. The developer won't budge. What are my options?
**Answer:** Your options: (a) increase your public liability cover to R5 million (contact your broker — increasing from R1m to R5m typically adds R300–R800/month), (b) negotiate a lower limit (R2m or R3m), (c) accept the contract and arrange a top-up policy for this specific project, (d) walk away. R5 million is standard for commercial construction contracts — it is not unreasonable for the developer to require this. Many contractors start with R1m cover and increase it as they grow. The cost of R5m cover is a small price for access to commercial work. Contact your insurance broker to quote the increase before responding to the developer — showing you are arranging the cover demonstrates professionalism and may improve your negotiating position.

#### Q: 163 I was tiling a house and the neighbour complained that dust from my tile cutting was settling on her washing. She threw a bucket of dirty water over my labourer. Can I lay a charge, and can she stop my work?
**Answer:** Throwing dirty water over someone constitutes common assault — you can lay a charge at SAPS. She cannot unilaterally stop your work — only a court order (interdict) or a lawful directive from a municipal authority can stop construction work. However, her complaint about dust is valid — you have a duty under the OHSA and municipal bylaws to control dust and nuisance. Mitigate dust by: cutting tiles outdoors away from neighbours, using wet cutting (water suppression), erecting dust barriers, and scheduling cutting during reasonable hours. If you take reasonable dust control measures and she still complains, her assault is unjustified. Document the assault (photos of your labourer's condition, witness statements) and lay the charge. Do not escalate the confrontation — remain professional.

#### Q: 164 A client's property is on a steep slope in Cape Town. My truck can't access the site — I need to carry all materials up 80 steps. This will double my labour cost. Can I claim a variation?
**Answer:** Yes. Difficult site access that was not apparent from the initial site inspection (or was not disclosed by the client) constitutes a changed condition entitling you to a variation order. Document the access difficulty with photographs and video. Calculate the additional cost: extra labour for manual carrying, additional time, potential need for smaller vehicles, and equipment hire (e.g., material hoist). Submit a variation claim before commencing the additional work — if you proceed without notifying the client of the extra cost, you may struggle to claim later. Include a standard clause in your quotes: "Pricing is based on normal vehicular access to the work area. Additional costs arising from restricted access will be charged as a variation."

#### Q: 165 I discovered human remains (old bones) while excavating for a new screed in a heritage area. The client wants me to keep digging and "just bury them deeper." What do I do?
**Answer:** STOP immediately. Under the National Heritage Resources Act and the Human Tissue Act, the discovery of human remains must be reported to SAPS and the relevant heritage authority immediately. Moving, disturbing, or concealing human remains is a criminal offence. Do not touch the remains or the surrounding area. Contact SAPS and the South African Heritage Resources Agency (SAHRA) or provincial heritage authority. The area may be declared a heritage site requiring archaeological investigation before work can resume. The delay and associated costs (archaeological monitoring, potential redesign) are the client's risk. Do not follow the client's instruction to bury the remains deeper — this would make you criminally liable as an accessory.

#### Q: 166 I was tiling a house and the municipal inspector arrived, saying I need a building plan approval for "alterations and additions" because the floor level is changing by more than the threshold. Is this correct?
**Answer:** Municipal bylaws vary, but most require building plan approval for: changes to floor levels that affect door thresholds or step heights, structural alterations, changes to drainage, and any work affecting the building envelope. If your tiling changes the floor level such that door clearances are affected or step heights change, building plan approval may indeed be required. However, cosmetic tiling (same floor level, no structural changes) typically does not require approval. The inspector's authority is limited to their municipality's bylaws — ask them to specify which bylaw provision requires approval. If you proceed without approval and it is required, the municipality can issue a stop-work order and fine the property owner. The responsibility for obtaining plan approval lies with the owner, not you — but you should verify approval before starting if structural changes are involved.

#### Q: 167 I tiled a client's house and his wife loved it, but the husband hates it. He's demanding I remove it and redo it in a different colour — at my cost. They are married in community of property. Does the husband's dissatisfaction override the wife's approval?
**Answer:** In a marriage in community of property, either spouse can bind the joint estate for household necessaries and improvements — tiling a home qualifies. The wife's approval of the work (and presumably her instruction to proceed) is binding on the joint estate. The husband's personal aesthetic preference does not override the contract or constitute a defect. If the work is technically sound and the wife (who engaged you) is satisfied, the husband has no legal basis to demand removal at your cost. If the couple disagrees, that is a domestic matter, not your contractual issue. Do not engage in their marital dispute — your contract is with the person who engaged you (the wife), and her approval of completed work constitutes acceptance. If he is violent or threatening, remove yourself from the situation and communicate only with your contracting party.

#### Q: 168 I was tiling in a block of flats and the tenant in the flat below complained that water from my wet cutting was leaking through her ceiling. I was cutting in the client's bathroom with the drain open. How did water get downstairs?
**Answer:** Water from wet cutting finds the path of least resistance — if the floor has cracks, unsealed pipe penetrations, or gaps around drainage pipes, water will seep through to the unit below. This indicates either: (a) a latent defect in the floor slab (insufficient waterproofing around pipe penetrations — the building developer's fault), or (b) your failure to contain cutting water (your responsibility). When wet cutting indoors, always: use a cutting tray to capture water, avoid cutting directly over drains where water can flow down pipe penetrations, use minimal water, and place absorbent mats around the cutting area. If the water ingress was due to building defects (unsealed penetrations), the body corporate is liable for the downstairs damage. If it was due to your water management, you are liable.

#### Q: 169 I'm a one-person contractor. I injured my back on site and can't work for 6 weeks. I have no income and no insurance. Is there any government support I can claim?
**Answer:** As a self-employed person, you are not covered by the Compensation Fund (COIDA covers employees, not principals). You have no UIF benefits because you don't contribute to UIF as a self-employed person (unless you voluntarily registered). Your options are limited: (a) if you have a provident fund or retirement annuity, check for disability benefits, (b) if the injury was caused by a third party's negligence (e.g., unsafe site conditions provided by the client), you may have a civil claim against them, (c) SASSA disability grant has strict criteria and long processing times — unlikely to help immediately. Going forward: consider income protection insurance for self-employed contractors (available from major insurers). It typically covers 75% of average monthly income during disability periods and is the single most important insurance for a sole trader.

#### Q: 170 I was offered a government housing project (RDP house tiling) through a contact in the municipality. He says I need to pay a "facilitation fee" of R15,000 to "secure the tender." Is this how government tenders work?
**Answer:** No. This is bribery, which is a criminal offence under the Prevention and Combating of Corrupt Activities Act (PRECCA). Paying a facilitation fee to secure a government tender constitutes corruption — both you and the municipal official face criminal prosecution, imprisonment of up to life for severe cases, and blacklisting from all government procurement. All government tenders must be advertised publicly and awarded through a transparent process (preferential procurement points system based on price and B-BBEE). Any "shortcut" involving payment to an official is corruption. Report the approach to the National Treasury's Tender Fraud hotline or the Public Protector. Do not pay — even if you "win" the tender, the contract can be set aside, and you will be prosecuted.
## 11. WARRANTY, RETENTION & POST-COMPLETION HEADACHES

#### Q: 171 I completed a tiling job 14 months ago. The client is now claiming grout cracking and demanding I fix it for free, saying I "guaranteed the work for 5 years." I never said that. What's my actual warranty obligation?
**Answer:** Under common law, a contractor's workmanship warranty is typically 1 year for non-structural work (tiling falls into this category). The NHBRC 5-year warranty applies only to new home construction enrolled with the NHBRC and covers structural defects, not tiling finishes. The CPA Section 56 provides an implied 6-month warranty for defects that existed at the time of supply (the consumer can demand repair, replacement, or refund within 6 months without proving the defect existed). After 6 months, the consumer must prove the defect existed at the time of supply. At 14 months, grout cracking is more likely caused by: substrate movement, structural settlement, temperature cycling, or inadequate maintenance — not by your original workmanship. Offer to inspect at a call-out fee, but do not accept a free repair obligation unless the defect is proven to be your fault.

#### Q: 172 A client is holding 10% retention on a R120,000 residential tiling job for 12 months. I need the cash flow now. Is 12-month retention on residential tiling even legal?
**Answer:** Retention is a contractual matter — if you agreed to 12-month retention in your contract, it is legally binding. However, 12-month retention for residential tiling is excessive by industry standards. Standard practice for residential work is: no retention (payment on completion), or 5% retention for 3 months (defects liability period). For commercial work under JBCC, 5% is standard until practical completion, then release at final completion. If you did not agree to retention in your contract and the client is unilaterally withholding payment, that is breach of contract — sue for the full amount. If you did agree but now need cash flow, you can offer the client a retention bond (guarantee from an insurance company) in exchange for early release — the client holds the bond instead of cash, and you get your money (minus the bond premium).

#### Q: 173 I subcontracted a plumbing contractor to do the wet area waterproofing before I tiled. His waterproofing failed and I had to remove my tiles to fix it. He's gone out of business. Can I claim from my professional indemnity insurance?
**Answer:** Professional indemnity insurance covers YOUR professional negligence, not the negligence of subcontractors you engage. If the waterproofing failure was the plumber's fault (not yours), your PI insurance will not respond — the claim is against the plumber. Since he's out of business, your claim may be unenforceable. This illustrates why you should: (a) verify subcontractor insurance before engagement, (b) include indemnity clauses in subcontract agreements, (c) consider contractors' all-risk insurance with "subcontractor default" coverage. If you had a duty to inspect and approve the waterproofing before tiling, and you failed to do so, your negligence may trigger PI cover — but only for your portion of liability, not the plumber's. Going forward: always verify subcontractor waterproofing with a flood test before tiling.

#### Q: 174 A client from a job I completed 3 years ago called me back because she wants a "warranty inspection." I never offered an ongoing warranty inspection service. Do I have any obligation to return?
**Answer:** No. Your warranty obligation is limited to repairing defects that existed at the time of installation and that manifest within the warranty period (typically 1 year for workmanship). A "warranty inspection" 3 years later is not a standard obligation — the warranty has likely expired, and even if it hadn't, you are only required to respond to reported defects, not conduct proactive inspections. You can offer a paid inspection service (call-out fee plus hourly rate) as a goodwill gesture, but you have no contractual or legal obligation to conduct free inspections years after completion. Be polite but clear: "Our workmanship warranty covered the first year. I'd be happy to conduct a paid inspection if you have specific concerns."

#### Q: 175 I repaired a tile defect under warranty. The repair required a slightly different shade of grout because the original was discontinued. The client says the repair is unacceptable because the grout doesn't match exactly. Do I need to redo the entire floor?
**Answer:** No. Warranty repairs are intended to restore functionality, not necessarily achieve perfect aesthetic match — especially when the original materials are discontinued. The legal standard is "reasonable repair" — a repair that restores the floor to functional use with acceptable appearance, considering the circumstances. If the colour difference is minor and only visible upon close inspection, the repair meets the standard. If the client wants a perfect match, she would need to accept a full re-grout (at her cost, not warranty). Document that the original grout was discontinued and that you used the closest available match. Offer a good-faith discount on a full re-grout if she insists on perfection — but do not absorb the cost of aesthetic perfection when you have met your warranty obligation.

#### Q: 176 I sold my tiling business to another contractor. A former client is now claiming defects on work I did before the sale. Is the new owner liable, or am I?
**Answer:** Warranty liability depends on the sale agreement. If the business was sold as a going concern (including all assets, liabilities, and ongoing contracts), the buyer typically assumes warranty obligations for work done before the sale — unless the contract specifically excluded pre-sale warranties. If you sold only the assets (tools, vehicle, client list) without assuming liabilities, you remain liable for pre-sale defects. The client can sue either: you (as the original contractor who did the work) or the new owner (if he holds himself out as continuing the business). To protect yourself in a business sale: include a clause requiring the buyer to honour all warranties, obtain warranty run-off insurance, and retain records of completed work for the warranty period.

#### Q: 177 I completed a job and the client signed off, paid in full, and gave me a glowing review. Six months later, she sold the house and the new owner is claiming the same work is defective. Can the new owner claim against me?
**Answer:** The new owner's ability to claim depends on: (a) whether the defect was latent (not discoverable on reasonable inspection) at the time of the original client's sign-off, and (b) whether there is a contractual or statutory warranty that transfers to subsequent owners. Under common law, the original client's sign-off with full knowledge of the condition generally prevents later claims for patent (visible) defects. However, latent defects that were not discoverable at sign-off may still support a claim. The CPA Section 56 warranty applies to the "consumer" — the person who contracted for the service. The new owner was not the consumer and cannot claim under the CPA. His claim, if any, would be against the seller (previous owner) who sold him a property with defects. Your contractual warranty was to the original client, not to future owners.

#### Q: 178 A client wants me to give a 10-year written warranty on a tiling job. I normally give 1 year. If I sign her 10-year document, what am I actually committing to?
**Answer:** A 10-year written warranty is a contractual guarantee that creates a legally binding obligation to repair any defects arising within 10 years, regardless of cause (unless the warranty document specifies exclusions). This is significantly beyond industry standard (1 year for workmanship) and exposes you to enormous liability — structural movement, subfloor failure, building settlement, and material degradation over 10 years are not within your control as a tiler. If you are considering this: (a) limit the warranty to "defects in workmanship and materials due to faulty installation only" — excluding substrate movement, structural defects, and misuse, (b) require the client to maintain the tiles per manufacturer guidelines, (c) cap your liability at the original contract value, (d) exclude consequential damages. Better yet: refuse. Explain that 1 year is industry standard and that longer warranties require insurance backing that significantly increases the project cost.

#### Q: 179 I repaired a defect under warranty. The same defect recurred 3 months later in the same area. Is my warranty period extended by the repair, or does the original warranty still expire on the original date?
**Answer:** Under common law, a warranty repair does not automatically extend the original warranty period unless the warranty document or your contract specifically states that repairs carry their own warranty period. However, if the same defect recurs after a repair, this suggests the original repair was inadequate — you may have a fresh obligation to repair again if the recurrence is within a reasonable time of the original repair. Some contractors offer a "90-day repair warranty" as a goodwill gesture. The fairest approach: if the same defect recurs shortly after your repair, treat it as an extension of the original warranty obligation and repair it without charge. This builds reputation and avoids disputes. If the recurrence is due to a different cause (e.g., new substrate movement), that is a new issue, not a warranty repair.

#### Q: 180 A client is demanding I replace her entire bathroom floor because one tile has a hairline crack. The bathroom is 3 years old. She says the crack proves poor workmanship. Is replacing one tile reasonable, or must I redo the whole floor?
**Answer:** Replacing one tile is the reasonable remedy. A single hairline crack in a 3-year-old bathroom does not justify full floor replacement unless: (a) the crack indicates a systemic failure (e.g., all tiles will crack soon), or (b) the exact tile is discontinued and no close match exists. At 3 years, your workmanship warranty has expired. The crack could be caused by: dropped object impact, structural movement, temperature shock (hot water spill), or normal wear. Offer to replace the single tile at your cost (if you feel goodwill is warranted) or at the client's cost. If the client wants the entire floor redone, she must prove systemic failure caused by your original workmanship — which is unlikely after 3 years. Do not allow unreasonable demands to escalate — offer reasonable solutions and stand firm.
## 12. TAX, ACCOUNTING & CASH-IN-HAND TRAPS

#### Q: 181 A client paid me R35,000 cash and asked for no invoice because "she doesn't want her husband to know how much she spent." I deposited the cash in my bank account. Can SARS question this deposit?
**Answer:** Yes. SARS has extensive data-matching capabilities and can flag unexplained cash deposits. Under the Tax Administration Act, SARS can require you to explain the source of any deposit. If the amount represents taxable income that you did not declare, you are evading tax. The client's motive (hiding spending from her husband) is irrelevant to your tax obligation. You must declare all income, whether invoiced or not, whether cash or EFT. If SARS determines you deliberately understated income, penalties range from 10% to 200% of the tax shortfall, plus interest and potential criminal prosecution. The correct action: issue the invoice (the client's marital issues are not your problem), declare the income, and pay the tax. If she doesn't want an invoice, that is her choice — but your obligation to declare income is independent of invoicing.

#### Q: 182 I quoted a job excluding materials (labour only). The client bought materials and wants a "labour only" invoice from me. She says she'll claim the materials on her tax. Is there any risk to me in issuing a labour-only invoice when I know she's inflating her material claim?
**Answer:** If you are genuinely providing labour only, your labour-only invoice is accurate and creates no risk for you. The client's material claims are between her and SARS — you are not responsible for her tax claims. However, if she asks you to invoice for "materials" that you did not supply (so she can claim a larger deduction), that is tax fraud and you are complicit if you issue the false invoice. Only invoice for what you actually supplied or did. If your relationship with the client involves you procuring materials and on-selling them, you should include materials on your invoice at cost (or with your markup) and declare the full amount as income. Separate labour and material invoicing is common and legitimate — just ensure it reflects reality.

#### Q: 183 I'm a sole proprietor. Can I pay my child's school fees from my business account and claim it as a business expense because I "need him in school so I can work"?
**Answer:** No. School fees are a personal expense, not a business expense. SARS applies the "in the production of income" test — expenses must be incurred for the purpose of producing income, not for personal or domestic purposes. The fact that having your child in school enables you to work does not make school fees a deductible business expense. Claiming personal expenses as business expenses constitutes tax fraud. If SARS audits you and disallows the deduction, you will owe the additional tax plus penalties (up to 200% for intentional evasion) and interest. Keep personal and business expenses strictly separate. If you pay personal expenses from your business account, record them as "owner's drawings" — not business expenses.

#### Q: 184 A client paid me in full but the payment was from a third-party account (her son's account). SARS is asking me to explain why the payer name doesn't match the client name. Is this a problem?
**Answer:** It is not inherently a problem — third-party payments are common and legal (e.g., family members paying on behalf of the contracting party). The issue for SARS is whether the payment represents taxable income and whether all income is properly declared. As long as: (a) you declared the full income, (b) you have a contract or invoice linking the work to the client (not the son), and (c) the amount is reasonable for the work done, there is no tax issue. Provide SARS with: the contract with the original client, your invoice, and an explanation that the son paid on her behalf (a simple affidavit from the client confirming this is helpful). If the amount is large and unexplained, SARS may investigate further under anti-money laundering legislation (FICA). Keep documentation linking the payment to legitimate business income.

#### Q: 185 I take materials from my business stock for my own home renovation. I don't record this as a sale — I just use them. What's the tax treatment?
**Answer:** Taking business stock for personal use is called "stock appropriation" or "drawings in kind." SARS requires you to account for this as if you sold the materials to yourself at market value. The market value of the materials is taxable income to your business (you cannot reduce your business income by the cost of materials you personally consumed) and represents drawings from the business. If you don't declare this, you are understating business income. The correct treatment: record the market value of materials taken as a "drawing" (reducing your equity/capital account) and include the value in your business turnover. If you are VAT-registered, you must account for output VAT on the market value of the goods taken for personal use — this is a "deemed supply" under the VAT Act.

#### Q: 186 I "employ" my 16-year-old son on weekends to help carry tiles. Do I need to register him for UIF and PAYE, and are there child labour laws I need to worry about?
**Answer:** Under the Basic Conditions of Employment Act, children under 15 cannot be employed (with limited exceptions for family businesses and light work). At 16, your son can work but with restrictions: no hazardous work (construction sites are hazardous), limited hours (not during school hours, maximum hours per week), and he must have a certificate of fitness if required by the Sectoral Determination. Construction work by a 16-year-old raises child labour concerns — tile carrying on an active construction site is arguably hazardous. UIF and PAYE: if he works more than 24 hours per month, UIF applies. PAYE applies if his earnings exceed the tax threshold (currently R95,750 per year for under-65s). The safer approach: keep his hours minimal, ensure no hazardous tasks, pay him an allowance rather than wages, and document that his participation is family assistance, not employment.

#### Q: 187 I received a SARS auto-assessment that shows I owe R12,000 more tax than I calculated. The auto-assessment includes income from clients I invoiced but who never paid me. Can SARS tax me on income I never actually received?
**Answer:** If you are on the invoice (accrual) basis for tax, SARS taxes you when you invoice, not when you receive payment. Most small contractors are automatically on the accrual basis unless they qualify for and elect the cash basis (turnover below R2.5 million and meet other criteria). If you are on the accrual basis, SARS is correct — you are taxed on invoiced income regardless of whether it was paid. However, if the debt has become irrecoverable (you've exhausted collection efforts and can prove the debtor cannot pay), you can claim a bad debt deduction under Section 11(i) of the Income Tax Act. To avoid this issue going forward: consider electing the cash basis if eligible (you are taxed only on income actually received), or write off bad debts promptly and claim the deduction.

#### Q: 188 I'm paying my casual labourers cash daily without any records. A labour inspector is coming tomorrow. How much trouble am I in?
**Answer:** Significant trouble. Paying employees cash without records violates: Section 33 of the BCEA (payslip requirement), Section 4 of the UIF Act (failure to register and contribute), Section 81 of COIDA (failure to register), and potentially the Income Tax Act (failure to deduct PAYE if applicable). The labour inspector can issue compliance notices for each violation, with fines escalating for repeat offences. For a first offence, expect: a written warning and compliance order, fines per employee per violation (potentially R10,000+ total), and a requirement to regularise all workers within a specified period. Your best course of action: be cooperative, acknowledge the issue, explain that you are a small business learning compliance, and commit to immediate regularisation. The inspector has discretion — cooperation and genuine intent to comply often result in more lenient treatment than confrontation.

#### Q: 189 I used my personal vehicle for both business and private use. Can I claim the full cost of the vehicle (fuel, maintenance, insurance) as a business expense?
**Answer:** No. You can only claim the portion of vehicle expenses attributable to business use. SARS requires a logbook recording business and private kilometres. The deductible amount is calculated as: (business kilometres / total kilometres) x total vehicle expenses. If you don't keep a logbook, SARS will disallow the deduction entirely. You cannot claim the full cost unless the vehicle is used 100% for business (rare for a small contractor who drives home). Keep a detailed logbook: date, starting odometer, destination, purpose of trip, and closing odometer. SARS audits vehicle claims frequently — inadequate documentation is one of the most common reasons for additional tax assessments.

#### Q: 190 I bought a laptop and smartphone for my tiling business. Can I claim the full cost in the year of purchase, or must I depreciate them over several years?
**Answer:** For income tax purposes, electronic equipment falls into different wear-and-tear categories. Laptops and smartphones are typically written off over 3 years (one-third per year) under SARS wear-and-tear allowances. However, if the cost of each item is relatively low (under R7,000 per item), SARS may allow immediate write-off as a "small item" under the de minimis principle. If you are VAT-registered, you can claim the input VAT in the period of purchase. Keep all invoices and record the assets in an asset register. When you eventually sell or dispose of the assets, there may be tax implications (recoupment of wear-and-tear or capital gains). For a small contractor, the key point is: claim the deduction over the wear-and-tear period (3 years), not all at once, unless the item falls below the small asset threshold.
## 13. THE TRULY BIZARRE — EDGE CASES & STRANGE BUT REAL

#### Q: 191 A client's cat died while I was tiling her house. She says the tile adhesive fumes poisoned it and she wants R25,000 for "emotional damages" and a new purebred cat. The cat was 16 years old. Am I liable?
**Answer:** Cementitious tile adhesives contain Portland cement, sand, and polymer additives — none of which release toxic fumes at concentrations that would harm a cat at normal room ventilation. A 16-year-old cat has exceeded the average lifespan and death from natural causes is far more likely than adhesive poisoning. For liability: (a) she must prove causation (the adhesive caused the death) — a veterinary post-mortem would be required, (b) she must prove negligence (you used the product incorrectly or failed to ventilate), (c) emotional damages for pet death are limited in South African law — pets are property, and damages are limited to replacement value (not emotional value). Her claim is highly unlikely to succeed. Do not admit any connection between your work and the cat's death. Offer sympathy but not compensation. If she sues in Small Claims Court, her lack of evidence will doom her case.

#### Q: 192 I was tiling a synagogue and accidentally damaged a mezuzah (a small religious scroll case) on the door frame. The rabbi says it must be replaced by a kosher scribe at a cost of R8,000 and the room must be "re-consecrated." Am I liable for the religious ceremony costs, or just the physical damage?
**Answer:** Under South African delict law, liability is limited to patrimonial loss — actual financial loss directly caused by your negligence. The replacement cost of the mezuzah (the physical object) is your liability. The cost of a kosher scribe and religious ceremony is more complex: if the ceremony is a necessary part of the replacement process (i.e., a mezuzah is not considered valid without proper installation by a scribe), that cost may be part of the direct loss. However, "re-consecration" of the room is likely a separate religious matter, not a direct financial loss caused by the physical damage. Offer to pay for the mezuzah replacement and the scribe's installation. Dispute the re-consecration cost as not being patrimonial loss — it is a spiritual/religious matter, not compensable in a South African court. Be respectful but firm.

#### Q: 193 A client's pet python escaped its enclosure while I was working and slithered under the newly laid tiles through an expansion joint gap. The client wants me to remove the tiles to rescue the snake. I haven't been paid yet. Do I have to destroy my work to rescue a snake?
**Answer:** This is a truly unusual situation. You do not have a legal obligation to destroy your completed work to rescue a pet, even one trapped in the structure. However, as a matter of practicality and goodwill: if the tiles have not yet fully set (within 24–48 hours of installation), removing a few tiles to rescue the snake is feasible without destroying the entire installation. After the adhesive has cured, removing tiles causes damage. Your position: the snake's escape is not your fault — you did not create the expansion joint gaps (they are part of normal tiling practice), and you did not release the snake. Offer to remove a minimal number of tiles at the client's cost (materials and labour) if the rescue is possible with minimal damage. Do not agree to remove the entire floor. If the client refuses to pay because of the snake situation, that is not a valid defence — the work was completed and payment is due.

#### Q: 194 I tiled a floor for a client who is a sangoma. She now says the tile pattern I created "disrupted the ancestral energy lines" in her consultation room and is causing her to lose clients. She wants a full refund and R30,000 for lost income. Is this claim legally valid?
**Answer:** No. A claim for lost income due to "disrupted ancestral energy lines" is not a recognised cause of action under South African law. Courts do not recognise spiritual or supernatural harm as compensable damage unless it involves recognised delictual wrongs (e.g., defamation, theft, property damage). The tiles were installed as a physical product — their alleged spiritual effects are not a defect under the CPA or any building standard. Her claim would fail in any court. However, be culturally sensitive in your response — explain that while you respect her beliefs, your obligation is limited to the physical installation of tiles, and there is no defect in the workmanship or materials. If she sues in Small Claims Court, the commissioner will dismiss the claim on the basis that no legally recognisable damage has been established.

#### Q: 195 I was tiling a house and the client's husband accused me of having an affair with his wife because I was "in the house too long." He threatened to assault me and told me to leave immediately. I'd completed 70% of the work and hadn't been paid. What do I do?
**Answer:** Your personal safety is the priority — leave immediately if threatened with violence. Once safe: document everything (the threat, what was said, any witnesses), lay a charge of assault or crimen injuria at SAPS, and contact the wife (your contracting party) in writing to confirm the work suspension and the reason. The husband's threats constitute breach of the peaceful possession obligation — the client must provide a safe working environment. You are entitled to: (a) payment for work completed (quantum meruit), (b) costs of materials ordered, (c) demobilisation costs, and (d) lost profit on the uncompleted portion (if you can prove it). The marital dispute is between the spouses — do not engage in it. Communicate only with your contracting party (the wife) in writing. If she confirms the contract continues, arrange access when the husband is not present.

#### Q: 196 A client asked me to install "smart tiles" with embedded LED lights she bought from a Chinese website. The tiles have no SANS certification, no CE marking, and no IP rating. I refused because they're electrically unsafe. She's threatening bad reviews. Should I stand my ground?
**Answer:** Absolutely stand your ground. Installing uncertified electrical products is a criminal offence under the Electrical Installation Regulations (OHSA) and the National Regulator for Compulsory Specifications Act. If the LED tiles cause a fire or electrocution, you face criminal prosecution, civil liability, and professional ruin. No certification (SANS, CE, or NRCS mark) means the product has not been tested for South African electrical safety standards. IP rating (Ingress Protection) is critical for any electrical product in a bathroom or wet area — without it, the product is dangerous. Explain to the client that you are legally prohibited from installing uncertified electrical products and that her supplier has sold her an illegal product in South Africa. Offer to install certified LED lighting solutions from reputable local suppliers instead. Bad reviews are preferable to a criminal record or a death on your conscience.

#### Q: 197 I was tiling in a client's house and accidentally broke a framed photo of her deceased mother. She's demanding R50,000 for the "irreplaceable sentimental value." The frame was worth R200. How much am I actually liable for?
**Answer:** Under South African law, damages for damage to property are limited to patrimonial loss — the actual financial value of the item. For a framed photograph, this is: the cost of the frame (R200) plus the cost of reprinting the photograph (if a negative or digital file exists). South African courts do not award damages for sentimental value of personal items unless the item is unique and has objective value (e.g., an antique, an original artwork). A standard framed photograph, while sentimentally valuable, is not irreplaceable if the image can be reprinted. Offer to pay for: (a) a replacement frame of similar quality, (b) professional photo restoration and reprinting, and (c) a sincere apology. If no digital file or negative exists, the emotional impact is unfortunate but not legally compensable beyond the frame's actual value.

#### Q: 198 A client wants me to tile a room that she uses for marijuana cultivation. The tiles are for the grow room floor. The cultivation is illegal (she has no medical licence). If I do the work, am I committing a crime?
**Answer:** Under the Drugs and Drug Trafficking Act 140 of 1992 and the recent Constitutional Court ruling (Prince v President), private cannabis use and cultivation for personal use is decriminalised (up to a reasonable quantity for personal use). However, commercial-scale cultivation without a licence remains illegal. If the grow room is clearly for commercial sale (not personal use), you may be committing the common law crime of aiding and abetting an illegal activity by facilitating it. While prosecution of a tiler is unlikely, you are associating with criminal activity. If police raid the premises, you may be questioned and your tools seized as evidence. The ethical and legal answer is: decline the job if the cultivation is clearly illegal commercial activity. If it is for personal medical use (with a valid prescription), the legal risk is minimal.

#### Q: 199 I completed a tiling job and the client paid me. Two weeks later, she called to say her feng shui consultant says the tiles are laid in an "inauspicious direction" and I must remove and relay them facing a different way — at my cost. Do I have any obligation?
**Answer:** No. Feng shui is not a building standard, not part of your contract, and not a defect under the CPA or any South African law. Tile direction is an aesthetic choice — unless the contract specified a particular direction, you laid the tiles in a professional manner and the work is complete.
