The client's post-hoc aesthetic preference, however motivated, does not create a warranty obligation. Offer to relay the tiles at her cost as a variation (full price for removal and reinstallation), but do not accept this as a warranty claim. If she threatens non-payment for future work or negative reviews, remind her that the work was completed to specification and paid for — the feng shui issue is a personal preference that arose after completion and acceptance.

#### Q: 200 I've been a tiling contractor for 20 years. A client asked me the strangest question ever: "If my house burns down, will the tiles survive, and will you reinstall them for free?" What's the actual answer?
**Answer:** Ceramic and porcelain tiles are non-combustible and can survive temperatures up to 1,000°C+ — they may survive a house fire while the rest of the house burns. However, the adhesive and grout will likely fail due to thermal shock and the screed/subfloor will be damaged. "Surviving" tiles will almost certainly need to be removed because the substrate beneath them is compromised. Regarding reinstallation: your warranty covers defects in workmanship under normal conditions — a house fire is not a workmanship defect. If the fire was caused by your negligence (e.g., faulty electrical work you did — though you shouldn't be doing electrical work as a tiler), you might have liability. Otherwise, fire damage is an insurance matter — the client's building insurance covers rebuilding, including new tiling. No, you do not reinstall for free after a fire. Charge your normal rates for the rebuild — insurance typically covers contractor costs.

---

_END OF Q&A — 200 PAIRS FOR PERSONA 3: SMALLER CONTRACTORS_

# CURVEBALL Q&A — PERSONA 4: DEVELOPERS
## 200 Awkward, Strange & Unexpected But TRUE Questions South African Developers Actually Ask
### SECTION A: NHBRC WARRANTY & ENROLMENT NIGHTMARES

#### Q: 1 We forgot to enrol 12 townhouses with the NHBRC before breaking ground. The foundations are already in. What's the actual penalty per unit, and is there any way to negotiate it down?
**Answer:** The penalty for late enrolment is a levy calculated on the total construction cost, typically around R2,000 to R4,000 per dwelling depending on value, plus administrative fees. There is no formal negotiation process — the NHBRC's system calculates the levy automatically based on the enrolled value. You cannot obtain an occupation certificate without proof of enrolment. If foundations are already laid, you must enrol immediately and pay the late penalty. The NHBRC may also conduct a "stage inspection" late assessment which can flag defects retrospectively.

#### Q: 2 A buyer is claiming the porcelain tiles in their unit are "structurally defective" under the NHBRC 5-year warranty because they make a hollow sound when tapped. Is tile debonding a structural defect?
**Answer:** Tile debonding alone is not classified as a structural defect under the NHBRC warranty, which covers major structural elements (foundations, walls, roof structure). However, if the debonding is caused by structural movement — such as foundation settlement, slab deflection, or wall cracking — the NHBRC may accept it as evidence of a structural defect. The builder must prove the substrate was stable at the time of tiling. Get a structural engineer's report. Hollow-sounding tiles alone fall under finish quality (1-year maintenance period), not the 5-year structural warranty.

#### Q: 3 We sold a unit, transferred it, and now the new owner (6 months later) wants to claim under the NHBRC warranty. But we never enrolled the home because it was a "custom build for a client on their own land." Does the NHBRC warranty still apply?
**Answer:** Yes. Section 10 of the Housing Consumers Protection Measures Act requires ALL new homes to be enrolled with the NHBRC, regardless of whether the consumer owns the land. If the home was built for a housing consumer (not for resale as an investment property by a registered builder), it must be enrolled. Non-enrolment is an offence. The housing consumer can report you to the NHBRC, which may impose penalties and require enrolment retroactively. You cannot contract out of this obligation.

#### Q: 4 The NHBRC inspector failed our roof slab because of hairline cracks in the screed beneath the tiles. The screed contractor says the cracks are "shrinkage only" and don't affect structural integrity. Can we tile over cracked screed if the engineer certifies it structurally sound?
**Answer:** You can tile over cracked screed if a professional engineer certifies the cracks are non-structural (shrinkage or plastic settlement) and the screed's residual compressive strength meets design requirements (typically minimum 15 MPa for residential floors). However, the NHBRC inspector's decision can only be overturned through the formal appeals process. You must seal the cracks with an epoxy or polyurethane crack-sealing system, apply a bonded screed topping if necessary, and install a crack-isolation membrane before tiling. The engineer's sign-off and the membrane specification must be submitted to NHBRC as part of your re-inspection application.

#### Q: 5 We have 40 completed units that the NHBRC passed, but now 15 owners report cracked tiles after the first winter. The cracks follow the line of the construction joints in the concrete slab. Is this the NHBRC's problem or ours?
**Answer:** If the NHBRC passed the units, the warranty responsibility still sits with you as the developer for the 5-year structural warranty period. Hairline cracks following construction joints typically indicate either inadequate load transfer across the joint, dowel misalignment, or failure to install a movement joint in the tile bed directly above the construction joint. SANS 10107 and SANS 10400-J require that movement joints in the tile assembly align with structural joints in the substrate. This is a design/installation error, not an NHBRC inspection failure. You must repair the affected units; consider claiming from your tiling subcontractor if they failed to install movement joints.

#### Q: 6 Our development includes both free-standing houses and sectional title apartments. Do we need separate NHBRC enrolments for each, and does the 5-year warranty apply differently?
**Answer:** Each individual dwelling unit must be separately enrolled with the NHBRC. Free-standing houses are enrolled as individual homes. For sectional title schemes, each apartment unit is enrolled separately. The 5-year structural warranty applies identically to both. However, common property (lobbies, staircases, corridors) is NOT covered by the NHBRC warranty — it falls under the developer's contractual warranty to the body corporate and your professional indemnity insurance. Ensure your enrolment count matches the number of saleable units exactly.

#### Q: 7 A buyer threatened to lay a criminal charge because we sold them a home that wasn't NHBRC enrolled. Can they actually do that, or is it a civil matter only?
**Answer:** Non-enrolment of a home with the NHBRC is a criminal offence in terms of Section 24 of the Housing Consumers Protection Measures Act. A private individual cannot lay a criminal charge directly in the sense of prosecuting it, but they can report the offence to the NHBRC and the SAPS. The NHBRC has investigative powers and can refer the matter to the National Prosecuting Authority. Fines can be substantial, and directors of the development company may face personal liability. This is both a criminal and civil matter — the buyer can also sue for damages.

#### Q: 8 We're building 80 units in phases over 3 years. Can we enrol all 80 upfront and pay the levy in one go, or must we enrol as each phase breaks ground?
**Answer:** The NHBRC enrolment system ties to actual construction commencement. You cannot enrol units before breaking ground, and phased enrolment is the standard practice. However, you can apply for a "project registration" that covers the full development, with individual unit enrolments triggered as each phase commences. This allows you to lock in levy rates and avoid annual increases applying to later phases. Contact the NHBRC project liaison office to set up a master enrolment agreement.

#### Q: 9 The NHBRC inspector passed our floor tiles during construction, but 18 months later, efflorescence is pushing through the grout joints in 30% of units. The warranty period is 5 years. Is efflorescence a defect?
**Answer:** Efflorescence is generally classified as a maintenance issue, not a structural defect, under the NHBRC warranty. However, if the efflorescence is caused by a failure in the waterproofing system beneath the tile bed (such as inadequate damp-proofing or a breached membrane in wet areas), it may constitute a defect under the general building warranty. For the NHBRC 5-year structural warranty specifically, efflorescence alone does not qualify. You should address it under your 1-year maintenance obligation or investigate whether the waterproofing subcontractor's 10-year guarantee applies.

#### Q: 10 We subcontracted the entire build to a turnkey contractor. The NHBRC warranty certificate was issued in their name, not ours. Now buyers are suing us directly. Are we protected?
**Answer:** No. As the developer and seller of the units, you remain the responsible party to the housing consumer under the CPA and the sale agreement. The NHBRC warranty runs with the home, not the contractor. If your turnkey contractor holds the NHBRC certificate, you must claim an indemnity from them, but the buyer's primary claim is against you. Your sale agreement should include a hold-harmless clause from the contractor, and you should ensure the contractor is registered with the NHBRC as a "home builder." Always obtain your own NHBRC enrolment as the developer.
### SECTION B: CPA CLAIMS & CONSUMER RIGHTS

#### Q: 11 A buyer is rejecting their unit because the floor tiles are "a slightly warmer shade of beige" than the show house tiles, which they say constitutes a false representation under CPA Section 41. Are tile shade variations a CPA issue?
**Answer:** Section 41 of the CPA prohibits false, misleading, or deceptive representations. Tile shade variation is a normal manufacturing characteristic — porcelain and ceramic tiles from the same batch can vary within ISO 10545 tolerances. However, if you displayed a specific tile in the show house and specified it in the sale agreement, you must deliver that exact tile or an equivalent within reasonable commercial tolerances. Your sales documentation should include a clause stating that "tile shades may vary from show house samples due to manufacturing batch differences." Without such a disclaimer, a motivated buyer could pursue a CPA claim. The test is whether a reasonable consumer would have been misled.

#### Q: 12 We used rectified porcelain tiles in the show house but supplied non-rectified in the sold units to save costs. The difference is barely visible. A buyer discovered this and claims it's a material breach. Is it?
**Answer:** Yes, this is a material breach of contract. Rectified tiles (cut to precise dimensions after firing) have sharp, square edges and require minimal grout joints (1.5mm–2mm). Non-rectified tiles have slightly rounded edges and require wider grout joints (3mm–5mm). The finished appearance is visibly different. Under CPA Section 55, goods must be reasonably suitable for the purposes they are generally used for, and if you displayed a rectified tile, that becomes the expected standard. You must either replace the tiles or compensate the buyer for the cost difference. This is an expensive lesson — never substitute tile types without written buyer consent.

#### Q: 13 A buyer wants us to replace their kitchen floor tiles because they're "too slippery" when wet, claiming this is unsafe and breaches CPA Section 55 (safe, good-quality goods). The tiles are standard glazed porcelain with no slip-rating specified. Do we have to replace them?
**Answer:** Under SANS 10107 and SANS 10400-S (facilities for persons with disabilities), dry interior floors in residential units must achieve a minimum R9 slip rating (pendulum test value ≥ 36). Standard glazed porcelain typically meets this for dry conditions. However, if the tiles are in a kitchen where wet conditions are foreseeable, the buyer's argument has merit. If you did not specify a slip rating and the tiles are genuinely slippery when wet (below R9 when tested pendulum wet), a court or tribunal may find a breach. For future developments, specify R9 or R10 rated tiles for all residential floors and document this in the sales pack.

#### Q: 14 We advertised "imported Italian porcelain tiles" but switched to a Turkish supplier due to shipping delays. The Turkish tiles are equivalent spec and lower cost. A buyer claims false advertising under the CPA. Do they have a case?
**Answer:** Yes. "Imported Italian porcelain tiles" is a specific representation about place of origin. Switching to Turkish tiles without disclosure constitutes a misrepresentation under CPA Section 41(1)(c), regardless of whether the Turkish tiles are technically equivalent. The CPA provides for damages, refund, or replacement at the consumer's election. You must either reinstall Italian tiles, compensate the buyer for the perceived value difference (Italian tiles carry a premium), or obtain written consent for the substitution. Going forward, use generic language like "imported premium porcelain tiles" in marketing.

#### Q: 15 A buyer is demanding we replace all tiles in their unit because three tiles have minor chips from the transportation process. The chips are less than 5mm on the tile edges. Does the CPA require full replacement?
**Answer:** No. The CPA requires goods to be free of defects, but minor edge chips that do not affect the structural integrity or general appearance of the floor when grouted are considered acceptable under SANS 1449 and industry norms. You are required to replace the three affected tiles, not the entire floor. If the chips are in a conspicuous location and visible after grouting, offer to replace those specific tiles and compensate for inconvenience. Document the damage with photos and obtain the buyer's sign-off on the repair. Full replacement of an entire tiled floor for three minor chips would be considered an unreasonable remedy.

#### Q: 16 We built a show house with upgraded 600x600mm tiles, but the standard spec in the sales contract is 450x450mm. A buyer claims the show house constitutes a "sample" under CPA Section 42 and demands 600x600mm at no extra cost. Are they right?
**Answer:** Section 42 of the CPA states that if goods are exhibited as a sample, the bulk must correspond with the sample in quality. However, the test is whether the show house was presented as the standard product or as a display with upgrades. Your sales contract must clearly state: "The show house features optional upgrades not included in the standard specification. The standard specification is as set out in Annexure A." If this disclaimer is absent or unclear, the buyer has a strong CPA claim. Ensure show house viewing is accompanied by a specification sheet highlighting upgrades versus standard inclusions.

#### Q: 17 A buyer claims our tiles "emit a chemical smell" that causes headaches and wants the entire installation removed under CPA health and safety provisions. They've produced a "mould air quality report" from a private company. What are our obligations?
**Answer:** Porcelain and ceramic tiles are inert, fired-clay products that do not emit VOCs or chemical fumes. The "smell" is likely from the tile adhesive, grout, or sealant used during installation — these do release minor VOCs during curing, which dissipate within days. Request the buyer's report and have it reviewed by an occupational hygienist registered with the South African Institute of Occupational Hygiene. If the report lacks scientific rigour (no control samples, no calibrated instruments), challenge it. If there is a legitimate odour from adhesives, ventilate the unit for 72 hours and document the resolution. Tile replacement is not a reasonable remedy unless hazardous materials are proven present.

#### Q: 18 We sold a penthouse unit where the purchaser specified their own imported tiles, supplied by them. The tiles cracked during installation. They're demanding we pay for replacement under CPA warranty. Do we have any liability?
**Answer:** Under CPA Section 55, if the consumer supplied their own goods, the supplier (you) is only liable if the goods were damaged due to your negligence or improper installation. If the tiles cracked due to inherent defects (poor quality, inadequate thickness for the application, or unsuitability for floor use), the consumer must claim from the tile supplier or manufacturer. However, if your installer failed to prepare the substrate properly, used incorrect adhesive, or omitted expansion joints, you remain liable for installation defects. Obtain an independent assessment of the cracking cause before admitting liability.

#### Q: 19 A buyer wants to know the exact country of origin, factory name, and batch numbers of every tile in their unit for "CPA transparency rights." Do we have to provide this?
**Answer:** The CPA does not require disclosure of factory names or batch numbers as a general right. However, Section 22 requires disclosure of material facts, and if the buyer specifically requests information about product origin, you must answer truthfully. You should maintain records of tile specifications, supplier invoices, and country of origin for warranty and traceability purposes. Provide the buyer with the tile specification sheet (including country of origin and SANS/ISO compliance), but factory names and batch numbers are commercial information you can withhold unless subpoenaed. Never falsify country of origin — this constitutes fraud.

#### Q: 20 We offered a "10-year tile warranty" in our marketing brochure. Our attorney says this exceeds the NHBRC warranty and creates unnecessary liability. Can we withdraw it for units not yet sold?
**Answer:** For unsold units, you can amend your marketing materials and remove the 10-year tile warranty. However, for units already sold where the brochure formed part of the sale agreement or was relied upon by the purchaser, the warranty stands as an express contractual undertaking. Under CPA Section 56, any warranty you voluntarily offer becomes binding. A 10-year tile warranty is unusual — porcelain tiles typically last decades, but the installation (adhesion, grouting) does not. If you offer such a warranty, define it precisely: does it cover tile fracture only, or installation defects? Consider limiting it to manufacturer defects in the tile body itself.
### SECTION C: B-BBEE, COMPLIANCE & SUPPLIER ISSUES

#### Q: 21 Our tiling supplier's B-BBEE certificate expired three months into our 8-month project. They're a Level 4 but now have no valid certificate. Does this affect our B-BBEE scorecard if we continue using them?
**Answer:** For your measured entity scorecard, only supplier B-BBEE certificates that are valid at the time of procurement count. Payments to suppliers after their certificate expiry cannot be claimed for procurement recognition unless they obtain a renewed certificate. You should immediately request an updated certificate from the supplier. If they fail to provide one within a reasonable period, you must either source from an alternative supplier with a valid certificate or accept that those procurement points will not contribute to your scorecard. For large developments, include a clause in your supply agreement requiring maintenance of a valid B-BBEE certificate throughout the contract period.

#### Q: 22 We're a Level 2 B-BBEE contributor but our main tiling subcontractor is non-compliant (Level 8, no black ownership). For a government-subsidised housing project, can we still appoint them as a subcontractor?
**Answer:** For government-subsidised housing (RDP/gap housing), the Department of Human Settlements requires all contractors and subcontractors to meet minimum B-BBEE thresholds. A non-compliant subcontractor puts your entire project at risk of cancellation and may disqualify you from future tenders. You need a minimum of 51% black ownership in your subcontractor base for government housing projects. Either require your subcontractor to achieve compliance through a joint venture or consortium arrangement, or replace them with a compliant CIDB-registered contractor. Document all B-BBEE verification letters before appointing any subcontractor.

#### Q: 23 We imported tiles directly from Italy for a luxury development. Can we claim B-BBEE procurement points for the import value, even though the foreign supplier has no B-BBEE certificate?
**Answer:** No. B-BBEE procurement recognition only applies to South African-registered suppliers with valid B-BBEE certificates. Imports from foreign entities receive zero procurement points on your scorecard. To maximise points, you could either purchase through the South African distributor or subsidiary of the Italian manufacturer (if they have a valid B-BBEE certificate) or ensure that other aspects of your scorecard (ownership, skills development, enterprise development) compensate for the lost procurement points. The DTIC B-BBEE Codes of Good Practice are clear on this — there is no import exemption.

#### Q: 24 Our B-BBEE verification agency is no longer accredited by SANAS. The certificates they issued us for the past two years — are they still valid?
**Answer:** SANAS-accredited verification agencies must maintain their accreditation. If an agency loses SANAS accreditation, certificates issued AFTER the date of withdrawal are invalid. Certificates issued BEFORE the loss of accreditation remain valid for their 12-month validity period, provided the agency was accredited at the time of issue. However, a SANAS site audit of your measured entity may scrutinise these certificates more closely. If you suspect your agency's accreditation status, verify it on the SANAS and IRBA websites. For a large development, obtain a new certificate from an alternative accredited agency to be safe.

#### Q: 25 We're a foreign-owned developer (100% offshore parent company) building in South Africa. Do B-BBEE requirements apply to us, and if so, how do we achieve any level of compliance with zero local ownership?
**Answer:** B-BBEE applies to all entities operating in South Africa, including foreign-owned subsidiaries. With zero local ownership, you score zero on the ownership element (25 points). However, you can still achieve a meaningful overall score through the other scorecard elements: management control (even foreign companies can appoint black South African directors), skills development (train black South African employees), enterprise and supplier development (support local black-owned businesses), and socio-economic development. Foreign-owned entities that achieve Level 4 or better can still trade successfully in SA. Consider a sale of shares to a B-BBEE partner if long-term competitiveness is a priority.

#### Q: 26 A main contractor on our development has fronting concerns — their "black owner" is an employee with no real economic interest. We're the developer, not the contractor. Could we be implicated if fronting is proven?
**Answer:** Fronting is a criminal offence under the B-BBEE Amendment Act. While the primary liability rests with the measured entity (the contractor), you as the developer could face reputational damage, cancellation of contracts, and blacklisting from future government or SOE projects if you knowingly benefited from a fronting arrangement. The B-BBEE Commission has investigative powers and can fine any party involved in fronting up to 10% of annual turnover or imprison individuals for up to 10 years. Conduct due diligence on your contractors' ownership structures, and include an anti-fronting warranty in your subcontract agreements.

#### Q: 27 For a Transnet-adjacent industrial park development, we need a Level 1 B-BBEE rating. Our spend on tiles is 15% of total project cost. How much does the tile supplier's rating actually affect our overall scorecard?
**Answer:** Procurement contributes up to 25 points on the generic B-BBEE scorecard (25% weighting). Your tile supplier's rating directly affects the procurement points you can claim. If your tile supplier is Level 1, you receive full recognition (multiply spend by 1.35 for preferential procurement). If they are Level 4, the multiplier drops to 1.0. Level 8 = 0.5. For a Level 1 target, every procurement point matters. A Level 1 tile supplier could contribute approximately 3–4 points to your overall scorecard from tile procurement alone. Given that Level 1 requires 100+ points out of 109, this is material. Source from a Level 1–2 supplier if possible.

#### Q: 28 Our development company has a B-BBEE certificate, but the SPV (special purpose vehicle) created for this specific project is newly incorporated and has no certificate. Do we need a separate certificate for the SPV?
**Answer:** Yes. Each juristic person is a separate measured entity and requires its own B-BBEE certificate. If the SPV is newly incorporated with no trading history, it may qualify for an EME (Exempted Micro Enterprise) certificate (annual turnover below R10 million) issued by a CIPC-registered auditor, accounting officer, or accredited B-BBEE verification agency. An EME automatically achieves Level 4 (or Level 2 if 100% black-owned). This is often sufficient for project-level requirements. Obtain the EME certificate before tendering or applying for development finance.
### SECTION D: PERFORMANCE BONDS, PENALTIES & FINANCIAL INSTRUMENTS

#### Q: 29 Our development finance agreement requires a performance bond for the tiling contractor (R12 million contract value). The bank wants 2.5% annual premium and a 15% cash margin. Is this market standard in South Africa?
**Answer:** For a performance bond on a R12 million tiling contract, 2.5% annual premium is at the upper end of market range (standard is 1.5%–2.5%). The 15% cash margin is steep — typically 10%–15% for lesser-known contractors, but 5%–10% for established contractors with strong balance sheets. Counter-offer: provide a parent company guarantee from a listed entity, offer a bank guarantee instead of an insurance-backed bond (often cheaper), or negotiate a reducing bond that decreases as milestones are reached. For a reputable CIDB-registered contractor with Grade 6 or higher, you should push for 10% margin maximum.

#### Q: 30 The municipality imposed a penalty of R25,000 per day for late completion past the agreed practical completion date. Load shedding caused 45 days of delays. Can we claim force majeure to void the penalty?
**Answer:** Load shedding is increasingly being recognised as a force majeure event in South African construction contracts, but only if the contract specifically includes it in the force majeure clause. Standard JBCC agreements now include load shedding as a compensable delay event. You must: (1) prove the delays were solely attributable to load shedding and not concurrent delay, (2) provide load shedding schedules from Eskom showing Stage 3+ during the delay period, (3) demonstrate that you took reasonable measures (generator backup, shift scheduling) to mitigate, and (4) notify the principal agent within the contractual timeframes (typically 5 days of becoming aware). If all conditions are met, the penalty should be waived for the proven delay period.

#### Q: 31 We signed a fixed-price contract with our tiling contractor in March. Since then, the Rand has depreciated 18% against the Euro, and the imported tiles specified have increased in price by 22%. The contractor wants a price adjustment. Do we have to pay?
**Answer:** Under a fixed-price contract, the contractor bears the currency risk unless the contract includes a currency fluctuation clause or material escalation provision. If no such clause exists, you are not contractually obligated to pay the increase. However, if the contractor becomes insolvent or walks off site, your project delays may cost more than the price increase. The pragmatic approach: negotiate a partial adjustment (e.g., 50% of the increase) in exchange for a written commitment to maintain the programme. For future contracts, include a currency fluctuation cap (e.g., adjustment only if Rand moves more than 10% against the basket currency).

#### Q: 32 Our development has a R500,000/day liquidated damages clause for late sectional title registration. The tiling contractor's delay pushed us past the deadline by 3 weeks. Can we claim the full R10.5 million from the contractor?
**Answer:** Liquidated damages must be a genuine pre-estimate of loss to be enforceable. R500,000/day for delayed registration may be challengeable as a penalty if it vastly exceeds actual losses. However, if the amount is specified in your development finance agreement and you are paying interest on extended facilities, the losses may be real. You can claim from the tiling contractor if their delay was the direct cause, but you must prove causation and that you gave them notice of the consequences of delay. The contractor's liability is typically capped at the contract value or a specified percentage (e.g., 10%). Check your subcontract for the cap.

#### Q: 33 We need a performance bond for a R80 million mixed-use development. The bank quoted 3.5% for the bond — which seems high. What's the actual market rate, and are there alternatives to bank bonds?
**Answer:** 3.5% is above market for a standard performance bond. Current South African market rates are 1.5%–2.5% for established developers with strong financials. Alternatives: (1) Insurance-backed performance bonds from short-term insurers (sometimes cheaper, but may include more exclusions), (2) Parent company guarantees if you have a listed or investment-grade parent, (3) cash deposits held in trust (tie up capital but cost-effective), (4) retention monies (typically 5%–10% of contract value held until practical completion). For a R80 million project, shop around — Santam, Hollard, and Guardrisk all offer construction bonds. Also consider splitting the bond between bank and insurance to reduce concentration risk.

#### Q: 34 The main contractor has demanded we provide an advance payment guarantee for 20% of the tiling contract value. We've never done this before. Is it standard practice, and what are the risks?
**Answer:** Advance payment guarantees are standard when the contractor needs upfront capital for material procurement, especially for imported tiles with long lead times. The guarantee ensures that if the contractor defaults, you can recall the advance. The risk is that the guarantee is unconditional — the bank pays on demand without investigating the default. Structure it as a "conditional" advance payment guarantee if possible, or ensure the guarantee reduces pro-rata as materials are delivered to site and verified. The cost (0.5%–1.5% of guarantee value) is typically borne by the party receiving the advance.
### SECTION E: SPECIFICATION CHANGES, MUNICIPAL & REGULATORY DELAYS

#### Q: 35 We want to change from 300x300mm ceramic floor tiles to 600x600mm porcelain tiles on a development where municipal plans are already approved. The floor slabs were designed for the lighter ceramic tiles. Do we need plan amendments?
**Answer:** Yes. The change from ceramic to porcelain tiles increases the dead load on the slab — porcelain is denser (typically 2,300–2,400 kg/m³) than ceramic (1,800–2,000 kg/m³), and the larger format may require a thicker adhesive bed. If the original slab was designed for the lighter load, the increase could affect structural compliance under SANS 10400-B. You must have a professional engineer assess whether the slab can accommodate the additional load. If the slab is adequate, a professional sign-off is required. If not, you need amended structural drawings, which trigger a plan amendment at the municipality (4–8 week delay). Do not proceed without engineer sign-off — it voids your NHBRC enrolment.

#### Q: 36 Our municipal building inspector rejected the tiling in the common area corridors because we used a rectified edge tile with 1.5mm grout joints, and he claims SANS 10400 requires 3mm minimum. Is there a minimum grout joint width in the building regulations?
**Answer:** SANS 10400 does not specify a minimum grout joint width — it refers to SANS 10107 (the tile installation code) for workmanship standards. SANS 10107 allows grout joint widths down to 1.0mm for rectified tiles, provided the tiles are installed by a competent tiler and the substrate is sufficiently flat (maximum 3mm deviation over 2m). The inspector may be conflating grout width with general workmanship requirements. Provide the inspector with a copy of SANS 10107, the tile manufacturer's installation guidelines, and a flatness survey of the substrate. If they persist, escalate to the municipal building control manager — an incorrect interpretation by a junior inspector should not delay your project.

#### Q: 37 The municipality's town planning department says our building plans with tile specifications are "incomplete" because we didn't specify the tile weight per m². Is this a valid objection?
**Answer:** No, this is not a standard plan submission requirement. Building plans must include floor finishes (tile type, slip rating for certain applications), but the exact weight per m² is not a submission requirement under SANS 10400-A. The municipality may be requesting this because the structural engineer's slab design must account for superimposed dead loads, and tile weight is part of that calculation. Provide the superimposed dead load specification from your structural engineer (typically 1.0–1.5 kPa for standard floor tiles), which should satisfy the town planning department. If they insist on tile weight per m², it indicates they are confusing town planning and building control functions — escalate.

#### Q: 38 Our approved plans specify "ceramic floor tiles" but we've decided to use SPC vinyl flooring in the bedrooms instead. The walls and floors are already built. Do we need to amend plans for this change?
**Answer:** SANS vinyl flooring is lighter than ceramic tiles, so there are no structural load concerns. However, the National Building Regulations require that approved plans accurately reflect the completed building. A material change to specified finishes may require a plan amendment, depending on your municipality's interpretation. In practice, many municipalities do not require amendment for finish changes within the same building classification (e.g., Class 2 residential), provided the change does not affect fire ratings, structural loads, or disability access. Submit a letter of amendment to the building control officer and request written confirmation of whether an amended plan submission is required. Budget 2–4 weeks for a response.

#### Q: 39 We've been waiting 14 weeks for municipal water connection to our 120-unit development. The units are complete but we can't issue occupation certificates. Can we claim damages from the municipality?
**Answer:** Claiming damages from a South African municipality for delayed service connections is technically possible but practically very difficult. Municipalities enjoy significant legal protection, and their service level agreements typically exclude liability for delays. However, you can: (1) escalate through the municipal manager and mayoral office, (2) apply for a "provisional connection" or tanker water supply with written municipality approval, (3) engage a private contractor for temporary bulk water supply if the municipality consents, (4) claim the delay as a force majeure event in your sale agreements to avoid purchaser penalties. Document all correspondence. For future projects, apply for water connection at the same time as plan submission, not after construction completion.

#### Q: 40 The municipality's electrical inspector failed our installation because the tile layer covered the floor junction boxes in the screed. The tiling is complete in 40 units. Who pays for the rectification?
**Answer:** The tile layer is responsible for not locating and preserving junction boxes before tiling. However, as the developer, you bear the rectification cost initially and must recover from the subcontractor. The SANS 10142-1 wiring code requires junction boxes to remain accessible — they cannot be permanently covered by tiles or screed. Rectification involves cutting open the tiles, relocating the junction boxes to accessible positions (in the wall or a pull-box at floor level), and re-tiling. This is a significant cost. Deduct the rectification cost from the tiling contractor's final payment and, if necessary, claim against their performance guarantee. Always require the electrical contractor to mark junction box locations before screeding and tiling begin.
### SECTION F: FIRE RATINGS, ACOUSTICS & THERMAL PERFORMANCE

#### Q: 41 Our 8-storey residential building requires fire-rated floors between units. The tiles are porcelain on a concrete slab. Does the tile layer contribute to the fire resistance level (FRL), and what FRL do we need?
**Answer:** Under SANS 10400-T, residential buildings above two storeys require fire-rated compartment floors. The concrete slab itself typically provides the FRL — a 150mm reinforced concrete slab achieves approximately FRL 120/120/120. The tile layer does not structurally contribute to the fire rating but must not compromise it. Tiles and adhesive must not contain combustible materials that could fuel a fire or produce toxic smoke. Standard porcelain tiles and cement-based adhesives are non-combustible and comply. However, if you use adhesive with polymer additives, check the fire rating of the additive — some organic compounds are combustible. For an 8-storey building, you typically need FRL 60/60/60 minimum for compartment floors. The tile layer is "in addition to" the slab rating, not a substitute.

#### Q: 42 An acoustic engineer says our inter-unit floor/ceiling assembly must achieve an Impact Sound Rating (ISR) of 55dB and Airborne Sound Rating of 50dB under SANS 10400-T. Our tiles are on a concrete slab with no acoustic underlay. Will this comply?
**Answer:** A standard 150mm concrete slab with hard porcelain tiles directly bonded will NOT achieve ISR 55dB. Hard surfaces reflect impact sound (footsteps, dropped objects) directly into the ceiling cavity below. To achieve ISR 55dB, you need an acoustic underlayment (resilient layer) between the screed and the tiles, or a floating screed system. Options: (1) 5mm rubber crumb underlay beneath the screed, (2) acoustic matting systems specifically designed for tile installations, (3) raised access floor systems with acoustic infill. The airborne rating of 50dB is achievable with the concrete slab alone. Budget R150–R300/m² additional for acoustic flooring systems. Non-compliance with SANS 10400-T acoustic requirements can result in refusal of occupation certificate.

#### Q: 43 We're building a glass tower in Sandton where the façade creates a greenhouse effect. The interior floor tiles reach surface temperatures of 55°C in summer. Does SANS 10400-XA (energy efficiency) regulate tile surface temperatures?
**Answer:** SANS 10400-XA regulates the building envelope's thermal performance, not interior surface temperatures directly. However, excessive solar heat gain through glazing will cause your building to fail the XA requirements for annual energy consumption. The solution is not the tile selection but the glazing specification — you need high-performance glass with a Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC) below 0.35 and visible light transmittance above 0.50. Tiles with lighter colours and higher reflectance (LRV > 50) will reduce the radiant heat absorbed and re-emitted into the space. Dark tiles (LRV < 20) in a glass building act as heat sinks and increase cooling loads significantly. Specify light-coloured tiles for sun-exposed areas and ensure your HVAC engineer accounts for the additional heat load.

#### Q: 44 The energy efficiency assessor says our tiled concrete floors need to be included in the thermal simulation because they form part of the "thermal mass" calculation. Is a tiled floor actually thermal mass?
**Answer:** Yes. Concrete slabs with ceramic or porcelain tiles have high thermal mass (the ability to store and slowly release heat). Under SANS 10400-XA, thermal mass is beneficial in climates with high diurnal temperature variation (Highveld, Karoo) because it stabilises indoor temperatures. However, in humid coastal climates (Durban, Richards Bay), high thermal mass can work against you — the floor stays cool and damp, increasing condensation risk. The XA simulation must include the floor slab's thermal properties (density, specific heat capacity, thermal conductivity). Tiled floors increase the exposed thermal mass surface area compared to carpeted floors. Your assessor is correct — include it.

#### Q: 45 Our building passed the fire inspection but failed the acoustic test between units because the tile layer grouted right up to the wall without leaving a perimeter isolation joint. How does grouting affect acoustic performance?
**Answer:** Acoustic flanking paths are a common failure mode in multi-unit buildings. When tiles are grouted hard against the wall, impact vibration transmits through the tile-screed-wall junction and re-radiates into the adjacent unit. SANS 10400-T and SANS 10177 (acoustic testing) require that floor finishes include a resilient perimeter isolation joint (minimum 5mm–8mm) filled with acoustic sealant where they abut walls and service penetrations. The tile layer must: (1) install a compressible foam strip around the perimeter before tiling, (2) leave a 5mm gap between the tile edge and the wall, (3) fill the gap with a flexible acoustic sealant (not grout). This simple detail can reduce flanking transmission by 5–10dB — often the difference between pass and fail.
### SECTION G: STORMWATER, ENVIRONMENTAL & HERITAGE ISSUES

#### Q: 46 Our development of 200 townhouses will have 8,000m² of tiled and paved surface. The municipality's stormwater department says we need attenuation ponds because the runoff coefficient exceeds their threshold. What's the threshold, and what does this cost?
**Answer:** Under SANS 10400-R and municipal stormwater bylaws, developments that increase impervious surface area typically require stormwater attenuation. The runoff coefficient threshold varies by municipality but is commonly 0.50–0.70 (meaning 50%–70% of rainfall becomes runoff). Tiled surfaces have a runoff coefficient of approximately 0.85–0.95 (almost all rain runs off). The requirement is to attenuate peak runoff to pre-development levels or to the municipality's design storm standard (typically 1:50 year return period). For 200 townhouses, expect 2–3 attenuation ponds at R300,000–R600,000 each, plus underground attenuation tanks if space is limited. Permeable paving in non-critical areas reduces the requirement — consider open-graded pavers for driveways and walkways.

#### Q: 47 We're building in the Bo-Kaap heritage protection overlay zone. The City of Cape Town requires that all external floor finishes be "sympathetic to the heritage character." Can we use modern large-format porcelain tiles, or are we restricted to traditional materials?
**Answer:** The Bo-Kaap Heritage Protection Overlay Zone (HPOZ) has strict aesthetic controls. External floor finishes in public-visible areas (stoep, courtyard) are typically required to use traditional materials — clay brick pavers, slate, or cement tiles — in keeping with the Victorian and Georgian architectural character. Large-format modern porcelain will almost certainly be rejected by the Heritage Impact Assessment (HIA) process. Internal finishes are less controlled but may require HIA approval if the building is Grade II heritage-protected. Engage a heritage consultant early in the design phase. Attempting to install non-compliant finishes after HIA rejection can result in a demolition order — the City has issued these for unauthorised alterations in heritage zones.

#### Q: 48 The Environmental Impact Assessment for our development flagged that tile cutting on-site generates silica dust, which is a listed activity under the National Environmental Management: Air Quality Act. Do we need an atmospheric emissions licence?
**Answer:** On-site tile cutting using water-cooled wet saws does not typically trigger the need for an atmospheric emissions licence, as the water suppresses dust. However, dry cutting of tiles, stone, or concrete is a listed activity under Category 3 of the listed activities and standards regulations. If your contractors dry-cut tiles on-site, you need an atmospheric emissions licence from the provincial environmental department, or you must ensure all cutting is done off-site at a licensed facility. Best practice: prohibit dry cutting in your contractor specifications, require wet-cutting methods, and include it in your Environmental Management Plan (EMP). Silica dust exposure also triggers Occupational Health and Safety obligations — provide respiratory protection if dust cannot be fully suppressed.

#### Q: 49 Our development borders a wetland. The environmental officer says our construction runoff, including grout and adhesive residue from tile washing, cannot enter the wetland. How do we manage this practically?
**Answer:** Construction runoff containing cement, grout, adhesive, or sealant residues is classified as contaminated water under the National Water Act and cannot discharge into a wetland, river, or watercourse. You must: (1) establish a sealed wash bay area with sediment traps, (2) collect all tile wash water and dispose of it at a licensed waste facility (not into the stormwater system), (3) install silt fences and check dams around the perimeter, (4) prepare a Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan (SWPPP) as part of your Environmental Management Programme. Fines for wetland pollution range from R100,000 to R5 million under the National Environmental Management Act. The Department of Forestry, Fisheries and Environment actively prosecutes developers for wetland damage.

#### Q: 50 We're required to conduct a Heritage Impact Assessment before demolishing an existing building on our development site. The building has no heritage status, but it's in an area with heritage sensitivity. How long does this take, and can we start construction while waiting?
**Answer:** A Heritage Impact Assessment (HIA) for a building with no formal heritage grading but in a sensitive area typically takes 4–8 weeks, including the public participation phase. You CANNOT commence demolition or construction until the HIA is approved by the provincial heritage resources authority (PHRA) — in the Western Cape, this is Heritage Western Cape; in Gauteng, the South African Heritage Resources Agency (SAHRA) regional office. Starting construction before HIA approval is a criminal offence under the National Heritage Resources Act (Section 48). The PHRA can issue a stop-work order and require you to restore any unauthorised demolition. Budget 2–3 months for the full heritage approval process.
### SECTION H: SHOW HOUSE, BODY CORPORATE & SECTIONAL TITLE OBLIGATIONS

#### Q: 51 Our show house has imported marble-look porcelain tiles (R850/m²). The standard spec in the sales contract is local ceramic tiles (R120/m²). A buyer who only viewed the show house (never read the contract) claims they were misled. What's our legal exposure?
**Answer:** Under CPA Section 41 and common law misrepresentation, your exposure is significant. If the buyer can prove they relied on the show house as representative of the standard product (and many buyers do), a court or tribunal may find a misrepresentation. The CPA shifts the burden — you must prove the buyer was not misled. Best defence: demonstrate that the buyer signed a specification schedule at the show house clearly distinguishing upgrades from standard, and that the sales contract was explained. Without this, you may be required to either upgrade the buyer's unit to match the show house or compensate for the difference. The cost of defending a CPA complaint at the National Consumer Tribunal, plus reputational damage, often exceeds the cost of simply upgrading the tiles.

#### Q: 52 The body corporate of our completed sectional title scheme wants to change the lobby tiles from the developer's specification to something "more modern." Can they do this without our consent, and do we have any ongoing obligation?
**Answer:** Once the sectional title scheme is registered and the body corporate is established, the body corporate — not the developer — controls the common property. Under the Sectional Titles Schemes Management Act (STSMA), the body corporate can, by special resolution (75% majority in value), make alterations to common property. As the developer, you have no ongoing obligation to maintain the common property finishes unless your development agreement included a specific warranty period for common areas (which is unusual). However, if the body corporate changes the tiles within the defects liability period and the work is defective, they may claim against you. Once transferred, the body corporate's decisions are their own.

#### Q: 53 We sold all units in our sectional title scheme but retained the penthouse as the developer's unit. The body corporate passed a rule requiring all unit owners to use the same tile specification for any renovations to ensure "visual uniformity." Does this rule apply to us as the developer?
**Answer:** Yes. As a unit owner, you are subject to the conduct rules of the body corporate regardless of your status as the developer. Under STSMA Section 11(3), conduct rules bind all owners and occupiers. If the body corporate has validly passed a rule requiring specific tile specifications for renovations (which must have been approved by the Chief Ombud for Sectional Titles), you must comply. The only exception is if the rule was passed after you took transfer and it imposes a restriction that was not disclosed in the sale agreement — you may have a claim for breach of contract. As developer, consider reserving rights in the development agreement or establishing the conduct rules yourself before selling the first unit.

#### Q: 54 A purchaser in our sectional title scheme is refusing to pay levies because they claim the developer-specified lobby tiles are "substandard and reduce the value of their unit." Can they withhold levies for this reason?
**Answer:** No. Levy payments and complaints about common property quality are separate legal issues. Under STSMA Section 3(1), owners are liable for levies from the date of transfer, regardless of disputes. The purchaser cannot unilaterally withhold levies — doing so exposes them to interest charges, legal costs, and potential foreclosure by the body corporate. Their remedy is to: (1) raise the issue at a body corporate general meeting, (2) propose a special levy for tile replacement, (3) if the tiles are genuinely defective (not merely "substandard" in the owner's subjective opinion), claim against the developer's defects liability warranty. If the tiles meet the specification and SANS standards, the owner has no valid claim.

#### Q: 55 We used the same tile specification in the show house as in the delivered units, but the batch numbers differ, and the show house tiles look slightly glossier. A buyer claims this is a "material difference" and wants compensation. Is batch variation a valid claim?
**Answer:** Batch variation in glaze appearance is an inherent characteristic of ceramic tile manufacturing. SANS 1449 and ISO 10545-16 allow for shade variation within defined tolerances (typically between V1 — uniform appearance — and V4 — substantial variation, as marked on the packaging). If the tiles delivered are from the same manufacturer, the same product line, and within the rated shade variation class, the buyer's claim has no technical basis. Your defence: produce the tile manufacturer's shade variation rating (V1–V4) and explain that the tiles delivered fall within the rated class. If the tiles are from different product lines or the variation exceeds the manufacturer's rating, you may have a legitimate quality issue. Always order the full tile quantity from a single production batch where possible.
### SECTION I: OCCUPANCY CERTIFICATES, PRACTICAL COMPLETION & DEFECTS LIABILITY

#### Q: 56 The municipality's building inspector refused to issue an Occupation Certificate because the floor tiles in the fire escape stairwell don't have the required slip rating. We used standard polished porcelain. What slip rating is required for emergency escape routes?
**Answer:** SANS 10400-T (fire protection) and SANS 10107 require that escape routes have slip-resistant finishes, especially stair treads. For fire escape staircases, the minimum slip resistance is R10 (pendulum test value ≥ 45 wet), with R11 preferred for commercial buildings. Polished porcelain typically achieves only R9 (or less when wet). You must replace the stair tread tiles with R10 or R11 rated slip-resistant tiles, or apply a certified slip-resistant treatment to the existing tiles. The inspector is correct to withhold the Occupation Certificate — an unsafe fire escape is a life safety issue. Budget for replacement; slip treatments are often rejected by inspectors as temporary solutions.

#### Q: 57 Our practical completion certificate was issued, but three weeks later, a purchaser found cracked tiles in the main bedroom. The tiling contractor says practical completion means their liability ended. Is this correct?
**Answer:** No. Practical completion under JBCC or NEC contracts marks the end of the construction period and triggers the defects liability period (DLP), typically 3–12 months. During the DLP, the contractor remains responsible for repairing defects that appear. Cracked tiles appearing three weeks after practical completion are almost certainly latent defects and fall squarely within the DLP. The contractor must repair them at their cost. Notify the contractor in writing within the notification period specified in your contract (typically 7–14 days of discovering the defect). If they refuse, you can engage another contractor and deduct the cost from retention monies or claim against their performance bond.

#### Q: 58 We have a 6-month defects liability period in our contracts. A buyer reported a leaking shower 7 months after practical completion. The leak is caused by a failure in the waterproofing beneath the tiles. Who is liable — us, the tiler, or the waterproofing subcontractor?
**Answer:** After the DLP expires, the developer's contractual liability to the buyer for patent defects ends, but the NHBRC 5-year structural warranty may apply if the leak is caused by structural movement or a major structural waterproofing failure. If the waterproofing system was a separate subcontract ( specialised waterproofing applicator), their warranty typically runs for 10 years and is independent of the DLP. The buyer should claim against the waterproofing subcontractor's warranty first. If the leak is due to poor tile installation (incorrect falls, grout failure, no silicone at junctions), the tiler may be liable under their workmanship warranty. As developer, your liability depends on the cause — engage a forensic plumber to determine whether the leak is structural (NHBRC), waterproofing (10-year warranty), or installation (tiler's liability).

#### Q: 59 The municipality won't issue the Occupation Certificate because the tiling contractor hasn't submitted their Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) file to the principal contractor. Can a subcontractor's paperwork block an OC?
**Answer:** Yes. Under the Construction Regulations 2014 (OHSA), the principal contractor must maintain a consolidated health and safety file for the project, including all subcontractor OHS files. The municipality's building inspector may request proof that the construction health and safety file is complete before issuing the Occupation Certificate — this is within their powers under SANS 10400-A. The tiling subcontractor must submit their OHS file, including risk assessments, method statements, and proof of inductions. This is not the tiler's choice — it's a legal requirement. Withhold final payment until the OHS file is submitted. If they refuse, you may need to reconstruct the file from site records.

#### Q: 60 A unit failed final inspection because the tile layer used black grout instead of the specified grey grout. The difference is purely aesthetic. Can an aesthetic deviation block an Occupation Certificate?
**Answer:** In theory, an Occupation Certificate should only be withheld for non-compliance with the National Building Regulations (structural, fire, health, safety). A grout colour deviation is not a building regulation issue. However, if the approved plans specify "grey grout" and the building inspector is being pedantic, they may refuse the OC on the grounds that the completed building does not match approved plans. The practical resolution: submit a minor amendment application for the grout colour change (if your municipality requires this) or simply re-grout the affected areas to match the specification. For future projects, include a note on plans: "grout colour subject to final selection by architect" to avoid this issue.
### SECTION J: GEOTECHNICAL, CLIMATE & SITE-SPECIFIC ISSUES

#### Q: 61 Our Pretoria development is built on expansive clay (smectite). The geotechnical report recommended a suspended floor slab, but we used a ground-bearing slab to save costs. Now tiles are cracking everywhere. What's our liability?
**Answer:** You are fully liable. Ignoring a geotechnical engineer's recommendation constitutes professional negligence. Expansive clay in Pretoria (the "Pretoria clay" formation) can heave 50mm–100mm seasonally as moisture content changes. A ground-bearing slab on this clay will crack, and no tile installation system can accommodate that movement. The NHBRC will likely classify this as a structural defect and require you to remediate. Remediation options: (1) underpin and convert to suspended slab (extremely expensive), (2) install a structural topping slab over the existing slab with movement joints, (3) in severe cases, demolish and rebuild correctly. Your professional indemnity insurance may cover the rectification cost, but expect a significant excess. Always follow geotechnical recommendations — the cost of a suspended slab is minor compared to remediation.

#### Q: 62 We're building on the East Rand near Benoni where there are abandoned mine shafts. The DMR (Department of Mineral Resources) has flagged our site as "undermined." Do we need special foundations, and how does this affect floor tiles?
**Answer:** Yes. Undermined areas in Gauteng require a DMR mining risk assessment before construction. If the risk assessment confirms undermining, you may need: (1) compacted mine-fill or grout injection to stabilise voids, (2) specially designed foundations (raft or pile foundations) to span potential subsidence zones, (3) flexible building design to accommodate predicted ground movement. For floor tiles, you must use a crack-isolation membrane between the structural slab and the tile bed to accommodate differential movement. Specify a membrane rated for the predicted movement (typically 2mm–5mm). Do NOT use rigid adhesive systems — use a flexible, polymer-modified adhesive (C2TES1 classification). Tile cracking in undermined areas is common; the membrane is your best defence.

#### Q: 63 Our Durban beachfront development has tiles lifting off the floor due to salt corrosion of the adhesive. The adhesive was a standard cement-based product. What should we have used?
**Answer:** Coastal environments with high airborne salt content (within 1km of the coastline) require specialised adhesive systems. Standard cement-based adhesives are susceptible to salt crystallisation within the adhesive bed, which causes expansion and debonding. You should have specified: (1) a cementitious adhesive classified as C2 (improved adhesive) with S1 (deformable) characteristics, (2) an epoxy-based adhesive for critical areas (though more expensive at R80–R120/m² material cost), (3) a salt-resistant primer applied to the concrete substrate before tiling. Additionally, the concrete substrate must have adequate cover over reinforcement (minimum 40mm in coastal zone 5) to prevent substrate spalling that destabilises the tile bed. Durban's coastal climate is one of the most hostile environments for tiling in South Africa.

#### Q: 64 We built in the Free State where winter temperatures drop to -8°C. External porcelain tiles on the verandah are spalling (surface flaking) after two winters. Is this a tile quality issue or an installation issue?
**Answer:** This is likely both. Spalling of tiles in freeze-thaw conditions occurs when: (1) the tiles have high water absorption (>0.5% for porcelain, >3% for ceramic), allowing water to freeze within the tile body and cause surface delamination, (2) the installation did not include adequate expansion joints to accommodate thermal cycling, (3) water is pooling on the surface and penetrating through grout joints. For external tiles in freeze-thaw zones (Highveld, Free State, Northern Cape), you must specify frost-resistant porcelain with water absorption below 0.1% (ISO 10545-12 frost resistance tested). Install expansion joints at maximum 4.5m intervals in both directions, use a flexible grout, and ensure adequate falls (minimum 1:80) to prevent water pooling.

#### Q: 65 Our Karoo development has extreme temperature swings — 40°C in summer, -5°C in winter. The tiles are making a "popping" sound during the day. Is this thermal expansion, and will they crack?
**Answer:** The "popping" sound is likely the tile bed releasing stress as the tiles expand under thermal load. In the Karoo, tiled surfaces can reach 60°C+ in summer, causing significant thermal expansion (porcelain expands approximately 0.006mm/m per °C). If the installation did not include adequate expansion joints, the accumulated stress causes either audible "popping" as the adhesive releases, or actual tile cracking/tenting. The expansion joint spacing should be reduced in extreme thermal environments: maximum 3m intervals in each direction for external tiles, 4.5m for internal tiles with direct solar exposure. Install movement joints NOW using a soft polyethylene rod and flexible polyurethane sealant. Without intervention, cracking is inevitable within one or two thermal cycles.
### SECTION K: SUBCONTRACTOR DEFAULT, LABOUR & DISPUTES

#### Q: 66 Our tiling subcontractor went into business rescue after completing 60% of the work. They owe their tile suppliers R800,000, and the suppliers have placed a lien on the remaining tiles on site. Can we use those tiles to finish the project?
**Answer:** This is legally complex. Under business rescue (Chapter 6 of the Companies Act), a moratorium applies on all legal proceedings against the company, but it does not automatically extinguish supplier rights. If the tile supplier has a valid retention of title (ROT) clause in their supply contract, the tiles on site remain their property until paid for. You cannot simply use them — the supplier could claim theft. Options: (1) negotiate with the supplier to purchase the tiles directly (they may prefer partial payment to a total loss), (2) apply to the business rescue practitioner for release of the materials, (3) if the tiles were fully paid for by the subcontractor before business rescue, they form part of the rescue estate and you need the practitioner's consent. Engage an attorney specialising in business rescue immediately.

#### Q: 67 The tiling crew on our site stopped work because they're demanding 13th cheques and the subcontractor says it's not in their contracts. We're 3 weeks from sectional title registration deadline. Can we dismiss them and bring in another crew?
**Answer:** The workers are employed by your subcontractor, not by you. You cannot directly dismiss them — that would constitute an unfair labour practice and potentially make you a "joint employer" under labour law. However, you CAN instruct your subcontractor to resolve the dispute or face termination of their contract for failure to perform. If the subcontractor's contract includes penalty clauses for delay, enforce them. If you need to engage a replacement subcontractor urgently, you must follow the contractual termination procedure (typically 7–14 days notice of breach with opportunity to remedy). The 13th cheque demand has no legal basis unless it's in the employment contract or an industry collective agreement — the tiling industry does not have a universal 13th cheque obligation under any bargaining council agreement.

#### Q: 68 COSATU representatives arrived on our site demanding we sign a "project labour agreement" recognising their shop steward and agreeing to union-scale wages for all workers. Our tiling subcontractor uses non-union casual labour. Are we obligated to engage with COSATU?
**Answer:** If your development qualifies as a "designated employer" under the Labour Relations Act (typically 50+ employees including subcontractors), you may be subject to organisational rights applications. However, COSATU cannot unilaterally impose a project labour agreement without following the statutory process: they must represent a majority of workers on the site and apply to the CCMA or Labour Court for organisational rights. Your obligation is to your direct employees only. The subcontractor's labour relationship is with them, not you. That said, site disruptions from union activity are a reality. Engage a labour law attorney, document all interactions, and do not sign any agreement under duress. If violence or intimidation occurs, report it to SAPS immediately.

#### Q: 69 Our tiling subcontractor's workers went on an unprotected strike, broke tiles on site, and damaged equipment. We have the incident on CCTV. What's our recourse against the workers and the subcontractor?
**Answer:** Unprotected strike action with property damage constitutes both a disciplinary offence and criminal conduct (malicious damage to property). You should: (1) lay criminal charges with SAPS for the damage, providing CCTV evidence, (2) claim damages from the subcontractor — they are vicariously liable for their employees' conduct during the course of employment, (3) if the subcontractor cannot pay, claim against their public liability insurance or your own Contractors All Risk (CAR) policy, (4) dismiss the striking workers (the subcontractor must follow fair procedure — they can dismiss for unprotected strike and gross misconduct). The subcontractor should also report the workers to the police. Your CAR policy typically covers malicious damage by third parties, subject to the excess.

#### Q: 70 We engaged an independent tiling contractor through a labour broker. The broker didn't pay UIF or PAYE for the workers. Now SARS is auditing us as the "responsible party." Are we liable?
**Answer:** Under the Tax Administration Act and the Fourth Schedule of the Income Tax Act, if you engage workers through a labour broker and the broker fails to pay UIF/PAYE, SARS can hold you jointly and severally liable if you were aware or should have been aware of the non-compliance. The Labour Relations Act Amendment Act also imposes joint and several liability for labour broker clients in certain circumstances. Your defence: demonstrate that you verified the broker's SARS tax clearance and UIF registration before engagement, and that you paid all fees to the broker (who then failed to remit to SARS). However, SARS may still pursue you for the shortfall. Always verify broker compliance monthly and obtain proof of PAYE/UIF payments. Use only reputable, established labour brokers.
### SECTION L: INSURANCE, CURRENCY & FINANCIAL RISK

#### Q: 71 Our Contractors All Risk (CAR) policy excludes "defective workmanship" — which means tile installation defects aren't covered. But the policy covers "resultant damage." If defective tiling causes water damage to the ceiling below, is the ceiling repair covered?
**Answer:** Yes, this is a classic CAR policy interpretation. CAR policies typically exclude the cost of rectifying the defective work itself (re-tiling) but cover the resultant damage caused by the defect (water-stained ceiling, damaged electrical wiring, warped cabinetry). Insurers call this the "defective workmanship exclusion with resultant damage extension." If leaking tiles damage the ceiling below, the insurer should pay for ceiling repair, repainting, and any electrical or furnishing damage, but NOT for re-tiling or re-waterproofing the shower. Always read your policy schedule carefully — some policies have a "total exclusion" that removes the resultant damage extension. Ensure your policy includes the extension.

#### Q: 72 We need to import R5 million worth of tiles from Italy, with delivery in 6 months. The Rand/Euro rate is volatile. Should we take a forward exchange contract, and what does it cost?
**Answer:** A forward exchange contract (FEC) locks in today's exchange rate for a future payment, eliminating currency risk. For a 6-month FEC, the cost is the forward points (the interest rate differential between ZAR and EUR), which is typically 2%–4% worse than the spot rate, depending on the term and market conditions. For R5 million, a 3% premium equals R150,000 — your cost of certainty. Alternative: a currency option (more expensive but allows you to benefit if Rand strengthens), or a 50% hedge (hedge half, leave half exposed). Most major banks (Standard Bank, FNB, Nedbank) offer FECs for importers. You need a trade finance facility. For a 6-month horizon with current Rand volatility, hedging is strongly recommended.

#### Q: 73 Our development finance bank requires "cost-to-complete" certificates every quarter. The quantity surveyor says we must include the full value of tiles already purchased but not yet installed. Is this correct?
**Answer:** Yes. Cost-to-complete (CTC) certificates must reflect the full project cost, including materials on site (MOS) that have been purchased but not yet installed. Tiles delivered to site and verified as present are a legitimate project cost, even if not yet laid. The CTC calculation is: total project budget minus costs incurred to date (including MOS) = cost to complete. The bank uses this to ensure the remaining facility is sufficient to finish the project. Ensure your quantity surveyor physically verifies tile stock on site (count pallets, check batch numbers) — banks may request photographic evidence. Tiles stored off-site (at a supplier's warehouse) are NOT included in CTC unless they are bonded and a valid cession of stock certificate exists.

#### Q: 74 A tile fell from a balcony on our construction site and injured a passing pedestrian. Our CAR policy covers "public liability" up to R10 million. But the pedestrian is claiming R15 million. What happens now?
**Answer:** Your CAR policy's public liability limit is R10 million. The claimant's demand of R15 million exceeds the policy limit by R5 million. Your options: (1) negotiate a settlement within the policy limit (insurer's lawyers will attempt this), (2) if the claim proceeds to court and the judgment exceeds R10 million, you are personally liable for the shortfall (R5 million plus legal costs), (3) you may have separate public liability insurance that provides additional cover — check your corporate insurance portfolio. To avoid this exposure in future, carry a minimum R20 million public liability limit for high-rise construction, or purchase an umbrella liability policy that provides excess coverage above the CAR limit. Also ensure all balcony edges have proper edge protection until handrails are installed — falling objects are a leading cause of construction-related injury claims.

#### Q: 75 Our professional indemnity (PI) insurer has excluded "waterproofing design" from our policy after a previous claim. We're now designing all wet area waterproofing ourselves. Are we exposed?
**Answer:** Yes, significantly. Without PI coverage for waterproofing design, any failure in a waterproofing system you designed exposes your company's balance sheet directly. A single failed basement waterproofing system can result in claims of R2–R5 million for water damage, mould remediation, and content replacement. Options: (1) engage a specialist waterproofing consultant (they carry their own PI) and have them sign off on your designs, (2) obtain a separate PI policy extension for waterproofing (expensive but necessary), (3) use proprietary waterproofing systems where the manufacturer provides a system warranty (e.g., Sika, Mapei) contingent on their approved applicator installing it. Never design waterproofing in-house without either PI coverage or specialist consultant sign-off.
### SECTION M: TRANSFER DUTY, VAT & TAX STRUCTURES

#### Q: 76 We're VAT-registered developers selling sectional title units. The sales contract says "VAT inclusive." Do we charge VAT on the full selling price including the tile specification, or is the tile cost somehow separated?
**Answer:** As a VAT-registered developer, you charge VAT on the full supply of the residential unit, which is a single composite supply. You cannot separate the tile cost and apply a different VAT treatment — the sale of the completed unit is standard-rated at 15% VAT on the total contract price. The tiles form part of the "goods" supplied as part of the construction and sale. However, you can claim input VAT on the tile purchase, the installation labour, and all other development costs. The "VAT inclusive" price means the stated price already includes 15% VAT — the VAT-exclusive price is the stated price divided by 1.15. Ensure your tax invoice to the purchaser clearly states the VAT amount.

#### Q: 77 We sold a unit and charged VAT, but the buyer is an individual purchasing their primary residence. They claim we should have charged transfer duty instead of VAT. Which is correct?
**Answer:** VAT and transfer duty are mutually exclusive on the same transaction. If you are a VAT-registered developer selling a unit in the course of your enterprise, the sale is a VATable supply at 15%, regardless of the buyer's status. The buyer does not pay transfer duty on a VAT transaction — transfer duty only applies to sales by non-vendors (individuals selling their own home, for example). The buyer may be confused because they are used to paying transfer duty on second-hand home purchases. Explain that as a VAT-registered developer, VAT applies, and the advantage to the buyer is that transfer duty (which has its own sliding scale and can exceed VAT on high-value properties) does not apply. The buyer cannot "choose" transfer duty over VAT — the tax treatment is determined by the seller's status.

#### Q: 78 We temporarily let 10 completed units for 8 months while waiting for buyers (rent-to-own scheme). SARS says we must pay VAT on the rental income AND the eventual sale is now a "second supply" subject to transfer duty. Is this correct?
**Answer:** SARS's position is partially correct. Rental income from residential letting is exempt from VAT (not zero-rated — exempt). This means you cannot claim input VAT on costs related to the letting period. When you subsequently sell the unit, if the letting period exceeds 6 months and the unit was used for residential letting, SARS may argue that the subsequent sale is a "second supply of a residential property" which is also VAT-exempt (not standard-rated). This means you cannot charge VAT on the sale price, and you must deregister those units from VAT. The buyer would pay transfer duty instead. This is a major tax trap for developers — always structure rent-to-own schemes with sale agreements signed before letting begins, or ensure the letting period is under 6 months to preserve VAT status. Consult a property tax specialist before implementing any letting strategy.

#### Q: 79 Our SPV (special purpose vehicle) developer company has never traded before this project and has no VAT registration. Must we register for VAT, and what's the threshold?
**Answer:** The compulsory VAT registration threshold is R1 million in taxable supplies over any 12-month period. For a development with total revenue exceeding R1 million, you must register for VAT. You can also register voluntarily if your taxable supplies exceed R50,000 in the past 12 months (which a single unit sale would trigger). Voluntary registration allows you to claim input VAT on development costs from day one, which improves cash flow significantly. Apply for VAT registration at least 2 months before the first unit sale — SARS registration can take 4–6 weeks. If you sell a unit before VAT registration, you cannot charge VAT or claim input VAT on that sale. The sale will be treated as a non-vendor sale subject to transfer duty.

#### Q: 80 We imported tiles and paid customs duty plus 15% VAT at the border. Our clearing agent says we can claim the import VAT as input VAT. What's the process?
**Answer:** Import VAT (paid at customs) is claimable as input VAT on your VAT201 return, provided you have the SARS customs receipt (the SAD 500 form with the VAT payment stamp) and the VAT number is correctly recorded. The clearing agent must ensure your VAT number appears on the customs declaration. You cannot claim VAT on customs duty itself — duty is a cost, not a VAT. The input VAT claim is made in the VAT period when the goods are imported (not when sold). If you are not yet VAT-registered when the tiles are imported, you lose the input VAT claim permanently — another reason to register for VAT before importing materials. Keep all customs documentation for 5 years — SARS audits import VAT claims rigorously.
### SECTION N: CONSTRUCTION WASTE, ASBESTOS & SAFETY

#### Q: 81 Our demolition contractor found asbestos-cement floor tiles (19x19cm patterned tiles, typical of the 1970s) under the existing floor covering. What are our legal obligations under the Asbestos Regulations 2020?
**Answer:** Asbestos-containing floor tiles are regulated under the Asbestos Abatement Regulations, 2020. You must: (1) immediately cease work in the affected area and cordon it off, (2) engage a registered asbestos inspector to confirm the asbestos content (polarised light microscopy), (3) if confirmed, appoint a registered asbestos removal contractor (not a general demolition contractor), (4) notify the Department of Employment and Labour 14 days before removal, (5) prepare an asbestos removal plan and obtain written approval, (6) dispose of the asbestos waste at a licensed hazardous waste facility (not a general landfill — Holfontein near Vereeniging and Vissershok near Cape Town are licensed). Removal costs are R150–R400/m² depending on access and encapsulation requirements. Failure to comply is a criminal offence under OHSA with fines up to R5 million and/or 5 years imprisonment.

#### Q: 82 We're generating approximately 30 tonnes of tile offcuts and broken tiles from our development. The waste contractor wants to take it to a general landfill, but we've heard tile waste is classified as "building and demolition waste" with specific disposal requirements. Is this correct?
