**Answer:** Building and demolition waste (including tile offcuts) is classified as General Waste under the National Waste Management Act, not hazardous waste, and can be disposed of at licensed general landfill sites that accept builders' rubble. However, not all landfills accept construction waste — you must use a licensed construction and demolition (C&D) waste facility. Some municipalities (City of Cape Town, City of Johannesburg) require waste manifest documentation tracking the waste from site to landfill. The National Norms and Standards for Assessment of Waste for Landfill Disposal require that you classify the waste using the SANS 10234 waste classification system. Tile offcuts are typically inert (Class A waste) and can be crushed for recycling as aggregate. Consider engaging a recycling facility — crushed ceramic tile is used in road base and concrete aggregate, and recycling is cheaper than landfill.

#### Q: 83 A worker on site slipped on tile adhesive that had spilled on the floor and fractured his wrist. He was not wearing the safety boots we provided. Is this a COIDA claim only, or can he sue us for negligence?
**Answer:** The worker's exclusive remedy is through the Compensation for Occupational Injuries and Diseases Act (COIDA) — he cannot sue you for damages in civil court (COIDA Section 35 provides the employer immunity from civil claims for workplace injuries). You must report the injury to the Compensation Commissioner within 7 days (Form W.CL.2), and the worker receives compensation through the fund. However, if the Department of Employment and Labour inspects and finds you failed to maintain a safe workplace (spilled adhesive not cleaned up, no housekeeping procedures), you can be prosecuted under OHSA and fined. The fact that he wasn't wearing safety boots is a mitigating factor but does not absolve you of the housekeeping obligation. Improve site housekeeping immediately.

#### Q: 84 Our site manager was arrested because a subcontractor's employee fell through an unprotected floor opening where tiles were being laid. The charge is "contravention of OHSA Construction Regulations." What's the potential liability?
**Answer:** This is extremely serious. Under the Construction Regulations 2014, any person who controls a construction site (including the developer's representative) has a legal duty to ensure health and safety. Falling from an unprotected opening is a "fall from height" incident — the regulations require all openings to be covered or guarded while work is in progress. The site manager faces: (1) criminal prosecution under OHSA with potential fines up to R100,000 and/or 2 years imprisonment for a first offence, (2) if gross negligence is proven (recklessness), up to R2 million and/or 5 years imprisonment, (3) permanent criminal record affecting future employment. The company also faces fines. Engage a criminal attorney specialising in occupational health and safety immediately. The subcontractor shares liability — they are required to have their own health and safety plan and supervision.

#### Q: 85 Tile cutting on site generates silica dust. We provided dust masks but workers say they're uncomfortable and don't wear them. A health inspector is visiting next week. What are the consequences if she finds workers cutting tiles dry without masks?
**Answer:** Silica dust exposure is regulated under the Hazardous Chemical Substances Regulations (HCS) and the OHSA. Respirable crystalline silica is a known carcinogen. If the inspector finds workers exposed to silica dust without respiratory protection: (1) she can issue a prohibition notice — immediate shutdown of tile cutting operations, (2) fines of up to R50,000 per contravention per worker exposed, (3) potential criminal prosecution of the employer, (4) the company may be listed on the "name and shame" register. Best practice: eliminate the hazard by requiring WET cutting only (water suppresses 99% of silica dust), which removes the need for respiratory protection in most cases. If dry cutting is unavoidable, you must conduct air monitoring, provide approved respiratory protection (not dust masks — P2 or FFP3 rated respirators), and train workers on proper use. Wet cutting is the only sustainable solution.
### SECTION O: DISABILITY ACCESS, SECTIONAL TITLE & NEIGHBOUR DISPUTES

#### Q: 86 SANS 10400 Part S requires tactile paving at level changes in our apartment building lobby. We've installed polished porcelain tiles throughout. Can we install tactile indicators on top of the existing tiles, or must we replace the floor finish?
**Answer:** Tactile paving (truncated dome or bar patterns) can be surface-applied to existing tiled floors using self-adhesive polyurethane or stainless-steel tactile indicators. You do not need to replace the floor finish. The indicators must comply with SANS 784 (tactile paving indicators), with a luminance contrast of at least 30% against the background surface, and height of 4mm–5mm. They must be installed at all changes in level (single step, ramp onset), at the top and bottom of staircases, and along accessible routes. Polished porcelain may present a slip hazard if wet — ensure the tactile indicators themselves have a slip rating of at least R10 and that the surrounding floor achieves R9 minimum. Surface-applied indicators cost R80–R150 per unit; a full lobby installation is R3,000–R8,000.

#### Q: 87 A purchaser in our wheelchair-friendly unit says the bathroom floor tiles are "too cold" and wants us to install underfloor heating at our cost, claiming it's a "reasonable accommodation" under the Promotion of Equality and Prevention of Unfair Discrimination Act (PEPUDA). Do we have to comply?
**Answer:** PEPUDA requires reasonable accommodation for persons with disabilities, but "too cold tiles" does not constitute discrimination or a barrier to equal enjoyment of the accommodation. Under SANS 10400-S, accessible units must meet specific dimensional requirements (door widths, turning circles, grab rail positions, floor surface firmness and stability). Thermal comfort is NOT a specified requirement. If the tiles meet the slip resistance, firmness, and surface stability requirements of SANS 10400-S, you have complied. Underfloor heating is an amenity, not a disability access requirement. If the buyer requires underfloor heating for a specific medical condition (circulatory disorder), they may request it as a reasonable accommodation, but the cost would typically be borne by the requestor unless you agreed otherwise in the sale contract.

#### Q: 88 Our development's homeowners association (HOA) constitution requires that all external paving and driveway tiles be "earth-toned clay pavers." A buyer wants to install grey porcelain tiles. Can the HOA enforce this restriction after transfer?
**Answer:** Yes, if the title deed includes a condition binding the property to the HOA's architectural guidelines, and the buyer was made aware of these restrictions before purchase. The HOA can enforce aesthetic restrictions through the title deed conditions, and non-compliance can result in enforcement action including court orders for removal. However, the restrictions must be reasonable and cannot be discriminatory or capricious. "Earth-toned clay pavers" is a reasonable aesthetic guideline. As developer, you must ensure all buyers are provided with a copy of the HOA design guidelines before signing the sale agreement. Failure to disclose HOA restrictions can result in a CPA claim for misrepresentation — the buyer may rescind the contract if they were unaware of the restrictions.

#### Q: 89 A neighbour's property was damaged when our tiling contractor's waterproofing failed and water seeped through the common wall. The neighbour is suing us, not the contractor. Can they do that?
**Answer:** Yes. Under the neighbour law principles (derived from both common law and the National Building Regulations), an owner of a property is liable for damage caused to an adjoining property by defects in their building, regardless of whether they caused the defect personally. This is based on the principle that a property owner must not use their property in a way that causes harm to neighbours (the sic utere tuo ut alienum non laedas principle). You must pay the neighbour's damages and then claim an indemnity from your tiling contractor and/or waterproofing subcontractor. Ensure your sale contracts with buyers include a warranty that the waterproofing complies with SANS 10400-XA and that the buyer must maintain it — this protects you from claims by subsequent owners.

#### Q: 90 We're building a row of townhouses with zero-lot-line walls. The tile layer needs to work from the neighbour's side to install tiles on the party wall. The neighbour refuses access. What can we do?
**Answer:** Under common law, you have no automatic right of access to a neighbour's property unless an access servitude is registered against their title deed. For zero-lot-line construction, you should have secured a temporary access servitude or "building line relaxation" before construction began. Without it: (1) negotiate with the neighbour — offer compensation for the inconvenience, (2) if the neighbour unreasonably refuses, apply to the High Court for a temporary access order under the Access to Neighbouring Land Act, which allows courts to grant access for essential construction work if the neighbour's refusal is unreasonable, (3) as a last resort, use a swing-stage or cherry picker from your side if physically possible. Prevention: always negotiate access servitudes during the planning phase, before breaking ground.
### SECTION P: TITLE DEED RESTRICTIONS, BANKING & ARBITRATION

#### Q: 91 The title deed for our development land has a restrictive condition: "No building shall exceed two storeys in height." We want to build four storeys. Can we apply to have the restriction removed, and how long does this take?
**Answer:** Title deed restrictions can only be removed by: (1) application to the High Court for removal (if the restriction is obsolete or unreasonable), which takes 6–12 months and costs R100,000+, or (2) application to the original creator of the restriction or their successors (if a private party imposed it), or (3) in some cases, the local municipality can assist if the restriction was imposed by a predecessor municipality. If the restriction was imposed by a previous landowner for aesthetic or neighbourhood character reasons, removal is difficult — the court will consider whether the restriction still serves a legitimate purpose and whether neighbouring properties would be prejudiced. Engage a conveyancer specialising in title restrictions before purchasing land with restrictive conditions.

#### Q: 92 Our development finance agreement has an arbitration clause referring disputes to AFSA (Association of Arbitrators). A tile supplier has sued us in the Magistrate's Court for non-payment. Can we enforce the arbitration clause and stop the court action?
**Answer:** If the tile supplier is not a party to your development finance agreement, the arbitration clause does not bind them — they can sue you in court. Arbitration clauses only bind the parties who signed the agreement. If the supplier's contract with you (or your main contractor) also contains an arbitration clause, you can apply for a stay of the court proceedings under Section 6 of the Arbitration Act. The court will grant the stay unless there are compelling reasons not to. However, if there is no arbitration agreement with the supplier, you must defend the court action. For future developments, include arbitration clauses in all supply agreements, not just the finance agreement.

#### Q: 93 We appointed an arbitrator through AFSA for a dispute with our tiling contractor. The arbitrator's fees are R4,500 per hour, and the hearing is scheduled for 5 days. The dispute is worth R800,000. Is arbitration cost-effective?
**Answer:** In this case, probably not. Five days at R4,500/hour equals approximately R180,000 (assuming 8-hour days), plus your attorney's fees (R3,000–R5,000/hour for preparation and hearing), plus the arbitrator's preparation time. Total arbitration costs could reach R400,000–R500,000 for an R800,000 dispute. AFSA has a "fast-track" procedure for disputes under R1 million with capped costs. Alternatively, consider: (1) mediation through AFSA or private mediation (typically R15,000–R30,000 total), (2) expert determination (a single expert decides the technical issue), (3) the Small Claims Court if the amount falls within jurisdiction. Arbitration is excellent for complex, high-value disputes with technical issues, but for smaller disputes, mediation or court litigation may be more cost-effective.

#### Q: 94 Our bank won't release the next tranche of development finance because the quantity surveyor's cost report shows we've exceeded the tile budget by 18%. The overrun is due to a specification upgrade. Can the bank legally withhold funding?
**Answer:** Yes. Development finance agreements typically include a "cost overrun" clause allowing the bank to withhold further drawdowns if the project exceeds budget by more than a specified percentage (commonly 5%–10%). The bank's concern is that their security (the completed development) may be insufficient to cover the loan if costs spiral. To release the tranche, you must either: (1) inject additional equity to cover the overrun, (2) provide additional security (another property or personal surety), (3) reduce costs elsewhere in the project to bring the overall budget back in line, or (4) negotiate a facility increase (new credit application, new valuations). The bank is contractually entitled to withhold funds — this is not a dispute, it's standard facility management. Always obtain bank approval before specification changes that affect cost.

#### Q: 95 The bank requires that our development company directors personally guarantee the development loan. We're uncomfortable with this. Is there any way to avoid personal suretyship?
**Answer:** For first-time or smaller developers, personal suretyship is almost always required by South African banks — it's non-negotiable unless you have significant track record and balance sheet strength. For experienced developers with multiple completed projects: (1) offer a corporate guarantee from a listed parent company instead, (2) increase the loan-to-value ratio (more equity, less debt), (3) provide additional security in the form of unbonded properties, (4) use a mezzanine lender for part of the funding (more expensive but less restrictive on suretyship), (5) as a last resort, negotiate a "limited suretyship" capped at a specific amount rather than unlimited joint and several suretyship. Never sign unlimited suretyship without understanding that your personal assets (house, savings) are at risk if the project fails.
### SECTION Q: WATERPROOFING WARRANTIES, MUNICIPAL CONNECTIONS & ENERGY

#### Q: 96 The waterproofing subcontractor provided a "10-year waterproofing guarantee" for our bathroom floors. Who is the guarantee actually in favour of — us as the developer, or the end purchaser?
**Answer:** Unless the guarantee is specifically ceded to the purchaser, it is a contract between the waterproofing subcontractor and you as the developer. The end purchaser has no direct contractual right to enforce it. However, you can cede the guarantee to the purchaser at transfer — this is best practice and adds value to the sale. The guarantee should specify: (1) what is covered (water ingress through the waterproofing membrane only, not through plumbing leaks or structural cracks), (2) maintenance requirements (re-sealant at junctions every 3–5 years), (3) the claims procedure (notification in writing, inspection by the guarantor), (4) exclusions (damage from structural movement, alterations by the owner). A 10-year guarantee is market standard for torch-on and liquid membranes in South Africa.

#### Q: 97 We've been waiting 5 months for electricity connection from City Power. The units are complete, buyers are clamouring, and we're paying interest on the full facility. Can we issue temporary occupation and use generators?
**Answer:** Issuing occupation without a valid Occupation Certificate is illegal under the National Building Regulations and the municipal by-laws. However, some municipalities will issue a "temporary occupation certificate" or "conditional approval" if the only outstanding item is the electricity connection, provided: (1) fire safety systems are operational, (2) alternative power is provided (generators), (3) the municipality confirms the connection is imminent (within 30–60 days), (4) buyers sign an acknowledgement of temporary conditions. Not all municipalities offer this — City of Johannesburg has been more flexible than Ekurhuleni or Tshwane in recent years. Contact the municipal building control manager and request a meeting. The cost of running generators for common area services (R5,000–R15,000/day for a 50-unit block) is usually less than the interest cost of delayed sales.

#### Q: 98 Our development includes a solar PV system for common area power. The body corporate wants to tile the roof area around the panels with porcelain pavers to create a "roof terrace." Will this affect the solar system's performance warranty?
**Answer:** Installing porcelain pavers around solar panels does not directly affect the PV system's performance if the panels themselves are not shaded or covered. However: (1) the additional dead load of the pavers on the roof slab must be checked by a structural engineer — porcelain pavers at 25kg/m² plus screed and adhesive can add 40–50kg/m², (2) the roof waterproofing must be intact before tiling — any penetration for fixing the paver support system compromises the waterproofing, (3) the solar installer may void their warranty if the panel access for maintenance is obstructed (panels need 500mm clear walkways for cleaning and maintenance), (4) SANS 10400-X requires roof insulation — tiling the roof surface may trap heat and reduce panel efficiency by 5%–10%. Specify elevated pedestal paver systems that allow airflow beneath the tiles.

#### Q: 99 The body corporate of our completed scheme wants to install EV charging stations in the basement parking. The floor is tiled with ceramic tiles. Do they need to lift the tiles to run cabling?
**Answer:** EV charging station cabling can be surface-mounted on walls and columns in the basement without lifting floor tiles, provided the cable route follows existing wall/ceiling cavities. However, if the charging station locations require new floor-mounted distribution boards or floor boxes, the tiles must be lifted in those specific areas. The body corporate should engage an electrician to design a cable route that minimises floor penetration. Any penetrations through the floor slab must be fire-stopped (SANS 10400-T) to maintain fire compartmentation. The cost of lifting and re-tiling is approximately R500–R800/m². For a basement with 50 charging points, budget R200,000–R400,000 for floor-related work if surface routing is not possible.

#### Q: 100 We installed a greywater recycling system, and the treated water is being used for toilet flushing. The bathroom floor tiles are showing efflorescence around the toilet bases. Is the recycled water causing this?
**Answer:** Treated greywater typically has higher dissolved solids and mineral content than potable water, which can accelerate efflorescence if there are any leaks at the toilet base seal. However, the root cause is more likely a failed wax ring or silicone seal at the toilet-to-floor junction, allowing water (grey or otherwise) to seep into the tile bed. The salts in the greywater crystallise as the water evaporates, producing the white efflorescence. Solution: (1) reseal all toilet bases with marine-grade polyurethane sealant, (2) check the toilet pan connectors for leaks, (3) if greywater is indeed leaking, the system must be redesigned to ensure positive sealing at all junctions. Efflorescence from greywater is not a structural defect but an ongoing maintenance issue — the body corporate must budget for annual re-sealing.
### SECTION R: LOAD SHEDDING, ENERGY & CONTINGENCY PLANNING

#### Q: 101 Load shedding has delayed our project by 6 weeks. We have a penalty clause of R15,000/day for late completion to the main contractor. Can we claim the full 6 weeks from Eskom or the municipality?
**Answer:** No. You cannot claim from Eskom or the municipality for load shedding delays unless there is a specific contractual relationship with them (which there isn't for a private development). Your recourse depends on your contract terms. Under JBCC 2014 (and later editions), load shedding is a "change in legislation" or "statutory change" event that entitles the contractor to a time extension but not necessarily cost compensation (check your contract's Schedule of Variables). The penalty clause may not be enforceable against the contractor if the delay was caused by a compensable delay event. Review your contracts: if the penalty clause is "liquidated damages," they must be a genuine pre-estimate of loss — you cannot profit from the penalty. For the contractor's delay claim, you need a concurrent delay analysis to apportion responsibility.

#### Q: 102 We installed a generator backup system, but the fuel fumes are discolouring the lobby floor tiles (travertine-look porcelain). The tiles have a matte finish. Is this permanent damage?
**Answer:** Generator exhaust fumes contain hydrocarbons and sulphur compounds that can deposit as a thin film on porous or textured tile surfaces, causing a yellow-brown discolouration. On porcelain tiles (even matte finish), this is typically a surface deposit, not permanent staining, because porcelain has near-zero porosity. Clean the affected tiles with an alkaline degreaser (pH 11–13) followed by a neutral cleaner. If the tiles are unglazed porcelain with a textured surface, the deposits may have settled in the surface texture — use a stiff nylon brush. To prevent recurrence: (1) extend the generator exhaust pipe to discharge outside the building or above roof level, (2) ensure adequate ventilation in the generator room, (3) consider a catalytic converter on the exhaust. This is not a tile defect — it's an environmental exposure issue.

#### Q: 103 During Stage 6 load shedding, our concrete supplier couldn't deliver screed for the tile bed. The screed that was already placed set too quickly and cracked extensively. Is the concrete supplier liable?
**Answer:** The concrete supplier is liable only if the screed mix was defective (incorrect cement content, excessive water, or inadequate curing time specification). However, rapid setting during load shedding is an environmental issue, not a product defect. The concrete supplier's terms of sale typically exclude liability for delays caused by force majeure events. Your claim would be against your contractor for failing to programme screed placement to avoid load shedding periods, or for failing to have generator backup for critical concrete pours. Under SANS 1200, screed must be cured for a minimum period (typically 7 days for light traffic, 28 days for tiling) — load shedding should not interrupt curing if proper wet-curing methods are used. The cracked screed must be removed and replaced before tiling.

#### Q: 104 We designed the building with underfloor heating beneath the tiles. During load shedding, the heating is off for 4–6 hours at a time. Will the thermal cycling (heating/cooling) damage the tiles or adhesive?
**Answer:** No. Underfloor heating systems cycle on and off naturally as the thermostat controls them. A 4–6 hour off-period during load shedding is no different from the system being off overnight. Standard porcelain tiles with a flexible adhesive (C2TES1) and appropriate expansion joints can handle normal thermal cycling without damage. The only risk is if the underfloor heating is turned on to maximum immediately after load shedding ends, causing rapid thermal shock. Program the thermostat to ramp up gradually (no more than 5°C per hour). Ensure the tile adhesive and grout are rated for underfloor heating (temperature resistance to 45°C surface temperature). Most quality S1 classified adhesives in South Africa are UFH-compatible.

#### Q: 105 Load shedding caused our site security system to fail, and thieves stole R400,000 worth of imported tiles from site overnight. Our insurance policy has a "security warranty" requiring 24/7 armed response. Does the power failure void our claim?
**Answer:** The insurer may attempt to repudiate the claim on the grounds that the security warranty was breached. However, if the security system failure was solely attributable to load shedding (a nationwide event beyond your control) and you had taken reasonable alternative measures (backup battery for the alarm, patrolling guards during outages), you have a strong argument that the breach was not material or wilful. South African courts have increasingly recognised load shedding as an extraordinary circumstance. To support your claim: (1) provide evidence that the alarm battery was functional (it may have simply exceeded capacity during extended Stage 6), (2) show that guards were present but overwhelmed, (3) demonstrate that the security system was maintained and tested regularly. If the insurer repudiates, escalate to the Ombudsman for Short-Term Insurance.
### SECTION S: UNUSUAL EDGE CASES, RURAL ISSUES & TRIBAL LAND

#### Q: 106 We're developing on tribal land in KZN under a Permission to Occupy (PTO) from the traditional council. The PTO says nothing about building standards. Do SANS 10400 and the NHBRC still apply?
**Answer:** The National Building Regulations (SANS 10400) and NHBRC requirements apply to ALL new residential construction in South Africa, regardless of land tenure. A Permission to Occupy does not exempt you from national law. However, enforcement on tribal land is often weaker because the municipal building control office may not have jurisdiction or capacity in those areas. If you are selling units commercially, the NHBRC and CPA definitely apply — you cannot sell homes to the public without NHBRC enrolment and CPA compliance. If the development is for communal or traditional council use, the legal position is greyer, but SANS 10400 standards should still be followed for safety. The Ingonyama Trust and traditional councils have their own approval processes that run parallel to municipal requirements — you need both.

#### Q: 107 A traditional council in the Eastern Cape is demanding "ischibi" (a traditional levy of R50,000) before we can start construction on communal land we've already leased. Is this legal?
**Answer:** The payment of traditional levies or "ischibi" is a customary practice recognised in many rural areas of the Eastern Cape and KZN. Whether it is legally enforceable depends on the terms of your lease agreement with the traditional council and whether the levy was disclosed upfront. If the lease explicitly requires the payment of traditional levies, you are contractually bound. If not, the council cannot unilaterally impose new financial obligations after the lease is signed. However, refusing to pay may result in social and political obstacles that stall your project. Many developers budget for these levies as a cost of doing business in rural areas — they are typically 1%–3% of project value. Obtain a written receipt and ensure the payment is acknowledged in the lease. Do not pay cash without documentation.

#### Q: 108 We're building a luxury lodge in a private game reserve near Hoedspruit. There are no municipal services and no building inspector. Do we need to comply with SANS 10400 at all?
**Answer:** Yes. SANS 10400 is part of the National Building Regulations, which apply throughout South Africa. The absence of municipal building control does not exempt you. If you do not obtain a building plan approval and inspection, you face: (1) inability to obtain insurance for the completed building, (2) liability if any guest is injured due to non-compliant construction, (3) difficulties when selling the property in future (no approved plans means the building is "illegal" and the purchaser cannot obtain a bond), (4) potential prosecution if the local municipality later extends its services and discovers unapproved construction. For game reserves in Limpopo, engage a private building inspector registered with the South African Council for the Project and Construction Management Professions (SACPCMP) to conduct independent inspections and issue a compliance certificate.

#### Q: 109 Our development is in a mining town where the mine owns all the land and leases plots to employees. The mine requires us to use their "approved" tile supplier, who charges 40% above market. Is this an abuse of dominance under the Competition Act?
**Answer:** If the mine has a dominant position in the relevant market (i.e., they control virtually all residential land in the town), requiring the use of a specific supplier at inflated prices may constitute an abuse of dominance under Section 8 of the Competition Act — specifically, "excluding competitors" or "an excessive price." However, proving abuse of dominance requires demonstrating that the mine's conduct is both anti-competitive and has no efficiency justification. The mine may argue that their supplier requirement ensures quality control and bulk purchasing efficiency. Before lodging a complaint with the Competition Commission, gather evidence: (1) the price differential between the approved supplier and market alternatives, (2) any evidence that the mine benefits financially from the arrangement (kickbacks), (3) attempts to negotiate with the mine. Also consider approaching the Minerals Council for mediation — mining companies are sensitive to competition complaints.

#### Q: 110 We're building on a farm outside Port Elizabeth that has no municipal connection. We're using a borehole for water and septic tanks for sewage. The tile supplier says their warranty is void if tiles are installed in areas with "aggressive groundwater." How do we test for this?
**Answer:** "Aggressive groundwater" refers to water with high sulphate content (above 500mg/L), low pH (below 6.5), or high chloride content that attacks cement-based adhesives and grouts. To test: (1) take a groundwater sample from your borehole, (2) send it to a SANAS-accredited laboratory for full chemical analysis (SANS 10802 standard), (3) specifically request sulphate (SO4), chloride (Cl), pH, and total dissolved solids (TDS). If sulphates exceed 500mg/L, you must use sulphate-resistant adhesive (SR classification, available from major manufacturers), or switch to an epoxy-based adhesive system which is chemically inert. If chlorides exceed 200mg/L in a coastal environment, use corrosion-resistant fixings. The test costs approximately R1,500–R3,000 and takes 5–7 working days. It's essential for farm and rural developments.
### SECTION T: COMPLEX LIABILITY, INSURANCE & LEGAL TRAPS

#### Q: 111 A tile in a common area lobby came loose, and a visitor tripped and broke their hip. They are suing the body corporate, the developer (us), and the tiling contractor. Who is actually liable?
**Answer:** Liability depends on when the incident occurred and what caused the tile to come loose. If within the defects liability period: the tiling contractor is primarily liable for defective workmanship; the developer is secondarily liable as the employer. If after the DLP: the body corporate is liable for failure to maintain common property under STSMA Section 3(1)(b), unless the defect is structural (NHBRC 5-year warranty). The developer's liability post-transfer is limited to: (1) undisclosed latent defects known at the time of sale (common law), (2) NHBRC structural warranty claims, (3) CPA claims for defects present at sale. If the tile came loose due to substrate failure (structural), the NHBRC warranty applies and the developer remains liable. If due to poor adhesion or lack of expansion joints, it's the contractor's workmanship. Each defendant's attorney will argue for apportionment under the Apportionment of Damages Act.

#### Q: 112 Our development was completed and all units sold. Two years later, a body corporate claims the waterproofing in the basement has failed and water is coming up through the floor tiles. The waterproofing contractor provided a 10-year warranty. Can the body corporate claim directly against the contractor?
**Answer:** If the 10-year waterproofing warranty was ceded to the body corporate at transfer, yes — they can claim directly. If not, the body corporate must claim against the developer, who then claims against the contractor. However, many developers do not properly cede warranties. Under common law, the body corporate has no direct contractual relationship with the waterproofing contractor unless the warranty was assigned. The practical path: the body corporate claims against the developer, the developer invokes the waterproofing warranty against the contractor, and the contractor repairs. If the developer has dissolved the SPV (common practice), the body corporate may be left with no recourse unless they can prove the developer knew of the defect and failed to disclose it (fraudulent concealment). Always cede all warranties to the body corporate before dissolving the SPV.

#### Q: 113 We sold a unit with a "developer's warranty" that covered "all building defects for 5 years." A buyer claims a cracked tile in year 4. We believe it's due to the owner dropping a heavy object. Does the warranty cover impact damage?
**Answer:** A generic "all building defects" warranty is dangerously broad. Unless the warranty explicitly excludes impact damage, abuse, or accidental damage, a court may interpret it as covering any defect that manifests during the warranty period — including the cracked tile. Your defence: commission an expert assessment of the crack pattern. Impact cracks are typically radial (spider-web) with a central impact point, whereas adhesion failure or substrate movement cracks follow different patterns. If the crack is impact-related, it is not a "building defect" — it is damage caused by the owner's negligence. However, the burden of proving this may fall on you. For future developments, define the warranty precisely: "This warranty covers defects in materials and workmanship only. It excludes damage caused by impact, abuse, negligence, alterations, or failure to maintain."

#### Q: 114 A tile installer on site was an independent contractor, not an employee. He injured his back lifting a tile box. He's claiming against our Workers Compensation fund, but we never registered him because he's independent. What now?
**Answer:** COIDA only covers employees, not independent contractors. If the tile installer is genuinely independent (registered as a business, provides own tools, controls how the work is done, bears his own financial risk), he cannot claim from your COID fund. However, he may sue you in civil court for negligence if you controlled the work environment (e.g., you required him to lift heavy boxes without assistance, the site was unsafe). The line between employee and independent contractor is often blurred in construction. If the Labour Court finds he was a "deemed employee" (you controlled his hours, methods, and provided materials), you face: (1) COIDA penalties for non-registration, (2) liability for the injury, (3) potential prosecution. Always ensure independent contractors have their own COIDA or personal accident insurance, and document the independence relationship in writing.

#### Q: 115 We're being sued by a purchaser for "defective tiles" but we didn't manufacture the tiles — we bought them from a local distributor. Can we join the distributor and the Italian manufacturer as third parties to the action?
**Answer:** Yes. Under the CPA, the consumer has a claim against the supplier in the supply chain (you as the developer/seller), and you have a right of recourse against the upstream suppliers — the distributor and the manufacturer. You can issue a third-party notice joining the distributor and manufacturer to the litigation. The CPA provides that all parties in the supply chain are jointly and severally liable for product defects. If the tiles are genuinely defective (manufacturing flaw, not installation error), the manufacturer or distributor should indemnify you. However, joining an Italian manufacturer is complex — you may need to serve proceedings in Italy, and the manufacturer may argue that South African courts do not have jurisdiction. Your supply agreement with the distributor should include an indemnity for product defects and a jurisdiction clause favouring South Africa.
### SECTION U: PROCUREMENT, SPECIFICATION & DESIGN DECISIONS

#### Q: 116 Our architect specified large-format 900x900mm porcelain tiles for the lobby without consulting the structural engineer. The tiles weigh 28kg/m² plus adhesive and screed. The suspended slab was designed for 1.5kPa live load. Have we exceeded the design load?
**Answer:** 900x900mm porcelain tiles at 28kg/m² plus 15kg/m² for adhesive and screed adds approximately 0.43kPa of dead load. If the slab was designed for 1.5kPa total superimposed load and the original specification accounted for lighter finishes (e.g., carpet at 2kg/m²), you've added approximately 0.4kPa — which may push the total load close to or over the design capacity. The structural engineer must recalculate: (1) total dead load including the new tile specification, (2) live load capacity remaining, (3) deflection under the increased dead load. For large-format tiles, deflection is critical — L/360 is the maximum allowable deflection for rigid tile finishes. If the slab deflects more than this, the tiles will crack. Get the engineer's sign-off before ordering — changing tile specification without structural review is a common and costly mistake.

#### Q: 117 The interior designer specified natural stone tiles (travertine) for all bathrooms. We substituted with porcelain lookalike tiles to save cost. The buyer is furious and claims this changes the "character" of the unit. Is "character" a legally enforceable concept?
**Answer:** "Character" is not a legally defined term unless the sales contract or specification schedule explicitly defines the materials that create it. If the contract specifies "travertine tiles" and you substituted porcelain, you are in breach of contract — but the remedy is damages or specific performance (replacement), not a claim for "character." If the contract used generic terms like "premium stone-look tiles," you are within your rights to substitute. The lesson: never allow designers to specify materials by trade name in the sales contract without a "or equal approved" clause. Also, porcelain performs better than travertine in wet areas (lower porosity, better slip resistance) — you can argue the substitution is an improvement. But without a contract clause allowing substitution, you're exposed.

#### Q: 118 Our tiling contractor has proposed using a "tile adhesive with built-in waterproofing" for the shower floors instead of a separate waterproofing membrane. This saves R120/m². Is this compliant with SANS 10400?
**Answer:** No. SANS 10400-XA (waterproofing) requires a separate, continuous waterproofing membrane in wet areas (showers, bathrooms) beneath the tile bed. "Waterproof" adhesive is water-resistant adhesive — it prevents water penetration through the adhesive bed but does not provide a continuous membrane beneath the screed. Water will still penetrate through grout joints and reach the structural slab. A proper waterproofing system requires: (1) a primed substrate, (2) a torch-on or liquid-applied membrane (minimum 1.5mm thick for sheet membranes), (3) protection board, (4) screed bed, (5) tiles. The R120/m² saving is false economy — a single waterproofing failure costs R15,000–R30,000 to remediate per unit, plus damage to the unit below. Never accept "waterproof adhesive" as a substitute for a proper membrane.

#### Q: 119 We want to use recycled glass tiles in our development for sustainability marketing. The supplier can't provide a SANS certificate for the tiles because they're imported and "not tested in South Africa." Can we use them?
**Answer:** SANS 1449 is a voluntary standard for tile classification in South Africa — it is not legally mandatory. However, under SANS 10400-A, building materials must be "fit for purpose" and must not create health or safety hazards. If the glass tiles meet the ISO 10545 international standard (which the supplier should provide), they can be used, but you should: (1) obtain the ISO test certificates (water absorption, breaking strength, chemical resistance, slip resistance), (2) have an independent laboratory test a sample batch to SANS 1449 parameters for your records, (3) ensure the tiles are rated for the intended application (floor tiles must have higher breaking strength than wall tiles), (4) check that the adhesive manufacturer's warranty covers use with glass tiles. Glass tiles are typically more slippery than porcelain — ensure the slip rating meets SANS 10400-S for the application.

#### Q: 120 The main contractor wants to use a "tile on tile" system — tiling over existing tiles in the renovation portion of our project to save time. What's the risk?
**Answer:** Tiling over existing tiles is technically possible but carries significant risk: (1) the existing tiles must be well-bonded — tap test every tile, remove any hollow-sounding ones, (2) the existing surface must be thoroughly cleaned and degreased, (3) the additional height (typically 15–20mm) affects door thresholds, skirting alignment, and plumbing fixture connections, (4) the double layer increases dead load on the slab — check with the structural engineer, (5) if the original tiles fail (debond) in future, the new tiles fail with them, (6) warranty issues — most adhesive manufacturers will not warrant tile-on-tile installations unless their specific system is used and the substrate is approved. If any original tiles are loose, remove them all and start fresh. Tile-on-tile is a cost-saving shortcut that often results in call-backs.
### SECTION V: NEIGHBOUR ISSUES, EASEMENTS & PARTY WALLS

#### Q: 121 The neighbour's building is so close that our tile layer needs to lean a ladder against their wall to tile our external wall. They refuse permission. Can we get a court order for access?
**Answer:** Under the Access to Neighbouring Land Act, you can apply to the Magistrate's Court or High Court for an order granting temporary access to a neighbour's property if: (1) the work you are doing is necessary and cannot reasonably be done without access, (2) the neighbour's refusal is unreasonable, (3) you offer adequate compensation for any damage or inconvenience. The court will consider the duration of access required, the impact on the neighbour's use of their property, and whether alternative methods exist. For tiling work that requires only a few hours of ladder access, a court is likely to grant the order with compensation of R500–R2,000 per day. However, the court process takes 2–6 weeks — it's faster and cheaper to negotiate a cash payment with the neighbour. Always address access requirements before construction begins.

#### Q: 122 Water from our development's balcony is dripping onto the neighbour's driveway because the tile layer didn't install a drip edge. The neighbour is threatening to sue for "aquae pluviae" (rainwater damage). Is this a real cause of action?
**Answer:** The aquae pluviae action is an ancient Roman-Dutch law remedy that allows a lower landowner to sue an upper landowner for diverting or concentrating rainwater onto their property in a way that causes damage. While this specific cause of action has been superseded by modern neighbour law principles in many contexts, the underlying principle remains: you must not alter the natural flow of water from your property in a way that causes damage to neighbours. The balcony drip edge is a standard detail (SANS 10400-XA requires water to be directed away from the building). You are liable for the cost of installing the drip edge and any damage caused. Fix the drip edge immediately — it's a R50 detail that prevents a R50,000 lawsuit.

#### Q: 123 Our townhouse development has party walls between units. The tile layer tiled right across the party wall without leaving a movement joint at the boundary. Now both owners are complaining about cracking. Who pays for the repair?
**Answer:** Party walls are structural elements shared between two properties. Under the Sectional Titles Schemes Management Act (for sectional title) or common law (for freehold townhouses), both owners share responsibility for the structural wall. The tile layer should have installed a movement joint at the party wall boundary to allow independent movement of the two structural bays. Failure to do so is defective workmanship. The repair cost should be borne by the developer (if within the DLP) or the body corporate (if the wall is common property). If the development is completed and transferred, the affected owners may need to share the repair cost and then claim against the developer if the DLP has not expired. For new developments, specify movement joints at all party wall junctions in the tiling specification.

#### Q: 124 A purchaser claims their unit has "noise transmission" from the adjacent unit through the party wall, and the tiles on their side amplify the sound. They want us to install acoustic insulation and re-tile. Is this our responsibility?
**Answer:** Under SANS 10400-T (Part T for fire and Part K for acoustic control), inter-unit walls in multi-unit residential buildings must achieve minimum airborne and impact sound insulation ratings. The tile finish itself does not provide acoustic insulation — the wall construction does (typically double-skin masonry with cavity fill, or insulated drywall). If the wall meets the SANS 10400 acoustic requirements, the tile finish is irrelevant to the rating. "Amplification" of sound by hard tiles is a room acoustics issue, not a building code issue — the buyer's remedy is rugs, curtains, or wall panels. However, if the wall does not meet the acoustic rating (insufficient mass, missing cavity fill, unsealed penetrations), the developer is responsible for remediation. Commission an acoustic test (SANS 10177) to determine if the wall complies.

#### Q: 125 Our estate has a shared driveway paved with clay brick pavers. One homeowner replaced their section with polished porcelain tiles, which are dangerously slippery when wet. The HOA wants them to remove the tiles. Can the HOA enforce this?
**Answer:** Yes, if the HOA's design guidelines or conduct rules prohibit changes to shared driveways or specify minimum slip ratings for external surfaces. Shared driveways are typically common property or servitude areas, and individual owners cannot unilaterally alter the surface. Even if the driveway is privately owned, the title deed may include aesthetic restrictions or maintenance obligations. Polished porcelain (R9 or lower when wet) on an external driveway is a safety hazard — slip resistance standards (SANS 10107) require R10 minimum for external pedestrian areas and R11 for ramps. The HOA can issue a compliance notice and, if necessary, obtain a court order for removal. The owner may also face civil liability if someone slips and is injured.
### SECTION W: MORE UNUSUAL & AWKWARD DEVELOPER QUESTIONS

#### Q: 126 Can we specify different tile grades for different areas — Grade 1 (premium) for show units and Grade 3 for the actual sold units — provided the sales contract doesn't specify the grade?
**Answer:** While legally possible if the contract is silent on grade, this is ethically questionable and commercially dangerous. SANS 1449 defines tile grades: Grade 1 (minor visual defects only), Grade 2 (minor defects, some colour variation), Grade 3 (visible defects). A Grade 3 tile is fit for purpose but will have noticeable colour variation, pinholes, or size variation. If a buyer discovers they received Grade 3 while the show house had Grade 1, they may claim under CPA Section 55 that the goods are not of "good quality" and not "fit for purpose" for a residential unit. The CPA's "reasonable consumer" test considers the overall context — a buyer of a new home expects consistent quality. If you use different grades, disclose it in the contract. The saving (typically R20–R50/m²) is not worth the reputational risk.

#### Q: 127 A buyer wants to install underfloor heating over our tiles after taking occupation. Does this void our NHBRC warranty on the floor slab?
**Answer:** The NHBRC warranty covers structural defects in the floor slab, not the tile finish. Installing underfloor heating over the tiles does not affect the slab's structural integrity. However, if the underfloor heating installation involves drilling into or cutting the slab (for conduit routing), this could compromise the structural element and void the NHBRC warranty for the affected area. The NHBRC warranty is transferrable to subsequent owners but only covers defects present at construction. Post-occupation alterations are the owner's responsibility. Your sale agreement should include a clause stating that the NHBRC warranty does not cover damage caused by post-transfer alterations. The buyer should ensure their UFH installer has professional indemnity insurance.

#### Q: 128 We're required to provide a certain percentage of units as "affordable housing" under the municipal zoning conditions. Can we use lower-specification tiles in the affordable units compared to the market-rate units?
**Answer:** Municipal affordable housing conditions typically regulate unit size, price point, and income targeting — they do not specify interior finishes. However, using significantly lower finishes in affordable units compared to market-rate units in the same development may expose you to: (1) claims of discriminatory treatment under PEPUDA (Promotion of Equality and Prevention of Unfair Discrimination Act), (2) reputational damage and media scrutiny, (3) if the affordable units are visibly inferior, municipal housing officials may refuse to sign off on the affordable housing certificate. Best practice: use the same tile specification across all units, or if cost constraints require a difference, ensure the affordable unit tiles are still durable, attractive, and compliant with all standards. Never use substandard tiles in affordable units — it perpetuates inequality and invites legal challenge.

#### Q: 129 A purchaser in our sectional title scheme wants to tile over the existing bedroom carpet with vinyl tiles. The body corporate says this is an alteration to the unit and requires their consent. Is the body corporate correct?
**Answer:** For internal alterations within a sectional title unit, the owner's freedom depends on the conduct rules. Standard STSMA conduct rules require body corporate consent for alterations that affect the building's structure, common property, or external appearance. Tiling over carpet inside a unit does not affect any of these — it's purely internal. The body corporate cannot unreasonably withhold consent for internal finish changes. However, if the new tiles increase the floor load significantly or involve waterproofing (e.g., converting a bedroom to a wet area), consent may be required. Vinyl tiles on a bedroom floor is a minor internal alteration — the body corporate has no basis to interfere. The owner should cite STSMA Section 13(1)(c) which protects the right to use and enjoy their section.

#### Q: 130 We're being audited by SARS and they want to know why our tile procurement cost per unit decreased by 22% between Phase 1 and Phase 2. The truth is we switched to a cheaper supplier. Could this trigger a transfer pricing investigation?
**Answer:** A 22% cost reduction between phases is unlikely to trigger a transfer pricing investigation on its own — transfer pricing applies to cross-border transactions between connected persons (related parties), not domestic arm's-length supplier switches. SARS is likely checking whether: (1) you inflated Phase 1 costs to reduce taxable income, (2) the cheaper tiles in Phase 2 are reflected in the selling price, (3) there was any side agreement or kickback arrangement. Provide SARS with: (1) the supplier invoices for both phases, (2) the specification sheets showing the tiles are equivalent, (3) the B-BBEE certificates showing the switch was compliance-driven, (4) minutes of the procurement decision. If the switch was legitimate and at arm's length, there is no issue. Keep all procurement documentation for 5 years.

#### Q: 131 A tile manufacturer offered us a "marketing contribution" of R50,000 if we specify their tiles exclusively across the entire development. Is this a kickback, and is it legal?
**Answer:** A "marketing contribution" from a supplier to a developer for exclusive specification is not inherently illegal if: (1) it is properly documented as a sponsorship or marketing agreement, (2) the amount is commercially reasonable and disclosed to the buyer (if material), (3) it does not result in you specifying inferior or overpriced products to the buyer's detriment. However, if the payment is secret, not documented, or results in you charging the buyer the full price while pocketing the contribution, it constitutes fraud. Under the CPA, you have a duty to act in good faith — secret commissions breach this duty. If the tile specification is appropriate and the price to the buyer is fair, document the marketing agreement, declare it in your financial statements, and ensure it does not compromise the buyer's interests. If in doubt, disclose it to the buyer.

#### Q: 132 Our tiling specification calls for "white body porcelain." A supplier delivered "red body porcelain" (which is cheaper). The project manager didn't notice and the tiles were installed in 15 units. The red body has higher water absorption. Do we need to replace them?
**Answer:** Red body (also called "double-fire" or "white-fire red body") porcelain has a different clay composition than white body porcelain. True porcelain should have water absorption below 0.5% (ISO 10545-3). Some "red body" products marketed as porcelain may have water absorption above 0.5%, which technically classifies them as "stoneware" or "fine stoneware" rather than porcelain under SANS 1449. If the water absorption exceeds 0.5%, the tiles do not meet the "porcelain" specification and must be replaced. Even if absorption is below 0.5%, if the contract specified "white body" and "red body" was delivered without approval, it's a breach of contract. Commission independent testing of the installed tiles (cost: R2,000–R3,000 per sample). If they fail the porcelain specification, replace them — the cost of replacement is less than the CPA and reputational risk.

#### Q: 133 A buyer has requested that we install "extra reinforcement" in the floor slab beneath the tiles in their unit because they plan to install a grand piano (400kg concentrated load). Who pays for this — us or the buyer?
**Answer:** Standard residential floor slabs are designed for a distributed live load of 1.5kPa–2.0kPa (approximately 150–200kg/m²). A 400kg grand piano on small castors creates a concentrated load that may exceed the slab's capacity, causing cracking or excessive deflection. If the buyer's request is made before construction, the additional reinforcement (typically a thicker slab or additional reinforcing steel in the piano area) is an alteration to the standard specification. The buyer should pay the additional cost. Document this as a variation order with: (1) the structural engineer's assessment, (2) the reinforcement specification, (3) the cost breakdown, (4) the buyer's written acceptance. If the request comes after slab construction, it's usually impractical to reinforce — the buyer may need a structural engineer to assess whether the existing slab can accommodate the load.

#### Q: 134 The body corporate in our completed scheme wants to install CCTV cameras in the common area corridors. The cameras need to be mounted on the tiled walls. Do they need our permission as the developer?
**Answer:** Once the sectional title register is opened and the body corporate is established, the developer has no ongoing rights over the common property unless specifically reserved in the development agreement. The body corporate can make alterations to common property by special resolution (STSMA Section 5(1)(h)). Mounting CCTV cameras on tiled walls is a minor alteration. They do not need your permission. However, the installation must be done by a competent contractor who does not damage the waterproofing or fire-rating of the wall. If the camera installation damages tiles, the body corporate is responsible for repair. As a courtesy, the body corporate should notify all owners of the camera locations for privacy reasons under POPIA (Protection of Personal Information Act).

#### Q: 135 We're building student accommodation near a university. The target market is students who are notoriously hard on finishes. Can we use commercial-grade tiles (Class 5 PEI) in a residential application, and does this affect our NHBRC warranty?
**Answer:** Yes, you can use Class 5 PEI (Porcelain Enamel Institute) tiles in residential applications — Class 5 is the highest wear rating, designed for heavy commercial traffic (shopping centres, hotel lobbies). Using Class 5 tiles in student accommodation is actually excellent practice — it reduces maintenance and replacement costs over the building's life. The NHBRC warranty is not affected by tile classification — it covers structural defects, not finish wear. The only consideration is cost: Class 5 tiles are 20%–40% more expensive than Class 3 (standard residential). For student accommodation, this premium pays for itself within 3–5 years through reduced maintenance. Specify dark-coloured, matte-finish Class 5 tiles with R10 slip rating — students will appreciate the durability and safety.

#### Q: 136 Our development includes a mosque with prayer hall floors. The Imam requires that the floor tiles be "clean and appropriate for prayer" — meaning no images, no gold, and no animal motifs. Is this a reasonable accommodation under religious freedom laws?
**Answer:** Under Section 15 of the Constitution (freedom of religion) and PEPUDA (promotion of equality), religious accommodation is a protected right. If you are building a mosque for a Muslim community, specifying tiles that comply with Islamic aesthetic principles is not just reasonable — it's necessary. Tiles with geometric patterns, calligraphy (Quranic verses), or plain designs are appropriate. Tiles with human figures, animals, or gold leaf would violate Islamic principles and potentially constitute indirect discrimination if insisted upon. For any religious building, consult the religious authority on appropriate finishes. This applies equally to churches, temples, and synagogues — each has specific requirements that must be respected. Document the religious requirements in the specification to avoid disputes.

#### Q: 137 A purchaser wants to know if our tiles contain "toxic chemicals" because they read online that ceramic tiles emit radiation. How do we respond?
**Answer:** Ceramic and porcelain tiles are made from natural clay and minerals that contain trace amounts of naturally occurring radioactive elements (uranium, thorium, potassium-40) — this is true of all natural stone and clay products. However, the levels are extremely low and well within international safety standards. Tiles do not emit harmful "chemicals" — they are inert after firing at 1,200°C. Provide the buyer with the tile manufacturer's safety data sheet (SDS) and any available radiation test certificates (some European manufacturers provide these). The Health Physics Society states that the radiation exposure from ceramic tiles is negligible compared to background radiation. If the buyer remains concerned, offer an independent radiation test (cost approximately R5,000), but this is almost certainly unnecessary and the buyer should bear the cost.

#### Q: 138 We're building a retirement village and the occupational therapist recommends non-slip tiles with "high visual contrast" at level changes to assist residents with vision impairment. Does SANS 10400-S mandate this?
**Answer:** SANS 10400-S (facilities for persons with disabilities) requires luminance contrast (visual contrast) at critical locations: stair nosing edges, tactile indicators, door frames, and level changes. For retirement villages and care facilities (Class H3 occupancy), the requirements are more stringent than standard residential. The standard specifies a minimum luminance contrast of 30% between the critical surface and adjacent surfaces. For floor level changes, a tile with LRV (Light Reflectance Value) difference of 30 points or more from the surrounding floor meets this requirement. For example, if the main floor tiles have LRV 50, the step edge tiles should have LRV ≤ 20 (dark) or ≥ 80 (light). Many tile manufacturers publish LRV data. If unavailable, a colour consultant can measure it (cost: R3,000–R5,000 for a report). This is a requirement for retirement village approval.

#### Q: 139 Our development has an underground parking basement where the floor tiles are constantly damp. The waterproofing contractor says it's "rising damp" and not covered by their warranty. Is rising damp from the ground covered?
**Answer:** Rising damp (capillary moisture from the water table or subsoil) is a hydrostatic pressure issue, not a waterproofing membrane defect — unless the waterproofing system was explicitly designed to resist hydrostatic pressure and has failed. Standard waterproofing warranties cover water ingress through the membrane due to installation defects or material failure. They do NOT cover water that bypasses the membrane through construction joints, tie holes, or inadequate subsoil drainage. For underground basements, the correct approach is: (1) positive-side waterproofing (external membrane on the outside of the slab and walls), (2) subsoil drainage system (French drains, sump pumps), (3) vapour barrier beneath the screed. If you only have an internal membrane, rising damp will eventually penetrate. The solution is external remediation (digging around the basement) or installing a dehumidification system. Neither is cheap — R500–R1,500/m² for external remediation.

#### Q: 140 A tile manufacturer's representative offered to "certify our installation" if we use their adhesive system, saying this gives us a "10-year system warranty." What does this actually mean?
**Answer:** A "system warranty" from a tile manufacturer means that the manufacturer warrants the performance of their tiles, adhesive, grout, and specified installation method AS A SYSTEM for the warranty period. This is valuable because: (1) if anything fails, there's a single point of accountability — the manufacturer cannot blame the adhesive or the installer, (2) the warranty is typically backed by the manufacturer's product liability insurance, (3) the manufacturer will inspect the installation at key stages and issue a compliance certificate. However, read the fine print: (1) the warranty may only cover material defects, not installation errors by your contractor, (2) the manufacturer may require use of their certified installers, (3) the warranty may be voided by structural movement, incorrect cleaning methods, or failure to maintain expansion joints, (4) claim procedures may require immediate notification and site access. A system warranty typically costs 5%–10% more than buying components separately. For large developments, it's worth it.

#### Q: 141 We're developing a floating apartment building (houseboat marina) in the Vaal River. Do SANS 10400 building regulations apply, and what tile adhesive works on a constantly moving substrate?
**Answer:** SANS 10400 applies to "buildings" defined as "any structure," which includes houseboats and floating structures used for residential purposes. The local municipality (Emfuleni or Metsimaholo, depending on the Vaal location) will require building plan approval. For a constantly flexing substrate, standard cement-based adhesives will fail. You need: (1) a structural engineer to calculate the maximum deflection and movement at the floor level, (2) a marine-grade flexible adhesive (polyurethane or epoxy-based, classified as R2 or S2), (3) a decoupling membrane between the structural deck and the tile bed to absorb differential movement, (4) silicone-filled expansion joints at maximum 2m intervals in both directions. Marine environments also require stainless steel or brass tile trim — aluminium will corrode rapidly. Budget 40%–60% more for marine-grade installation systems compared to standard residential.

#### Q: 142 Our development borders a nature reserve, and the environmental management plan prohibits any light pollution that could affect nocturnal animals. Do polished tiles contribute to light pollution, and do we need to use matte finishes only?
**Answer:** Polished tiles have a high gloss factor (90+ GU) and can reflect artificial light, potentially contributing to light pollution if used on external surfaces. However, light pollution from floor tiles is negligible compared to direct light sources (floodlights, security lighting). The environmental management plan likely focuses on upward light spill from fixtures, not floor reflectance. That said, if external walkways are illuminated at night, polished tiles will create glare that disorients nocturnal animals. Specify matte or textured external tiles (LRV 30–50, gloss factor below 15 GU) for any areas visible from the nature reserve. Internal polished tiles are not an environmental issue. Consult the environmental management plan's specific light pollution clauses — they typically specify lumens and fixture direction, not floor finishes.

#### Q: 143 A buyer wants to pay for a tile upgrade directly to the tiling contractor (cash) to avoid paying our markup. The contractor has agreed. Should we allow this?
**Answer:** No. Allowing a buyer to contract directly with your subcontractor creates multiple problems: (1) the subcontractor's priority shifts to the paying customer rather than the developer, (2) quality control becomes impossible — the upgraded tiles may not meet the project's overall aesthetic, (3) the NHBRC warranty may be compromised if non-approved materials are used, (4) your insurance may not cover defects arising from unsupervised work, (5) the buyer may later claim against you if the direct-contract work is defective, (6) the contractor may neglect your standard units to focus on the lucrative upgrade. All upgrades should go through you as the developer. Charge a reasonable administration fee (10%–15% of upgrade cost) for processing the variation. Include a formal variation order process in your sale agreements.

#### Q: 144 We're building a gated estate and the HOA wants to mandate that all driveways use the same tile pattern to maintain "architectural harmony." Can an HOA actually enforce tile colour rules on private property?
**Answer:** Yes, if the architectural guidelines are registered as a condition in the title deeds of all properties within the estate. Title deed conditions are enforceable against all subsequent owners. The HOA can enforce through: (1) written notice requiring compliance, (2) fines for non-compliance (if the HOA rules provide for this), (3) court interdict for removal of non-compliant finishes, (4) in some cases, the HOA can perform the work and recover costs from the owner. However, the restrictions must be reasonable and must have been disclosed to the buyer at the time of purchase. As developer, you must ensure all buyers receive and acknowledge the architectural guidelines before transfer. Unreasonable restrictions (e.g., mandating a specific supplier at inflated prices) may be challengeable, but aesthetic uniformity rules have been upheld by South African courts.

#### Q: 145 A tile batch we received has a subtle but visible pattern misalignment — the veining on the porcelain tiles doesn't flow consistently. The manufacturer says this is "within tolerance for a natural stone look." The buyer says it's unacceptable. Who is right?
**Answer:** For tiles marketed as "natural stone look" or "marble effect," variation in veining and pattern is expected and desirable — it mimics natural stone, which is inherently variable. However, the tiles should still achieve a harmonious overall appearance when laid. SANS 1449 and ISO 10545-16 address shade variation but not pattern alignment. The key is the manufacturer's published specification: if they state "tiles are designed for random laying and pattern variation is a feature," the buyer's claim is weak. If the manufacturer markets the tiles as "book-matched" or "consistent veining," they must deliver that. Best practice: require the tiling contractor to dry-lay a representative area (2m x 2m minimum) before fixing, and obtain the buyer's approval of the layout. This manages expectations and prevents disputes.

#### Q: 146 Our project manager authorised the tile layer to work overtime on a Saturday to catch up on the programme. The overtime rate is double time. The tile layer's weekly earnings now exceed the BCEA overtime threshold. Are we in breach of the Basic Conditions of Employment Act?
**Answer:** The BCEA (Basic Conditions of Employment Act) limits overtime to 10 hours per week and requires payment at 1.5x the normal rate (or double time on Sundays/public holidays). There is no "overtime threshold" that prohibits payment — the threshold determines whether the employee is covered by certain BCEA provisions. If the tile layer is a "commission earner" or earns above the BCEA earnings threshold (currently R224,080 per annum as of 2024), some BCEA provisions (like overtime limits) do not apply. However, if they are an ordinary employee, the 10-hour weekly overtime limit applies regardless of earnings. Exceeding this limit without a specific exemption from the Department of Labour is a breach. The exemption process takes 2–4 weeks and requires justification. Plan your programme to avoid unplanned overtime.

#### Q: 147 We have a unit where the floor slope is 1:120 (slightly steeper than the 1:150 ideal for tiling). The tile layer says the tiles will "slide" during adhesive curing. Is this a real risk, and what's the solution?
**Answer:** A 1:120 slope (approximately 0.48°) is not steep enough to cause tile sliding with any normal adhesive system. Even a 1:50 slope (1.15°) can be tiled with standard C2 adhesive without sliding. The tile layer's concern suggests they are unfamiliar with sloped installations or are using an adhesive with poor initial grab (open time too long). The solution: (1) use a C2TES1 adhesive (improved adhesive with deformable and non-slip properties) — the "T" classification includes thixotropic properties that prevent sagging on walls and slopes, (2) work in smaller sections (2–3m² at a time) so the adhesive doesn't skin over before the tile is placed, (3) use a contact adhesive system (two-coat method) for very steep slopes. A 1:120 slope is standard for wet area falls — any competent tiler should handle it without issue. If they can't, find a different tiler.

#### Q: 148 A buyer has threatened to post a video on social media showing cracked tiles in their unit "unless we fix it immediately." The unit is past the defects liability period, and the cracks are from the owner dragging furniture. Is this extortion, and how do we respond?
**Answer:** Threatening to publish damaging information unless a demand is met can constitute extortion under the Prevention and Combating of Corrupt Activities Act (PRECCA) if the threat is accompanied by an unlawful demand. However, a consumer posting a legitimate complaint about perceived defects (even if they are wrong about the cause) is protected consumer speech under the CPA. The line is crossed if the buyer explicitly demands payment or benefits "or else" the video goes viral. Your response: (1) do not be pressured into unwarranted repairs by social media threats, (2) document your assessment (cracks caused by furniture impact, not defective workmanship), (3) offer a goodwill inspection by an independent expert at your cost, (4) if the threat is explicit extortion, report it to SAPS, (5) engage a defamation attorney if the published statements are false and damaging. Consider a written response setting out the factual basis for your position. Social media shakedowns are increasingly common — stand your ground if the facts support you.

#### Q: 149 Our development is in a complex with mixed-use zoning (retail on ground floor, residential above). The retail tenant wants to install heavy display shelving on the tiled floor. Do we need to reinforce the slab, and does the tenant need our permission?
**Answer:** The retail tenant's right to install fixtures depends on their lease agreement. Typically, the tenant needs landlord consent for any fixture that affects the structure or permanently alters the premises. Heavy display shelving (concentrated loads) may exceed the standard retail floor loading of 3.0–5.0kPa. You need a structural engineer to assess: (1) the weight of the shelving plus maximum stock loading, (2) the load distribution (point loads from shelving legs are more critical than distributed loads), (3) whether the existing slab can accommodate the load. If reinforcement is needed, the tenant typically pays for it (subject to lease negotiation). The tiled floor finish may need to be lifted to install reinforcement plates or additional support — this is at the tenant's cost with landlord approval. Always require a structural assessment before granting consent for heavy retail fixtures.

#### Q: 150 We're building 30km from the nearest town and the municipal building inspector refuses to travel to our site for inspections. We've completed the building and the tiles are installed. How do we get an Occupation Certificate?
**Answer:** Municipalities are legally obligated to provide building control services within their jurisdiction, regardless of location. If the inspector refuses to travel, escalate to: (1) the Municipal Manager, (2) the MEC for Local Government in the province, (3) the National Department of Public Works and Infrastructure. In practice, for remote sites, you can: (1) engage a private building surveyor registered with SACPCMP to conduct inspections and issue a compliance certificate, (2) request the municipality to appoint the private surveyor as an "authorised person" under the Building Control Act, (3) in some provinces (Western Cape, Gauteng), accredited private building control officers can issue compliance certificates directly. The NHBRC can also conduct inspections independently. Document all requests to the municipality — if they fail to inspect within a reasonable time, their refusal may be challengeable in court as administrative failure.
### SECTION X: EVEN MORE OBSCURE DEVELOPER QUESTIONS

#### Q: 151 Our tiling contractor wants to use a "rapid-set" adhesive that allows grouting after 2 hours instead of the standard 24 hours. This would save 3 days on the programme. Is there any risk?
**Answer:** Rapid-set adhesives (classified as F — fast-setting under EN 12004) achieve initial set in 2–3 hours and can be grouted within 3–6 hours under ideal conditions. The risk is that rapid-set adhesives have a shorter open time (the time between spreading and placing the tile), typically 10–15 minutes compared to 20–30 minutes for standard adhesives. In hot or windy conditions, the adhesive may skin over before the tile is placed, resulting in poor adhesion. They also have higher cement content, which increases shrinkage stress. Rapid-set is excellent for time-critical projects IF: (1) the tiler is experienced with rapid-set products, (2) ambient temperature is between 15°C and 25°C, (3) batch sizes are small, (4) the substrate has been properly primed. For a 3-day programme saving, the risk is probably worth it — but do a trial area first.

#### Q: 152 A purchaser claims the tiles in their unit "resonate" when they walk on them, creating an echoing sound. They want the entire floor replaced. Is "resonance" a recognised tile defect?
**Answer:** "Resonance" or hollow-sounding tiles is not a defect per se — it's an acoustic phenomenon caused by a partial void between the tile and the substrate. If the tile is adequately bonded (adhesion strength above 0.5 MPa as per SANS 10107), a slight hollow sound is not a defect. However, if the void exceeds 10% of the tile area or the adhesion strength is below 0.5 MPa, it's a debonding defect that must be repaired. Commission a pull-off test (destructive test on a spare tile) to measure adhesion strength. If the adhesion meets the standard, the "resonance" is a characteristic of the installation system and not a defect. Explain to the buyer that the tile is securely bonded and the sound is cosmetic. Offer to inject epoxy resin into any significant voids as a goodwill gesture. Full replacement is not warranted.

#### Q: 153 We have a heritage-protected building in the Cape Town CBD where the National Monuments Council requires us to retain the original 1920s floor tiles. But the tiles are cracked and loose. Can we replace them with replicas?
**Answer:** Under the National Heritage Resources Act, any alteration to a heritage-protected building requires a permit from Heritage Western Cape (or the relevant Amafa/Heritage agency). If the original tiles are damaged beyond repair, you can apply for a permit to remove and replace them. The application must include: (1) a condition assessment by a heritage consultant, (2) photographic documentation of every tile, (3) a sample of the original tile for matching, (4) details of the proposed replacement tile (reproduction cement tiles are available from specialist manufacturers in Cape Town and Durban), (5) methodology for removal and reinstallation. Heritage authorities generally prefer repair over replacement. If 70%+ of the tiles can be salvaged and re-laid, they will likely require this. Budget R2,000–R4,000/m² for heritage tile restoration versus R300–R500/m² for standard replacement. Unauthorised removal of heritage tiles is a criminal offence.

#### Q: 154 Our development's basement parking has epoxy floor paint, but the body corporate now wants to tile it with porcelain for "a premium look." The basement has no natural ventilation. Is this safe?
**Answer:** Tiling a basement parking area is technically possible but raises several concerns: (1) exhaust fumes from vehicles contain carbon monoxide, nitrogen oxides, and hydrocarbons — a tiled floor does not absorb these fumes the way an epoxy or concrete floor might (though this is minor), (2) tiled surfaces are more slippery than textured epoxy when wet (oil, water, coolant spills) — specify R11 or R12 slip-rated textured tiles, (3) tyre marks are harder to remove from textured tiles than from smooth epoxy, (4) the additional dead load of tiles plus screed (approximately 40–50kg/m²) must be checked against the suspended slab capacity, (5) cost: tiling a basement costs R350–R600/m² versus R80–R150/m² for epoxy.
