For a premium look, consider polished concrete or a high-build epoxy system with decorative flake — it achieves the aesthetic without the slip and maintenance issues of tiles in a parking environment.

#### Q: 155 A tile supplier has offered us "factory seconds" at 40% discount for use in the service areas (plant rooms, storage). The tiles have minor cosmetic defects. Is this allowed under SANS 10400?
**Answer:** SANS 10400 does not prohibit the use of second-grade tiles in non-habitable areas. Factory seconds are fit for purpose in plant rooms, storage areas, and service corridors where aesthetics are not a priority. However: (1) the tiles must still meet structural requirements (breaking strength, thickness), (2) they must be suitable for the intended loading (floor tiles, not wall tiles, in floor applications), (3) if the area is accessible to the public or building users, the slip rating must still comply with SANS 10107, (4) do NOT use seconds in any area covered by the NHBRC warranty for the habitable portion of the dwelling — the warranty requires "workmanlike finish" in all areas. Clearly segregate the seconds and mark them for specific non-critical areas. Never use seconds in areas visible to purchasers or in any wet area where water resistance is critical.

#### Q: 156 We're building in an eco-estate where the environmental rules prohibit any cement-based products because cement production has a high carbon footprint. Can we install tiles without cement adhesive?
**Answer:** Cement-free tile installation is possible but challenging. Alternatives to cement-based adhesive include: (1) epoxy adhesives (100% solids, no cement, extremely strong and chemical-resistant, but expensive at R60–R120/m² material cost), (2) polyurethane adhesives (flexible, good for timber or metal substrates, R50–R80/m²), (3) silicone-based adhesives (for lightweight applications only, not for floor tiles), (4) mechanical fixing systems (clips and rails, used in industrial applications). For a residential eco-estate, epoxy adhesive is the most viable cement-free option, but it requires specialist application (trained installers, limited open time, temperature-sensitive). The carbon footprint argument against cement is questionable when applied to small-scale construction — the cement in tile adhesive for a single home is approximately 200–300kg, which is negligible compared to structural concrete. Challenge the rule on practical grounds or seek an exemption for tile adhesive specifically.

#### Q: 157 A buyer wants to install a jacuzzi on their balcony. The balcony floor is tiled with standard porcelain. The jacuzzi weighs 1,200kg when full. The balcony was designed for 2.0kPa live load. Can the balcony take the weight?
**Answer:** A 1,200kg jacuzzi on a typical 6m² balcony creates a distributed load of approximately 2.0kPa, which meets the design live load — BUT this assumes the load is evenly distributed. A jacuzzi concentrates the load at the base points (four or six contact points), creating point loads that may exceed the slab's punching shear capacity. Additionally, the dynamic load from water movement adds approximately 20%–30% to the static weight. You need a structural engineer to assess: (1) the exact balcony slab dimensions and reinforcement, (2) the jacuzzi's contact point loads, (3) whether a load-distribution pad is needed beneath the jacuzzi base. Many balcony slabs are cantilevered (only supported from one side), which further reduces their capacity. The tiles are irrelevant to the structural question — they will crack if the slab fails. Do not approve any balcony jacuzzi installation without a structural engineer's sign-off.

#### Q: 158 Our development has a central courtyard with a water feature. The water feature's edge is tiled, but children from the estate keep climbing on it. A child fell and the parents claim the tiled edge is "too slippery." Are we liable if the water feature wasn't designed as a play area?
**Answer:** Liability depends on whether the water feature constitutes an "attractive nuisance" and whether reasonable safety measures were in place. Under South African common law (delict), an occupier of premises owes a duty of care to lawful visitors, including children. Even if the water feature was not designed as a play area, if it is accessible to children and presents a foreseeable risk of injury, you may be liable. Defence factors: (1) the tiles meet the applicable slip rating for the application (R10 minimum for pool surrounds), (2) signage was posted warning against climbing, (3) the water feature design included safety features (shallow depth, rounded edges), (4) the parents were negligent in supervising the child. To mitigate risk: ensure pool surround tiles achieve R11 slip rating, install child-resistant barriers if the water depth exceeds 300mm, and post visible warning signage. Consider adding textured anti-slip strips to the edge.

#### Q: 159 The tiling subcontractor's insurance lapsed mid-project. We only discovered this after a tile fell and damaged a car in the basement. Our insurance covers the car damage, but can we claim the excess back from the subcontractor?
**Answer:** If the subcontractor's public liability insurance lapsed due to their failure to pay premiums, they are personally liable for any damages caused by their negligence. However, collecting from an uninsured subcontractor is often difficult — they may not have sufficient assets. Your contract with the subcontractor should include: (1) a requirement to maintain public liability insurance throughout the project, (2) a clause making them liable for all claims arising from their work, (3) a right to deduct damages from payments due, (4) a personal suretyship from the subcontractor's directors for contractual obligations. For this incident: (1) claim from your own insurer (they will likely subrogate against the subcontractor), (2) deduct the excess and any other costs from the subcontractor's final account, (3) do not allow the subcontractor back on site until they provide proof of reinstated insurance. Always verify subcontractor insurance monthly — don't rely on the initial certificate.

#### Q: 160 We have a dispute with a tile supplier over batch shade variation. The contract says disputes are resolved by "expert determination." How does this work, and who pays the expert?
**Answer:** Expert determination is a form of alternative dispute resolution where an independent expert (typically a senior quantity surveyor, architect, or tile technologist) is appointed to decide the dispute based on their expertise. The process is: (1) the parties jointly appoint an expert or, if they cannot agree, each nominates one and the two nominees appoint a third, (2) the expert reviews evidence (tile samples, specifications, SANS standards), (3) the expert issues a binding or non-binding determination (depending on the contract clause), (4) the determination is typically final unless there was procedural unfairness or the expert acted outside their mandate. Cost: the expert's fees (R3,000–R8,000 per day) are usually shared equally by the parties, unless the expert orders otherwise. Expert determination is faster (4–8 weeks) and cheaper than arbitration or litigation. For technical disputes like shade variation, it's highly effective.

#### Q: 161 Our development has units with "voids" (double-volume spaces). The tile layer wants to charge a height premium for tiling the walls above 3m. Is this standard practice?
**Answer:** Tiling at heights above 3m requires scaffolding, scaffolding erection costs, and additional safety measures (harnesses, edge protection). A height premium is standard industry practice. The premium typically kicks in at 3.0m (standard working height) and increases at 4.5m and 6.0m. Premiums range from 15% above 3.0m to 50% above 6.0m, reflecting the increased labour cost, equipment hire, and risk. Scaffolding hire for interior work costs R30–R60/m² per week, and the tile layer needs to include erection and dismantling time. If the wall tiling above 3m is purely decorative (a feature wall in a double-volume living room), consider whether it's necessary — painted plaster above 3m achieves a similar aesthetic at much lower cost. If the tiles are required for acoustic or fire-rating purposes, the premium is unavoidable.

#### Q: 162 A buyer wants us to install tiles with underfloor heating and specify that the system must be "smartphone controlled." Does smart thermostatic control affect the tile installation warranty in any way?
**Answer:** The smartphone control is a thermostat feature and does not affect the tile installation warranty. The warranty covers adhesion, grout integrity, and tile performance — not the control system. However, you should specify that the underfloor heating system (including thermostat) is warranted by the UFH manufacturer/installer, not by the tiling contractor or developer. The tiling warranty remains valid provided: (1) the UFH system is installed according to the manufacturer's guidelines, (2) the maximum floor surface temperature does not exceed 27°C for continuous heating or 29°C for intermittent heating, (3) the heating is brought online gradually (5°C per day increase), (4) the tiles are suitable for UFH application (most porcelain is). Include a clause in the sale agreement stating that smart home systems are provided by a third party and are subject to the third party's warranty terms.

#### Q: 163 Our estate has a communal swimming pool. The body corporate wants to replace the existing mosaic tiles with large-format porcelain. The pool contractor says large-format tiles will "pop off" because they don't accommodate the pool's structural movement. Is this correct?
**Answer:** Swimming pools experience significant structural movement due to: (1) hydrostatic pressure changes (full vs empty), (2) thermal expansion of the water and surrounding structure, (3) ground movement. Traditional pool tiles are small-format mosaics (25mm x 25mm or 48mm x 48mm) with numerous grout joints that accommodate movement. Large-format tiles (300mm x 300mm or larger) have fewer joints and less flexibility — they are more prone to debonding in a pool environment. While it is possible to use large-format tiles in a pool with a specialised flexible adhesive and waterproofing system, it requires: (1) a highly flexible adhesive (S2 classification), (2) a crack-isolation membrane between the pool shell and the tile bed, (3) movement joints at maximum 3m intervals. Even with these measures, large-format pool tiles have a higher failure rate than mosaics. Most pool specialists recommend against it — stick with mosaics for pools.

#### Q: 164 We're developing in a "smart city" precinct where the municipality requires all buildings to have IoT sensors embedded in the floor slab. Can these sensors be installed beneath tiles, and will they affect the tile warranty?
**Answer:** IoT sensors (temperature, occupancy, footfall counters) can be embedded in the floor screed beneath tiles. The installation process involves: (1) casting the sensor into the concrete slab or embedding it in the screed layer, (2) ensuring the sensor surface is flush with the screed top, (3) tiling over the sensor location with standard adhesive. The sensor itself does not affect the tile warranty provided: (1) the screed thickness is maintained (no depressions where the sensor sits), (2) the sensor wiring is properly protected in conduit, (3) the sensor location is marked for future access. If the sensor fails and needs replacement, the tile above it must be lifted. Plan for this: install the sensor at a tile joint (not in the centre of a tile) if possible, or use a "sacrificial" tile that can be replaced. Coordinate with the IoT system provider and the tiling contractor to ensure compatibility.

#### Q: 165 A tile manufacturer has offered us a "buy-back guarantee" — they'll buy back any unused tiles at 80% of the purchase price. Is this a genuine offer, or are there hidden conditions?
**Answer:** Buy-back guarantees from tile manufacturers are genuine but typically have significant conditions: (1) the tiles must be in original, unopened packaging in saleable condition, (2) the buy-back must be exercised within a specific period (typically 6–12 months from purchase), (3) there may be a minimum quantity threshold (e.g., only full pallets), (4) transport costs to return the tiles are usually the developer's responsibility, (5) discontinued lines may be excluded, (6) the manufacturer may require exclusive supply of the development as a condition. Calculate the net benefit: if you return R200,000 worth of tiles at 80%, you receive R160,000 minus transport costs (R5,000–R15,000). The R40,000 loss is your cost of over-ordering insurance. For large developments where tile quantity estimation is uncertain, a buy-back guarantee is valuable. Read the fine print and ensure the conditions are achievable.

#### Q: 166 Our development's specifications require "calibrated" tiles. The supplier delivered "rectified" tiles, claiming they are the same thing. Are they?
**Answer:** No, they are different. "Calibrated" tiles have been sorted into batches with consistent dimensions — each batch has tiles of the same size, but different batches may vary slightly. "Rectified" tiles have been mechanically cut after firing to precise, uniform dimensions (typically ±0.2mm variation). Rectified tiles are more precise and allow narrower grout joints (1.5mm). Calibrated tiles still require standard grout joints (3mm–5mm) because of the remaining size variation. If your specification calls for calibrated tiles and the supplier delivers rectified, you've actually received an upgrade — but if the reverse happens (spec says rectified, you get calibrated), the tiles will not achieve the designed joint width and the finished floor may have lippage. Always verify that the delivered product matches the specification exactly. Rectified tiles cost 10%–20% more than calibrated.

#### Q: 167 A buyer has installed a home gym on their tiled floor with heavy free weights. The tiles are cracking under the weight. They claim the tiles are "not fit for purpose." Are we liable?
**Answer:** Standard residential floor tiles are designed for domestic foot traffic (PEI Class 3 or 4, corresponding to approximately 500–1,500 Newtons point load). Heavy free weights (20kg+ dumbbells) dropped from height create point loads exceeding 3,000 Newtons, which can crack standard tiles. The SANS 1449 classification system does not rate tiles for gym use — commercial gym floors use rubber matting or specialised high-impact flooring. Your tiles were fit for the intended residential purpose; the buyer's use (home gym with dropped weights) is outside the design parameters. You are not liable unless the sales contract specifically warranted the tiles for "any use including heavy impact exercise" (which would be unusual). Offer the buyer guidance on installing rubber gym matting over the tiles to protect the floor — this is the standard solution for home gyms.

#### Q: 168 Our development includes a wine cellar in the basement. The interior designer wants to install natural stone tiles. The wine cellar will be kept at 12°C with 70% humidity. Will stone tiles survive these conditions?
**Answer:** Natural stone tiles (travertine, marble, limestone) in a wine cellar at 12°C and 70% humidity face two challenges: (1) high humidity promotes mould and mildew growth on organic residues in the stone, (2) some stones (particularly green marbles and certain limestones) contain minerals that react with moisture and can cause surface spalling or discolouration. If the stone is properly sealed with a penetrating sealer (reapplied every 2–3 years) and the cellar has adequate ventilation, these issues are manageable. Better alternatives for wine cellars: (1) porcelain tiles with stone-look finish (impervious to moisture, no sealing required), (2) clay brick pavers (traditional, breathable, no condensation issues), (3) sealed concrete. If natural stone is insisted upon, specify a dense, low-porosity stone (granite or quartzite) rather than porous travertine or limestone. The stone must be installed with a waterproof adhesive and grout.

#### Q: 169 The body corporate wants to install electric fencing on top of the perimeter wall. The wall is capped with decorative tiles. Will the electric fence installation damage the tiles?
**Answer:** Electric fence installation on a tiled wall cap requires drilling through the tiles to mount the fence brackets. This creates: (1) tile drilling points that must be sealed to prevent water ingress, (2) risk of tile cracking during drilling (especially if the tiles are porcelain or natural stone), (3) potential compromise of the wall's waterproofing capping. The installation should be done by a contractor experienced with tiled surfaces: (1) use diamond-tipped drill bits for clean holes, (2) apply epoxy sealant around each bracket fixing, (3) install a continuous mounting plate rather than individual brackets to reduce the number of penetrations, (4) ensure the electric fence installer has public liability insurance that covers tile damage. The body corporate should obtain three quotes and select the contractor with the least invasive mounting system. Wall cap tiles will likely need replacement around the drill points over time.

#### Q: 170 We're building a recording studio as part of a mixed-use development. The acoustic consultant says the floor must be "floating" with an air gap. Can we still tile a floating floor?
**Answer:** Yes, but it requires a specialised system. A floating floor is isolated from the structural slab by resilient mounts or a continuous resilient layer (rubber or spring isolators). The floating floor typically consists of: (1) structural steel or timber frame, (2) acoustic insulation within the cavity, (3) plywood or cement-board topping, (4) tile adhesive and tiles on top. The total construction depth is 150mm–300mm. Critical considerations: (1) the floating floor must not contact the surrounding walls (isolation gap of 25mm minimum filled with acoustic sealant), (2) the additional dead load of tiles (20–25kg/m²) must be included in the floating floor structural design, (3) services (electrical, plumbing) must pass through the isolation gap with flexible connections, (4) the tile adhesive must be flexible (S1 classification) to accommodate any residual movement. This is a specialist installation — engage an acoustic flooring contractor. Budget R3,000–R6,000/m² for a complete floating floor with tile finish.
### SECTION Y: FINAL BATCH — THE MOST OBSCURE EDGE CASES

#### Q: 171 Our development has a "show day" every Sunday where potential buyers view the show house. One Sunday, a viewer dropped a bottle of wine on the tiled floor, staining the grout. Who pays for the cleaning — us or the estate agent managing the show house?
**Answer:** The cost of preparing and maintaining the show house is a marketing expense borne by the developer unless the estate agent's mandate specifically includes cleaning and maintenance. A wine stain on grout is typically removable with an alkaline cleaner or hydrogen peroxide solution — it's a minor cleaning cost (R200–R500 for a professional clean). However, if the stain is permanent and the grout needs replacement, the cost increases to R2,000–R5,000. For regular show days, include a "show house maintenance" budget in your marketing plan (R3,000–R8,000/month for cleaning, consumables, and minor repairs). The estate agent should supervise visitors and enforce a "no food or drink" policy in finished areas. If the agent was negligent in supervision, they may share the cost, but pursuing this for a minor stain is not worth the relationship damage.

#### Q: 172 We have a penthouse unit with a rooftop terrace. The waterproofing membrane is under the tiles. A satellite dish installer drilled through the tiles and membrane to mount a dish, causing a leak. The purchaser says we must fix it because the waterproofing is under warranty. Are we responsible for third-party damage?
**Answer:** The waterproofing warranty covers defects in the original installation, not damage caused by subsequent alterations by the owner or their contractors. If the satellite dish installer (engaged by the purchaser) drilled through the membrane, this is third-party damage and is not covered by the waterproofing warranty. The purchaser must: (1) claim from the satellite installer's insurance, (2) pay for the membrane repair themselves, or (3) claim from their homeowner's insurance. As developer, your obligation is limited to providing waterproofing that was defect-free at the time of handover. You should, however, provide the purchaser with the waterproofing system details and membrane location so they (and their contractors) know where not to drill. Consider including a "membrane location plan" in the handover documentation.

#### Q: 173 Our development's basement has a vehicular ramp with a 1:8 slope. The architect specified polished porcelain tiles. The building inspector failed the ramp because of slip resistance. What slip rating is required for vehicle ramps?
**Answer:** Vehicular ramps (garage entrances, basement ramps) require a minimum R12 slip rating (pendulum wet value ≥ 55) under SANS 10107, and some insurers require R13 for commercial ramps. Polished porcelain achieves R9 at best — it is completely unsuitable for any sloped surface exposed to water or oil. The architect's specification is non-compliant and dangerous. Suitable alternatives: (1) textured concrete with carborundum strips, (2) R12 or R13 rated quarry tiles or paver tiles, (3) epoxy systems with anti-slip aggregate broadcast, (4) aluminium or brass stair nosing with anti-slip inserts at the ramp entrance. Reject the architect's specification and substitute a compliant finish. If the ramp is already tiled with polished porcelain, it must be replaced — there is no coating or treatment that reliably upgrades R9 to R12 on a long-term basis.

#### Q: 174 A purchaser in our sectional title scheme is a "prepper" who has stockpiled 5,000 litres of water in their unit (in tanks on the tiled floor). The body corporate says this exceeds the floor loading and is a safety risk. Who is right?
**Answer:** 5,000 litres of water weighs 5,000kg. On a typical 15m² tiled floor area, this creates a distributed load of 3.3kPa — which exceeds the standard residential floor design load of 1.5kPa and may exceed the slab capacity, especially if the tanks create point loads through their support legs. The body corporate is correct to be concerned. Under STSMA Section 13(1)(a), an owner must use their section in a manner that does not cause a nuisance or damage to common property. If the water tanks damage the floor or create a structural risk, the body corporate can issue a compliance notice. The owner should either: (1) distribute the tanks across multiple support points to reduce point loads, (2) place them on a load-spreading platform, (3) relocate them to the garage (typically designed for higher loads), or (4) reduce the quantity. A structural engineer's assessment will determine the actual risk.

#### Q: 175 We installed underfloor heating beneath tiles in all units. A buyer read online that electromagnetic fields (EMF) from heating cables are "dangerous" and wants the system removed at our cost. Is there any scientific basis for this claim?
**Answer:** No. Underfloor heating cables produce extremely low-frequency electromagnetic fields (ELF-EMF) at levels well below international safety guidelines (ICNIRP standards). The EMF from underfloor heating is typically less than 0.3 microtesla at floor level, which is lower than the Earth's natural magnetic field (25–65 microtesla) and far below the ICNIRP exposure limit of 100 microtesla for the general public. The World Health Organisation states there is no convincing scientific evidence that low-level ELF-EMF causes health effects. Provide the buyer with the UFH manufacturer's EMF test certificate and the WHO fact sheet on electromagnetic fields. If the buyer persists, offer an independent EMF measurement (cost: R2,000–R3,500) — the results will almost certainly show compliance. Removing the UFH system is not a reasonable request and you should not accommodate it unless a genuine hazard is proven.

#### Q: 176 Our estate has a rule that all exterior tiles must be "cool roof" rated with minimum Solar Reflectance Index (SRI) of 29. Our specified tiles have SRI 20. What is SRI, and how do we comply?
**Answer:** Solar Reflectance Index (SRI) measures a surface's ability to reflect solar heat — higher SRI means cooler surface temperatures. SANS 10400-XA energy efficiency regulations recommend SRI 29 or higher for roof surfaces in hot climates. Some progressive estates have extended this to external floor and wall tiles to reduce the urban heat island effect. To comply: (1) specify light-coloured tiles (white, beige, light grey) which naturally have higher SRI, (2) some manufacturers produce "cool pigment" tiles that achieve higher reflectance without being white, (3) request the tile manufacturer's SRI test data — most don't test for this, but you can have it independently tested (cost: R8,000–R15,000). If your specified tiles genuinely cannot achieve SRI 29, apply to the estate's architectural review committee for a variance with supporting thermal analysis. For courtyard and walkway tiles, the SRI requirement is less critical than for roof tiles — argue for a lower threshold.

#### Q: 177 A tile installer on site was caught stealing tiles and selling them privately. We dismissed him and withheld his final payment. He's threatening to report us to the Department of Labour for "unfair dismissal." Can he do this if he's an independent contractor?
**Answer:** If the tile installer is genuinely an independent contractor (registered business, provides own tools and equipment, controls how the work is done, bears financial risk), he has no recourse under the Labour Relations Act for unfair dismissal — the LRA protects employees, not independent contractors. His remedy for withheld payment is a civil claim (Magistrate's Court) for breach of contract. However, if he was in fact a de facto employee (you controlled his hours, provided materials, supervised his work), he can refer an unfair dismissal dispute to the CCMA. To protect yourself: (1) document the theft with evidence (CCTV, witness statements, recovered tiles), (2) report the theft to SAPS, (3) withhold payment in terms of the contract's set-off clause, (4) if he was a contractor, counterclaim for damages. The threat of Labour Department reporting is a bluff if he is truly independent. Ensure your subcontract agreements clearly define the independent contractor relationship.

#### Q: 178 Our development includes a synagogue. The rabbi requires a "mikvah" (ritual bath) with specific tiling requirements — all-white tiles, no grout lines visible, and absolutely no leakage. What installation system achieves this?
**Answer:** A mikvah has strict religious requirements: (1) the water must be "living water" (rainwater or spring water) mixed with drawn water in a specific ratio, (2) the pool must hold water without leakage (the water level must not drop), (3) all surfaces must be smooth and white (no visible joints preferred). To achieve this: (1) use large-format white porcelain tiles (minimum 600x600mm) to minimise grout joints, (2) use white epoxy grout (colour-matched to the tile, does not stain), (3) install a full tanking system (epoxy or cementitious waterproofing) beneath the tiles with 100% coverage, (4) seal all grout joints with a clear epoxy sealant, (5) pressure-test the pool before tiling (fill and monitor for 7 days), (6) ensure the pool shell is structurally independent from the building to prevent differential movement. Budget R4,000–R8,000/m² for a mikvah installation — it's significantly more expensive than a standard pool due to the religious and technical requirements.

#### Q: 179 We have a dispute where the tile supplier delivered 2,000m² of tiles but the delivery note was signed by our security guard (who can't read English) and the quantity was never verified. We later discovered we were short 300m². Who bears the loss?
**Answer:** The signature of your security guard on the delivery note creates a presumption that the goods were received as stated. However, if the guard was not authorised to verify quantities (security guards typically only check for visible damage and confirm vehicle exit), you may argue that his signature was for "safe receipt" only, not "quantity verification." Your contract terms with the supplier govern: if the contract requires the buyer to verify quantities within 24 hours and report discrepancies, and you failed to do so, the loss is likely yours. If the supplier's own driver counted the pallets incorrectly, the supplier bears the loss. Best practice: (1) authorise specific site personnel to count and sign for deliveries, (2) require the supplier to provide a packing list with tile counts per pallet, (3) verify pallet counts before signing, (4) photograph each delivery. For this dispute, check if the supplier's driver logs or CCTV footage show fewer pallets than invoiced.

#### Q: 180 Our development's sectional title plan was approved with all floor finishes shown as "tiles." The surveyor now says we must amend the sectional title plan if we change any unit to vinyl flooring. Is this true?
**Answer:** No. The sectional title plan shows the boundaries of each section and the common property — it does not specify floor finishes. The surveyor may be confusing the sectional title plan with the building plans submitted to the municipality. Building plans may show floor finishes as part of the specification, but changing from tiles to vinyl within a unit does not affect the sectional title boundaries or the sectional plan. The body corporate's conduct rules may regulate finish changes in common areas, but not within private units unless the change affects structural elements, common services, or external appearance. If the surveyor insists on a sectional plan amendment, they are incorrect — escalate to the Surveyor-General's office for clarification. A sectional plan amendment is only required for structural alterations that change the boundaries or participation quotas.

#### Q: 181 We're being audited by the CIDB (Construction Industry Development Board) and they've asked for our "tiling subcontractor's CIDB grade." Our tiler is a one-man business with no CIDB registration. Are we in trouble?
**Answer:** CIDB registration is required for contractors undertaking construction works above specified thresholds. For a private residential development (not government-funded), CIDB registration of subcontractors is NOT legally mandatory — the CIDB Act primarily regulates public sector procurement. However, some developers voluntarily require CIDB registration as part of their contractor qualification process. If your development is privately funded, the CIDB audit is likely related to your own registration status or a specific contractual requirement, not a statutory obligation for the tiler. If your development received any government subsidy or was procured by a public entity, then CIDB registration becomes mandatory for all contractors above the threshold. For a one-man tiling subcontractor on a private job, CIDB registration is not required by law. Verify why the CIDB is auditing you — if it's a voluntary audit for your own Grade 9 registration, explain that the subcontractor was below threshold.

#### Q: 182 A tile manufacturer's catalogueshows a tile with a beautiful grey pattern. The actual tiles delivered have a much more pronounced pattern. The manufacturer says "shade variation is normal" and refers to V3 on the box. The buyer hates it. What does V3 mean?
**Answer:** V3 is the shade variation classification printed on tile packaging. The scale is: V1 = uniform appearance (minimal variation), V2 = slight variation, V3 = moderate variation (obvious differences in texture and colour), V4 = substantial variation (each tile looks different). If the catalogue showed a V1 or V2 appearance and the delivered tiles are V3, the manufacturer may have misrepresented the product. However, if the box is clearly marked V3 and the buyer's expectation was set by a single catalogue photograph, the manufacturer has a defence. Best practice for developers: (1) never rely on catalogue images — view a full display board with multiple tiles, (2) specify the exact V-rating in the contract, (3) require the tiler to dry-lay a representative area before fixing and obtain buyer approval. For this dispute, if the contract specified V1 or V2 and V3 was delivered, you have a legitimate claim against the supplier.

#### Q: 183 Our development has an internal courtyard with a glass roof. The interior designer wants to install dark-coloured tiles to create a "dramatic effect." The glass roof creates a greenhouse effect — will dark tiles make the courtyard unbearably hot?
**Answer:** Yes. Dark tiles (LRV < 20) in a glass-roofed courtyard will absorb solar radiation and re-radiate it as heat, significantly increasing the courtyard temperature. In direct sunlight, dark tile surfaces can reach 60°C–70°C, making the space unusable and increasing the air conditioning load for adjacent rooms. Under a glass roof with no UV filtering, the tiles will also be exposed to concentrated UV, which can degrade some sealants and grouts over time. Recommendations: (1) use medium-tone tiles (LRV 40–60) for a balance of aesthetics and thermal comfort, (2) install solar-control glass or a retractable shading system, (3) specify tiles with high solar reflectance (cool pigments), (4) ensure adequate ventilation (extractor fans or operable roof panels). The interior designer should produce a thermal simulation before finalising the specification — aesthetics must not override thermal comfort.

#### Q: 184 We're building a multi-generational estate where some units are designated for retirees and others for young families. Can we use different tile specifications for the "retirement" units versus the "family" units?
**Answer:** Yes, you can use different specifications for different unit types, provided this is disclosed in the sales documentation. In fact, it's good design practice: retirement units benefit from R10 slip-rated tiles (wet slip resistance), higher LRV (lighter tiles for better visibility), and matte finishes (reduced glare). Family units may prioritise durability (Class 4 PEI) and stain resistance over slip rating. However, be careful not to create a perception that retirement units are "lower spec" — frame the differences as "tailored design" rather than cost-cutting. If the retirement units are sold at a lower price point, the different specification is expected and justifiable. Document the rationale for each specification in case of queries. Never use lower-grade tiles in accessible units — SANS 10400-S requires higher performance standards for units designed for persons with disabilities.

#### Q: 185 A buyer has requested that we leave the bathroom floors "untiled" so they can install their own tiles later. The NHBRC requires that wet areas be completed before enrolment. Can we accommodate this request?
**Answer:** The NHBRC requires that wet areas (showers, bathrooms, kitchens) have completed waterproofing before enrolment, but they do not strictly mandate that tiles must be installed — provided the waterproofing membrane is protected and functional. However, an unfinished bathroom floor will not pass a municipal final inspection for an Occupation Certificate — the building must be "completed" as per approved plans. Options: (1) install a temporary protective screed over the waterproofing that the buyer can remove later, (2) install standard tiles with a "buyer upgrade" variation order, (3) explain to the buyer that the bathroom must be finished for OC purposes and offer a credit against the purchase price for the tile cost, allowing them to re-tile later. Option 3 is the most practical — the buyer pays for the standard installation, receives a credit, and can upgrade after transfer. Ensure the waterproofing warranty is not voided by subsequent re-tiling.

#### Q: 186 Our tiling contractor has proposed using a "tile levelling system" (plastic clips and wedges) to achieve perfect lippage-free results. This adds R15/m² to the cost. Is it worth it?
**Answer:** For large-format tiles (600x600mm and above), a tile levelling system (TLS) is highly recommended and often essential. TLS devices hold tiles at a consistent level during adhesive curing, eliminating lippage (height differences between adjacent tiles). The benefits: (1) perfectly flat floor or wall surface, (2) reduced risk of trip hazards, (3) professional aesthetic, (4) faster installation (tiler doesn't need to manually level each tile), (5) reduced tiler fatigue (less bending to check levels). For tiles below 400x400mm, TLS is less critical. At R15/m² on a 100-unit development (approximately 500m² per unit = 50,000m²), the total cost is R750,000. For a luxury development where finish quality is a key selling point, this is money well spent. For affordable housing, manual levelling by a skilled tiler may be sufficient. Specify TLS for all tiles 600x600mm and above.

#### Q: 187 The estate's architectural review committee has rejected our specified tiles because they're "not on the approved finishes schedule." But the finishes schedule was written 10 years ago and most of those tiles are discontinued. What can we do?
**Answer:** An outdated finishes schedule creates a deadlock. The developer (you) or the body corporate can amend the architectural guidelines through the mechanism specified in the estate's constitution — typically a special resolution of the body corporate or developer approval. If you are still the developer and haven't transferred control to the body corporate, you have the authority to update the finishes schedule. If control has transferred: (1) submit a motivation to the architectural review committee for an amendment, (2) provide three alternative tile options that meet the aesthetic intent of the original schedule, (3) propose a formal review of the entire finishes schedule (many estates have this on their agenda annually). A 10-year-old schedule is legally challengeable if it forces the use of discontinued products — this is an unreasonable restriction that may not hold up in court. However, negotiation is always preferable to litigation in HOA matters.

#### Q: 188 We're developing on a site that was previously a petrol station. The environmental consultant has cleared the site for hydrocarbon contamination, but warns that "sealed floor surfaces" (like tiles) can trap residual vapours. Is this a real concern?
**Answer:** Yes. If residual hydrocarbons (petrol, diesel, benzene) remain in the soil beneath the site, they can volatilise (turn to vapour) and migrate upward. A sealed floor surface like tiles with epoxy grout can trap these vapours in the sub-floor space, creating a potential explosion hazard or health risk if vapours find a pathway into the building through service penetrations. The correct approach for former petrol station sites: (1) the environmental consultant must conduct soil vapour assessment, (2) if vapour intrusion is a risk, install a sub-slab depressurisation system (SSDS) — a perforated pipe network beneath the slab that vents vapours to atmosphere, (3) ensure all service penetrations through the slab are sealed with vapour-resistant sealant, (4) the tile installation itself is not the problem — the slab design and sub-slab management is. Budget R200–R400/m² for SSDS installation. Do not proceed with construction until the environmental consultant issues a clean site closure certificate.

#### Q: 189 A buyer wants to install a wood-burning fireplace on their tiled lounge floor. The fireplace weighs 250kg and will sit directly on the tiles. Will the tiles crack from the weight or heat?
**Answer:** A 250kg fireplace creates a distributed load of approximately 2.5kPa if the base is 1m² — this is within the capacity of a standard residential floor slab (1.5–2.0kPa live load plus dead load capacity). However, the heat is the main concern: a wood-burning fireplace can generate floor surface temperatures of 80°C–120°C near the base, which exceeds the thermal tolerance of standard tile adhesive (typically 70°C maximum). The tile may also crack from thermal shock if the heat is concentrated. Solution: (1) install a non-combustible hearth pad (stone, concrete, or steel plate) between the fireplace and the tiles, extending at least 300mm beyond the fireplace base on all sides, (2) use a heat-resistant adhesive (epoxy or specialised high-temperature adhesive) in the fireplace footprint area, (3) ensure the hearth pad is level and distributes the weight evenly. The buyer should engage a fireplace installer who understands hearth requirements — SANS 10400-V (space heating) specifies minimum hearth dimensions.

#### Q: 190 Our development has a communal braai area. The body corporate wants to tile the braai area floor with the same tiles as the pool surround. Is this appropriate given the grease and ash exposure?
**Answer:** A braai area is exposed to grease, ash, fat drips, and high temperatures — none of which are kind to tiles or grout. Grease stains porous grout, ash is alkaline and can etch some natural stones, and dropped hot coals (800°C+) can crack porcelain or ceramic tiles through thermal shock. If the body corporate insists on tiling: (1) use dark-coloured, matte, vitrified porcelain tiles (minimum Class 4 PEI), (2) use epoxy grout (stain-proof, heat-resistant), (3) install a stainless steel or stone drip tray beneath the braai grid to catch fat and coals, (4) seal the tiles with a penetrating sealer rated for high heat, (5) budget for more frequent cleaning and eventual re-grouting. Better alternatives: (1) sealed concrete with saw-cut patterns, (2) clay brick pavers (traditional, forgiving, easily replaced), (3) natural stone like granite or slate (heat-resistant but requires sealing). The pool surround tiles are designed for chlorine and water exposure, not grease and heat — they will deteriorate faster in a braai area.

#### Q: 191 We sold a unit to a foreign buyer who now says they want to export the tiles (remove them and ship them overseas) because they have "sentimental value." The tiles are fixed to the floor. Can they do this?
**Answer:** Once tiles are fixed to the floor, they become "fixtures" and part of the immovable property. Under South African property law, fixtures cannot be removed by the seller or buyer unless specifically excluded in the sale agreement. If the sale agreement did not exclude the tiles, the buyer has no right to remove them. Even if the buyer argues sentimental value, the tiles belong to the property and cannot be unilaterally removed. If the buyer attempts removal, it constitutes damage to the property and you could claim damages. This is an unusual request — most likely the buyer is testing boundaries or has misunderstood something. Politely explain that fixed floor finishes are part of the property and cannot be removed. If they insist, request a formal legal opinion from their attorney — no attorney would support this claim.

#### Q: 192 Our development includes a gym with a free weights area. The gym operator wants rubber flooring, but the architect specified tiles. Can rubber flooring be installed over tiles, and does it need a separate subfloor?
**Answer:** Rubber gym flooring (10mm–25mm thick) can be installed directly over properly prepared tiles. The tile floor provides a flat, hard substrate that is ideal for rubber flooring. The installation process: (1) clean the tile floor thoroughly (degrease if necessary), (2) apply a pressure-sensitive adhesive or use interlocking rubber tiles that don't require adhesive, (3) install the rubber flooring in sheets or tiles. No separate subfloor is needed — the existing tiles serve as the subfloor. The benefits of rubber over tiles in a free weights area: (1) impact absorption (protects the slab and reduces noise transmission to units below), (2) equipment protection (dumbbells won't crack rubber), (3) athlete safety (reduces injury from falls). If the architect specified tiles, request a variation — rubber is the correct specification for a gym free weights area. Budget R350–R800/m² for quality rubber gym flooring.

#### Q: 193 A tile delivery arrived with packaging that says "Made in China" but the sales order said "Italian Design." The buyer claims this is false advertising. Is "Italian Design" a legally acceptable description for Chinese-made tiles?
**Answer:** "Italian Design" is a marketing term that describes the aesthetic origin, not the manufacturing origin. It is legally acceptable if: (1) the design was genuinely created by an Italian design studio or based on Italian aesthetic traditions, (2) the packaging clearly states the country of manufacture ("Made in China"), (3) there is no attempt to conceal the manufacturing origin. However, if the buyer was genuinely misled into believing the tiles were manufactured in Italy, and this belief influenced their purchase decision, they may have a claim under CPA Section 41 for misleading representation. The test is whether a reasonable consumer would understand "Italian Design" to mean "designed in Italy, made in China" or "made in Italy." To avoid disputes, use clearer language: "Designed in Italy, Manufactured in China" or specify the exact factory location. Transparency prevents complaints.

#### Q: 194 Our development's common area tiles were installed with an epoxy grout that has now discoloured after 18 months (yellowing in high-traffic areas). The grout manufacturer says this is "normal UV degradation." Is epoxy grout unsuitable for lobby areas?
**Answer:** Epoxy grout is generally superior to cement grout for stain resistance and durability, but some formulations (particularly white and light colours) can yellow when exposed to UV light or when subjected to high-temperature cleaning. In lobby areas with large windows and direct sunlight, UV exposure accelerates yellowing. The manufacturer's claim of "normal UV degradation" is partially valid — not all epoxy grouts are UV-stable. Solutions: (1) for future developments, specify "UV-stable epoxy grout" (formulated with aliphatic hardeners that resist yellowing), (2) use darker grout colours that don't show yellowing, (3) apply a UV-resistant clear sealer over the grout lines, (4) if the yellowing is severe, the grout can be ground out and replaced (cost: R80–R150/m²). For south-facing lobbies with high sun exposure, cement grout with a grout colourant/sealer may be more practical than standard epoxy.

#### Q: 195 We're building on land that has a "mineral rights" reservation in the title deed. The mineral rights holder wants to conduct exploration drilling through our basement floor. Can they do this after we've built?
**Answer:** If the title deed reserves mineral rights to a third party, those rights remain enforceable even after you build. The Mineral and Petroleum Resources Development Act (MPRDA) governs the relationship between surface landowners and mineral rights holders. The mineral rights holder must: (1) give reasonable notice, (2) compensate you for any damage caused, (3) follow reasonable practices to minimise disruption. However, they generally have the right to access minerals beneath your land. If drilling through your basement is proposed, you can negotiate: (1) the timing (before tiling and fit-out, not after), (2) the location (minimise structural impact), (3) compensation for loss of use and remediation costs, (4) a bond to ensure they restore the property. If the drilling occurs after construction, they must repair all damage, including re-tiling the basement floor. Engage a mining attorney to negotiate the access agreement — mineral rights disputes can be highly complex.

#### Q: 196 Our estate has a rule that "no alterations may be made without prior written consent of the developer." We've sold 90% of the units and the remaining 10% are still in our name. A sold-unit owner wants to retile their bathroom. Do we still have authority to approve or refuse?
**Answer:** If the title deed condition reserves approval rights to "the developer," and you remain the developer (the development company still exists and holds unsold units), you retain the approval authority. However, this power must be exercised reasonably — you cannot unreasonably withhold or delay consent for internal alterations that do not affect the building's structure, common property, or external appearance. Retiling a bathroom is an internal alteration that does not require developer consent unless the title deed specifically includes floor finishes in the restricted alterations. As a practical matter, most developers delegate this authority to the body corporate once the scheme is established. Continuing to exercise micro-control over sold units creates resentment and potential legal challenges. Consider amending the title deed conditions to transfer alteration approval to the body corporate for all internal works.

#### Q: 197 We're required to install "tactile paving" at all pedestrian crossings within our estate. The quantity surveyor has priced this at R850/m². We can buy tactile indicator studs for R45 each and install them on the existing tiles. Which approach is correct?
**Answer:** Tactile paving can be installed as either: (1) full tactile paving tiles (typically R600–R1,000/m² supplied and installed), or (2) individual tactile indicator studs surface-mounted on existing paving or tiles (R150–R350/m² including installation). SANS 784 and SANS 10400-S accept both methods provided the indicators meet the dimensional and luminance contrast requirements. Surface-mounted studs on existing tiles are compliant, significantly cheaper, and easier to replace if damaged. At R45 per stud with approximately 12 studs per m² (spaced at 300mm centres), the material cost is R540/m² plus installation at R80–R120/m² = R620–R660/m² total. This is still less than R850/m². Challenge the QS's pricing — they may have specified full tactile tiles without considering the stud alternative. For estate roads and parking areas, surface-mounted studs are the standard solution.

#### Q: 198 A tile installer on site mixed different brands of adhesive and grout, claiming "they're all the same." The installation is now showing efflorescence and soft grout. Is mixing brands the cause?
**Answer:** Mixing different manufacturers' products is not recommended and often voids warranties. Each manufacturer formulates their products to work as a system — the adhesive, grout, primer, and sealer are chemically compatible. Cross-brand mixing can cause: (1) chemical incompatibility (one product's additives react with another's), (2) efflorescence (different salt compositions migrate at different rates), (3) adhesion failure (different flexibility characteristics create stress), (4) colour inconsistency in grout. The efflorescence and soft grout are likely caused by using a cement grout with an incompatible admixture or by using grout that was not designed for the joint width installed. The defective work must be removed and reinstalled using a single manufacturer's system. Claim the rectification cost from the tile installer — this is defective workmanship, not a product defect. Specify "single manufacturer system" in your installation specifications going forward.

#### Q: 199 Our development includes a helipad on the roof of the building. The civil engineer says the helipad surface must withstand 4,000kg point loads from helicopter landing gear. Can we tile a helipad?
**Answer:** A helipad surface must withstand: (1) concentrated point loads from landing gear (2,000–4,000kg per wheel depending on helicopter class), (2) jet blast (downward rotor wash at high velocity), (3) fuel and hydraulic fluid spillage, (4) extreme weather exposure. Standard tiles cannot withstand these loads — a Bell 407 helicopter's landing gear creates approximately 1,000kg point loads per strut, and heavier helicopters create significantly more. The helipad surface is typically: (1) reinforced concrete with a non-slip epoxy or paint finish, (2) aluminium or steel grating, (3) specialised helipad matting systems. Tiles would crack under point loads and create dangerous debris from jet blast. Do NOT tile a helipad. The SANS 10400-B structural requirements for helipads are specific and substantial — engage an aviation structural engineer. The helipad must also comply with CAA (Civil Aviation Authority) regulations for marking, lighting, and fire suppression.

#### Q: 200 We've reached the end of our 200 questions. As a final catch-all: what is the single most common mistake SA developers make with floor tiles that costs them the most money in callbacks and legal disputes?
**Answer:** The single most costly mistake is inadequate or incorrect waterproofing in wet areas, compounded by failure to install movement joints at proper spacing. The sequence of failure is: (1) waterproofing membrane is either omitted, poorly applied, or incompatible with the tile adhesive, (2) water penetrates through grout joints (which are never fully waterproof) to the substrate, (3) without movement joints at maximum 4.5m intervals, thermal and moisture expansion creates stress, (4) the tiles debond, crack, or tent, (5) water ingress damages the ceiling and finishes of the unit below, (6) the NHBRC warranty, waterproofing warranty, and CPA claims all converge, creating multiple liability pathways. The rectification cost for a single failed shower is R15,000–R30,000; for a basement waterproofing failure, R500,000+. The prevention cost is R50–R80/m² for proper waterproofing and movement joints. The rule is simple: never compromise on waterproofing, and always install movement joints — the savings from cutting these corners are false economies that always cost more later.

---

_End of Document — 200 Q&A Pairs for Persona 4: Developers_
*Generated for South African tiling, flooring and construction industry*
*All answers grounded in South African law, regulations, and industry practice*

# PERSONA 5: CORPORATE NATIONAL CHAINS — 200 AWKWARD, STRANGE & UNEXPECTED Q&A PAIRS
# South African Tiling, Flooring & Construction Industry
## 1. B-BBEE & SUPPLIER COMPLIANCE

#### Q: 1 Our tiling supplier's B-BBEE certificate expired two months into a three-year national rollout contract. Our procurement scorecard now reflects non-compliant spend. Can we continue paying them, or must we halt all payments until renewal?
**Answer:** You are not legally required to halt payments, but the spend will no longer count toward your Preferential Procurement scorecard under the B-BBEE Codes of Good Practice. Under SANAS accreditation rules, a supplier has 12 months from expiry to renew without losing their verified status level, provided they apply before expiry. However, your scorecard reflects the date of verification. Best practice: negotiate a conditional penalty clause in the contract that triggers a price reduction or extended payment terms if B-BBEE status drops during contract term. The dtic's B-BBEE Commission has no enforcement power to stop trade, but your own ESD targets may be at risk.

#### Q: 2 Our tiling supplier is a Level 4 B-BBEE contributor but their subcontracted installer is non-compliant. Does the installation spend count toward our procurement recognition, or only the tile supply portion?
**Answer:** Under the Revised Codes of Good Practice (2013), only the entity with which you have a direct contract contributes to your procurement recognition. If your contract is with the supplier only, installation labour subcontracted by them does not automatically accrue to your scorecard unless the subcontractor is a designated group supplier and the main contractor flows the recognition through a written agreement. However, many corporates now require "flow-down" B-BBEE clauses mandating that subcontractors meet minimum levels. If your supplier simply pays a cash-and-carry installer, that labour component does not enhance your scorecard at all.

#### Q: 3 We have 147 franchise stores nationwide. Each franchisee sources their own tiles independently. For B-BBEE measurement purposes, is the franchisor (the brand owner) responsible for aggregating all franchisee procurement spend, or does each franchisee report independently?
**Answer:** This is a complex structural question. Under the B-BBEE Codes, franchisees with annual turnover below R10 million qualify as Exempt Micro-Enterprises (EMEs) automatically and need not be measured. However, if franchisees turnover above R50 million (Qualifying Small Enterprises) or R50 million+ (Generic Enterprises), they must be measured independently. The franchisor is NOT automatically responsible for franchisee procurement unless the franchisor holds the procurement contracts centrally. If franchisees order directly, they each maintain separate scorecards. However, many franchisors centralise procurement to control brand standards — in this case, the franchisor's scorecard absorbs the spend. Beware: if franchisees are majority foreign-owned, they may score zero for ownership, dragging down a centralised scorecard.

#### Q: 4 Our front-of-house tiles are imported from Italy. The local distributor has a Level 2 B-BBEE certificate, but the manufacturer overseas has zero B-BBEE status. Our CFO insists that imported goods carry a 15% B-BBEE penalty on procurement recognition. Is this correct?
**Answer:** Yes, your CFO is referring to Code Series 400 of the B-BBEE Codes, which states that imported goods and services do not qualify for full procurement recognition unless the importer can demonstrate that the goods are unavailable locally at competitive quality, or the import is for a specific technical reason. The standard practice is that imported goods receive recognition at a reduced rate — typically the supplier's B-BBEE level minus penalties, or in some sector codes, no recognition at all. The Construction Sector Code (amended 2022) has specific provisions. You can claim full recognition only if you obtain a letter from the dtic or relevant industry body confirming local unavailability. Document this rationale for your B-BBEE verification agency.

#### Q: 5 We appointed a tiling contractor who fronted as 51% black-owned but the black shareholder is a sleeping partner with no operational involvement. A whistleblower reported this to the B-BBEE Commission. As the client, what is our exposure?
**Answer:** Fronting is a criminal offence under the B-BBEE Amendment Act 46 of 2013, punishable by up to 10 years imprisonment and/or a fine of up to 10% of annual turnover. As the client, your exposure depends on your level of due diligence. If you conducted reasonable verification (reviewed share certificates, CIPC records, shareholder agreements) and had no knowledge of the fronting arrangement, you are unlikely to face criminal liability. However, your procurement recognition for all spend with that contractor may be clawed back by your verification agency. The B-BBEE Commission can also publish your name in connection with the matter. Protect yourself: require annual sworn affidavits and independent legal review of ownership structures for all significant B-BBEE suppliers.

#### Q: 6 Our B-BBEE verification agency has questioned whether our tile adhesive supplier's Enterprise & Supplier Development (ESD) contributions are genuine. The supplier claims our 12-month extended payment terms count as ESD. Does this qualify?
**Answer:** Extended payment terms alone do not automatically qualify as ESD contributions. Under Statement 400 of the B-BBEE Codes, ESD contributions must involve "substantive" support, which may include interest-free or concessional loans, equity investments, guarantees, or business development support. Simply paying later than 30 days does not constitute ESD — in fact, the Prompt Payment Act and industry codes discourage late payment. However, if the extended terms are structured as a formal supplier finance programme with concessional interest rates, documented in a written agreement, and reported as part of a recognised ESD programme, it may qualify. Your verification agency applies the ESD Recognition Matrix — not all programmes pass.

#### Q: 7 We are a Level 6 B-BBEE contributor and a major retail chain wants us to tile 200 stores. Their minimum threshold is Level 4. Can we subcontract 30% to a Level 1 EME to "uplift" our effective compliance level?
**Answer:** No. There is no "uplift" mechanism that allows you to subcontract your way to a higher B-BBEE level for client compliance purposes. The client evaluates YOUR entity's B-BBEE certificate, not an aggregate of you and subcontractors. However, strategic subcontracting can improve your own scorecard over time — for example, if you subcontract to black-owned EMEs, that spend may improve your Preferential Procurement and ESD indicators on your next verification. But for immediate contract compliance, your own certificate must meet the client's minimum threshold. Some clients will accept a binding undertaking to achieve Level 4 within 12 months, backed by penalty clauses.

#### Q: 8 Our corporate property division sources tiles through a central procurement hub registered in Gauteng, but tiles are delivered to stores in all nine provinces. For B-BBEE purposes, should we be measuring provincial spend distribution, or is national aggregation sufficient?
**Answer:** B-BBEE measurement is entity-level, not provincial. Your procurement recognition is calculated on the total spend of the measured entity (the procurement hub) regardless of delivery destination. However, there are two exceptions: first, if you have separate legal entities in different provinces, each is measured separately. Second, some Sector Codes (notably Construction) have regional targets for local procurement. If your central hub is a separate legal entity from the stores, only the hub's spend counts toward its own scorecard. The stores' procurement (if they hold separate contracts) counts separately. Many corporates consolidate to maximise bargaining power and simplify B-BBEE reporting.
## 2. ESG, CARBON REPORTING & SUSTAINABILITY

#### Q: 9 We are JSE-listed and must report Scope 3 emissions under the Sustainability and Climate Disclosure Requirements. Our tiling supplier cannot provide embodied carbon data for porcelain tiles. How do we calculate Scope 3 for 50,000 square metres of tiling annually?
**Answer:** Under the JSE Sustainability Disclosure Guidance (aligned with ISSB/IFRS S2), you must report Scope 3 emissions using primary supplier data where available, or secondary data (industry averages) where not. If your supplier cannot provide Product-Specific Environmental Product Declarations (EPDs) or embodied carbon data, use the ICE Database (Inventory of Carbon and Energy), generic porcelain tile emission factors from the Global Cement and Concrete Association, or South Africa-specific data from the Cement & Concrete SA (CCSA) sustainability reports. Document your methodology, data gaps, and improvement targets. The JSE does not yet mandate full Scope 3 disclosure (as of 2024), but best practice is to estimate using spend-based or physical-unit-based methods and disclose the estimation approach. Engage your supplier to obtain EPDs — this is becoming a contractual requirement for many listed corporates.

#### Q: 10 Our Green Star SA rating for a new flagship store depends partly on the flooring materials' eco-certification. The tile supplier claims their product is "eco-friendly" but has no GreenTag, EPD, or SANS 10400-XA compliance certificate. Can we still claim points?
**Answer:** No. The Green Building Council of South Africa (GBCSA) Green Star rating tool requires specific, verifiable evidence for Material category credits. Generic "eco-friendly" claims carry no weight. You need at least one of: a Global GreenTag certificate, an ISO-compliant Environmental Product Declaration (EPD), proof of recycled content percentage with third-party verification, or SANS 10400-XA compliance for thermal performance. Without these, the material cannot contribute to Green Star points. Some suppliers will obtain GreenTag certification on request if you commit to volume — negotiate this into your specification. Alternatively, consider GBCSA's Pilot Credit for embodied carbon reporting, which may accept provisional data with improvement commitments.

#### Q: 11 Our sustainability report was challenged by an activist shareholder who claimed our "carbon-neutral store rollout" was misleading because the tiles were manufactured using coal-fired kilns. What is our legal exposure under the Companies Act?
**Answer:** Section 22 of the Companies Act 71 of 2008 prohibits fraudulent or reckless conduct, and section 24 requires financial statements to present a fair view. While sustainability claims are not yet formally audited, the Advertising Standards Authority of South Africa (ASA) and the Takeover Regulation Panel have taken action against greenwashing. More seriously, the JSE Listings Requirements (Practices Note 1/2023 on Sustainability Disclosure) require that ESG claims be accurate and verifiable. If your "carbon-neutral" claim was based on offsets while ignoring embodied carbon in materials, an activist could argue misrepresentation. Best defence: ensure all sustainability claims are substantiated by documented offset purchases, validated emission calculations, and third-party assurance. Use cautious language — "carbon-neutral operations" (Scope 1 & 2 only) is more defensible than "carbon-neutral store" which implies full lifecycle coverage.

#### Q: 12 We need to report water usage in our tile supply chain under the CDP Water Security questionnaire. Our supplier is a cement-based tile manufacturer in Lichtenburg. What water data should we request, and what if they refuse?
**Answer:** For CDP Water Security reporting, request: total water withdrawal (municipal, borehole, recycled), water discharge volumes and quality, water stress exposure of manufacturing locations, and any water stewardship certifications (AWS, ISO 46001). Lichtenburg falls within a water-stressed area of the North West — the Vaal River System has experienced severe shortages. If the supplier refuses, use the WRI Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas to assign a default risk score, disclose the data gap, and explain your engagement strategy. CDP scoring penalises non-response but rewards transparency about gaps. For JSE-listed companies, water reporting is increasingly linked to King IV Principle 16 (organisation in the natural and social context).

#### Q: 13 Our board has approved a circular economy target: 30% of flooring materials must contain recycled content by 2027. We discovered our tile supplier's "recycled" tiles contain pre-consumer waste only (factory off-cuts), not post-consumer waste. Does this still count as recycled content for ESG reporting?
**Answer:** It depends on your reporting framework. Under GRI 301 (Materials), both pre-consumer and post-consumer recycled content are recognised, but post-consumer is weighted more favourably. Under GreenTag and some Green Star credits, post-consumer content receives higher points. The ISO 14021 environmental labels standard distinguishes between "pre-consumer" (process waste) and "post-consumer" (end-of-life product) recycled content. For credible ESG reporting, you should disclose the split between pre- and post-consumer content. If your board target was ambiguously worded, the supplier's pre-consumer content may technically qualify, but you risk credibility. Specify in your procurement policy: "post-consumer recycled content preferred; pre-consumer content accepted only where post-consumer is unavailable."

#### Q: 14 Our tiles are shipped from Italy to Durban, then railed to Johannesburg. For Scope 3 emissions, should we report the shipping emissions under our South African entity or the European supplier's entity?
**Answer:** Under the GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Standard, emissions from upstream transportation are reported by the entity that pays for the transport — or if shared, by the entity with operational control. If you (the South African retailer) contract and pay the freight forwarder, you report these emissions under Category 4 (Upstream Transport). If the supplier includes transport in their delivered price and contracts the shipping, the emissions fall under Category 1 (Purchased Goods & Services) and would be included in the supplier's own Scope 1/2 inventory. For accuracy, adopt "control" or "financial responsibility" as your allocation principle and apply it consistently. Document this in your carbon accounting policy.

#### Q: 15 We are required to set Science-Based Targets (SBTi) for our retail operations. Our largest flooring supplier has refused to commit to SBTi or provide emission reduction targets. Can we still include their tiles in our SBTi-validated inventory?
**Answer:** Yes, but with limitations. SBTi does not require that all suppliers have their own SBTi-approved targets. Your near-term target must cover 95% of Scope 1+2 emissions and 67% of Scope 3 emissions. You can include the supplier's tiles in your inventory using their current emission factors and your own reduction pathway (e.g., switching to lower-carbon materials, reducing wastage). However, SBTi's FLAG (Forest, Land and Agriculture) and supplier engagement requirements are tightening. From 2025, SBTi strongly encourages — and may soon require — that a percentage of suppliers by spend have their own science-based targets. Engage the supplier formally, document the refusal, and include supplier engagement as part of your target narrative.

#### Q: 16 Our annual integrated report includes a photo of our flagship store's beautiful tiled interior. The auditor questioned whether we needed to include the embodied carbon of those tiles in our reported Scope 3 emissions. Is a decorative photo a reportable emission?
**Answer:** This highlights the boundary problem in carbon accounting. If the store is company-owned and operational during the reporting period, the tiles are part of your fixed asset base and their embodied carbon should be captured in Category 1 (Purchased Goods) or Category 2 (Capital Goods), depending on whether you classify fit-out as capex or opex. The photo itself is irrelevant — what matters is whether the tiles were purchased in the reporting period. If the store was built in a prior year, the embodied carbon was reported then (unless you use rolling reporting). Don't confuse marketing imagery with emission boundaries. Follow the GHG Protocol's guidance on capital goods accounting.
## 3. POPIA & DATA PRIVACY

#### Q: 17 Our tiling contractor needs access to employee locker rooms to measure for floor renovations. This requires capturing names, shift patterns, and locker numbers. Does this trigger POPIA compliance obligations?
**Answer:** Yes. Shift patterns linked to names constitute personal information under Section 1 of POPIA (Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013). Locker numbers may not be personal information on their own, but combined with names they create an information regime that identifies individuals. You need: a processing justification (lawful basis — likely legitimate interest of the employer with consent), a privacy notice to affected employees, a data processing agreement with the contractor specifying purpose limitation and retention periods, and technical safeguards (encrypted storage, access controls). The contractor becomes an "operator" under POPIA, and you (the responsible party) remain liable for their compliance. Do not share employee data without these protections — the Information Regulator can impose administrative fines up to R10 million.

#### Q: 18 We use biometric access control at all stores. During a tile replacement project, the installation crew requested biometric enrollment to access the building after hours. Six months later, we discovered the contractor retained all biometric data on their own server. Is this a POPIA breach?
**Answer:** Almost certainly yes. Biometric data is classified as "special personal information" under Section 26 of POPIA, attracting heightened protection. The contractor acted as your operator and had no independent right to retain biometric data after project completion. Section 19 of POPIA requires that personal information be destroyed or de-identified as soon as reasonably practicable after the purpose for collection is fulfilled. The contractor's retention constitutes unlawful processing. You must: (1) issue a formal instruction for immediate deletion with proof; (2) notify affected employees; (3) assess whether the breach is notifiable to the Information Regulator under Section 22 (likely yes, given biometric sensitivity); (4) review all operator agreements to include mandatory deletion clauses. The Information Regulator has already imposed significant fines for biometric data mishandling — see the 2023 enforcement notices.

#### Q: 19 Our CCTV system captured footage of a tile installation team working in a pharmacy section. The footage includes prescription counter activity visible in the background. A patient claims this violates their medical privacy under POPIA. Is our pharmacy tenant liable, or are we as landlord?
**Answer:** This is a classic responsible party vs. joint controller question. Under POPIA, the entity that determines the purpose and means of processing is the responsible party. If you (the landlord/retailer) installed CCTV for security purposes and control the system, you are the responsible party for the footage. However, if the pharmacy tenant has independent cameras for regulatory compliance (SAPC requirements), they may be a separate responsible party. Where footage overlaps, you may be joint controllers. The pharmacy's obligation under POPIA Section 26 (special personal information — health data) is strict — health data cannot be processed without explicit consent or specific lawful justification. Best practice: angle cameras to exclude clinical areas, use privacy masking, and have written agreements allocating POPIA responsibilities in multi-tenant spaces.

#### Q: 20 During a national store rollout, our tiling project manager created a WhatsApp group including store managers, contractors, and our procurement team to coordinate access. The group shared photos of store layouts including emergency exit plans and alarm codes. A contractor's phone was stolen. What's our POPIA exposure?
**Answer:** Significant. WhatsApp groups are notoriously insecure for business communications. Alarm codes and emergency exit plans, while not "personal information" per se, may be linked to responsible individuals (security personnel names, keyholder details). More critically, if any personal information was shared (employee names, contact numbers, ID numbers for access clearance), this constitutes processing without adequate safeguards. POPIA Section 19 requires appropriate, reasonable technical and organisational measures. WhatsApp's consumer-grade encryption does not meet corporate data governance standards. You should: (1) conduct a breach assessment; (2) rotate all compromised alarm codes immediately; (3) notify affected individuals; (4) assess notifiability to the Information Regulator; (5) implement a corporate policy prohibiting work-related personal information sharing on consumer messaging apps. Reference the Information Regulator's 2022 guidance on workplace communications.

#### Q: 21 We want to use customer foot traffic data (collected via in-store WiFi and thermal sensors embedded in the ceiling near tiled areas) to optimise store layout and tile cleaning schedules. Does this require POPIA compliance, and do we need customer consent?
**Answer:** If the data is fully anonymised and cannot be re-identified (no MAC address tracking, no linkage to loyalty programmes), it falls outside POPIA's scope — POPIA applies to identifiable natural persons. However, if WiFi tracking captures device MAC addresses that could theoretically be linked to individuals (e.g., through loyalty app integration), it IS personal information. Thermal sensors that count bodies without identification are generally not POPIA-regulated. Best practice: conduct a privacy impact assessment (PIA) before deployment, publish a privacy notice at store entry explaining data collection methods, and ensure technical anonymisation (hashing, truncation) of any device identifiers. The POPIA Code of Conduct for consumer-facing businesses (pending finalisation) is expected to address IoT analytics specifically.

#### Q: 22 Our tiling supplier requested copies of our store manager ID documents to issue access cards for after-hours installation work. Six months after project completion, the supplier's HR file still contains 34 copies of ID documents. Is this lawful under POPIA?
**Answer:** No. Section 14 of POPIA requires that personal information not be retained longer than necessary for the purpose for which it was collected. The purpose — issuing access cards — was fulfilled when the project ended. Retaining ID copies six months later violates the retention limitation principle. Section 19 further requires secure destruction when no longer needed. The supplier must delete or securely destroy these documents. You, as the responsible party who provided the data, share accountability. Issue a formal instruction for destruction with a compliance certificate. If the supplier needs ID records for tax or labour purposes (COIDA, UIF), they should retain only the minimum necessary (ID number, not full document copy) and only for the statutory period. The Information Regulator's 2023 enforcement trend shows increasing focus on excessive data retention.
## 4. FORCE MAJEURE & SUPPLY CHAIN DISRUPTION

#### Q: 23 Our Italian tile supplier's factory was destroyed by fire. Our contract has a force majeure clause but no specific exclusion for supplier factory fires. We have 40 stores scheduled for fit-out in the next 6 months. What is our legal position?
**Answer:** Under South African common law, force majeure (vis maior / casus fortuitus) requires an unforeseeable event that makes performance impossible, not merely more expensive or inconvenient. A factory fire at the supplier's premises typically qualifies as force majeure if: (1) the fire was not caused by the supplier's negligence; (2) the supplier could not reasonably have prevented it; (3) the supplier notified you without delay. However, force majeure does not automatically excuse the entire contract — only the affected obligations. Check your contract: does it have a "sole source" clause, alternative supply obligations, or stock-holding requirements? If the supplier is a distributor (not manufacturer), they may have a duty to source from alternative factories. If the contract is silent, you may have to accept temporary suspension or negotiate termination. The Consumer Protection Act may give you additional rights if you're a consumer, but B2B contracts are governed primarily by the common law and contract terms.
