Course of action: (1) notify your insurer that the restoration contractor caused additional damage; (2) demand the insurer covers replacement of the etched tiles (their contractor, their responsibility); (3) if the insurer refuses, claim directly against the restoration contractor's professional indemnity insurance; (4) document the before/after condition with photos and an independent assessment. The Financial Services Conduct Authority (FSCA) guidelines on claims handling require insurers to act fairly — causing additional damage through appointed contractors and refusing to cover it would likely constitute unfair claims handling.
## 17. CRIME, SECURITY & VANDALISM

#### Q: 82 Our store in a high-crime area has experienced repeated theft of copper earthing cables from the floor slab during installation. The contractors are working overnight when the mall is closed. Who bears the cost of replacement and delay?
**Answer:** This is a risk allocation issue. Under standard construction contracts (JBCC, NEC), the contractor bears responsibility for site security and protection of works unless the employer (you) has taken control of the site. If the mall landlord controls after-hours access, they may share liability for inadequate security. However: (1) the contractor should have secured materials and installed earthing immediately, not left copper exposed overnight; (2) if your contract requires after-hours work in a high-risk area, you may bear some responsibility for specifying unsafe working conditions; (3) the contractor's insurance (Contractors All Risk) may cover theft of materials. Best approach: (1) require the contractor to install earthing as a continuous operation and not leave exposed copper overnight; (2) require security escort or CCTV monitoring for high-value materials; (3) specify that the contractor bears theft risk unless security is provided by the employer. For the current situation: if the contractor's negligence (poor material security) contributed, they bear the cost; if the theft was despite reasonable precautions, it may be an insurance claim. The Criminal Matters Amendment Act 2015 provides for property damage during trespass — file a police case for insurance purposes.

#### Q: 83 Our jewellery store (tenant in our mall) has demanded ballistic-rated flooring as part of their robbery prevention measures. They want a floor that cannot be easily dug through to access the vault below. Is there such a thing as "ballistic" or "forced-entry-resistant" tiled flooring?
**Answer:** There is no "ballistic tile" standard, but forced-entry-resistant flooring systems do exist. The requirement is not about the tile surface but the COMPOSITE FLOOR SYSTEM: (1) a reinforced concrete slab minimum 150mm thick with rebar mesh; (2) a steel plate layer (minimum 3mm) embedded in the slab; (3) standard tiles installed over this structural base. The tiles themselves provide minimal resistance — it's the structural slab below that prevents access. For jewellery and banking applications: (1) specify the floor slab to SASO (South African Police Service) security standards for protected premises; (2) install seismic sensors in the floor to detect drilling or cutting; (3) use anti-vandal tiles (thick, impact-resistant porcelain) that resist casual damage but do not claim ballistic protection; (4) ensure the vault itself meets SANS 1294 (safes and strongrooms). The tenant's demand for "ballistic flooring" should be translated into proper structural engineering specifications — engage a security consultant registered with SAPS. The floor above a vault is typically a structural/engineering issue, not a tiling specification issue.

#### Q: 84 Our store's tiled facade (cladding tiles on the external wall) has been repeatedly damaged by vandals throwing stones. The body corporate refuses to pay for repairs, claiming this is our tenant responsibility. Our lease says the landlord maintains the "structure." Who pays?
**Answer:** This depends on lease interpretation. Typically: (1) the landlord (body corporate in a sectional title scheme) maintains the common property and structural elements; (2) the tenant maintains interior fit-out and tenant-specific improvements. External cladding tiles on a store facade: if the tiles are part of the original building structure (installed by the developer), they are the body corporate's responsibility under Section 37(1)(b) of the Sectional Titles Act; if YOU installed the tiles as tenant improvements, they are your responsibility. The "structure" vs. "fit-out" distinction is key. Even if the body corporate is responsible for repairs, they may claim the cost from you if the damage was caused by your specific risk (e.g., if your store is the target of repeated vandalism due to your business activity). Check your lease: does it include a "make good" clause for damage beyond fair wear and tear? For persistent vandalism, consider: anti-graffiti coating; CCTV; security shutters; or specifying impact-resistant cladding tiles (minimum 10mm thickness, through-body colour to hide chips).
## 18. FLOOD & ENVIRONMENTAL DAMAGE

#### Q: 85 Our Durban store was flooded in the April 2022 KZN floods. The tiles have lifted completely due to hydrostatic pressure from underneath the slab. The landlord says this is an "act of God" and not their responsibility. Is this correct, and who pays for replacement?
**Answer:** "Act of God" (vis maior) is a defence in South African common law, but its application to flooding depends on foreseeability and mitigation. The April 2022 floods were extreme but KZN has a well-documented history of flooding — the 1987 floods were similarly catastrophic. A landlord in a known floodplain may be expected to have: (1) adequate stormwater management; (2) proper slab waterproofing; (3) raised floor levels; (4) sump pumps. If the landlord failed to maintain drainage or the building design was inadequate for the flood risk, their "act of God" defence weakens. Under the lease: (1) if it's a "full repair and maintain" lease, the tenant may be responsible regardless of cause; (2) if the landlord maintains the structure, they are responsible for structural failures including hydrostatic pressure damage. For the tiling specifically: if the tiles lifted due to inadequate subfloor preparation or waterproofing (not pure hydrostatic pressure), the original contractor or landlord may be liable. Engage a forensic engineer to determine the root cause. The National Disaster Management Act 57 of 2002 may provide limited relief for disaster-affected businesses.

#### Q: 86 After the KZN floods, our store's tiles developed a persistent musty odour. An environmental consultant identified mould growth under the tiles due to trapped moisture. Our insurer has denied the claim, saying mould is excluded as a "gradually operating cause." Is this exclusion enforceable?
**Answer:** Gradually operating cause exclusions are common in South African commercial policies and are generally enforceable. However, there is a causation question here: the mould was CAUSED by a sudden, insured event (the flood). The "efficient cause" doctrine in South African insurance law (Leyland v Norwich Union) holds that if an insured peril sets in motion a chain of events leading to loss, the loss is covered even if the final damage occurs gradually. Your argument: the flood was the proximate cause; the mould was the direct consequence; without the flood, there would be no mould. This is distinct from mould that develops from chronic poor maintenance (gradually operating). If the insurer maintains the exclusion, escalate to the Ombudsman for Short-Term Insurance. Prevention: after any flooding, tiles must be lifted, the subfloor thoroughly dried (dehumidifiers for 2-4 weeks), and moisture content verified below 75% RH before reinstallation. The Occupational Health and Safety Act requires employers to provide a safe working environment — if mould affects indoor air quality, you may need to close the store until remediation.

#### Q: 87 Our Limpopo store experienced severe hail damage — hailstones cracked outdoor patio tiles. The supplier claims their warranty excludes "weather damage." Is this a valid warranty exclusion under South African law?
**Answer:** Warranty exclusions for "weather damage" are generally valid if clearly stated and not unconscionable under the CPA. However: (1) if the tiles were sold as "frost-resistant" or "suitable for outdoor use," the supplier impliedly warranted weather resistance — hail is a normal weather event in Limpopo; (2) SANS 1449 includes frost resistance testing (ISO 10545-12) for tiles marketed as frost-resistant — if the tiles failed this test, they are defective regardless of warranty exclusions; (3) the CPA Section 55 implied warranty of quality cannot be excluded for consumer transactions. For B2B contracts, warranty exclusions are more freely enforceable. Check: was the tile specifically rated for outdoor use? What size hail caused the damage (golf-ball sized hail can damage almost anything)? Was the installation method appropriate for the exposure? Large-format thin tiles installed without adequate movement joints are more vulnerable to impact. For outdoor areas in hail-prone regions: specify minimum 10mm thickness; use small-format tiles (less likely to crack than large); ensure full bedding (not spot-fixed); provide adequate movement accommodation.
## 19. SEISMIC & STRUCTURAL REQUIREMENTS

#### Q: 88 Our Cape Town CBD store is in a building designated as "heritage" under the National Heritage Resources Act. We want to replace the 1920s mosaic floor with modern tiles. The architect says we need SAHRA (South African Heritage Resources Agency) approval. Is this correct, and what is the process?
**Answer:** Yes, this is correct. Section 48 of the National Heritage Resources Act 25 of 1999 prohibits alteration or demolition of heritage structures without a permit from the relevant heritage authority (SAHRA for Grade I and II national heritage sites; Provincial Heritage Resources Authority for Grade II provincial and Grade III local sites). Original mosaic floors in heritage buildings are typically protected features. Process: (1) determine the heritage grading of your building — check with the local PHRA or municipality; (2) if Grade I or II, apply to SAHRA for an alteration permit — this requires a heritage impact assessment (HIA) by a SAHRA-accredited heritage practitioner; (3) if the mosaic is original and in good condition, SAHRA will almost certainly refuse removal — they may require repair and retention; (4) if the mosaic is severely damaged, they may permit replacement with a sympathetic alternative or require documentation (photographic record, sample retention) before removal. Penalties for unauthorised alteration: fines up to R1 million and/or imprisonment up to 10 years (Section 189). The Western Cape has particularly strict heritage enforcement in the Cape Town CBD. Engage a heritage practitioner early — the process takes 3-6 months.

#### Q: 89 Our building in the Cape Town CBD is in a moderate seismic zone (Zone 1 per SANS 10160). The structural engineer has specified a flexible joint system for all floor finishes to accommodate seismic movement. Our tiling contractor has never installed seismic joints. What is required?
**Answer:** SANS 10160:2011 (South African loading code) includes seismic design requirements for buildings in seismic zones. For floor finishes: (1) EXPANSION/SEISMIC JOINTS must be provided at all structural movement joints — these are typically 15-25mm wide and must extend through the full tile installation (tile + adhesive + screed); (2) JOINT COVER SYSTEM — specify a rated movement joint cover system (e.g., CS Allway, Schluter DILEX) that accommodates the calculated movement (typically ±25mm for seismic joints in commercial buildings); (3) TILE INSTALLATION — tiles must not bridge structural joints; a gap must be maintained and covered by the joint system; (4) ADHESIVE — use flexible (S1 or S2) deformable adhesive that accommodates substrate movement without cracking; (5) EDGE PROTECTION — use metal edge trim profiles at all tile terminations to prevent chipping during movement. The joint cover system must be: fire-rated if it crosses fire compartments; load-rated for the expected traffic (heavy-duty for retail); and colour-matched where visible. Your contractor MUST be trained in seismic joint installation — engage the joint system manufacturer's technical team for supervision.

#### Q: 90 Our Port Elizabeth store is in a building that suffered structural settlement, creating a 15mm step in the concrete slab across the sales floor. We want to level this with a screed and retile. The structural engineer says we must first fix the settlement. Can we not just level and tile over it?
**Answer:** No — you must address the root cause first. Screeding over a settlement crack does not stop ongoing movement. If the building is still settling (active structural movement), your new screed and tiles WILL crack again — potentially within months. The correct sequence: (1) the structural engineer must determine if settlement is ACTIVE (ongoing) or DORMANT (historical, now stable); (2) if active, underpinning or other structural remediation is required before any floor replacement; (3) if dormant, the slab can be levelled with a self-levelling compound or screed, but a movement joint must be installed at the crack location; (4) if the step exceeds 10mm over 2m, a bonded screed (minimum 15mm) or unbonded screed (minimum 35mm) is required — follow SANS 10109 (screeds and floors); (5) specify flexible tile adhesive and provide movement joints aligned with the structural crack. Tiling over an unresolved structural problem is false economy — the NHBRC 5-year warranty would not cover it (it's not a tile defect, it's a structural issue), and your commercial insurer may exclude claims from known structural defects. Get a signed structural clearance before proceeding.
## 20. MINING, PRISON & SPECIALISED INDUSTRIAL

#### Q: 91 Our mine site canteen serves 2,000 workers per shift. The tiled floor is subject to heavy mud and dust ingress from work boots. The grout has turned black and cleaning has no effect. Is this a specification failure, and what should we have specified?
**Answer:** This is almost certainly a specification failure for the application. Mine site canteens are among the most demanding environments for flooring. The black grout is likely: (1) permanent staining from mine dust (often containing coal, iron oxide, or manganese); (2) bacterial colonisation in porous cement grout; (3) embedded grit that cannot be removed by standard cleaning. For this application, you should have specified: (1) EPOXY GROUT — stain-proof, chemical-resistant, and non-porous; (2) DARK-COLOURED GROUT — hides staining better than light colours; (3) LARGE FORMAT TILES — minimise joint quantity; (4) R12 or R13 SLIP RATING — essential for wet, muddy boots; (5) ENTRY SYSTEM — industrial-grade entrance matting (minimum 6m run-off) to remove bulk debris before the canteen; (6) PRESSURE WASHING CAPABILITY — the floor must withstand daily high-pressure cleaning. Mine dust often contains oily compounds from machinery — standard cement grout absorbs these and becomes permanently stained. For future: specify epoxy grout; use dark charcoal or black grout colour; install full drainage with slope to floor drains; specify hot water pressure washing compatibility. The Mine Health and Safety Act requires canteen facilities to be "clean and sanitary" — stained flooring may attract inspector attention.

#### Q: 92 Our correctional facility project requires flooring that meets Department of Correctional Services (DCS) security standards. The specification mentions "no loose items that can be used as weapons." How does this affect standard tile installation accessories like edge trims and movement joints?
**Answer:** DCS security standards (DCS Building Norms and Standards) require that all fixtures and fittings in inmate-accessible areas be tamper-proof and not readily detachable for use as weapons. For tiled floors: (1) EDGE TRIMS — use heavy-duty, screw-fixed metal trims with tamper-proof (security/Torx) screws, not clip-in or adhesive trims that can be pried off; (2) MOVEMENT JOINTS — use heavy-duty metal joint covers secured with security fasteners, not snap-in PVC covers; (3) FLOOR DRAINS — specify tamper-proof drain gratings secured with special tools; (4) SKIRTING — use coved, integral skirting (minimum 150mm) that cannot be separated from the wall or floor; (5) NO LOOSE THRESHOLDS — all transitions must be flush, fixed, and tamper-resistant. In high-security areas (maximum security cells), many facilities specify seamless poured resin flooring SPECIFICALLY because it eliminates all joints, edges, and fixings. Standard residential or commercial tile installations are NOT suitable for correctional facilities. The DCS technical team reviews all specifications before construction — engage them early in the design process.

#### Q: 93 Our coal mine's change house requires flooring resistant to coal dust, which is acidic and abrasive. Our standard commercial vinyl tiles have worn through in 18 months. What flooring specification should we use for mining change houses?
**Answer:** Coal dust (particularly fine coal dust) is both abrasive and mildly acidic. Standard commercial vinyl is completely inadequate. Specification for mining change houses: (1) HEAVY-DUTY EPOXY SCREED (4-6mm) — specifically formulated for industrial applications with high abrasion resistance (Taber Abraser CS-17, 1000 cycles, weight loss <100mg); (2) if tiles are preferred, specify 12mm thick QUARRY TILES with FURAN GROUT — quarry tiles have proven mining industry performance over decades; (3) ensure chemical resistance to pH 3-4 (coal dust leachate is acidic); (4) specify R11 or R12 slip rating — change houses are constantly wet; (5) include full floor drainage with sump and pump — coal dust-laden wash water must be contained; (6) the floor must withstand high-pressure hose-down cleaning daily. The Mine Health and Safety Act and DMR (Department of Mineral Resources) regulations require adequate sanitary facilities — flooring failure can result in compliance notices. Change houses are high-wear, high-moisture environments — budget for replacement every 7-10 years even with correct specification.
## 21. AUTOMOTIVE & WORKSHOP APPLICATIONS

#### Q: 94 Our vehicle dealership's showroom uses polished porcelain tiles that look stunning but are extremely slippery when wet (PTV 18 wet). A salesperson slipped during a rain event. Our public liability insurer has noted the claim and may increase premiums. What are our options without changing the aesthetic?
**Answer:** This is a common showroom dilemma — aesthetics vs. safety. Options that preserve the polished look: (1) ANTI-SLIP TREATMENT — apply a nano-etching anti-slip treatment (e.g., SureGrip, Slip No More) that micro-etches the surface, increasing wet PTV to 35-45 without visibly altering the appearance. These treatments last 2-5 years depending on traffic; (2) ENTRANCE MATTING — install 6-10m of high-performance entrance matting to remove moisture before the showroom floor; (3) TARGETED TREATMENT — treat only high-risk areas (entrance zones, around display cars) and leave the main floor polished; (4) MATS AND SIGNAGE — use branded mats at entrances during wet weather with "caution" signage. Note: nano-etching treatments may affect the tile warranty — check with the tile manufacturer. Some manufacturers void warranties if chemical treatments are applied. The SANS 10107:2018 slip resistance code recommends PTV ≥ 36 for commercial entrances and ≥ 45 for areas with water exposure. Your PTV of 18 is dangerously low for any public area. The Department of Labour can issue a notice under OHSA if slip hazards are reported.

#### Q: 95 Our tyre fitment centre uses a compressed air system that creates oil mist in the workshop. The oil has soaked into our unglazed quarry tile floor, creating a persistent slip hazard. Standard degreasers don't work. What is the solution?
**Answer:** Oil-soaked unglazed tiles are extremely difficult to remediate because the oil penetrates deep into the pores. Solutions: (1) DEEP EXTRACTION — use an alkaline degreaser (pH 12-13) with hot water extraction equipment (industrial carpet extractor adapted for hard floors). Multiple treatments may be needed; (2) ABSORBENT POUltice — apply fuller's earth or diatomaceous earth poultice, leave for 24-48 hours to draw out oil, repeat as needed; (3) STEAM CLEANING — industrial steam cleaner (150°C+) can emulsify and extract embedded oil; (4) If these fail, the floor needs replacement — oil-impregnated tiles cannot be effectively cleaned. For the replacement specification: (1) use SEALED PORCELAIN tiles (water absorption ≤0.5%) that do not absorb oil; (2) apply a penetrating, oil-repellent sealer (fluoropolymer-based); (3) specify EPOXY GROUT (oil-resistant); (4) install oil-resistant, heavy-duty epoxy screed in the highest-exposure areas; (5) improve workshop ventilation to reduce oil mist concentration. The OHSA General Safety Regulations require safe flooring — an oil-soaked floor is a clear safety violation.

#### Q: 96 Our car wash facility has tiled walls and floors. The high-pressure water jets (150 bar) have eroded the grout within 12 months. The contractor used standard cement grout. What should have been specified, and is the contractor liable?
**Answer:** The contractor is liable for using an inappropriate grout for a known high-pressure environment. Standard cement grout has no resistance to 150 bar water pressure — it erodes rapidly. For car wash facilities: (1) use EPOXY GROUT — specifically formulated for industrial wet environments with chemical resistance to detergents and waxes; (2) specify TILES with low water absorption (≤0.5% — fully vitrified porcelain) — high-pressure water can force water into porous tiles, causing internal pressure damage; (3) use FLEXIBLE ADHESIVE (S1 or S2) to accommodate vibration from pressure washers; (4) ensure FULL BEDDING of tiles — spot-fixing will fail under pressure; (5) seal all perimeter joints with polyurethane sealant; (6) specify coved skirting to eliminate wall-floor junction gaps. The contractor should have known that standard cement grout is unsuitable for car wash applications — this is basic industry knowledge. If they tendered on a performance specification ("suitable for car wash environment"), they are clearly in breach. If they were given a generic specification with no application detail, liability may be shared. For future: include the operating conditions (pressure, chemicals, temperature) in the specification.
## 22. UNIFORMITY & BRAND CONSISTENCY

#### Q: 97 We are rolling out 200 stores over 5 years. The tile manufacturer has warned that production colour varies between batches (shade variation). How do we ensure colour consistency across all stores when orders are placed years apart?
**Answer:** Shade variation is inherent in tile manufacturing — even premium brands cannot guarantee identical colour across multi-year rollouts. Strategies: (1) ORDER FULL QUANTITY UPFRONT — negotiate a single-batch order for the entire rollout, store excess stock at the supplier's warehouse or a third-party logistics facility. This guarantees consistency but ties up capital and warehouse space; (2) SPECIFY BATCH SAMPLES — require the supplier to retain a master sample from the initial order; all subsequent orders must be matched to this sample with ΔE ≤1.0 (perceptible colour difference threshold); (3) USE "INTER-batch" TILES — order 10% extra from each batch to allow blending between batches at transition stores; (4) ACCEPTABLE VARIATION CLAUSE — include in the contract that shade variation within the manufacturer's published tolerance (typically "V2" or "V3" on the Ceramic Tile Distributors Association scale) is acceptable; (5) STORE SEQUENCE — cluster stores by batch (e.g., all KZN stores from Batch 1, all Gauteng from Batch 2) so variation is not visible between adjacent stores. Document the shade variation policy in your brand guidelines — some variation is acceptable if customers never see two stores side-by-side.

#### Q: 98 Our brand refresh requires changing from beige to grey tiles. We have 23 stores still using the old beige tiles that are only 3 years old. Our sustainability policy prohibits waste. Can we overlay new tiles on the old ones, or do we need to remove and replace?
**Answer:** Overlaying (tiling over existing tiles) is technically possible and is done commercially, but with significant caveats: (1) HEIGHT — overlaying adds 15-25mm thickness (tile + adhesive), which affects door clearances, transition strips, and equipment levels; (2) ADHESION — the existing tiles must be thoroughly cleaned, degreased, and mechanically abraded (scarified) for the new adhesive to bond; (3) WEIGHT — the additional load must be within the slab's capacity; a porcelain tile overlay weighs approximately 25-30kg/m² additional; (4) WARRANTY — most adhesive manufacturers will not warranty overlay installations unless specific primers and flexible adhesives are used; (5) LIFESPAN — overlay installations have higher failure rates than full removals, particularly if the original tiles are loose or the substrate has movement. If your sustainability policy prohibits waste, consider: (1) overlay with a bonding primer and flexible adhesive from a manufacturer who warranties the system (Mapei, Laticrete, Tilepac have overlay systems); (2) remove and recycle the old tiles — some suppliers accept old porcelain for recycling into new tiles or road base; (3) donate removed tiles to Habitat for Humanity or similar. Calculate the lifecycle carbon impact — overlay avoids demolition waste but may result in earlier replacement if it fails.

#### Q: 99 Our store design includes a specific tile pattern (herringbone) that requires skilled installation. Our Limpopo contractor has never done herringbone and has produced visibly uneven joints. Our brand standards require consistency nationwide. Can we reject the work and demand replacement?
**Answer:** Yes, if the brand standard specification clearly defines the installation pattern, joint width, and tolerance (e.g., "herringbone pattern, 2mm joints, maximum 1mm variation in joint width, maximum 2mm lippage"). Under the Consumer Protection Act (if applicable) or common law, you are entitled to work that meets the agreed specification. However: (1) was the contractor adequately briefed and trained? If you provided no pattern guidance or training, you share responsibility; (2) is there a reasonable rectification option? Minor joint variation may be acceptable within tolerances; (3) replacement cost — if the tiles are damaged during removal, who bears the cost? Best practice for national rollouts with complex patterns: (1) produce a detailed installation guide with photographs; (2) require a sample panel (minimum 2m²) approved before full installation; (3) use only contractors who have completed the specific pattern before, or provide on-site training by a specialist; (4) include pattern complexity in the contract pricing — herringbone costs 30-50% more in labour than straight lay. For Limpopo: either bring in an experienced installer or accept straight lay for that store if brand consistency allows.
## 23. PAYMENT, VAT & FINANCIAL

#### Q: 100 Our tiling contractor has invoiced us including VAT at 15%, but their VAT registration number on the invoice is invalid (checked on SARS eFiling). We have already paid the invoice. Can we claim the input VAT, and what is our risk?
**Answer:** You CANNOT claim input VAT if the supplier's VAT registration is invalid. SARS requires a valid tax invoice with a correct VAT registration number for input tax claims. If you have already paid: (1) you cannot claim the VAT portion as input tax; (2) if you already claimed it and SARS audits, you will face penalties and interest on disallowed input tax; (3) you have a claim against the contractor for recovery of the VAT paid (they charged VAT without being registered, which is illegal); (4) the contractor may face criminal prosecution under the VAT Act for charging VAT while unregistered (Section 58). Immediate action: (1) demand the contractor provide their valid VAT registration certificate; (2) if they are not VAT registered, request a revised invoice without VAT (at the net amount) and a refund of VAT paid; (3) if they refuse, report to SARS and consider civil recovery; (4) adjust your VAT return to remove the input tax claim if already submitted. For future: verify every new supplier's VAT status on SARS eFiling before first payment. Include a warranty in all contracts that the supplier is duly VAT registered and will remain so.

#### Q: 101 Our supplier has offered a 5% early payment discount if we pay within 7 days instead of 30. Our cost of capital is 12% per annum. Should we take the discount, and what is the effective annualised saving?
**Answer:** Calculate the effective annualised rate of the discount. A 5% discount for paying 23 days early (day 7 vs. day 30) translates to: (5% / 95%) × (365 / 23) = 83.5% effective annualised return. This far exceeds your 12% cost of capital — taking the discount is highly advantageous. The formula: Effective Annual Rate = (Discount % / (100% - Discount %)) × (365 / (Normal term - Discount term)). At these numbers, the early payment discount is one of the best returns on cash available. However: (1) ensure your supplier is financially stable — early payment to a distressed supplier may mean you become an unsecured creditor if they fail; (2) verify that the discount applies to the full invoice value including VAT; (3) check if the discount is conditional on anything else (minimum order quantities, specific products); (4) ensure your accounts payable system can process within 7 days — many corporate systems have longer approval cycles. Negotiate: if 5% is offered for 7-day payment, what discount applies for 14-day payment? The supplier's cost of capital is likely higher than yours (SMEs often pay 20-30% for working capital), so there is room to negotiate a middle ground.

#### Q: 102 Our tiling supplier is a Level 1 B-BBEE EME (Exempt Micro-Enterprise) with annual turnover below R10 million. They have no VAT registration. We want to claim B-BBEE procurement recognition. Can we claim on a non-VAT invoice?
**Answer:** Yes. B-BBEE procurement recognition is based on the total value of procurement from B-BBEE-compliant suppliers, regardless of VAT status. For non-VAT-registered EMEs: (1) the invoice value is the total amount paid (no VAT separation needed); (2) you can claim the full invoice value as B-BBEE-compliant procurement; (3) the EME's B-BBEE status (Level 1 or Level 2 for 51%+ black-owned, or Level 4 for 100% black-owned with turnover between R10m-R50m) is based on their sworn affidavit or CIPC certificate, not VAT registration. However: (1) the payment must be properly documented (invoice, proof of payment, delivery note); (2) the supplier must be a legitimate trading entity (registered with CIPC or have a valid business license); (3) if the supplier is not registered for VAT, they cannot issue tax invoices — a standard invoice is sufficient for B-BBEE but not for your VAT input tax claim. Ensure the supplier's B-BBEE certificate or sworn affidavit is current and stored with the procurement record. Your verification agency will request this evidence.
## 24. DIGITAL INTEGRATION & E-PROCUREMENT

#### Q: 103 Our head office requires all suppliers to integrate with SAP Ariba for electronic procurement. Our tile supplier is a small family business with no IT capability. Who bears the cost of Ariba integration, and is there an alternative?
**Answer:** SAP Ariba integration costs vary depending on the level: (1) ARIBA NETWORK (basic) — free for suppliers to join and receive POs; (2) CATALOG (cXML punch-out) — requires IT development, typically R50,000-R200,000 depending on the supplier's existing systems; (3) FULL INTEGRATION (EDI, invoicing, ASN) — significant IT investment. For small suppliers: SAP Ariba offers a "Lite" version that is free to join. The cost allocation depends on your procurement policy: (1) some large corporates absorb the integration cost as part of supplier onboarding; (2) others require suppliers to self-fund as a condition of doing business; (3) some offer a hybrid (corporate pays for initial setup, supplier pays for ongoing maintenance). For a small tile supplier unable to integrate: (1) use the Ariba Network basic tier (free) for PO and invoice exchange; (2) host a static catalog (non-punch-out) on Ariba — lower functionality but no integration cost; (3) use a third-party catalog hosting service that intermediates between the supplier and Ariba; (4) if the supplier represents critical spend, consider direct integration investment as part of your ESD programme — this supports small supplier development. The PAIA and B-BBEE frameworks encourage preferential procurement from SMEs — requiring expensive IT integration may exclude small B-BBEE suppliers, undermining your procurement strategy.

#### Q: 104 Our procurement system requires each store to raise individual purchase orders for maintenance tiles. The supplier has imposed a R500 minimum order charge per PO. With 200 stores, this is costing us R100,000 annually in minimum charges alone. How should we restructure this?
**Answer:** This is a procurement process inefficiency. Solutions: (1) CENTRALISED PROCUREMENT — consolidate all store tile requirements into a single national purchase order with scheduled deliveries to individual stores. One PO, one delivery schedule, no minimum order charges; (2) BLANKET PURCHASE ORDER — negotiate a 12-month framework PO with call-off facilities. Stores order against the framework without raising new POs; (3) STOCKING HUB — establish regional distribution hubs (e.g., Gauteng hub, Western Cape hub, KZN hub) where stores collect or receive small-quantity deliveries from local stock; (4) CROSS-DOCKING — supplier delivers to a central DC, which breaks bulk and distributes to stores as part of existing logistics flows; (5) NEGOTIATE — if decentralised ordering must continue, negotiate a waiver of minimum order charges for stores ordering above a threshold, or a flat annual administration fee instead of per-PO charges. Calculate the total cost of each option — centralised procurement typically saves 15-25% on total procurement cost through volume leverage and reduced transaction costs. The supplier will also prefer fewer, larger orders — use this as negotiation leverage.

#### Q: 105 Our e-procurement system automatically assigns product codes to each tile SKU. The supplier changed their product code for the same tile (new packaging), and our system created a duplicate entry. We now have two product codes for identical tiles at different prices. How do we prevent this?
**Answer:** This is a master data management (MDM) issue common in large procurement systems. Prevention: (1) IMPLEMENT A GOLDEN RECORD SYSTEM — assign internal product codes that are independent of supplier codes; map supplier codes to your internal codes; when a supplier changes their code, only the mapping changes, not the master record; (2) SUPPLIER MASTER DATA AGREEMENT — contractually require the supplier to notify you 90 days in advance of any product code changes; (3) DUPLICATE DETECTION — implement fuzzy matching algorithms that flag potential duplicates based on product description, dimensions, and specification; (4) REGULAR AUDIT — quarterly review of all active SKUs to identify duplicates and price discrepancies; (5) ATTRIBUTE-BASED CODING — use a coding structure based on product attributes (material, size, colour, finish) rather than supplier codes. For the immediate problem: conduct a full SKU rationalisation; identify all duplicates; benchmark the two prices against market; lock the lower price for all future orders; update the master data record. Consider implementing a Product Information Management (PIM) system if SKU count exceeds 1,000 — manual management becomes unmanageable at scale.
## 25. LEGAL, REGULATORY & DISPUTE

#### Q: 106 Our tiling contractor has placed a lien on our store, refusing to allow access until final payment is made. We dispute the final account (defective work). What does the law say about construction liens in South Africa?
**Answer:** South African common law recognises the contractor's right of retention (ius retentionis or lien) over the property until payment is received. However: (1) the lien only applies to the contractor's own work — they cannot lien the entire store for a tiling dispute; (2) the lien is lost if the contractor voluntarily surrenders possession (they must maintain physical control); (3) the lien cannot be used to enforce an undisputed amount — if there is a genuine dispute over defective work, the contractor must resolve the dispute or accept the reduced payment; (4) the contractor cannot prevent access by changing locks or using force — this constitutes trespass or criminal intimidation. Your response: (1) engage legal counsel immediately; (2) if the contractor is occupying your premises unlawfully, apply for an eviction order under the Prevention of Illegal Eviction Act; (3) withhold the disputed amount but pay any undisputed portion — this weakens the contractor's lien argument; (4) document the defects with photographs and independent assessment; (5) if the contractor has valid security of payment rights under the contract (e.g., JBCC payment certificate system), follow the contractual dispute resolution procedure. Do NOT attempt forced entry without legal clearance — this escalates to criminal charges.

#### Q: 107 Our franchise agreement contains a "sole supplier" clause for tiles. The Competition Commission has announced an investigation into anti-competitive practices in the building materials sector. Could our sole supplier arrangement be challenged?
**Answer:** Potentially yes. Section 5 of the Competition Act 89 of 1998 prohibits exclusive dealing arrangements that substantially prevent or lessen competition. A franchise sole supplier clause: (1) is NOT automatically illegal — the Competition Tribunal recognises that franchise systems require brand consistency; (2) may be challenged if the sole supplier arrangement forecloses competitors from the market, particularly if the franchisor receives rebates or benefits from the nominated supplier; (3) is more vulnerable if the supplier charges above-market prices and franchisees cannot source alternatives. The Competition Commission's 2023-2024 focus on building materials (cement, steel, timber) may extend to tiling. To protect yourself: (1) ensure the nominated supplier was selected through competitive tender, not favouritism; (2) ensure the arrangement is disclosed in the franchise disclosure document (required under CPA Section 7(2)); (3) allow franchisees to request alternative suppliers if the nominated supplier cannot meet specification, price, or delivery requirements; (4) do not receive secret rebates — disclose any financial benefits from the supplier arrangement. The Competition Act allows for administrative penalties up to 10% of annual turnover for anti-competitive conduct.

#### Q: 108 Our tiling contractor was found to be employing undocumented foreign workers (from Mozambique and Zimbabwe). As the client, what is our legal exposure under the Immigration Act?
**Answer:** The Immigration Act 13 of 2002 (as amended) criminalises the employment of illegal foreigners. Section 38 makes it an offence for any person to "employ an illegal foreigner." As the CLIENT, you are not the employer — the contractor is. However: (1) if you KNOWINGLY contracted a contractor who employs illegal workers, you may face charges of aiding and abetting; (2) if the contractor is deemed your "employee" under labour law (the dominant impression test), you may be deemed the employer; (3) OHSA makes you responsible for site safety regardless of who employs the workers — if undocumented workers are injured, you face liability; (4) the contractor's B-BBEE status may be revoked if found to be engaging in illegal labour practices (fronting investigation trigger). Immediate action: (1) terminate the contractor's appointment if the illegal employment is confirmed; (2) report to the Department of Home Affairs; (3) verify replacement contractors' labour compliance before appointment; (4) include contractual warranties that all workers are legally documented. The contractor faces: fines, imprisonment, and deportation of workers. Your brand reputation risk is significant if media reports illegal labour in your stores.

#### Q: 109 Our store's tiled floor was damaged during a protest march (service delivery protest in the area). The mob threw stones and set a bin alight on our doorstep, cracking the tiles with the heat. Our insurance policy excludes "damage arising from civil commotion, riot, or insurrection." Is this exclusion enforceable, and do we have any other recourse?
**Answer:** The exclusion is likely enforceable if clearly stated in the policy. "Civil commotion" and "riot" are standard insurance exclusions in South Africa due to the high frequency of protest-related damage. However: (1) check if the damage was caused by FIRE — fire following riot may be covered under the fire section even if riot is excluded; (2) check for SASRIA cover — the South African Special Risks Insurance Association provides cover for politically motivated damage, riots, strikes, and terrorism. SASRIA is compulsory for all commercial policies in South Africa — your broker should have arranged it. If you have SASRIA cover (check your policy schedule), claim through them; (3) if neither insurance nor SASRIA applies, you have a civil claim against the identified perpetrators — practically difficult unless individuals are arrested and solvent; (4) the Riotous Assemblies Act and Trespass Act provide criminal sanctions but not compensation. Prevention: many retailers in high-protest areas now specify impact-resistant tiles, remove loose objects from storefronts, and install security shutters. Update your insurance: ensure SASRIA premiums are current — many businesses discovered they were not covered during the July 2021 unrest.

#### Q: 110 Our landlord has issued a compliance notice requiring us to remove our floor tiles and reinstate the original screed at lease end. The lease says we must "make good." We installed R800,000 of tiles. Do we really have to rip them out?
**Answer:** "Make good" clauses are interpreted based on the specific lease wording and the nature of the landlord's property. Generally: (1) if the lease requires reinstatement of the original condition, you may be obligated to remove tenant improvements; (2) however, many landlords will accept the improvements in lieu of reinstatement if they add value — negotiate a surrender payment or simply leave the tiles (the landlord may prefer them); (3) if the landlord genuinely wants reinstatement, they must give reasonable notice and the cost of removal is yours; (4) the Consumer Protection Act may limit "make good" clauses if they are unfair — but the CPA primarily protects consumers, not commercial tenants. Check your lease: (1) does it define "make good" specifically? (2) is there a "surrender in lieu" clause? (3) does the landlord have the right to accept improvements at a valuation? Practical approach: negotiate with the landlord — most commercial landlords prefer quality improvements to remain. If removal is required, the landlord cannot claim both reinstatement AND payment for the value of the improvements. Document all negotiations. The R800,000 should have been depreciated over the lease term in your accounts — any remaining book value is a loss on disposal.
## 26. HERITAGE, CULTURAL & COMMUNITY

#### Q: 111 We are building a store on land that was historically a sacred site for the local Khoi-San community. During site preparation, workers discovered stone tools and ochre fragments in the subfloor. What are our obligations under the National Heritage Resources Act?
**Answer:** Section 35 of the National Heritage Resources Act 25 of 1999 requires that ANY discovery of archaeological or palaeontological material must be reported to the responsible heritage authority (SAHRA for archaeological material; DMR for fossils) immediately. You must: (1) STOP all work in the affected area immediately; (2) secure the find — do not disturb or remove artefacts; (3) notify SAHRA within 24 hours — they will assess and may issue a permit for continued work with conditions, or require a full archaeological investigation; (4) engage a SAHRA-accredited archaeologist to conduct a Heritage Impact Assessment if required; (5) budget for delays — archaeological investigations typically take 3-6 months. The Khoi-San community may also have rights under the Traditional Leadership and Governance Framework Act — engage with the local traditional council. Failure to report archaeological finds is a criminal offence under Section 189 (fine up to R1 million or imprisonment up to 10 years). For future developments: conduct a pre-construction heritage survey (archaeological walkover) before breaking ground — this identifies risks early and allows planning.

#### Q: 112 Our store in a township is subject to the Local Government: Municipal Finance Management Act requirements for municipal service charges. We have been billed "refuse removal" charges for construction debris from our tiling installation, even though we used a private waste contractor. Can the municipality charge us twice?
**Answer:** No — the municipality cannot charge for refuse removal if you did not use their service, provided your private contractor is licensed and lawfully disposed of the waste. However: (1) some municipalities levy a "availability charge" for refuse removal regardless of use — check your municipal bylaws; (2) if the charge is based on property rates and not usage, it may be a non-negotiable municipal levy; (3) you must provide proof that private waste removal was used (waste manifest, disposal certificate from licensed facility). Challenge the charge: (1) write to the municipal billing department with your waste disposal certificates attached; (2) request reversal of the charge; (3) if refused, lodge a dispute in terms of the Municipal Finance Management Act Section 96; (4) escalate to the provincial MEC for local government if unresolved. Document everything — the PFMA requires municipalities to justify all charges. For future projects: notify the municipality in writing before construction that private waste removal will be used, and request confirmation that no municipal refuse charges will apply.

#### Q: 113 Our store in a rural Eastern Cape location requires tiling work. The local traditional council (inkosi) has demanded a "community development levy" of R50,000 before work can begin. Is this a legitimate charge, and what is its legal basis?
**Answer:** This is a complex issue of customary law vs. statutory law. Under the Traditional Leadership and Governance Framework Act 41 of 2003, traditional councils have certain powers over communal land, but they cannot levy arbitrary charges on commercial operations on privately owned or municipally zoned land. Assessment: (1) is your store on communal land (under traditional council jurisdiction) or privately owned/leased land? If the latter, the council has no legal authority to levy charges; (2) if the land is communal, the council may have legitimate authority to impose conditions; (3) even on private land, community engagement may be prudent for social licence to operate. The R50,000 demand: (1) if it is a legitimate community benefit sharing agreement (e.g., local employment, local procurement), it may be justifiable as part of your SED (Socio-Economic Development) spend under B-BBEE; (2) if it is an arbitrary extraction with no benefit in return, it may constitute extortion. Approach: (1) verify the legal status of the land; (2) engage the council formally through a written meeting; (3) negotiate a structured community benefit (local employment, training, or infrastructure contribution) rather than a cash payment; (4) document any payment as SED spend for B-BBEE purposes; (5) if the demand is clearly extortionate, report to the SAPS and engage legal counsel. Be aware that challenging traditional authority can affect community relations — balance legal rights with practical community engagement.
## 27. EMPLOYEE, LABOUR & OHSA

#### Q: 114 Our tiling contractor's employees are working in our store during trading hours. A customer has complained that the contractors are not wearing uniforms and look "unprofessional." Our brand standards require all on-site personnel to wear branded attire. Can we enforce this on a contractor's employees?
**Answer:** Yes, through your contract. As the client, you can specify dress code and appearance standards for all personnel working on your premises — this is a standard occupational health and safety and brand protection requirement. The contractor's employees, while employed by the contractor, are working in YOUR controlled environment and represent YOUR brand to customers. Your contract should include: (1) a code of conduct for contractor personnel including dress code, hygiene, and behaviour standards; (2) the right to refuse access to any contractor employee who does not comply; (3) a requirement for branded PPE or uniforms (which you may provide or specify); (4) the right to require removal of any employee for misconduct. This is not an employment law issue — you are not employing the workers, you are setting conditions for access to your premises. The contractor must ensure compliance or face penalties, suspension, or termination. Note: if you specify branded uniforms, consider whether this creates a "dominant impression" of employment — if workers wear your logo exclusively, they may be deemed your employees for labour law purposes. Use joint branding (contractor logo + your logo) or specify "client site attire" rather than your full uniform.

#### Q: 115 A contractor's employee was injured when a tile stack collapsed while they were working alone in our store after hours. The contractor had no first aid kit on site. What are our liabilities as the occupier under OHSA?
**Answer:** As the occupier of the premises, you have duties under OHSA Section 8 to maintain a safe environment for all persons on your premises, including contractors. The contractor also has duties under OHSA as the employer. Liabilities: (1) OCCUPIER LIABILITY — if the tile stack was your property and was inherently unstable, or if you knew of the hazard and did not warn the contractor, you may be liable; (2) CONTRACTOR LIABILITY — the contractor failed to provide first aid (OHSA General Safety Regulations Regulation 3), failed to supervise the employee, and failed to conduct a risk assessment — these are clear OHSA violations; (3) JOINT LIABILITY — both parties may share liability depending on the facts. Immediate actions: (1) report the incident to the Department of Labour within the prescribed timeframe (serious injuries must be reported within 7 days); (2) preserve the scene for investigation; (3) provide the injured worker with access to medical treatment; (4) review your contractor management programme — require proof of OHSA compliance, first aid kits, and supervision plans before work commences. The contractor faces criminal charges under OHSA for the first aid violation (fine up to R100,000 or 2 years imprisonment). Your insurance (public liability) should cover occupier liability claims.

#### Q: 116 Our cleaning staff use a commercial steam cleaner (150°C) on our tiled floors daily. The grout has started to crumble. We specified the grout but the cleaning company (contracted) chose the cleaning method. Who is responsible for the damage?
**Answer:** Responsibility depends on the contract terms and specification clarity. If YOU specified the grout without reference to cleaning methods, and the cleaning company used industry-standard equipment, there may be shared liability: (1) the GROUT may have been inadequate for the specified cleaning regime — if you required steam cleaning in the original specification, the grout should have been steam-resistant; (2) the CLEANING COMPANY may have exceeded recommended parameters (150°C is extremely high for daily use — most epoxy grouts are rated to 80-100°C regular exposure); (3) if the cleaning method was not discussed during specification, neither party considered it. Resolution: (1) test the grout to identify the failure mode; (2) review the cleaning company's method statement — did it specify 150°C? (3) check the grout manufacturer's technical data sheet for temperature limits; (4) if the grout was unsuitable for steam cleaning, the original tiling contractor may be liable for defective specification; (5) if the cleaning company exceeded manufacturer's recommendations, they are liable. For future: include the cleaning method (steam temperature, pressure, frequency) in the flooring specification; select grout rated for the actual cleaning regime; provide cleaning method training to all facilities staff.
## 28. TECHNICAL, WARRANTY & DEFECTS

#### Q: 117 Our large-format tiles (1200×600mm) have developed "lipping" — height differences between adjacent tiles — of up to 3mm. The tile supplier blames the adhesive; the adhesive supplier blames the substrate; the contractor blames the tiles (inconsistent thickness). How do we determine liability?
**Answer:** This requires independent forensic investigation. Lipping in large-format tiles (LFT) can result from multiple causes: (1) TILE THICKNESS VARIATION — measure tile thickness at multiple points across 10 random tiles using digital calipers. SANS 1449 allows ±5% thickness variation, but for rectified LFT, tighter tolerances are expected. Variation >0.5mm across a single tile causes lipping regardless of installation quality; (2) SUBSTRATE FLATNESS — measure substrate deviation with a 2m straightedge. For tiles >600mm, substrate must be flat to ±3mm under a 2m straightedge (conforming to SANS 10109). If the substrate exceeds this, the adhesive bed cannot compensate; (3) ADHESIVE APPLICATION — check for voids (hollow-sounding tiles), notched trowel pattern (should be correct size for tile format), and whether back-buttering was used (essential for LFT); (4) INSTALLER TECHNIQUE — was a levelling system used (clips/wedges)? LFT should not be installed without a levelling system. Engage a registered professional engineer or the Tile Council of South Africa (TCSA) for independent assessment. Document everything with photographs and measurements. The party responsible for the defective element bears the remediation cost — but recovery depends on your contract terms with each party.

#### Q: 118 Our tile supplier has provided a 10-year product warranty but specifies that claims must be made within 30 days of discovering the defect. We discovered a batch defect 18 months after installation in a store that was not yet open to the public. Is the 30-day claim period enforceable?
**Answer:** Under the Consumer Protection Act Section 55, goods must be reasonably durable and free of defects. A 30-day claim period for a 10-year warranty is likely unenforceable against a consumer — the CPA deems such terms unfair, unreasonable, or unjust (Section 48). For B2B contracts, the position is less clear — courts may uphold contractual time limits if they are reasonable and clearly communicated. However: (1) 30 days is unreasonably short for a defect that manifests gradually or in a store not yet operational; (2) if the defect is a latent manufacturing defect (not visible on installation), you could not reasonably have discovered it within 30 days; (3) the CPA's 6-month implied warranty (Section 56) overrides any shorter contractual period for consumer transactions. Your argument: the claim period starts from when the defect was DISCOVERED or should reasonably have been discovered, not from installation. If the store was unoccupied, discovery was delayed. Challenge the 30-day clause as an unfair contract term under CPA Section 48. For future contracts: negotiate a "reasonable time after discovery" claim period, minimum 12 months from discovery. The NCR (National Consumer Regulator) has issued guidance that unreasonably short claim periods are presumptively unfair.

#### Q: 119 Our store's tiles make a "hollow" sound when tapped, indicating voids under the tiles (lack of adhesive contact). The contractor claims this is "normal for large format tiles" and doesn't affect performance. Is this correct?
**Answer:** No — hollow-sounding tiles indicate inadequate adhesive bedding and ARE a defect. SANS 10107 and manufacturer specifications require full bedding (minimum 65% adhesive contact for walls, 80% for floors in dry areas, 95%+ in wet areas). Hollow voids: (1) create weakness under point loads — the tile can crack; (2) allow moisture ingress in wet areas; (3) cause the tile to sound different (hollow) which is itself a quality issue; (4) may indicate the contractor used an incorrect trowel notch size, incorrect adhesive, or did not back-butter large format tiles. The "hollow sound is normal" claim is false. Test method: ASTM D4541 (pull-off test) measures actual adhesion strength. If adhesion is below 0.5MPa for floor tiles, the installation is defective. Rectification: the affected tiles must be removed and reinstalled with proper adhesive application. For large format tiles (>900mm on any side), back-buttering (applying adhesive to both substrate and tile back) is mandatory per SANS 10109. Do not accept the contractor's explanation — demand independent testing.

#### Q: 120 Our tile expansion joints have filled with hard debris (sand, grit, small stones) over 5 years of store operation. The tiles are now showing stress cracks at the joints. Is this a maintenance issue or an installation defect?
**Answer:** This is a maintenance issue, not an installation defect. Expansion/movement joints are designed to compress and expand — they must be kept clean and free of rigid debris to function. When joints fill with hard material, they lose their compressibility. Thermal and structural movement then transfers stress to the tiles, causing cracking at the joint edges. Responsibility: (1) the FACILITIES MANAGER / STORE OPERATIONS team is responsible for routine joint maintenance — joints should be inspected quarterly and cleaned/resealed annually; (2) the original installer is NOT responsible for maintenance failures years after handover; (3) the tile supplier is not responsible. Remediation: (1) remove all debris from affected joints; (2) inspect joint depth — if debris has compressed the joint filler below the required depth, the joint must be re-cut and re-filled; (3) replace cracked tiles adjacent to the joints; (4) implement a joint maintenance schedule in your store maintenance manual. For future: specify recessed joint covers that prevent debris ingress; or specify flush compression seals rather than open joints in high-traffic retail areas.
## 29. NEIGHBOUR & LANDLORD DISPUTES

#### Q: 121 Our tiling contractor started work at 6:30 AM. The neighbouring tenant (a medical clinic) has complained that the noise violates the mall's operational rules (trading hours start at 9 AM). The mall manager has threatened to stop work. Who is correct?
**Answer:** Mall operational rules typically restrict construction noise to before and after trading hours, but the specific hours vary by mall. Standard mall rules: (1) construction work is permitted from 6:00 PM to 8:00 AM (after-hours) OR from 6:00 AM to 9:00 AM (early morning) depending on the mall; (2) NO noisy work during trading hours (9 AM – 9 PM); (3) the tenant (you) is responsible for your contractor's compliance with mall rules. If your mall rules specify no work before 9 AM, the clinic is correct and the mall manager can enforce the rules. Check: (1) your lease annexure specifying construction hours; (2) the mall's tenant handbook or rules; (3) whether you obtained prior written approval for the work schedule. Even if the mall allows early work, the municipal noise bylaw may restrict construction noise before 7 AM — check your local municipality's Environmental Management Bylaw. Johannesburg and Cape Town both regulate construction noise hours. Resolution: reschedule noisy work (cutting, demolition) to approved hours; request a variance from the mall manager with specific justification; offer to notify neighbouring tenants of the schedule in advance.

#### Q: 122 Our upstairs neighbour (another tenant) has a water leak that has stained our ceiling tiles and damaged our floor tiles below. Our landlord says this is a "tenant-to-tenant issue" and refuses to intervene. Who is responsible for repairs?
**Answer:** The landlord IS responsible. Under the lease and common law: (1) the landlord must maintain the structural elements of the building, including waterproofing between floors; (2) the upstairs tenant may be liable if their negligence caused the leak (e.g., overflowing equipment), but the building envelope is the landlord's responsibility; (3) your landlord cannot simply declare it a "tenant-to-tenant issue" — they have a contractual obligation to provide premises fit for purpose. Your course of action: (1) notify the landlord in WRITING of the leak and damage, with photographs; (2) request immediate repair of the source (upstairs) and remediation of your damage; (3) if the landlord refuses, you have the right to: withhold rent (do this cautiously and with legal advice); arrange repairs yourself and deduct from rent (only if the lease permits or if the landlord has been given reasonable time to act); claim damages for loss of use. The landlord can recover from the upstairs tenant if negligence is proven, but that is their problem, not yours. If the landlord is a body corporate in a sectional title scheme, Section 37 of the Sectional Titles Act makes them responsible for maintenance of common property and structural elements.

#### Q: 123 Our landlord has refused to allow our tiling contractor to use the mall's loading dock, claiming it's reserved for "major deliveries only." Our tiles must come through the front entrance, which is disruptive. Does the landlord have this right?
**Answer:** Check your lease: (1) does it include a right to use common facilities including loading docks for fit-out and maintenance? (2) does the landlord's refusal contravene the "quiet enjoyment" clause? Most commercial leases grant tenants reasonable access to service areas for fit-out, maintenance, and delivery purposes. However, the landlord can impose reasonable conditions (time restrictions, prior booking, size limits). If your lease grants loading dock access, the landlord cannot arbitrarily refuse. If the lease is silent, the landlord's position is stronger but still subject to the principle of reasonable use of common property. Approach: (1) cite the lease clause granting access; (2) offer to schedule deliveries at approved times; (3) if the landlord persists, escalate to the mall's centre manager or regional property manager; (4) if this is a genuine obstruction affecting your ability to trade, it may constitute a breach of lease giving you the right to claim damages. For future leases: negotiate explicit loading dock access rights for fit-out and maintenance, including after-hours access if needed.
## 30. RURAL, TRIBAL & SPECIAL JURISDICTION

#### Q: 124 We are tiling a new store on tribal land in rural KwaZulu-Natal. The lease is with the tribal council, not a formal landlord. The inkosi has verbally approved the work but insists his "induna" must supervise all construction. What legal protections do we have if something goes wrong?
**Answer:** Construction on tribal land in KZN involves complex legal frameworks: the Traditional Leadership and Governance Framework Act, the KwaZulu-Natal Traditional Leadership and Governance Act, and the Communal Land Rights Act (ACT 11 of 2004). Key risks: (1) VERBAL APPROVALS are unenforceable — insist on written authority from the inkosi AND the tribal council; (2) without formal land tenure documentation, eviction or work stoppage is a real risk; (3) the induna's "supervision" does not create professional liability — they have no engineering or construction qualifications. Protect yourself: (1) obtain a formal resolution from the tribal council authorising the work; (2) ensure the lease or permission to occupy (PTO) is registered with the Department of Land Affairs; (3) engage a local attorney familiar with customary law to formalise the arrangement; (4) require the tribal council to warrant that they have authority to grant the lease (some land may be under competing claims); (5) obtain construction insurance that covers work on non-freehold land; (6) do not pay "facilitation fees" to individuals — any community benefit should be formalised and documented. The Ingonyama Trust controls much land in KZN — verify whether the land falls under Trust jurisdiction, which requires separate approvals.

#### Q: 125 Our Eastern Cape store is in a former homeland area where the municipality has limited capacity. Building plans submitted 8 months ago have not been approved. We have a contractual store opening deadline. Can we start work without approved plans?
**Answer:** Absolutely not. Section 4 of the National Building Regulations and Building Standards Act 103 of 1977 makes it illegal to commence construction without approved plans. Penalties: the municipality can issue a stop-work order; fines; and require demolition of unauthorised work. In the former homeland areas, municipal capacity issues are well-documented but do not excuse non-compliance. Options: (1) escalate within the municipality — meet with the building control officer directly; (2) engage a professional architect or project manager who has relationships with the municipality; (3) if the delay is unreasonable (beyond the 30-60 day statutory review period), apply for a Section 4(2) certificate from the provincial government authorising construction pending approval; (4) consider appointing a private building surveyor if the municipality has accredited private sector involvement (some Eastern Cape municipalities have this arrangement); (5) engage the MEC for Human Settlements if the municipality is clearly dysfunctional. Do NOT start work without plans approval — the risk of stop-work and demolition far outweighs the delay cost. Document all escalation efforts for your landlord and franchisor.

#### Q: 126 Our contractor in a rural Limpopo town has completed the tiling work but cannot provide a tax invoice because they are not registered for tax. We need the invoice for our records. What are our options?
**Answer:** A non-registered taxpayer cannot issue a tax invoice (which includes VAT). However, they can and must issue a standard invoice for the work performed. Options: (1) accept a standard invoice (without VAT) — this is legally valid for accounting purposes, though you cannot claim input VAT; (2) if the contractor is an individual (not a company), they may need to register as a provisional taxpayer if their income exceeds the tax threshold — encourage them to register with SARS; (3) if they refuse to provide any invoice, withhold payment until they do — a written invoice is a legal requirement under the Tax Administration Act for proof of expenditure; (4) for amounts above R10,000, you need proper documentation to claim the expense for tax purposes. Note: if the contractor is a casual labourer (not running a business), payments may be subject to PAYE or SITE deductions. For future rural projects: specify in the contract that the contractor must provide valid invoices; consider using a labour broker or payroll service if dealing with informal workers; engage a local accountant to assist contractors with compliance.
## 31. INTERNATIONAL TRADE & IMPORT

#### Q: 127 Our imported Italian tiles have been held at Customs for 3 weeks due to an ITAC (International Trade Administration Commission) investigation into alleged dumping by Italian manufacturers. We have store openings scheduled. What is ITAC's process, and can we get our tiles released?
**Answer:** ITAC investigates dumping (selling below normal value) and subsidisation under the International Trade Administration Act 71 of 2002. If your tiles are from a manufacturer under investigation: (1) all imports from that manufacturer may be subject to provisional payments or security deposits pending the investigation outcome; (2) SARS will hold the shipment until the provisional duty is paid or the investigation is resolved; (3) ITAC investigations typically take 6-12 months; provisional measures can be imposed within 60 days. To secure release: (1) pay any provisional anti-dumping duty or security deposit required by SARS; (2) if you believe your supplier is not dumping, provide evidence to ITAC (cost of production data, market pricing); (3) engage a customs broker with ITAC experience; (4) consider requesting an exemption if the tiles are not like products to those under investigation (specification argument). The South African Ceramic Tile Industry has successfully pursued anti-dumping duties against several countries — check if Italy is specifically targeted or if your supplier is named. For critical path items: hold 3 months' safety stock in-country; diversify supplier base across multiple countries; include force majeure clauses covering trade remedy actions.

#### Q: 128 We imported tiles from China with a declared customs value of R85/m². SARS has reassessed the value to R140/m² based on their internal benchmarks, demanding additional customs duty and VAT of R280,000. We believe the declared value is correct. What is our recourse?
**Answer:** SARS has broad powers of customs valuation under the Customs and Excise Act. They can reject the transaction value if they have reason to believe it does not reflect the true value (e.g., related-party transaction, abnormally low pricing, or benchmarking against similar imports). Your recourse: (1) REQUEST A REASONS LETTER from SARS explaining the basis for the reassessment; (2) provide SUPPORTING DOCUMENTATION — purchase orders, supplier invoices, proof of payment, manufacturer's price list, evidence that the price is at arm's length; (3) FILE AN OBJECTION within the prescribed timeframe (typically 30 days from assessment) to the Customs Appeals Unit; (4) if the objection is rejected, appeal to the Tax Board or Tax Court; (5) consider applying for a binding customs ruling (Section 47 of the Customs Control Act) for future shipments to prevent recurrence. Common issues: (1) under-valuation to save duty is a criminal offence — ensure your declared value is accurate; (2) SARS often uses WTO customs valuation methods (transaction value, identical goods, similar goods, deductive value, computed value, fall-back); (3) if your supplier is a related party, SARS will scrutinise the transfer pricing. Engage a customs attorney or specialist broker — customs valuation disputes can take 12-24 months to resolve. The additional amount will accrue interest during the dispute.

#### Q: 129 Our tile shipment from Spain arrived with 15% of tiles broken due to inadequate packaging. The supplier claims this is "shipping risk" and our responsibility under FOB terms. Our marine insurance has a R25,000 excess. The damage is R40,000. What is our position?
**Answer:** Under FOB (Free On Board) Incoterms 2020: (1) risk transfers when goods pass the ship's rail at the port of shipment (Spain); (2) the seller is responsible for export clearance and loading; (3) the buyer (you) bears all risk from loading onward, including sea transit and unloading. However: (1) the seller must provide adequate packaging — if inadequate packaging caused the breakage, the seller breached their obligation regardless of risk transfer; (2) the carrier may be liable if handling was negligent — file a claim with the shipping line under the Hague-Visby Rules; (3) your marine cargo insurance should cover transit damage (subject to the R25,000 excess). Course of action: (1) document the damage immediately with photographs before unloading; (2) obtain a surveyor's report (insurance requirement); (3) claim against marine insurance for R15,000 (R40,000 minus R25,000 excess); (4) claim the R25,000 excess from the supplier for inadequate packaging; (5) if the carrier was negligent, subrogate your claim to the insurer. For future: negotiate CIF (Cost, Insurance, Freight) terms where the seller arranges insurance; or DDP (Delivered Duty Paid) where the seller bears all risk until delivery. Specify packaging standards in the purchase order.
## 32. WARRANTY, RETENTION & FINAL ACCOUNT

#### Q: 130 Our tiling contract includes a 5% retention held for 12 months after practical completion. The 12 months expired, but we have discovered latent defects ( grout cracking) in 3 of the 50 stores completed under the contract. Can we withhold the full 5% retention for all 50 stores, or only for the defective 3?
**Answer:** Retention must be apportioned to the defective work only — you cannot withhold the entire retention for all stores due to defects in a minority. Under common law and standard JBCC provisions: (1) retention is security for the due performance of the contractor's obligations; (2) if defects appear, the employer can use the retention to fund repairs, but only to the extent of the repair cost; (3) the balance of the retention for defect-free work must be released on the due date; (4) you must give the contractor notice of defects and a reasonable opportunity to rectify before using retention funds. For the 3 defective stores: obtain repair quotes; notify the contractor; if they fail to rectify within the specified period, engage another contractor and deduct the cost from the retention. Release the balance of the retention for the 47 defect-free stores immediately. Unjustified withholding of retention may expose you to interest claims and legal costs. The CPA's fairness provisions (if applicable) reinforce this — retention must be reasonable and proportionate.

#### Q: 131 Our contractor has submitted a final account including R180,000 for "dayworks" (additional labour for unexpected substrate preparation) that were not pre-approved by our site agent. The contract requires written authorisation for all dayworks. Must we pay?
**Answer:** Under standard construction contract terms (JBCC, NEC, FIDIC), dayworks require prior authorisation to be claimable. The principle: the contractor cannot unilaterally incur additional cost and then claim it — the employer must have the opportunity to approve or refuse. However: (1) if the dayworks were genuinely necessary and the contractor could not reasonably have obtained prior approval (urgent safety issue, for example), an equitable claim may succeed; (2) if your site agent witnessed the work and did not object, there may be an implied authorisation; (3) if you benefited from the work (a better subfloor than specified), quantum meruit (reasonable value) may apply. Your position: (1) reject the dayworks claim due to lack of authorisation; (2) offer to pay only for dayworks that your site agent witnessed and acknowledged; (3) request detailed timesheets, labour records, and photographs for each daywork item; (4) if the contractor disputes, refer to the contract's dispute resolution mechanism (typically mediation or adjudication). For future: enforce the prior authorisation requirement strictly; ensure site agents understand their authority limits; implement a daily sign-off procedure for all extra work.

#### Q: 132 Our store's tiles failed within the NHBRC 5-year warranty period. However, the builder claims the failure was caused by our tenant's heavy equipment (reach truck), not defective construction. The NHBRC has declined the claim as "not a structural defect." What is our recourse?
**Answer:** The NHBRC 5-year warranty covers "major structural defects" — defined as defects that threaten the stability or safety of the structure. Tile failures are typically NOT structural defects unless they indicate a broader structural problem (slab movement, foundation failure). The NHBRC's decision is likely correct for a standalone tile issue. Your recourse: (1) against the TENANT — if the reach truck exceeded the floor's design load capacity, the tenant is liable for damage caused by overloading. Check your lease: does it specify maximum floor loads? Does it require tenant to notify you of heavy equipment? (2) against the BUILDER — if the floor was not designed for the intended use (retail with material handling equipment), the builder may be liable for defective design specification; (3) against the TILE SUPPLIER — if the tiles were not rated for the actual loads, product liability may apply; (4) INSURANCE — commercial policies may cover accidental damage. Commission a structural engineer to determine: the floor's design load; the actual loads imposed; whether the failure is due to overloading, inadequate design, or defective materials. This forensic evidence determines liability. For future: specify floor load ratings in all leases; require tenant disclosure of material handling equipment; install appropriate flooring in logistics areas.
## 33. ENVIRONMENTAL, ASBESTOS & HAZARDOUS MATERIALS

#### Q: 133 During store renovation, our contractor discovered asbestos-containing floor tiles (9×9 inch vinyl tiles, common in 1970s buildings). Work stopped immediately. Our landlord claims it's our responsibility as the tenant who initiated the renovation. Who is legally responsible for asbestos removal?
**Answer:** Under the Asbestos Regulations 2020 (promulgated under OHSA), the "employer" (the person who owns or controls the premises) is responsible for asbestos management. In a landlord-tenant relationship: (1) the LANDLORD is typically the "owner" and bears primary responsibility for asbestos identification, management, and removal; (2) the TENANT (you) has a duty not to disturb asbestos and to report its presence; (3) if the tenant's renovation work disturbed the asbestos, both parties may share liability. The Asbestos Regulations 2020 require: (1) an asbestos survey by a registered asbestos inspector before any demolition or renovation; (2) notification to the Department of Employment and Labour before removal; (3) removal only by licensed asbestos removal contractors; (4) disposal at licensed hazardous waste facilities. The landlord should have conducted an asbestos survey before leasing the premises — failure to disclose known asbestos is a breach of the lease and potentially a criminal offence. Do NOT allow unauthorised removal — penalties under the 2020 Regulations include fines up to R5 million and/or 5 years imprisonment. Demand the landlord engages a licensed asbestos removal contractor at their cost.

#### Q: 134 Our contractor removed vinyl tiles from our old store and disposed of them at a general landfill. We later discovered the tiles contained asbestos. What are our legal liabilities, and can we retrospectively remediate?
**Answer:** This is a serious environmental and criminal law issue.
