Costs incurred: (1) Standing time for mobilised gang: R15,000-25,000/week; (2) Site security during the stoppage; (3) Remobilisation costs; (4) Programme extension preliminaries. The contractor may claim under the "prevention principle" if the client failed to secure necessary permissions or community agreements. As QS, treat it as a delay event — assess cost and time impact, determine liability based on contract terms and the cause of the protest.

#### Q: 180. A contractor's tile delivery was hijacked en route from the supplier to site. The tiles were R340,000 value and uninsured. Who carries this loss?
**Answer:** Risk in building materials passes as specified in the contract. Under JBCC: (1) If the contractor arranged delivery (FOB or ex-works), risk passed to the contractor when the goods left the supplier — contractor's loss; (2) If the client arranged delivery (delivered to site), risk may not have passed to the contractor — client's loss; (3) If the contract is silent, common law applies — the party in possession bears the risk. In practice, the contractor usually carries risk from delivery to site. The lesson: the contractor should have goods-in-transit insurance (typically 0.3-0.5% of goods value). As QS, verify insurance provisions are adequate before certifying payment for materials not yet on site.
## CONTRACT ADMINISTRATION, DISPUTES & FINAL ACCOUNT

#### Q: 181. A contractor's final account includes R45,000 for "disposal of broken tiles" but his waste manifest shows only 12 tons were disposed of at a licensed facility. The BOQ allowed 5% wastage on 2,000m2 (100m2 = approximately 3 tons). How do I deal with the discrepancy?
**Answer:** The discrepancy suggests either: (1) The contractor disposed of waste at an unlicensed facility for part of the project; (2) The contractor is claiming for tiles that were broken due to his own negligence; (3) The waste manifest is incomplete. As QS: (1) Request all weighbridge tickets for the full project period; (2) Compare actual waste tonnage against the BOQ wastage allowance (3 tons allowed); (3) If actual waste exceeds the allowance significantly, investigate why — was the substrate defective? Were there design changes? Was there contractor negligence? (4) Only certify disposal costs for waste generated by the contract scope, not for contractor-caused breakage. If the contractor cannot substantiate licensed disposal for all waste, withhold payment pending documentation — illegal dumping is a criminal offence.

#### Q: 182. A subcontractor has issued a Section 77 notice (intention to suspend work for non-payment) after the main contractor failed to pay two progress certificates. The main contractor says the tiling is defective. What is the QS's role?
**Answer:** The QS's role is to independently quantify what is properly due. Under the Construction Industry Development Board Act (prompt payment provisions) and the subcontract, the subcontractor has a right to suspend if not paid within the contract period. As QS: (1) Re-measure the completed work — is the amount certified correct? (2) Identify any defects and quantify the cost of rectification; (3) Calculate the nett amount properly due after deducting retention and defects; (4) Recommend payment of the nett amount to avoid suspension. If the main contractor's defect claim is spurious (a common tactic to delay payment), say so in your report. Suspension will cost the project more than prompt payment.

#### Q: 183. The architect has issued a "direction" to change the tile colour from white to grey. The contractor says this is a variation; the architect says it's "just a colour change, same price." Who is right, and how do I cost it?
**Answer:** A colour change is a variation if the original colour was specified in the contract documents and the contractor priced for it. Even if the tile price is the same (white and grey may be the same cost from the manufacturer), the variation generates costs: (1) Tiles already ordered in white may need to be returned — restocking fee 15-25%; (2) New tiles must be ordered — potential lead time delay; (3) If white tiles were already delivered, storage or return costs; (4) Programme impact if the change occurs after ordering. As QS, value the variation at the contract rates for the tile supply (same rate if same price) but add the quantifiable costs of disruption. Do not accept "same price" without assessing these consequential costs.

#### Q: 184. A contractor has submitted a "loss and expense" claim of R180,000 due to "disruption caused by other trades working in tiling areas." What substantiation must I demand before assessing this claim?
**Answer:** For a disruption claim, demand: (1) A detailed diary showing dates, times, and descriptions of disruptive events — corroborated by site instructions or daily reports; (2) Evidence that the contractor gave written notice of the disruption at the time (not months later); (3) Records showing the actual gang deployment vs planned deployment — were tilers standing idle? (4) Comparison of actual productivity vs tender allowance for the disrupted period; (5) Financial records showing the additional cost incurred (overtime, additional supervision, extended preliminaries); (6) Mitigation efforts — did the contractor try to work elsewhere on site? Without contemporaneous records, the claim lacks credibility. Assess only the substantiated portion — typically 30-50% of initial disruption claims are unsubstantiated.

#### Q: 185. The final account has been agreed at R4.85 million. The contractor now discovers he underpriced the adhesive quantity by 15% — he used 20% more than his tender allowance but the BOQ rates are fixed. Can he claim the additional adhesive cost?
**Answer:** No. Under a remeasurable contract, the contractor is paid for the measured quantity at the contract rate — if he used more adhesive than his rate allows, that is his pricing error, not a basis for additional payment. The contract rate is deemed to include all materials, labour, and methodology necessary to complete the work. The only exception: if the specification changed (e.g., from standard to full-bed adhesion), the additional consumption is a variation. If the specification remained the same and the contractor simply underestimated his own consumption, he has no claim. This is a fundamental principle of lump sum and remeasurement contracts — the contractor carries his own rate build-up risk.

#### Q: 186. A client's representative on site has been signing daywork sheets without verifying the work. R280,000 of daywork has been certified. At final account, the client disputes the daywork as unsubstantiated. What is the QS's position?
**Answer:** This is a difficult position. If the client's representative signed the daywork sheets, the client is generally bound by those signatures — the representative had ostensible authority to sign. However, if the daywork was clearly fraudulent or excessive, the client may challenge it. As QS: (1) Verify the daywork sheets have the required signatures; (2) Compare the daywork against the contract's daywork provisions (were the correct rates applied? Was daywork only used where no contract rate exists?); (3) If procedurally correct, recommend payment — the client's recourse is against their representative, not the contractor; (4) If procedurally defective (unsigned, incorrect rates, applied where contract rates exist), adjust the certification. Going forward, advise the client to brief their representatives on signing authority and verification requirements.

#### Q: 187. The contract includes a "set-off" clause allowing the client to deduct costs of rectifying defects from amounts due to the contractor. The client has engaged another contractor to rectify tiling defects at R95,000 without giving the original contractor opportunity to rectify. Is the set-off valid?
**Answer:** Potentially not. Most standard contracts (JBCC included) require the client to give the contractor reasonable notice and opportunity to rectify defects before engaging others. If the client unilaterally engaged a replacement without notice, the set-off may be challengeable. As QS: (1) Verify whether written notice was given to the original contractor; (2) If no notice, the set-off is procedurally defective — the client should pay the certified amount and pursue the contractor separately for rectification costs; (3) If notice was given and the contractor failed to respond within the contract period (typically 7-14 days), the set-off is valid. Document the notice trail carefully — set-off disputes are common in final accounts.

#### Q: 188. A contractor's final account claim includes R45,000 for "professional fees for QS services to prepare the final account." Is this a legitimate contract cost?
**Answer:** No. A contractor's cost of preparing his own payment application or final account is part of his overhead — included in the preliminaries and profit percentage, not a separate claimable item. The contractor engaged his own QS (if he has one) at his own cost. The client's QS fees are also not claimable by the contractor — they are the client's professional fees. Reject this item. The only exception: if the contract specifically provides for reimbursement of the contractor's professional fees in dispute resolution or adjudication proceedings, and the final account preparation was part of a formal dispute process. Standard final account preparation is never a claimable extra.

#### Q: 189. A project has reached Practical Completion but 3% of tiling defects remain unresolved. The contractor says he will fix them during the defects liability period. The client wants to withhold the full retention (R420,000) until all defects are fixed. Who is correct?
**Answer:** Under JBCC, half retention is released at Practical Completion (subject to any deductions for defects). The remaining half is released at Final Completion. If defects exist at PC but the contractor has committed to rectify during DLP, the standard treatment is: (1) Certify PC; (2) Release 50% of retention (less any amounts for defects the contractor refuses to rectify); (3) Hold the remaining 50% until Final Completion; (4) List the defects on the defects list. The client cannot withhold the full retention solely because defects exist — the DLP exists precisely for this purpose. If the defects are major (structural, waterproofing failure), PC may not be achievable. But minor defects at PC are normal and expected.

#### Q: 190. A tiling contractor has submitted a "global claim" for R680,000 citing "numerous delays and disruptions" without apportioning the amount to specific events. How should a QS respond to a global claim?
**Answer:** Global claims (lump-sum claims without causal linkage) are generally not accepted in South African construction adjudication. The QS should respond: (1) Request the contractor to apportion the claim to specific delay/disruption events with causal linkage (event X caused delay Y which cost Z); (2) For each event, demand: date, description, notice given, substantiation of delay, financial quantification; (3) If the contractor cannot apportion because events were so intertwined that separation is impossible, accept a "rolled-up" claim only if: the contractor can prove the client caused the delay, the contractor's tender was realistic, and there is no better way to quantify the loss; (4) Apply a sceptical but fair assessment — global claims often contain inflated or duplicated amounts. Assess the substantiated portion only.
## THE TRULY UNUSUAL — EDGE CASES THAT ACTUALLY HAPPENED

#### Q: 191. Atiling contractor has included a "psychometric assessment fee" of R12,000 per tiler because the project is in a maximum-security forensic psychiatric hospital. Is this claimable?
**Answer:** Yes. High-security psychiatric facilities require all workers to pass psychological assessment and security vetting before site access. The assessment fee (R8,000-15,000 per person) is a real cost, as is the 3-6 week vetting delay. Additionally: (1) Tilers must be escorted at all times — productivity reduction of 20-30%; (2) No power tools with blades longer than 100mm (security restriction) — increased cutting time; (3) Tool inventory checks at every entry/exit; (4) Work only during designated hours when patients are in secure areas. Total premium over standard tiling: 40-60%. The psychometric fee is claimable as a preliminary item if it was a known contract requirement.

#### Q: 192. A mining company's head office wants floor tiles that incorporate their crushed ore waste (gold-bearing quartz) as a decorative aggregate. They want 5,000m2 of these custom tiles. How do I cost this?
**Answer:** This is a custom manufacturing project, not standard tiling procurement. Cost components: (1) Tile development and sampling: R150,000-250,000 (3-6 months); (2) Mould making for the custom size: R80,000-120,000; (3) Custom tile supply at R450-750/m2 (10x standard due to small-batch production); (4) Trial production batch: R50,000-80,000; (5) Quality testing and certification: R25,000-40,000; (6) Higher wastage allowance — 10-15% for custom tiles vs 5% for standard. Total: R2.5-4.0 million for 5,000m2. Treat as a design-and-build provisional sum with performance specification. The mining company may accept the high cost for the corporate branding value.

#### Q: 193. A contractor has claimed R35,000 for "tile acclimatisation" — storing imported tiles in an air-conditioned room for 14 days before installation because the site was in a humid coastal location. Is this legitimate?
**Answer:** Partially. Tile acclimatisation (allowing tiles to reach ambient temperature and moisture equilibrium before installation) is good practice, especially for large-format tiles in humid conditions where thermal/moisture shock can cause cracking. However, full air-conditioned storage is excessive — ambient storage under cover is sufficient for most tiles. A legitimate acclimatisation cost would be: covered storage space (R2,000-4,000 for 2 weeks), not air-conditioning. If the tiles are natural stone (particularly moisture-sensitive like green marble), controlled acclimatisation is justified. For standard porcelain, reject the air-conditioning cost but allow for covered on-site storage.

#### Q: 194. A zoo's primate enclosure requires floor tiles that are "gorilla-proof" — the primates have been known to tear up flooring. What specification and cost applies?
**Answer:** Gorilla and large primate enclosures require extreme-duty flooring: (1) Minimum 20mm thick full-body porcelain (not glazed — the glaze layer can be chipped); (2) Epoxy adhesive with steel reinforcement mesh embedded in the screed; (3) No perimeter gaps — tiles must be cut to fit tightly against walls with no edge to lift; (4) Screed must be minimum 75mm thick reinforced concrete, not standard 25mm screed; (5) Edge protection — steel angle strips at all exposed edges. Cost: R850-1,200/m2 — comparable to industrial flooring. The enclosure must be empty during installation (animal relocation costs are separate). This is a specialist zoo design consultant's domain — do not attempt to specify without specialist input.

#### Q: 195. A client wants to install LED lighting strips embedded in the grout joints between tiles on a feature wall. How does this affect the tiling cost, and who installs the LEDs?
**Answer:** LED grout lines are a specialist feature requiring coordination between tiling and electrical trades. Cost impacts: (1) Wider grout joints (8-10mm vs standard 3mm) to accommodate LED strip: minimal material cost difference; (2) LED channel extrusion recessed into the grout joint: R250-450/linear metre; (3) LED strip and driver: R180-350/linear metre; (4) Installation labour: tilers install the channel extrusion during grouting, electricians connect the LED strips after grouting — dual trade involvement adds 30-40% to labour; (5) Programming and commissioning: separate M&E cost. Total: R450-850/linear metre of LED grout line. Measure the linear metres of LED channels separately from tiling. The tiling contractor installs the channels; the electrical contractor installs the LED components.

#### Q: 196. A maritime museum wants tiles from a shipwreck (recovered ceramic cargo) installed as a feature floor. The tiles are irregular, 300 years old, and of varying thickness. How is this costed?
**Answer:** Shipwreck tile installation is specialist conservation work, not standard tiling. Cost approach: (1) Conservation cleaning and stabilisation of recovered tiles: R500-1,000 per tile; (2) Substrate preparation with adjustable bed system to accommodate varying tile thickness: R180-250/m2; (3) Hand-laying by conservator (not tiler) at 2-3 tiles per hour: labour constant of 2.0-3.0 hrs/m2; (4) Specialist adhesive compatible with archaeological ceramics: R65-95/m2; (5) No grout — tiles are butted with lime mortar infill; (6) Museum lighting and climate control must be operational during installation. Total: R2,500-4,000/m2. This is a provisional sum with a heritage conservator as the specialist contractor. Standard tiling rates are irrelevant.

#### Q: 197. A project requires tiling inside a Faraday cage room (EMI shielded). The specification says "standard floor tiles." What is the concern?
**Answer:** A Faraday cage room requires continuous electromagnetic shielding — all penetrations, including floor joints, must maintain electrical continuity. Standard tiles with cement grout create electrical discontinuity at every joint. The floor system must: (1) Use conductive epoxy adhesive and grout to maintain electrical continuity; (2) Incorporate a conductive underlayment bonded to the Faraday cage floor; (3) Have all tile joints bridged with conductive strips if epoxy grout is insufficient; (4) Be tested for shielding effectiveness after installation (R25,000-40,000 test). Cost premium: 50-70% over standard tiling. If the Faraday cage integrity is compromised by the tiling, the entire room fails certification. This is an M&E specialist specification, not a standard tiling item.

#### Q: 198. A client wants a "living floor" — tiles with embedded seeds that will germinate and grow moss between the tiles over time. Is this buildable, and what does it cost?
**Answer:** This is an experimental landscape architecture concept, not standard tiling. Technical approach: (1) Large-format porous tiles (terracotta or unglazed porcelain) with 20-30mm joints; (2) Substrate is a 150mm depth of growing medium, not standard screed; (3) Irrigation system embedded in the substrate; (4) Seeds or moss plugs planted in the joints after tile installation; (5) Drainage layer below the growing medium. Cost: R850-1,500/m2 for the complete system — comparable to intensive green roofing. The "tiling" component is minor; the growing system dominates. Maintenance is critical — moss requires shade and moisture. This should be priced as a landscape architecture provisional sum, not a tiling item. Most tiling contractors will not tender for this.

#### Q: 199. A contractor has included a "sacrificial anode" system to protect reinforcement in the concrete slab beneath the tiles. The project is 50km inland. Is this necessary, and what does it cost?
**Answer:** Sacrificial anode cathodic protection is used for reinforced concrete in chloride environments (coastal, industrial) or where carbonation has reached reinforcement depth. It is unnecessary 50km inland unless: (1) The concrete has severe carbonation (pH below 9); (2) There is a specific chloride contamination source (de-icing salts, industrial effluent). Cost: R450-850/m2 for impressed current cathodic protection, R250-450/m2 for sacrificial anodes. If the slab is sound and the environment is not corrosive, reject the item as unnecessary. If there is genuine reinforcement corrosion risk (confirmed by petrographic analysis), the issue is structural repair, not a tiling preliminary. The anode system is a structural maintenance item, not a tiling cost.

#### Q: 200. A client has asked me to prepare a "whole-life cost analysis" comparing 600x600mm porcelain tiles (R285/m2, 50-year life) with 500x500mm ceramic tiles (R195/m2, 25-year life). How do I present this comparison?
**Answer:** Whole-life cost analysis over 50 years: (1) Porcelain: initial cost R285/m2, no replacement, annual maintenance R5/m2 (cleaning, joint repair), 50-year cost = R285 + (R5 x 50) = R535/m2; (2) Ceramic: initial cost R195/m2, replacement at year 25 (R195/m2 in future value, discounted to present value at 8% = R28.50/m2 PV), annual maintenance R8/m2 (higher due to more frequent joint issues), 50-year cost = R195 + R28.50 + (R8 x 50) = R623.50/m2. The porcelain has lower whole-life cost despite higher initial cost. However, if the building has a 20-year design life, ceramic may be optimal. Present both scenarios with the discount rate and assumptions clearly stated. Whole-life cost is a powerful decision-making tool — it often justifies higher initial quality.

## END OF Q&A — 200 PAIRS FOR PERSONA 8: QUANTITY SURVEYORS

# PERSONA 9: SMALLER FLOORING MERCHANTS (WHOLESALE) — CURVEBALL Q&A
## EXCLUSIVE DISTRIBUTION RIGHTS & TERRITORIAL PROTECTION

#### Q: 1. I stock a ceramic range from a supplier who now gave exclusivity to a new CTM branch 800 metres from me. My supply agreement never mentioned radius protection. Do I have any legal recourse under the CPA?
**Answer:** If your written supply agreement does not contain a radius or territorial exclusivity clause, you unfortunately have no contractual claim. The CPA does not create territorial protection where none was agreed. Going forward, negotiate an exclusive distribution rider specifying a minimum radius (e.g., 5 km for urban, 25 km for peri-urban) and tie it to your MOQ commitment. Have it registered as a restraint of trade only if it meets the reasonableness test under South African common law — it must protect a legitimate proprietary interest and be no wider than necessary.

#### Q: 2. My supplier wants me to sign a new agreement giving me exclusivity for Limpopo but demands I take 3,000 m² per quarter. That is 40% more than my current turnover. Can I challenge this under the Competition Act as an abuse of dominance?
**Answer:** An exclusivity arrangement tied to an excessive MOQ can potentially be assessed under section 8 of the Competition Act if the supplier holds a dominant position in the relevant market (typically >35% market share). However, exclusivity agreements between non-dominant parties are generally permissible. Rather than a Competition Commission complaint, negotiate a ramp-up period — e.g., 1,500 m² in Q1, scaling to 3,000 m² by Q4 — with a clawback clause if you miss target through no fault of your own. Document your market size in Limpopo to justify a lower threshold.

#### Q: 3. A competitor merchant claims his "verbal exclusivity" from 2019 for a imported porcelain brand overrides my signed 2023 written distributorship. He has WhatsApp messages but no contract. Which agreement prevails?
**Answer:** Under the Alienation of Land Act and general contract principles, distributorship agreements need not be in writing to be valid, but exclusivity terms must be clear and certain. A vague WhatsApp exchange promising "you're my guy in Gauteng" is unlikely to constitute a binding exclusivity clause. Your signed written agreement with explicit territorial terms will almost certainly prevail. Advise the competitor that oral exclusivity claims are extremely difficult to prove and recommend he formalise any arrangement going forward.

#### Q: 4. I am the sole stockist of a Turkish tile brand in the Eastern Cape. The manufacturer now sells directly to a large Port Elizabeth developer via their website at prices below my landed cost. Is this parallel importation lawful?
**Answer:** If the manufacturer is selling directly into your territory — bypassing you as the authorised distributor — this constitutes a breach of your distribution agreement if it contains exclusivity and/or prohibition-on-direct-sales clauses. Under South African law, parallel importation of genuine goods (grey imports) is generally lawful unless restricted by contract or the Consumer Protection Act. Check if your agreement contains a "no direct sales" clause. If yes, issue a lawyer's letter demanding cessation and claiming damages. If your agreement is silent, this is a harsh lesson: always include anti-circumvention provisions.

#### Q: 5. My supplier granted me "non-exclusive" distribution rights but advertises my region on their website showing "find a stockist" with only my competitor listed. They are effectively steering customers away from me. Is this a CPA issue?
**Answer:** This is arguably misleading conduct under section 41 of the CPA — if the supplier's conduct creates the impression that your competitor is the only or preferred stockist. Write to the supplier demanding equal listing treatment, with a reasonable time limit. If they refuse, lodge a complaint with the National Consumer Commission. Simultaneously, build your own direct marketing presence (Google Business Profile, local SEO) so you are not dependent on their website. Consider whether this breach is material enough to justify termination of your supply agreement.
## CONSIGNMENT STOCK & RISK ALLOCATION

#### Q: 6. I want a supplier to place R800,000 of stock on consignment in my warehouse — they own it until sold. But their consignment agreement makes me liable for theft, fire, and water damage. Is this fair under the CPA?
**Answer:** This is a standard risk allocation in consignment arrangements but the CPA may regulate it. Under section 48, a term that makes the consumer (you) liable for damage not caused by your negligence may be an unfair contract term. Negotiate: (a) liability only for damage caused by your negligence or wilful misconduct; (b) require the supplier to carry their own goods-in-transit or stock-throughput insurance; (c) include a cap on your liability (e.g., 10% of consignment value). Get your insurer to confirm coverage — your commercial policy may not extend to goods you do not own.

#### Q: 7. A consignment stock tile range has been in my showroom for 14 months with minimal sales. The supplier now demands I buy it or pay storage fees. Our agreement said nothing about time limits. Must I comply?
**Answer:** If the consignment agreement is silent on duration and rotation requirements, the supplier cannot unilaterally impose time limits or storage fees. However, they can terminate the consignment arrangement with reasonable notice (typically 30-60 days) and demand return of their stock. You should negotiate a written consignment agreement upfront specifying: maximum consignment period (e.g., 12 months), agreed rotation date, what happens on expiry (return at supplier's cost vs. conversion to sale at discounted price). Without written terms, you are both exposed.

#### Q: 8. Consignment stock was stolen in a warehouse break-in. My security system was off because of load shedding and the inverter failed. The supplier's agreement says I am strictly liable regardless of cause. Can load shedding be a defence?
**Answer:** Strict liability means you are liable regardless of fault — but load shedding as a defence depends on your agreement's force majeure clause. Eskom load shedding is increasingly considered a foreseeable event in South Africa, making it harder to claim force majeure unless the specific stage (e.g., Stage 6) was unforeseeable. Your best argument is that the supplier's strict liability clause is an unfair contract term under CPA section 48. Going forward, install a generator or solar backup for your alarm system — courts expect businesses to take reasonable precautions against known risks. Claim on your commercial insurance if you have stock coverage that extends to consignment goods.

#### Q: 9. I sold consignment stock to a customer but the customer hasn't paid me yet. The supplier wants payment NOW because the stock left my warehouse. Do I owe before I have been paid?
**Answer:** This depends entirely on your consignment agreement terms. Standard consignment arrangements trigger payment obligation at point of sale (to the end customer) or at a defined event (e.g., 30 days after delivery to customer, whichever is earlier). If your cash flow depends on customer payment first, negotiate "pay-when-paid" or "pay-if-paid" terms in your consignment agreement. Be aware that "pay-if-paid" clauses are generally unenforceable in construction contracts under the MBA/NEC standard forms, but consignment of goods is different — it is governed by your specific agreement. Get a commercial lawyer to draft protective terms.

#### Q: 10. My supplier's consignment agreement requires me to segregate their stock physically and mark it as "property of [supplier]" in my accounting system. This is administratively burdensome. Can I refuse?
**Answer:** No — these requirements exist to protect the supplier's ownership rights under the Insolvency Act and the concept of "recognition of ownership" (RoO). If you become insolvent, properly segregated and documented consignment stock does not form part of your insolvent estate. Without segregation and marking, the stock becomes your property on insolvency and the supplier ranks as an unsecured creditor. This is non-negotiable from the supplier's perspective. Implement proper stock management software that can handle consignment tracking — it protects both parties.
## WHITE-LABEL / PRIVATE LABEL

#### Q: 11. I want to import tiles from China and sell them under my own private label brand. Do I need NRCS (National Regulator for Compulsory Specifications) approval for every SKU, or can I batch-approve by factory?
**Answer:** Ceramic and porcelain tiles fall under the compulsory specification VC9006, regulated by the NRCS. You need a Letter of Authority (LOA) for each tile classification — but this is typically per factory/product line, not per individual SKU. The LOA covers the manufacturing facility's compliance with SANS 1449. You must also ensure SANS 10107 compliance for slip resistance classification. Engage an NRCS-accredited testing laboratory for initial type testing. Budget R15,000-R25,000 per product line for testing and LOA application. Your private label branding does not create a separate approval requirement if the underlying product is already approved.

#### Q: 12. My private label tile has my brand printed on the box but the factory code on the back of the tile itself. A customer is demanding to know the "real manufacturer" claiming CPA right to information. Must I disclose?
**Answer:** Under section 23 of the CPA, consumers have the right to disclosure of the business name and address of the supplier (you) and, for imported goods, the country of origin. There is no specific CPA requirement to disclose the OEM manufacturer's identity. However, if the customer claims a product defect and initiates legal action, you may need to disclose the manufacturer for third-party proceedings. Your box should comply with SANS labelling requirements: country of origin, your SA business details, SANS classification, and batch number. The factory code on the tile is for your traceability — it does not create a disclosure obligation.

#### Q: 13. I want to private-label porcelain tiles from a Spanish factory. What is the realistic minimum order quantity (MOQ) for factory-direct private label packaging?
**Answer:** For Spanish and Italian porcelain manufacturers, private label MOQs typically start at one full 20-foot container (approximately 800-1,000 m² depending on tile format and thickness). Some factories require a minimum of 2-3 containers per design for custom box printing. Custom private label packaging (your brand on the box, insert cards, your barcode) adds 4-6 weeks to production lead time and costs approximately EUR0.50-1.20 per square metre extra. Very few European factories will private label for less than one container — the setup costs for printing plates and line changeover are not economical below that threshold.

#### Q: 14. If my private label tile fails and causes property damage, am I liable as the "manufacturer" under the CPA, or can I pass liability to the OEM factory in China?
**Answer:** Under section 61 of the CPA, strict product liability attaches to the "producer," which includes the manufacturer, importer, and anyone who puts their own brand on goods produced by another. As the private label brand owner, YOU are the "producer" for CPA liability purposes and bear strict liability to the consumer. You can seek recourse against the Chinese manufacturer via your supply contract (indemnity clause), but enforcement against a Chinese entity is extremely difficult. Protect yourself: (a) product liability insurance with minimum R5 million cover; (b) stringent QC inspections before shipment (hire a third-party inspector like SGS or Bureau Veritas in China); (c) contractual indemnity from the manufacturer with an escrow or letter of credit holdback.

#### Q: 15. A competitor is selling what looks identical to my private label tile but under their brand at 20% less. I suspect the same Chinese factory is double-dealing. How can I prove it and what can I do?
**Answer:** This is a common problem. Obtain samples of their tile and have both tiles independently tested (XRF analysis, water absorption, dimensional accuracy). If the technical specifications match within manufacturing tolerance, you have circumstantial evidence of the same source. Check if your supply agreement with the Chinese factory contains exclusivity for the South African market — Chinese factories often ignore verbal exclusivity promises. Your legal remedies: (a) breach of contract claim against the factory (enforceability issues in Chinese courts); (b) if the competitor is making misleading representations about origin or quality, a complaint to the Advertising Regulatory Board; (c) focus on differentiating through service, warranty, and local stockholding rather than trying to enforce exclusivity across borders.
## CONTAINER-DIRECT ORDERING & IMPORTING

#### Q: 16. I want to import directly from an Italian tile factory and skip the local agent who adds 35% margin. Can the local agent stop me if I deal factory-direct?
**Answer:** The local agent can only stop you if they hold an exclusive distributorship agreement with the Italian factory that covers your territory. Contact the factory directly and ask: (a) do they have an exclusive agent/distributor for South Africa? (b) what are their terms for direct accounts? (c) can they quote FOB (Free On Board) Italian port? If an exclusive agreement exists, the factory may be contractually bound not to sell to you directly — though many factories will quietly sell direct if your volume is significant. Be prepared to handle your own importing: freight forwarding, marine insurance, customs clearance, VAT (15% on CIF value + duty), and NRCS compliance.

#### Q: 17. What is the all-in landed cost difference between buying from a local distributor vs. importing a 20-foot container direct from Spain, considering current rand volatility?
**Answer:** As a rule of thumb, local distributor pricing typically reflects: factory ex-works price + 25-40% distributor margin + local logistics + stockholding cost. Container-direct savings can be 15-25% on a like-for-like product IF you achieve sufficient volume. All-in landed cost components for a Spain container: (a) FOB price per m²; (b) ocean freight (currently $1,200-$2,500 per 20ft container depending on carrier and port); (c) marine insurance (0.15-0.3% of CIF); (d) customs duty on ceramic tiles (typically 0-8% depending on HS code and EU origin — check ITAC tariff); (e) VAT at 15% on (CIF + duty); (f) clearing agent fees (R3,500-R6,000); (g) inland transport from Durban/Cape Town port to your warehouse; (h) financing cost for 90-120 days capital tied up. Factor in 8-12% currency depreciation risk over a 3-month order cycle — this can erase your margin entirely.

#### Q: 18. I ordered a container direct from Turkey. The tiles arrived with the wrong shade — batch A54 instead of A53. The factory says "shade variation is normal in ceramics." My customer rejected the batch. Who bears the cost?
**Answer:** Check your purchase order for the shade/batch specification. If you specified shade A53 and received A54, this is a breach of contract. Under Incoterms (if FOB or CIF), risk transfers at the port of shipment, but the seller remains liable for conformity of goods. Document everything: photographs, your PO showing shade A53, the packing list showing A54, your customer's rejection letter. Your remedies: (a) reject the goods and claim refund (if shipment was under letter of credit, invoke the LC terms); (b) accept at a reduced price; (c) if the factory refuses, you will need to pursue international arbitration if your contract has an arbitration clause — otherwise you are looking at costly cross-border litigation. Always specify shade tolerance and require pre-shipment sample approval in your PO.

#### Q: 19. I want to import a full 40-foot high-cube container of porcelain tiles from India. How much can it hold and what is the weight limit I need to worry about for road transport from Durban?
**Answer:** A 40-foot HC container has internal volume of approximately 76 cubic metres. Porcelain tiles (10mm thick, 600x600mm) weigh approximately 18-20 kg per box (1.44 m²). You can fit approximately 1,100-1,200 boxes (1,600-1,700 m²) before hitting the road weight limit. The maximum gross weight for a 40ft container is 30,480 kg (including container tare of ~3,700 kg), giving you a payload of ~26,700 kg. At 20 kg/box, your maximum is ~1,330 boxes. Road transport from Durban to Gauteng on an interlink can handle this. If delivering to the Western Cape, check axle weight restrictions on the N2. Always obtain a weightbridge certificate before the container leaves the port.

#### Q: 20. The rand dropped 12% between my placing the order and the factory invoice being due 60 days later. My forward exchange contract only covered 8%. Can I legally cancel the order citing "commercial impossibility"?
**Answer:** Commercial impossibility (also called supervening impossibility of performance) requires that performance has become objectively impossible, not merely more expensive or less profitable. A currency depreciation, even 12%, does not render performance impossible — it makes it more costly. South African courts have consistently held that currency fluctuations are a normal commercial risk. Unless your contract contains a "material adverse change" or hardship clause, you cannot cancel. Your options: (a) negotiate with the factory for partial discount; (b) draw on an existing credit facility to cover the shortfall; (c) if the factory agrees, convert to a later delivery date hoping for rand recovery. Going forward, cover 100% of foreign currency exposure with forward exchange contracts or currency options.
## CLEARANCE & DISCONTINUED STOCK

#### Q: 21. My supplier offered me discontinued stock at 45% discount but with a "no returns, no warranty" clause. If a customer later claims these tiles are defective, am I still liable under the CPA despite the "no warranty" term?
**Answer:** Yes. Under section 56 of the CPA, all goods sold to consumers carry an implied 6-month warranty of quality regardless of any disclaimer. A "no warranty" clause on clearance stock is an unfair contract term and is void under section 48. However, your supplier's discount offer may reflect this risk — they are effectively transferring CPA liability to you. Protect yourself: (a) sell discontinued stock with full disclosure to the customer ("end-of-line, sold as-is, no batch continuity for future purchases"); (b) inspect the stock thoroughly before accepting; (c) negotiate a back-to-back warranty from the supplier (e.g., 12 months latent defect coverage); (d) keep your product liability insurance current.

#### Q: 22. I bought 5,000 m² of clearance porcelain at 50% off. The batch numbers show it is 4 years old. Is there a shelf life for uninstalled porcelain tiles?
**Answer:** Technically, porcelain tiles have no shelf life — they are fired at over 1,200°C and are chemically stable indefinitely if stored correctly (dry, protected from impact). However, adhesive recommendations change over time — the adhesive system specified 4 years ago may no longer be available. More critically, older stock may not meet current SANS revisions if the standard has been updated. Check the batch date against current SANS 1449 and whether NRCS LOA requirements have changed. The real risk is not tile degradation but batch matching: if you cannot supply matching tiles later, customers will be dissatisfied. Sell old batch stock with a clear advisory: "limited stock, no continuity guaranteed."

#### Q: 23. A supplier cleared out a range to me at 40% off but the same range is still on their website at full price. I planned to sell at a modest markup but now online customers see the "full RRP" and expect that quality. Can I be accused of passing off or misrepresentation if I sell clearance stock as current range?
**Answer:** You can sell the tiles as the current range if they are genuine, unaltered product. The issue is pricing representation: if you advertise "was R450/m², now R270/m²" and the supplier's website still shows R450/m², that is truthful. However, if the range has been officially discontinued and you represent it as current stock without disclosing it is end-of-line, that could be misleading under section 41 of the CPA. Best practice: sell as "while stocks last" or "limited clearance" — honest disclosure builds trust. You are not passing off if you are selling genuine branded goods (first sale doctrine applies in South Africa).

#### Q: 24. My supplier has a "first in, first out" (FIFO) policy and refuses to sell me clearance stock until I have rotated existing stock of the same range. Can they enforce this?
**Answer:** This depends on your supply agreement terms. A FIFO clause is standard in supplier-merchant agreements to prevent the merchant from cherry-picking clearance stock while holding regular-priced stock. If your signed agreement contains a FIFO or stock rotation clause, the supplier can enforce it. If no such clause exists, they cannot unilaterally impose it. However, consider the relationship dimension: abusing clearance offers while your regular stock sits unsold will damage your supplier relationship. Negotiate a balanced approach: commit to moving X% of existing stock within Y months in exchange for clearance allocation.

#### Q: 25. I purchased end-of-line mosaic tiles at clearance prices. When my tiler installed them, the sheet backing adhesive had degraded and the mosaics fell off the mesh. The supplier says "clearance stock, no warranty." What are my rights?
**Answer:** Mosaic mesh backing can degrade over time, especially if stored in humid conditions. Under section 56 of the CPA, the tiles must be reasonably suitable for their purpose. If the degradation occurred due to supplier storage conditions (before you took delivery), the supplier remains liable despite "clearance" status — the CPA warranty is non-waivable. However, if the degradation occurred in YOUR warehouse due to poor storage, liability shifts to you. Document: (a) delivery date and storage conditions; (b) manufacture date from batch code; (c) photographic evidence. If the tiles were delivered to you already degraded, demand replacement or credit. If you stored them poorly for 18 months before sale, the liability is yours.
## FINANCING & STOCK LOANS

#### Q: 26. My supplier offered me a R2 million stock loan at prime + 3% over 12 months, secured by a general notarial bond over my warehouse stock. What are the risks of signing a general notarial bond?
**Answer:** A general notarial bond (GNB) under the Security by Means of Movable Property Act gives the supplier a security interest over all your movable stock without taking possession. The risks: (a) the bond may prevent you from obtaining other stock finance (your bank may refuse a second bond); (b) on default, the supplier can apply for a court order to attach your stock — ALL stock, not just their brand; (c) the GNB must be registered at the Deeds Office to be effective against third parties (cost: R1,500-R3,000); (d) your other creditors may be prejudiced. Negotiate: (a) a specific notarial bond limited to the supplier's stock only; (b) a maximum bond amount rather than unlimited; (c) no personal suretyship if possible; (d) a right to substitute stock (sell slow lines, replace with fast lines).

#### Q: 27. A tile manufacturer is offering me 90 days interest-free stock financing but requires me to cede my debtor's book as security. Is this normal and what does it mean practically?
**Answer:** Debtor book cession (cession of trade receivables) is increasingly common in supplier financing arrangements but carries significant implications: (a) you must notify your customers that their debt has been ceded — this can damage relationships; (b) payments from ceded debtors must go into a controlled bank account; (c) if a ceded debtor pays you directly by mistake, you may be in breach; (d) the cession may trigger a default under your existing bank overdraft facility (check your loan covenants). Negotiate alternatives: (a) limited cession of specific invoices rather than entire book; (b) a pledge of stock instead; (c) personal suretyship for a capped amount. Get your accountant and lawyer to review the cession agreement before signing.

#### Q: 28. I took stock financing from a supplier and the interest rate is linked to prime. Prime has gone up 2% since I signed. My agreement has no cap on interest rate increases. Can I challenge this under the National Credit Act?
**Answer:** The National Credit Act (NCA) applies to credit agreements below a prescribed threshold (currently R0 — all credit agreements fall under NCA if the credit provider is in the business of providing credit). If your supplier is providing stock financing as a regular part of their business, the NCA applies and the interest rate is capped. For commercial credit agreements (juristic persons with asset value or turnover above R1 million), the NCA does not apply — your agreement is governed by common law. If the NCA does apply, the maximum interest rate for commercial credit is Repo x 2.2 + 5%. If you are a small business (below R1 million threshold), check if the NCA applies and calculate if prime + 3% exceeds the NCA cap. If the NCA does not apply, you have no statutory protection against interest rate increases — negotiate a cap in future.

#### Q: 29. A supplier is offering me "supplier credit" of R500,000 but the fine print says they can convert this to a cash-on-delivery (COD) arrangement with 7 days' notice if my payment history shows any late payment. Is this enforceable?
**Answer:** This is a standard credit supplier protection clause and is generally enforceable under South African contract law — provided the 7-day notice period is reasonable for you to arrange alternative finance. Seven days is arguably tight for a R500,000 facility; courts have held that termination notice must be reasonable in the circumstances. Challenge it: negotiate for 30 days' notice of credit withdrawal, or a grace period of one late payment per quarter before conversion to COD. Also check if this arrangement triggers the National Credit Act — if so, the supplier must follow NCA termination procedures which are more protective. Document all correspondence if they invoke this clause.

#### Q: 30. My bank refuses to extend my stock finance facility because my debtors book is concentrated among three large contractors who represent 60% of my turnover. The supplier wants me to pay cash. What financing alternatives exist for a smaller tile merchant?
**Answer:** Concentration risk is a real problem for smaller merchants. Alternatives: (a) invoice discounting with a specialist funder (Lulalend, Merchant Capital, Grovest) — they advance 70-85% of invoice value within 24-48 hours; (b) trade finance from an import specialist (Tradeflow, Investec Trade and Working Capital Solutions) — they pay your foreign supplier and you repay over 90-120 days; (c) asset-based lending against your property (if you own your premises); (d) BEE supplier development funding — if you have a Level 1-4 BEE partner, they may have access to enterprise development grants; (e) joining a buying group (e.g., Independent Buying Group in building materials) for collective bargaining power and shared credit facilities. Diversify your customer base to reduce concentration risk going forward.
## COMPETITOR OPENS NEARBY

#### Q: 31. Italtile is opening a full-range store 300 metres from my shop in Menlyn. My supplier also supplies Italtile and I am certain they will get better pricing due to volume. Do I have any rights as a smaller stockist?
**Answer:** Volume-based pricing differentiation is lawful under the Competition Act unless it constitutes price discrimination that substantially prevents or lessens competition (section 9). Large chains like Italtile negotiate significant volume rebates and centralised distribution efficiencies. As a smaller merchant, your strategy should be: (a) differentiate on service — contractors want immediate stock availability, not a weekend wait; (b) specialise in niches (e.g., commercial-grade porcelain, specialised mosaics) that the chain does not stock; (c) build deep contractor relationships with credit terms and delivery flexibility; (d) negotiate a "most favoured customer" clause in your supplier agreement ensuring you receive the same net pricing as any other stockist for equivalent volumes. Realistically, matching Italtile on price is a race to the bottom you will lose.

#### Q: 32. CTM opened nearby and is advertising my key porcelain range at what appears to be below cost. Can I report them for predatory pricing under the Competition Act?
**Answer:** Predatory pricing (selling below cost to eliminate competition) is prohibited under section 8 of the Competition Act, but proving it requires evidence that: (a) CTM is pricing below average avoidable cost or average variable cost; (b) they have a reasonable prospect of recouping losses through future monopoly pricing; (c) their behaviour substantially prevents or lessens competition. Large retailers often have supplier rebates and marketing contributions that reduce their net cost — what looks like below-cost selling may actually be profitable. Before lodging a Competition Commission complaint (which can take 2-3 years), gather evidence: obtain written quotes, estimate their landed cost, document the duration of the low pricing. A more practical response: focus on where CTM is weak — custom orders, technical advice, out-of-hours service.

#### Q: 33. A competitor merchant 2 km away is selling the same imported range as me but at prices 15% lower because they are importing container-direct while I buy through the local agent. Should I confront the local agent about their double-standard pricing?
**Answer:** The local agent is likely selling to the competitor at the same landed cost but the competitor's lower retail price reflects their decision to accept lower margin, not preferential pricing. Alternatively, the competitor may have achieved volume-based tier pricing. Before confronting the agent, anonymously obtain a quote from the competitor to confirm their actual selling price. Then approach the agent diplomatically: "I am losing business to a stockist who is undercutting me significantly on your range. Can we review my pricing tier?" Frame it as a mutual business risk — if you stop stocking their range, they lose a sales channel. Consider whether container-direct importing is viable for your volume — if you can achieve one container per year, the savings may justify bypassing the agent.

#### Q: 34. Both Italtile and CTM have opened within 1 km of me in the past 18 months. My turnover has dropped 35%. Can I claim "passing off" or unfair competition because customers confuse my independent store with theirs?
**Answer:** Passing off requires proof of: (a) goodwill in your business name/trading style; (b) misrepresentation by the competitor causing confusion; (c) damage to your goodwill. Italtile and CTM have distinctive branding that is unlikely to be confused with an independent merchant's store. However, if a competitor has adopted a name, colour scheme, or layout deliberately similar to yours, you may have a passing off claim. Unfair competition is not a standalone cause of action in South African law — you need a specific legal basis (CPA, Competition Act, trademark infringement). Your practical response: rebrand with a strong independent identity, invest in local SEO, and differentiate through specialisation rather than competing head-on with chains on price and range.

#### Q: 35. My landlord is also the developer of the shopping centre. He has given Italtile a 12-month rent-free period to anchor the centre but refuses me any rental concession despite my 8-year tenancy. Is this discriminatory treatment challengeable?
**Answer:** Under the Consumer Protection Act, tenants of commercial premises have certain protections against unfair contract terms, but landlords are generally free to offer different deals to different tenants — this is not unlawful discrimination under the Equality Act unless based on a prohibited ground (race, gender, disability). Anchor tenant incentives (rent-free periods, contribution to fit-out) are standard commercial practice in retail leasing. Your lease may contain a "most favoured tenant" clause — check this. If not, negotiate: request a rent reduction tied to a performance metric (e.g., if your turnover drops >20% due to Italtile opening), or negotiate a shorter lease term with a break clause. If the landlord refuses, consider relocating to a location with better tenant mix for independent merchants.
## E-COMMERCE & ONLINE SELLING

#### Q: 36. My supplier discovered I am selling their tiles on my website at prices 10% below my showroom price and has threatened to terminate my distributorship citing a breach of their "MAP" (Minimum Advertised Price) policy. Is MAP enforcement legal in South Africa?
**Answer:** Minimum Advertised Price (MAP) policies are a form of resale price maintenance. Under section 5 of the Competition Act, minimum resale price maintenance is a per se prohibited restrictive horizontal practice. However, the Competition Tribunal has recognised that MAP policies that merely restrict ADVERTISED prices (not actual selling prices) may be treated differently from hard RPM. This is a grey area in South African competition law. Practically: (a) check if your supply agreement actually contains a MAP clause — if not, the supplier cannot unilaterally impose it; (b) if the clause exists, its enforceability is uncertain; (c) the more serious risk is termination of your distributorship — the supplier can terminate for any lawful reason or no reason (if your contract allows termination on notice). Negotiate: remove online pricing entirely and use "contact us for pricing" or "get a quote" instead.

#### Q: 37. I want to sell tiles on Takealot's marketplace. Their commission is 12-15% and they handle delivery. My supplier says I cannot sell their brand on third-party platforms. Can they legally restrict this?
**Answer:** Yes — your supplier can restrict authorised sales channels in your distributorship agreement. Under South African contract law, a supplier can specify where and how their brand is sold. If your agreement contains a channel restriction ("authorised sales channels: physical showroom only"), selling on Takealot is a breach. If no such restriction exists, the supplier cannot retroactively impose it without your consent. However, consider the strategic dimension: Takealot returns are notoriously high for building materials (customers order wrong quantities, wrong colours), and the 12-15% commission plus your margin plus supplier price may make the product uncompetitive. Many tile suppliers also have concerns about brand positioning on discount platforms.

#### Q: 38. A customer bought tiles from my online store, collected from my warehouse, and now wants to return them under the CPA "cooling off" provision for online purchases. But the CPA 5-day cooling-off only applies to direct marketing. Does it apply to my online store?
**Answer:** Section 16 of the CPA gives consumers a 5-business-day cooling-off right for transactions resulting from direct marketing. If the customer found your online store through organic search and purchased without any direct marketing (no email, SMS, or phone call from you initiating the transaction), the cooling-off right does NOT apply. However, section 44 gives consumers a general right to return defective or unsafe goods, and section 55 requires goods to be fit for purpose. Your online terms should clearly state that purchases are not direct marketing transactions and the cooling-off period does not apply. Still, offer a reasonable returns policy for unopened stock — it builds customer confidence.

#### Q: 39. I have an e-commerce website but the supplier's product images are copyrighted. Can I use their photos on my site, or must I take my own?
**Answer:** Product photographs provided by the supplier are typically licensed to authorised distributors for marketing purposes. Check your distributorship agreement for a marketing licence or image use clause. If silent, request written permission to use their images on your e-commerce platform — most suppliers will grant this as it drives sales. If you are NOT an authorised distributor, using their copyrighted images without permission constitutes copyright infringement under the Copyright Act. Taking your own photos is always safest but expensive for a full range. A practical solution: use the supplier images where permitted, and for clearance/private label stock, take your own photos. Watermark supplier images with your own branding to prevent competitors from copying them.

#### Q: 40. A competitor merchant copied my entire website layout, product descriptions, and pricing structure. They even used my Google reviews screenshots on their site. What can I do?
**Answer:** Multiple potential claims: (a) copyright infringement — your original product descriptions and website content are protected by copyright; (b) passing off — if their site creates confusion with yours; (c) unlawful competition — if they are free-riding on your marketing investment; (d) Google review screenshots used without permission may violate Google's terms of service and constitute misleading marketing under the CPA. Send a cease-and-desist letter from an attorney demanding removal within 7 days. If they refuse, you can lodge a complaint with the Advertising Regulatory Board for misleading advertising and consider litigation for copyright infringement and passing off. Document everything with screenshots (timestamped via Wayback Machine). Budget R15,000-R40,000 for legal action.
## SOCIAL MEDIA MARKETING

#### Q: 41. My supplier promised co-op marketing funds (1% of purchases) but has only paid out R2,000 of the R18,000 I am owed over 3 years. They say I didn't submit claims "in the prescribed format." Can I enforce this?
**Answer:** Co-operative marketing contributions are typically governed by a Marketing Development Fund (MDF) agreement or clause in your supply agreement. Check: (a) is the contribution automatic or claim-based? (b) what is the claim format and deadline? (c) is there a time limit on claims (e.g., "claims must be submitted within 90 days of quarter end")? If you missed claim deadlines, the supplier may be within their rights to refuse payment — this is common with MDF funds. However, if there was no clear claim process defined, you can argue the supplier's refusal is unfair. Going forward: (a) get the MDF terms in writing; (b) submit claims quarterly with proper documentation (invoices, screenshots, analytics); (c) request automatic credit notes instead of reimbursement claims.

#### Q: 42. I hired a local Instagram influencer to promote my tile business. She visited my showroom and posted negative content claiming our prices were "ridiculous" compared to CTM. Can I sue her for defamation?
**Answer:** If her statement that your prices are "ridiculous" is a statement of opinion based on disclosed facts (she compared specific prices), it is likely fair comment and protected. If she made false statements of fact (e.g., "these tiles are fake imports" when they are genuine), that could be defamatory. However, suing an influencer is expensive and generates further negative publicity. Your better course: (a) respond professionally in the comments explaining your value proposition; (b) if the post contains demonstrably false facts, request a correction via the influencer's management or the platform; (c) for future influencer partnerships, use written agreements specifying content approval rights, factual accuracy requirements, and non-disparagement clauses. Consider this a lesson in influencer vetting.

#### Q: 43. My supplier shared a Facebook post of a stunning installation using their tiles — but the installation was done by my competitor merchant who is also their stockist. The post implies my competitor is the "preferred" installer. Can I demand equal social media exposure?
**Answer:** Unless your supply agreement contains a clause guaranteeing equal marketing exposure (highly unlikely), the supplier has no obligation to feature you equally on their social media. However, this is a relationship issue: raise it with your supplier relationship manager. Frame it constructively: "I noticed a competitor was featured — I have some excellent installation photos I would love to share for your social media." Build a portfolio of professional installation photography and proactively share it. Many suppliers welcome quality content. Also build your own social media following — your owned channels are more valuable than anything the supplier does. Consider whether your competitor earned that feature through better engagement or relationship management.

#### Q: 44. I want to run Facebook ads targeting my competitor's followers. Is this allowed under South African advertising law?
**Answer:** Targeting competitor followers on social media is not unlawful per se — it is standard digital marketing practice. However, you must not: (a) use the competitor's trademark in your ad copy without permission (trademark infringement); (b) make false comparative claims ("we are 30% cheaper than Competitor X" unless demonstrably true and current); (c) use the competitor's branded content or images without permission. Your ads should focus on your own value proposition. The Advertising Regulatory Board Code of Advertising Practice requires honesty and substantiation of claims. Retargeting users who visited a competitor's website is legal but may be blocked by some platforms' policies — use interest-based and lookalike audience targeting instead.

#### Q: 45. A contractor posted a video on TikTok of a failed tile installation using tiles purchased from me. The video went viral with 200,000 views and comments blaming my tiles. The actual cause was the tiler's poor workmanship. What should I do?
**Answer:** This is a reputation management crisis. Do NOT engage in the comments section with accusations against the tiler — this will escalate. Instead: (a) contact the contractor directly and politely, requesting a correction or follow-up video explaining the actual cause; (b) offer to send a technical representative to assess the installation and provide a professional report; (c) if the contractor refuses, post a calm, factual response on your own channels explaining tile installation requirements without naming the contractor; (d) engage a reputation management service to push positive content up in search results; (e) consider legal action for defamation only if the contractor made objectively false statements of fact with intent to damage your business — but litigation is slow and public. Prevention: encourage satisfied customers to post positive reviews and installation videos.
## STAFF TRAINING & CPD

#### Q: 46. My showroom staff need product knowledge training but my supplier says I must pay R3,500 per person for their training course. Is there any government or SETA funding available for this training?
**Answer:** Yes — the Construction Education and Training Authority (CETA) and the Fibre Processing and Manufacturing SETA (FP&M SETA) both offer learnerships and skills programmes relevant to the building materials sector. If you are levy-paying (your payroll exceeds R500,000 annually), you can claim Mandatory Grant funding (20% of your skills levy) by submitting a Workplace Skills Plan (WSP) and Annual Training Report (ATR) by 30 April each year. For non-levy payers, discretionary grants may be available. Additionally, the supplier should provide product training as part of their merchant support — negotiate this as a condition of your distributorship, especially if you are achieving your sales targets. Document training attendance for BEE Skills Development points if applicable.

#### Q: 47. A customer slipped and fell on tiles in my showroom and is suing me. They claim my staff should have warned them the tiles were slippery when wet. Does my staff training on slip resistance actually reduce my legal liability?
**Answer:** Yes — demonstrating that your staff received proper training on slip resistance ratings (R9-R13 under SANS 10107/DIN 51130) and that they proactively advise customers on appropriate tile selection for wet areas creates a strong defence. Under the Occupational Health and Safety Act, you owe a duty of care to visitors to your premises. If you can show: (a) staff were trained to explain slip ratings; (b) the customer was advised of the slip rating; (c) signage was present; (d) the customer signed an acknowledgement — your liability is significantly reduced. However, for showroom floors themselves, ensure they meet minimum R10 slip resistance (wet) to prevent accidents on your own premises.

#### Q: 48. I sent my staff for a CPD-accredited course on tile installation techniques. Can I use this training to justify higher pricing to customers, and does it give me any marketing advantage?
**Answer:** CPD-accredited training demonstrates professional competence and can be a powerful marketing differentiator — especially when competing against mass-market retailers whose staff lack technical depth. You can market this as: "Our showroom consultants are CPD-accredited in tile specification and installation." However, be careful not to overstate the training: CPD courses provide general knowledge, not the specific expertise of a qualified tiler or architect. Do not claim your staff are "certified tile installers" unless they hold an artisan qualification (Red Seal or equivalent). The real marketing value is trust: customers are more likely to buy from consultants who can explain technical differences between porcelain grades, adhesive systems, and grouting options.

#### Q: 49. My supplier's training manual is 8 years old and references adhesive products that are no longer available. If my staff follow this outdated training and specify the wrong adhesive, who is liable for a failed installation?
**Answer:** You are. As the employer, you are responsible for ensuring your staff have current, accurate product knowledge. Relying on an 8-year-old training manual is negligent. The supplier may share some liability if they provided the outdated material knowing it was obsolete, but your primary duty is to your customer. Update your training materials: request current technical datasheets from all your adhesive suppliers (Mapei, Weber, TAL, Tile Tucker), compile an internal specification guide, and review it quarterly. Subscribe to product update notifications from your key suppliers. If a supplier's training is consistently outdated, raise it at management level — it reflects poorly on their brand and puts their entire dealer network at risk.

#### Q: 50. Can I claim a tax deduction for staff training expenses, including travel and accommodation for an out-of-town training course?
**Answer:** Yes. Under section 11(a) of the Income Tax Act, expenditure incurred in the production of income is deductible if it is not of a capital nature. Staff training expenses — including course fees, travel, accommodation, and meals — are generally fully deductible as operational expenses. If the training leads to a formal qualification or long-term skill (e.g., a diploma in construction management), SARS may argue it is capital in nature and disallow the deduction — though this is rare for short courses. Keep all invoices and proof of attendance. Skills Development Levy payments are also deductible. If you receive a SETA grant for the training, the grant is taxable income but the training expense remains deductible.
## POS DISPLAYS & MERCHANDISING

#### Q: 51. My supplier provided display stands on loan but now wants them back after I decided to stop stocking their brand. The stands are bolted to my floor and integrated into my showroom layout. Do I have to remove them?
**Answer:** Check your display stand agreement (often buried in the supply terms). Standard supplier display agreements state that stands remain the supplier's property and must be returned on termination, in good condition, at the merchant's cost. If they are bolted to the floor, they may constitute fixtures (permanently attached to the property) and ownership may have transferred to you or your landlord under the doctrine of fixtures. However, most suppliers include a "retention of ownership" clause overriding this. Practical approach: negotiate — offer to purchase the stands at written-down value, or request the supplier arrange removal at their cost. If you refuse to return them, the supplier can claim the replacement value and potentially offset it against any credit owed to you.

#### Q: 52. A supplier's sample boards are taking up 15% of my showroom wall space but represent only 3% of my sales. I want to return them but the supplier says they are on permanent loan and cannot be returned without 12 months' notice. Is this reasonable?
**Answer:** This depends on your written agreement. If the supplier provided sample boards under a "permanent display" clause with a notice period, it is enforceable. However, 12 months is arguably excessive — 3 months is more standard. Negotiate: give written notice of your intent to delist the brand or reduce display space, propose a 3-month wind-down, and offer to retain only the top-selling lines. If the supplier refuses, you must honour the agreement or risk termination of your supply terms. Going forward, negotiate display terms separately from supply terms: specify maximum display space, right to return slow-moving boards, and shorter notice periods. Track display space ROI — each square metre of wall space should generate proportional revenue.

#### Q: 53. My supplier promised a "shop-in-shop" concept with branded display units and lighting but has delivered nothing in 18 months despite repeated promises. Can I withhold payment or claim damages?
**Answer:** Unless your supply agreement specifically binds the supplier to deliver the shop-in-shop by a specific date, verbal promises are difficult to enforce. Check: (a) is there a written shop-in-shop agreement with milestones? (b) were the promises made in emails or formal proposals? (c) did you incur costs in anticipation (e.g., prepared floor space, electrical points)? If you have documented promises and incurred costs, you may have a claim for promissory estoppel or breach of collateral agreement. However, withholding payment for stock already delivered is a breach of your supply agreement and exposes you to termination and legal action. Better approach: write a formal letter of demand with a reasonable deadline, then escalate to the supplier's managing director. If unresolved, consider whether the relationship is worth maintaining.

#### Q: 54. A supplier's POS display includes a digital screen that plays promotional videos. The screen stopped working after 6 months. The supplier says warranty was only 3 months. Who pays for repair?
**Answer:** Under the CPA, goods supplied to a consumer (you, as the merchant, are a consumer of the supplier's display equipment) carry an implied 6-month warranty of quality for defects that existed at delivery. If the screen failure is due to a manufacturing defect (not physical damage or power surge), the supplier must repair or replace within 6 months of delivery. After 6 months, the implied CPA warranty expires but you may still have contractual warranty rights if the supplier offered a longer period. Negotiate: electronic equipment should carry at least a 12-month warranty. Going forward, specify warranty terms for all POS equipment in your display agreement. Consider whether it is worth repairing — a replacement 24-inch commercial display costs R2,500-R4,000.

#### Q: 55. I moved my showroom to a new location. A supplier refuses to relocate their branded display unit, saying relocation is my cost. But the unit was provided free originally. Can they charge me for the move?
**Answer:** This depends entirely on your display agreement terms. Most supplier-provided POS agreements state: "delivery and initial installation at supplier's cost; relocation at merchant's cost." If your agreement is silent, you bear the cost as the party requesting the move — the supplier has no obligation to pay for your relocation. However, you can negotiate: if you are a significant stockist, the supplier may contribute to relocation as a goodwill gesture. Alternatively, offer to return the unit and request a new one for the new location — some suppliers will provide new displays for relocated or expanded showrooms. Document everything: photograph the unit before and after the move to prove it was not damaged during relocation.
## RETURNS POLICY & SLOW-MOVING STOCK

#### Q: 56. My supplier allows returns of slow-moving stock within 12 months but charges a 25% restocking fee. Is this restocking fee enforceable under the CPA?
**Answer:** The CPA regulates terms between supplier and CONSUMER, not B2B transactions between supplier and merchant. Your supplier-merchant relationship is governed by contract. A 25% restocking fee is commercially standard for slow-moving stock returns — it covers the supplier's handling, inspection, and remarketing costs. It is enforceable if it is in your written agreement. If no restocking fee was agreed, the supplier cannot unilaterally impose one. Negotiate: a tiered restocking fee (15% if returned within 6 months, 25% after 12 months) or a maximum annual return allowance (e.g., 10% of purchases). The key is getting slow-moving stock return terms in writing before you place large orders.

#### Q: 57. I have R400,000 of tiles that have been in stock for 2 years. My supplier has discontinued the range and refuses returns. Is there any way to recover value?
**Answer:** Discontinued stock with no return rights is your loss unless you can find alternative disposal channels: (a) clearance sales at heavy discount (expect 40-60% markdown); (b) export to neighbouring countries (Botswana, Namibia) where the range may still have demand; (c) donate to a registered PBO (Public Benefit Organisation) for a section 18A tax deduction — the deduction is limited to cost price, not selling price; (d) job lot auctions — expect 10-20 cents in the rand; (e) repurpose as feature walls, outdoor cladding, or creative installations where shade matching is less critical. Going forward, negotiate a right of return for discontinued lines and set internal stock age limits (e.g., automatic markdown at 9 months, clearance at 12 months).

#### Q: 58. A customer returned unopened tiles after 4 months. My supplier's returns policy says "returns within 30 days only." The customer is threatening CPA action. Who is correct?
**Answer:** The CPA gives consumers the right to return goods that are defective or unsafe within 6 months (section 56). However, the CPA does not give consumers an automatic right to return non-defective goods simply because they changed their mind — unless the transaction was a result of direct marketing (5-day cooling off). For non-defective returns, your own returns policy applies, provided it is fair and communicated at point of sale. A 30-day return policy for non-defective goods is reasonable and enforceable. However, if the tiles were defective (shading issues, dimensional inaccuracy, cracking), the 6-month CPA warranty overrides your 30-day policy. Assess the actual reason for return before deciding.

#### Q: 59. A batch of tiles I sold to a contractor was later found to have a factory glaze defect. I gave the contractor a credit. My supplier says the defect claim period has expired (6 months from delivery to me). Can I force them to reimburse me?
**Answer:** This depends on your supply agreement's defect claim clause. Standard supplier terms require notification of defects within 30-90 days of delivery to you. However, under the CPA, latent defects (defects not discoverable on reasonable inspection at delivery) can be claimed within 6 months by the end consumer, and this liability flows up the chain. Your position: (a) if the defect was not discoverable on reasonable inspection when you received the stock, argue it is a latent defect; (b) if the supplier's 6-month limitation is shorter than the CPA's 6-month warranty, the CPA prevails for consumer sales; (c) check if the supplier offers a "dealer protection" policy covering post-sale defects. Going forward: negotiate a 12-month back-to-back warranty from your suppliers and consider product liability insurance.

#### Q: 60. My supplier accepts returns but issues credit notes that can only be used against future purchases, not refunded in cash. I need the cash for operational expenses. Is this legal?
**Answer:** This is a standard supplier practice — credit notes against future purchases rather than cash refunds. It is legal under South African contract law if your supply agreement specifies this. The rationale is that suppliers want to maintain the trading relationship, not fund your cash flow. However, you can negotiate: (a) a cash refund option with an administrative fee (e.g., 5%); (b) credit note conversion to cash after 90 days if not utilised; (c) a maximum credit note balance beyond which cash refunds are automatic. If the supplier refuses and you genuinely need cash flow, consider selling the credit note to a trade credit broker (at a discount) or using it to settle other obligations with the same supplier if they allow cross-utilisation.
## DAMAGED IN TRANSIT

#### Q: 61. A container arrived from Spain with visible seal tampering. On opening, 30% of pallets showed water damage from what appears to be a leak in the vessel's hold. The shipping line says damage occurred before loading. The factory says it was seaworthy. Who is responsible?
**Answer:** Under CIF (Cost Insurance Freight) Incoterms, risk transfers to you at the port of loading in Spain. However, the seller is responsible for procuring marine insurance covering the transit. File a claim with the marine insurance underwriter immediately — they will investigate and subrogate against the shipping line if the carrier was at fault.
