Your actions: (a) lay criminal charges with SAPS; (b) dismiss the labourer for gross misconduct after a disciplinary hearing (even if the stock is recovered, unauthorised removal is a dismissible offence); (c) claim the value of the tiles from the labourer via civil action (though recovery is unlikely); (d) review your access control — how did he get keys or access at 6 AM? Prevention: (a) no employee access to stock outside business hours without manager authorisation; (b) sign-out register for all stock movements; (c) CCTV with motion detection in the yard; (d) clear policy in employment contracts: "Removal of company property without written authorisation constitutes theft and will result in immediate dismissal and criminal prosecution."

#### Q: 134. I installed GPS trackers on all delivery vehicles after a driver diverted a load to sell to a competitor. The drivers' union says this is an invasion of privacy and a violation of POPIA. Are they right?
**Answer:** No — GPS tracking of company vehicles during working hours is a legitimate business interest and does not violate POPIA provided: (a) the drivers are informed that tracking is in place (transparency principle); (b) tracking is limited to business hours and company vehicles; (c) a clear policy explains the purpose (route optimisation, theft prevention, driver safety); (d) data is used only for legitimate business purposes, not to monitor personal activities during breaks. POPIA requires that personal information be collected for a specific, explicitly defined, lawful purpose — vehicle tracking during working hours meets this. Tracking outside working hours or personal vehicles would require consent. The union's objection is likely a bargaining position — address it through a collective agreement that codifies the tracking policy and protects driver rights. Do not track personal devices or personal vehicles without explicit consent.

#### Q: 135. My annual stock take shows a shrinkage rate of 4.2% (R420,000 on R10m turnover). Industry average is 1.5%. My insurer says this is "expected loss" not covered by insurance. How do I reduce shrinkage to insurable levels?
**Answer:** A 4.2% shrinkage rate is unsustainable and indicates systemic control failures. Your shrinkage comprises: theft (internal and external), administrative errors (incorrect receiving, dispatch mistakes), damage, and supplier short-shipping. Immediate actions: (a) install CCTV with 90-day retention covering all entry/exit points and high-value stock areas; (b) implement cycle counting — count 10% of SKUs weekly so every item is counted every 10 weeks; (c) segregate duties — receiving, storage, dispatch, and recording should be done by different people; (d) require dual authorisation for all stock adjustments; (e) weigh-check containers on receipt (compare actual weight to declared weight); (f) install an access control system with swipe cards logging all warehouse entries; (g) conduct unannounced spot checks. Once shrinkage drops below 2% for 6 consecutive months, renegotiate with your insurer for coverage. Some insurers offer premium reductions for verified shrinkage below 1%.
## FLOODING, THEFT & NATURAL DISASTER

#### Q: 136. During the April 2022 KZN floods, my warehouse was inundated. R2.3 million of stock was destroyed. My insurer paid R1.8 million after applying underinsurance (average). My landlord's building insurance did not cover my stock. Can I claim the R500,000 shortfall from the municipality for inadequate stormwater drainage?
**Answer:** Claims against municipalities for flood damage are theoretically possible under the Institution of Legal Proceedings Against Certain Organs of State Act if you can prove: (a) the municipality had a legal duty to maintain stormwater infrastructure; (b) they negligently failed to do so; (c) this failure caused or materially contributed to your loss; (d) the loss was foreseeable. In practice, these claims are extremely difficult: (a) the 2022 KZN floods were an unprecedented natural disaster — the municipality will argue vis major (act of God); (b) proving specific negligence in stormwater maintenance requires expert engineering evidence; (c) municipal claims must be lodged within 6 months of the event. Budget R100,000-R300,000 for legal costs with uncertain prospects. Your better recovery path: (a) SARS disaster relief if declared; (b) claim the R500,000 as a casualty loss for tax purposes (write off the uninsured portion against income); (c) UIF Temporary Employer/Employee Relief Scheme (TERS) if staff were unable to work.

#### Q: 137. My warehouse was burgled and R800,000 of stock stolen. SAPS investigated but no arrests. My insurer is demanding the SAPS case number and investigation report before paying. SAPS says the report is "not available." How do I get my claim paid?
**Answer:** Insurers routinely require a SAPS case number as proof that the theft was reported. They are not entitled to the full investigation report — that is SAPS property and subject to confidentiality. Your approach: (a) provide the SAPS case number and the stamped acknowledgement (CAS number) you received when reporting; (b) if the insurer insists on the investigation report, request a letter from the SAPS station commander confirming the case is under investigation and the report is not yet available; (c) if the insurer still refuses, escalate to the Ombudsman for Short-Term Insurance — they consistently rule that a CAS number is sufficient proof of reporting; (d) for substantial claims, the insurer may appoint their own investigator (loss adjuster) — cooperate fully. The insurer has 30 days to accept or reject a claim once all reasonable documentation is provided. If they exceed this without justification, interest accrues on the claim amount.

#### Q: 138. After a warehouse fire, my insurer appointed a loss adjuster who is taking 4 months to finalise the claim. I need cash to restock and keep the business running. Can I compel the insurer to make an interim payment?
**Answer:** Under the Policyholder Protection Rules (PPR), the insurer must decide on your claim within 30 days of receiving all required documentation. For large or complex claims, this may extend to 60-90 days with written explanation. A 4-month delay is unreasonable. Your remedies: (a) write to the insurer's claims manager demanding an interim payment (citing PPR Rule 6.1) — for undisputed portions of the claim, interim payments should be made; (b) if the delay is causing business failure, threaten to escalate to the Ombudsman and the Prudential Authority; (c) obtain bridging finance from your bank against the expected insurance payout (the bank will require the insurer's acknowledgement of the claim); (d) formally escalate to the Ombudsman — they can award interest on delayed payments at the prescribed rate plus costs. Document every communication — the Ombudsman favours policyholders where insurers delay without justification.

#### Q: 139. My neighbour's tree fell on my warehouse during a storm and damaged the roof, allowing rain to destroy R300,000 of stock. My insurer says this is "neighbour's liability" and I must claim from their insurance. But the neighbour says it was an act of God. Who is correct?
**Answer:** Both arguments have merit. If the tree fell solely due to an unforeseeable storm (no prior rot or disease), it is vis major and your neighbour is not negligent — your insurer should cover it under your own policy. If the tree was visibly diseased, dead, or unstable and the neighbour knew or should have known, they are negligent and liable for the damage. Your action plan: (a) claim from your own insurer immediately — they have a duty to indemnify you and then subrogate against the neighbour if negligence is proven; (b) if your insurer repudiates claiming "third party liability," challenge this — storm damage to your building is an insured peril regardless of the tree's origin; (c) photograph the tree stump for evidence of rot; (d) obtain a tree surgeon's report if possible. Do not accept your insurer's attempt to push you onto the neighbour's insurance — that is subrogation, your insurer's job, not yours.

#### Q: 140. After the KZN floods, I had to close my business for 6 weeks for clean-up and restocking. My business interruption insurance has a 4-week waiting period. I can only claim 2 weeks. But the real loss is the permanent customers who went to competitors. Can I claim for loss of future profits?
**Answer:** Standard business interruption insurance covers loss of gross profit during the indemnity period (typically 12 months) starting after the waiting period. It does NOT cover loss of future profits or market share beyond the indemnity period. Your 2 weeks of coverage will be calculated as: (expected turnover - actual turnover) x gross profit margin, adjusted for trends and seasonal variations. The permanent customer loss is uninsured — this is a business risk. To protect against this: (a) some insurers offer extended indemnity periods (24-36 months) at higher premium; (b) "customer loss" extensions are available as add-ons but are expensive and have specific conditions; (c) the best protection is business continuity planning: pre-arranged alternative supply sources, cloud-based systems, and a recovery plan that gets you operational within days, not weeks. The 6-week closure suggests inadequate disaster preparedness — use this as a catalyst for investment in resilience.
## PRICE MATCHING & COMPETITIVE PRICING

#### Q: 141. A customer showed me a WhatsApp message from a competitor offering identical tiles at 18% below my price. The competitor is selling below cost to clear stock. Must I match the price to keep the customer?
**Answer:** No — you are not obliged to match any competitor's price. Price matching is a commercial strategy, not a legal requirement. Consider: (a) can you afford the margin reduction on this specific job? (b) is the customer price-sensitive or relationship-driven? (c) will matching set a precedent for this customer? (d) is the competitor's offer genuine or a loss leader on a small quantity? Your options: (a) match partially (e.g., 10% discount, not 18%); (b) offer value-adds instead of price reduction (free delivery, extended payment terms, installation advice); (c) explain your value proposition (stock availability, technical support, warranty handling); (d) let the customer go if they are purely price-driven — they are not profitable long-term. If the competitor is a major chain pricing below cost consistently, consider a Competition Commission complaint for predatory pricing — but only if you have documented evidence of sustained below-cost selling.

#### Q: 142. A contractor claims CTM quoted him a price for my supplier's tiles that is lower than my cost price from the same supplier. How is this possible and is the supplier giving CTM preferential pricing?
**Answer:** Several possibilities: (a) CTM achieved a volume-based tier price that you do not qualify for; (b) CTM is buying a different grade or specification with the same face design (B-grade vs. first grade); (c) CTM negotiated a centralised distribution deal with significant rebate structure not available to independents; (d) CTM is selling at a loss to win the contractor's overall business (cross-subsidising); (e) the contractor is bluffing or comparing different products. Your response: do not automatically assume supplier favouritism. Ask the contractor for the written CTM quote with full product specifications and batch details. If it genuinely is the identical product at below your cost, approach your supplier for a pricing review citing competitive pressure. Request a "most favoured customer" clause for future protection. If the supplier refuses, evaluate whether the range is viable for you or whether you should focus on ranges where you have competitive positioning.

#### Q: 143. I want to run a "we will beat any written quote by 5%" promotion. What are the legal risks of this advertising claim?
**Answer:** This is a comparative pricing claim regulated by the Advertising Regulatory Board Code. Risks: (a) the claim must be genuine — you must actually beat any written quote by 5% when presented, with reasonable terms and conditions; (b) you must specify exclusions (e.g., clearance stock, imported specials, online-only prices, quotes from outside your trading area); (c) the competitor quote must be for an identical product (same brand, specification, grade, batch where relevant); (d) you must honour the claim promptly — failure to do so is misleading advertising; (e) you cannot require the customer to purchase from you to get the price match (that is conditional selling). Your terms and conditions must be clear and displayed prominently. Keep records of all price matches honoured — the Advertising Regulatory Board may request evidence if a competitor complains. The biggest risk is attracting price-shoppers who have no loyalty.

#### Q: 144. A supplier offers me a "price protection" scheme: if they reduce the list price of a range within 6 months of my purchase, they refund the difference. Is this common and are there hidden catches?
**Answer:** Price protection schemes are offered by some suppliers to encourage stock commitment but are not universal. Common catches: (a) the refund may be in the form of credit notes, not cash; (b) the protection applies only to list price reductions, not promotional or clearance pricing; (c) you must claim within a short window (e.g., 30 days of the price reduction); (d) the protection may be void if you have not paid for the original stock; (e) volume rebates or special deal pricing may be excluded from the protection calculation. Evaluate: price protection is valuable if you are buying ahead of a known price increase, but less valuable if the supplier rarely reduces list prices (they usually introduce new SKUs at lower prices instead). Get the terms in writing and model the scenarios before relying on it.

#### Q: 145. A competitor is advertising "factory prices to the public" and claiming to sell at the same price as wholesalers like me. Their prices are indeed at my cost level. How can they do this and is it false advertising?
**Answer:** "Factory prices" is a marketing term, not a legal standard. It typically means "low prices comparable to what you would pay at a factory outlet" — not literally the ex-factory price. It is not false advertising unless they explicitly claim to be selling at the manufacturer's ex-works price when they are not. They can achieve low prices by: (a) importing direct with lower overheads; (b) operating on razor-thin margins to build market share; (c) selling B-grade or factory seconds as first grade; (d) clearing stock with no warranty support. Your response: do not compete on price — emphasise the services and guarantees that justify your pricing: technical advice, stock availability, returns policy, CPA warranty handling, and established reputation. Some customers will always choose the cheapest option — they are not your target market. Focus on quality-conscious customers and commercial clients who need reliability.
## TRADE SHOWS & EXPO DEALS

#### Q: 146. At the Tile Africa Expo, a supplier offered me "show-only" pricing with 25% discount if I sign the order at the expo. I signed but now realise the "discounted" price is still 10% above what I could negotiate normally. Can I cancel the order?
**Answer:** Under the CPA, if the transaction was concluded at a trade show or expo, it may be considered a transaction "resulting from direct marketing" if the supplier actively solicited you at the show — giving you a 5-day cooling-off right. However, B2B transactions at trade shows are generally not covered by the direct marketing cooling-off because both parties are acting in the course of business. Your cancellation right depends on your order terms: (a) if the order contains a cancellation clause, follow it; (b) if the supplier made a fraudulent misrepresentation (knowingly inflated the "normal" price to create an artificial discount), you can rescind for fraud; (c) if it was merely aggressive salesmanship, you are bound. Your practical recourse: negotiate — most suppliers prefer to maintain the relationship rather than enforce a disputed expo order. Threaten to publicise the misleading "discount" within the industry if they refuse to negotiate.

#### Q: 147. I signed a large expo order under the influence of alcohol at the supplier's evening networking event. The next morning I realised I had committed to R1.2 million of stock I cannot afford. Is the contract voidable?
**Answer:** A contract entered into while intoxicated MAY be voidable if you can prove: (a) you were so intoxicated that you lacked contractual capacity (did not understand the nature of the transaction); (b) the supplier knew or should have known of your intoxication; (c) you repudiate promptly upon sobering up (next morning is prompt); (d) the supplier has not relied on the contract to their detriment (e.g., placed factory orders). This is a difficult defence — courts are sceptical of "I was drunk" claims because adults are expected to control their alcohol consumption. Your better approach: contact the supplier immediately, explain the situation honestly, and negotiate a reduction or cancellation. Most suppliers have encountered this and will work with you if you act quickly and in good faith. If the supplier insists on enforcement, consult a lawyer — but expect to pay something (restocking fee, administration cost) for the cancellation.

#### Q: 148. A supplier at a trade show offered me a "exclusive expo range" not available to non-attendees. I took 5,000 m². Two months later, I discovered the same range being sold by another merchant who did not attend the expo. Was I misled?
**Answer:** If the supplier represented the range as "exclusive to expo attendees" and you relied on this exclusivity in making your purchase decision, this could be misleading conduct under section 41 of the CPA and the Advertising Code. However: (a) "exclusive expo range" may mean "first available at the expo" not "permanently exclusive to attendees"; (b) if the other merchant bought the range after the expo from regular distribution, the supplier may not have breached any promise; (c) your remedy depends on what was expressly promised — check your expo order and any marketing materials. If there was a clear exclusivity promise that was breached, demand: (a) a volume rebate to compensate for lost exclusivity premium; (b) genuine exclusivity on the next range; (c) or return the unsold stock at full credit. Document the supplier's expo claims with photographs of their stand signage.

#### Q: 149. I attended a trade show in Milan and signed a purchase order with an Italian manufacturer. Back in South Africa, I want to cancel because the rand has weakened 15%. The manufacturer says the order is binding and will enforce it in Italy. What are my options?
**Answer:** An order signed at a trade show is binding unless it contains a cancellation clause. Your options: (a) check the Incoterms and payment terms — if no deposit has been paid and no letter of credit opened, the manufacturer's leverage is reduced (but they can still sue for damages); (b) negotiate a cancellation fee (typically 15-30% of order value for factory-direct orders); (c) if the manufacturer misrepresented any material fact at the show, you may have a claim for misrepresentation; (d) check if your order is subject to the UN Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (CISG) — both Italy and South Africa are signatories, which provides uniform rules for international sales including remedies for breach; (e) the rand weakening is not grounds for cancellation — it is a normal commercial risk. If you proceed, hedge your currency exposure immediately. If you cancel, expect to pay a negotiated exit fee rather than the full order value.

#### Q: 150. At a trade show, a supplier's sales rep verbally promised "permanent free delivery" as part of an expo deal. The written order confirmation excludes delivery. Which prevails — the verbal promise or the written terms?
**Answer:** Under the parol evidence rule, when parties have reduced their agreement to writing, extrinsic (verbal) evidence is generally inadmissible to contradict the written terms. However, exceptions apply: (a) if the verbal promise was a term that both parties intended to include but was omitted by mistake (rectification); (b) if the written terms are ambiguous, extrinsic evidence can clarify; (c) if there was a collateral contract (separate from the main written agreement). In practice, proving a verbal promise against written terms is very difficult. Your approach: (a) contact the sales rep and ask them to confirm the delivery promise in writing (email follow-up); (b) if they confirm, the supplier is bound; (c) if they deny it, you have a difficult evidentiary problem. The lesson: always confirm verbal promises in writing before signing, or ensure they are included in the written order. For expo deals, take photographs of any promotional signage that includes the verbal promise.
## B-BBEE REQUIREMENTS

#### Q: 151. A major developer client informed me that I must achieve BEE Level 4 by next year or they will remove me from their approved supplier list. I am a white-owned business with R18 million turnover. Is there a realistic path to Level 4?
**Answer:** Yes. As a QSE (Qualifying Small Enterprise, turnover R10m-R50m), you are measured on all 5 BEE elements but with relaxed thresholds compared to large enterprises. To achieve Level 4 as a white-owned QSE: (a) Ownership (25 points) — you score 0 on black ownership, but QSEs need not score points in every element; (b) Management Control (15 points) — appoint black senior managers and board members if possible; (c) Skills Development (25 points) — train black employees (including learnerships and apprenticeships); (d) Enterprise and Supplier Development (30 points) — procure from black-owned suppliers, offer payment terms to black-owned businesses; (e) Socio-Economic Development (5 points) — donate to qualifying beneficiaries. With strong performance in Skills Development, ESD, and SED, you can achieve Level 4 despite zero ownership points. Engage a BEE consultant — the cost (R8,000-R15,000) is recoverable through improved tender success.

#### Q: 152. A supplier offered me a "BEE partnership" where I effectively lend my BEE certificate to their business for a fee, but I have no real involvement. Is this fronting and what are the penalties?
**Answer:** This is classic fronting — a criminal offence under the B-BBEE Amendment Act 46 of 2013. Penalties include: (a) fines of up to 10% of annual turnover; (b) imprisonment of up to 10 years; (c) cancellation of contracts; (d) debarment from doing business with government for up to 10 years; (e) listing on a register of fronting practitioners. The B-BBEE Commission actively investigates fronting tip-offs. A genuine BEE partnership requires: real ownership transfer (not nominee holding), actual participation in management and control, real economic interest in profits and losses, and genuine operational involvement. If a supplier approaches you with a "lend your certificate" arrangement, report it to the B-BBEE Commission and decline immediately. The reputational and legal risk far exceeds any fee offered.

#### Q: 153. I am a white-owned tile merchant. To improve my BEE score, I am considering selling 51% of my business to a black partner. What are the risks of BEE ownership transactions that I should consider?
**Answer:** BEE ownership transactions carry significant risks if not properly structured: (a) funding — if the black partner cannot self-fund, the deal typically involves vendor financing (you lend them the money to buy your shares) — if the business underperforms, you may not recover the loan; (b) control — a 51% partner has voting control — ensure your shareholders' agreement protects your operational role and includes a "golden share" or veto on key decisions; (c) net value — the BEE Codes require the black partner to pay off the acquisition debt within a reasonable period (typically 7-10 years) — if they cannot, your BEE points may not be recognised; (d) exit — what happens if the relationship fails? Include buy-back provisions and dispute resolution mechanisms. Engage a specialist BEE ownership structuring lawyer — the cost of proper structuring (R30,000-R80,000) is minor compared to the value at risk.

#### Q: 154. My black business partner in a tile merchant venture wants to exit after 3 years and sell his shares back to me. Will I lose my BEE Level 2 status immediately?
**Answer:** BEE status is based on a verified scorecard at a specific measurement date. If your partner exits, your ownership points drop and your overall level may decrease. However: (a) if the transaction is structured as a "graduated sale" where ownership transfers incrementally, the BEE Codes may recognise partial ownership for a transition period; (b) if your partner sells to another black shareholder, your ownership points are preserved; (c) if you have built sufficient points in other elements (Skills Development, ESD, Management Control), you may maintain your overall level despite reduced ownership points. Plan the exit: (a) begin the sale process 12 months before your next BEE verification; (b) find a replacement black shareholder; (c) or invest heavily in Skills Development and ESD to compensate for ownership point loss. Do not let the exit happen without a BEE transition plan.

#### Q: 155. A government tender requires a BEE joint venture between a tile supplier and an installation contractor. The tender is worth R8 million. What BEE structure maximises our scoring?
**Answer:** For a joint venture (JV) tender, BEE recognition depends on the JV agreement structure: (a) if the JV is an unincorporated consortium, each party's BEE level contributes proportionally based on their work share; (b) if the JV is a incorporated vehicle, it is measured as a separate entity. For maximum BEE scoring: (a) ensure the JV entity is at least 51% black-owned (this qualifies for enhanced preference points under the 80/20 preference point system for tenders below R50 million); (b) allocate work share to maximise the black-owned partner's contribution; (c) ensure the black partner has genuine operational responsibility, not just a passive share; (d) both parties must have valid BEE certificates; (e) the JV agreement must specify BEE obligations and consequences of non-performance. The 80/20 system gives significant advantage to Level 1-4 BEE entities — a 51% black-owned JV can qualify for maximum preference points. Have the JV agreement reviewed by a BEE specialist before tender submission.
## LEGAL DISPUTES & PRODUCT LIABILITY

#### Q: 156. A customer slipped on tiles I sold them (polished porcelain in their entrance hall) and fractured their hip. They are suing me for R2.5 million. The tiles were correctly installed and met SANS standards. Am I liable?
**Answer:** Under section 61 of the CPA, producers (including distributors) face strict liability for harm caused by unsafe goods. However, polished porcelain is inherently slippery when wet — this is a known characteristic, not a defect, provided the tiles were correctly classified under SANS 10107 (slip resistance). Your defences: (a) the tiles were not unsafe — they performed as expected for their classification; (b) the customer was warned of the slip risk (in writing, at point of sale); (c) the customer chose polished porcelain for an entrance hall despite advice against it; (d) the harm was caused by the customer's failure to use entrance mats or clean up water promptly, not by the tiles. You need: (a) a slip resistance test report (R2,000-R4,000) confirming the tile's R-rating; (b) your sales documentation showing the customer was advised; (c) product liability insurance — this is exactly what it is for. Notify your insurer immediately.

#### Q: 157. A contractor installed tiles I supplied using a different adhesive than I recommended. The installation failed and the homeowner is suing both of us. Can I be held liable for the contractor's choice of adhesive?
**Answer:** Your liability depends on your role and what you represented: (a) if you merely supplied tiles with no installation involvement, you are not liable for the contractor's adhesive choice — the homeowner's claim against you will fail unless the tiles themselves were defective; (b) if you recommended or supplied the adhesive, you share liability for its suitability; (c) if you arranged the contractor (even as an introduction), you may have assumed a duty of care for their work. Your defence: the failed installation was caused by the contractor's deviation from manufacturer specifications, not by your product. However, the homeowner will sue both parties and let the court apportion liability. Ensure your sales documentation includes a disclaimer: "Tile installation should be performed by a qualified tiler following adhesive manufacturer specifications. The tile merchant is not responsible for installation method or materials selected by the installer."

#### Q: 158. A customer bought tiles from me, installed them, and 2 years later claims the tiles are "defective" because the colour has faded in the sun. Porcelain does not fade. Can they still claim under the CPA?
**Answer:** The CPA 6-month implied warranty (section 56) applies to defects that existed at the time of sale. A claim 2 years later is outside this period unless the customer can prove: (a) a latent defect that was not discoverable within 6 months; (b) an express warranty (e.g., "10-year colour warranty") that extends the protection period; (c) the tiles were unsafe (not applicable here). Fading in porcelain tiles after 2 years of sun exposure is virtually impossible — porcelain is UV-stable. Your response: (a) request photographic evidence of the "fading"; (b) offer an independent assessment by a tiling inspector (at the customer's cost if no defect is found); (c) explain that porcelain tiles are inert and do not fade — any colour change is due to surface soiling, grout discolouration, or cleaning chemical damage; (d) provide a cleaning and maintenance guide. If the customer persists, their claim is outside the CPA warranty period and lacks technical merit. Do not offer a goodwill replacement — it validates a false claim.

#### Q: 159. A developer is threatening to sue me for R5 million because tiles I supplied for a apartment complex allegedly caused water ingress into units below. The tiles were installed on balconies. My supplier says the tiles are not at fault. I am caught in the middle. What do I do?
**Answer:** This is a classic product liability chain dispute. Your immediate actions: (a) notify both your product liability insurer AND your supplier in writing within 24 hours — most policies have notification time limits; (b) do not admit liability to the developer — say "we are investigating and will respond through our legal representatives"; (c) engage an independent tiling inspector to assess the cause of failure — likely causes: incorrect fall to drain, missing or failed waterproofing membrane, incorrect adhesive/grout, cracked tiles due to substrate movement, or missing expansion joints. The tiles themselves are rarely the cause of water ingress on balconies; (d) if the inspection shows non-tile-related failure, share the report with the developer and deny liability; (e) if the tiles contributed (e.g., excessive water absorption for the application), claim against your supplier. The developer will likely sue all parties (you, the tiler, the architect, the waterproofing contractor) and let the court apportion liability. Your insurance should cover defence costs and any settlement.

#### Q: 160. A customer is threatening to lay criminal charges against me for "fraud" because I sold them tiles that were advertised as "Italian" but manufactured in Turkey under licence from an Italian company. Have I committed fraud?
**Answer:** Fraud requires intentional misrepresentation with intent to deceive and cause prejudice. If you genuinely believed the tiles were Italian-made and the packaging/branding did not mislead you, there is no fraud — at most, an innocent misrepresentation. However, if you KNEW the tiles were Turkish-made and represented them as Italian to command a premium price, this could be fraud. Your defence: (a) the tiles are branded by an Italian company — they are "Italian design" even if manufactured in Turkey; (b) this is common in the tile industry — many "Italian" brands manufacture in Turkey, India, and China; (c) you did not specifically state "made in Italy" — the customer assumed. Going forward: be precise in your origin claims. Use "Italian-designed" or "Italian brand" rather than "Italian" if manufacturing is offshore. If the Italian company owns the Turkish factory, "Italian-owned" is accurate. Train your sales staff on correct origin terminology to avoid these disputes.
## COVENANT RESTRICTIONS & LEASE ISSUES

#### Q: 161. My mall lease contains a "tenant mix" clause giving the landlord the right to object to my product range if it competes with an existing tenant. I want to add engineered wood flooring, but the landlord says the flooring specialist tenant has exclusivity. Can I challenge this?
**Answer:** Exclusivity clauses in mall leases are common and generally enforceable if clearly worded. Check: (a) does the flooring specialist's lease actually contain an exclusivity clause for "wood flooring"? (b) does the exclusivity cover "engineered wood" specifically or just "solid wood flooring"? (c) is there a radius restriction? If the exclusivity is genuine and your lease acknowledges the landlord's right to enforce tenant mix, you cannot add engineered wood without landlord consent. Negotiate: (a) request the landlord obtain a waiver from the flooring tenant (unlikely); (b) differentiate your offering — perhaps laminate or vinyl flooring is not covered by the exclusivity; (c) offer to relocate to a different position in the centre where the exclusivity does not apply; (d) if the exclusivity was not disclosed when you signed your lease, argue it was a material non-disclosure and request rent reduction or right to terminate. Going forward: read existing tenant exclusivities before signing any mall lease.

#### Q: 162. My warehouse lease says I cannot store "hazardous materials." I need to stock tile adhesive containing cement (which contains trace chromium VI). Is cement classified as hazardous, and can my landlord evict me?
**Answer:** Cement is classified as hazardous under the Globally Harmonised System (GHS) due to its alkaline nature and potential for skin/eye irritation. Chromium VI is a known carcinogen and regulated under the Hazardous Chemical Substances Regulations. However, bagged cement and cement-based adhesives in sealed packaging are generally considered "consumer products" rather than bulk hazardous materials. Your position: (a) tile adhesive in sealed bags is not "hazardous material storage" in the sense the lease intends (bulk chemicals, flammables, toxics); (b) cement is widely used in construction and its storage in a warehouse is standard practice; (c) if the landlord objects, offer to store adhesives in a designated area with spill containment and MSDS sheets available. If the landlord is inflexible, obtain a written opinion from an occupational hygienist confirming that sealed-bag adhesive storage does not constitute hazardous material storage under the HCS Regulations. Eviction for this would be unreasonable and challengeable.

#### Q: 163. My lease requires me to obtain "written landlord consent" for any alterations to the premises. I need to install a racking system for tile storage that bolts to the floor. Is this an "alteration" requiring consent?
**Answer:** Yes — bolted racking is a fixture (permanently attached to the property) and constitutes an alteration under virtually all commercial leases. Consequences of proceeding without consent: (a) breach of lease, potentially leading to termination; (b) the landlord can require you to remove the racking and make good at your cost; (c) on lease termination, the landlord may claim the racking as their property (doctrine of fixtures). Your approach: (a) submit detailed racking plans to the landlord with structural engineer sign-off; (b) offer to remove and make good at the end of the lease; (c) negotiate that the racking remains as a lease improvement (benefiting the landlord) with a rent adjustment; (d) obtain written consent before any installation. Some landlords welcome racking installation because it improves the premises for future warehouse tenants — frame it as a value-add.

#### Q: 164. My lease has 3 years remaining but the landlord has sold the property. The new owner wants to develop the site into residential apartments and has given me 6 months' notice to vacate. Can they do this?
**Answer:** No — the new owner inherits the existing lease under the "huur gaat voor koop" principle (lease prevails over sale). They cannot terminate before the lease expiry unless: (a) your lease contains a demolition/renovation clause allowing early termination for redevelopment; (b) you agree to a negotiated exit (compensation for moving costs, loss of profit, and potentially a "key money" payment); (c) they offer to relocate you to alternative premises of equivalent quality. If none of these apply, you can remain until lease expiry. The new owner may offer a financial incentive for early departure — negotiate hard: (a) moving costs (R50,000-R150,000); (b) loss of profits for the unexpired term (get a valuation); (c) fit-out cost compensation for improvements you made; (d) signage and marketing cost to announce the move. Do not accept the first offer — redevelopment pressure creates leverage for you.

#### Q: 165. My warehouse lease includes a "gross turnover rent" clause — I pay base rent plus 2% of turnover above a threshold. Do I include tile installation revenue (which I outsource) in my turnover calculation?
**Answer:** This depends on your lease's definition of "gross turnover." Standard definitions include "all revenue derived from the business conducted on the premises" — which could include installation revenue if the installation is arranged from your premises. However, if you merely introduce the contractor and the installation contract is between the customer and the contractor (with you taking a commission or referral fee), only your commission/fee is your revenue, not the full installation value. Check: (a) how do you invoice the customer — for tiles only, or tiles plus installation? (b) does the installation money pass through your account? (c) what does your lease say about "turnover" — is it defined as money received by you, or value of all transactions? Ambiguity favours the tenant in lease interpretation — if the definition is unclear, you can argue installation revenue is not your turnover. Get a legal opinion if the amount in dispute is significant.
## FIRE DEPARTMENT COMPLIANCE

#### Q: 166. My warehouse stores tiles on racking 5 metres high. The fire department inspected and says I need a "storage licence" because the racking exceeds 4 metres. Is this correct and what does it cost?
**Answer:** Under municipal fire by-laws (varies by municipality), high-rack storage may require a fire safety certificate or storage permit when exceeding specified heights. The 4-metre threshold is common. Requirements typically include: (a) sprinkler system designed for high-rack storage ( Ordinary Hazard Group III or High Hazard, depending on racking configuration); (b) fire detection and alarm system; (c) compartmentalisation (fire-rated walls every certain area); (d) adequate fire exits and emergency lighting; (e) flue spaces in racking to allow water penetration; (f) fire department access around the building. Costs: sprinkler installation for a 2,000 m² warehouse with 5m racking: R400,000-R800,000; fire certificate application: R5,000-R15,000 depending on municipality; annual inspection fees: R2,000-R5,000. Non-compliance can result in closure of the premises. Engage a fire engineer for a compliance assessment — they can often design a solution that meets requirements at lower cost than the fire department's initial demands.

#### Q: 167. The fire department says my tile warehouse needs a fire hydrant within 45 metres of the building. The nearest municipal hydrant is 120 metres away. They want me to install a private fire hydrant at my cost. Is this my responsibility?
**Answer:** Municipal by-laws typically require the property owner to ensure adequate firefighting water supply. If the municipal hydrant is beyond the effective distance, you may need to install: (a) a private fire hydrant connected to the municipal main (requires municipal consent and connection fee); (b) a fire water tank (minimum 20,000-45,000 litres depending on floor area and hazard classification); (c) a booster pump to achieve required pressure. The cost is yours as the property owner (or tenant, depending on lease terms). Budget: private hydrant connection R30,000-R60,000; fire water tank with pump R80,000-R150,000. Check your lease — if the landlord is responsible for building compliance, they should bear this cost. If you are responsible, factor it into your rent negotiation at lease renewal. This is a life safety requirement — non-compliance can result in criminal prosecution under the Fire Services Act.

#### Q: 168. I want to use part of my warehouse for adhesive and grout mixing (creating a small "wet area"). The fire department says this changes my occupancy classification from "storage" to "factory" with significantly higher fire safety requirements. Is this correct?
**Answer:** Yes — changing use from storage to manufacturing/processing changes your occupancy classification under SANS 10400-T (fire protection) and potentially SANS 10400-D (public safety). "Factory" occupancy typically requires: (a) higher fire resistance rating for structural elements; (b) more stringent exit requirements (travel distances to exits, exit width); (c) enhanced ventilation (especially for dust-generating processes); (d) potentially a change of use approval from the municipality. If the mixing area is incidental (less than 10% of floor area) and uses non-flammable materials (cement-based adhesives), you may negotiate a mixed occupancy classification with the building control officer. However, if solvents or flammable additives are involved, the fire safety requirements escalate significantly. Get a fire engineer's report before proceeding with any manufacturing activity in a storage facility.

#### Q: 169. My fire sprinkler system activated accidentally and water-damaged R200,000 of stock. The sprinkler maintenance company says the activation was due to a manufacturing defect in the head. My insurer says the damage is not covered because it resulted from "mechanical breakdown" not an insured peril. Is this right?
**Answer:** Check your policy wording carefully. Standard commercial policies cover "accidental damage" including sprinkler leakage. Some policies exclude damage caused by "mechanical or electrical breakdown" of equipment — but sprinkler heads are passive devices, not mechanical equipment. Your argument: (a) the sprinkler activation was an accidental discharge, which is a standard insured peril; (b) the maintenance company may be liable for negligence if they recently serviced the system; (c) the sprinkler manufacturer may be liable for the defective head under product liability. Your recovery paths: (a) claim from your insurer under accidental damage; (b) if insurer repudiates, escalate to the Ombudsman; (c) claim from the maintenance company's professional indemnity insurance; (d) claim from the sprinkler manufacturer. Do not accept the first repudiation — sprinkler discharge is one of the most common commercial property claims and is generally covered. Document: the head that failed, maintenance records, and the water damage extent.

#### Q: 170. The fire department has ordered me to clear a 1.5-metre aisle between all racking rows for fire access. This reduces my storage capacity by 20%. Can I claim compensation from the municipality for loss of storage income?
**Answer:** No — fire safety requirements are imposed in the public interest and the municipality is immune from claims for loss resulting from compliance with safety regulations (unless the requirement is ultra vires or applied discriminatorily). The 1.5-metre aisle requirement is standard in SANS 10400-T and international fire codes — it allows firefighters to access and suppress fires within racking systems. Your options: (a) comply — the fire department can close your premises for non-compliance; (b) install an in-rack sprinkler system, which may allow narrower aisles (consult a fire engineer); (c) reconfigure racking to optimise the remaining space — consider narrower racking with deeper bays; (d) rent additional off-site storage to compensate for the lost capacity. Factor fire compliance costs into your pricing — it is a cost of doing business. The fire department's order is not negotiable without professional engineering intervention.
## ENVIRONMENTAL COMPLIANCE

#### Q: 171. I generate approximately 8 tonnes of tile offcuts and broken tiles per year. The waste collector I use dumps them illegally in a field in Soweto. I discovered this when I saw my branded boxes in a news report about illegal dumping. Am I liable?
**Answer:** Yes — under the National Environmental Management Act (NEMA) and the Waste Act, the generator of waste is responsible for its lawful disposal from cradle to grave. Illegal dumping by your waste contractor does not absolve you — you have a duty of care to ensure lawful disposal. Penalties: (a) fines up to R10 million or imprisonment up to 10 years for serious offences; (b) cleanup costs; (c) reputational damage. Your immediate actions: (a) engage a reputable licensed waste management contractor with a Waste Management Licence; (b) request waste manifest documents for every collection (proof of lawful disposal at a licensed landfill); (c) if the illegal dumping is traced to you, cooperate with authorities and fund the cleanup — this may reduce penalties; (d) lay criminal charges against the rogue contractor. Going forward: only use contractors registered on the South African Waste Information Centre (SAWIC) database. Request quarterly waste disposal certificates. The cost of lawful disposal (R500-R1,500 per tonne) is far less than the liability for illegal dumping.

#### Q: 172. My tile cutting operation generates silica dust. An inspector from the Department of Employment and Labour visited and issued a compliance notice requiring dust extraction and health surveillance for workers. What must I do and what will it cost?
**Answer:** Silica dust is a regulated hazardous chemical substance under the Hazardous Chemical Substances Regulations (HCS). Your obligations: (a) install local exhaust ventilation (LEV) at cutting stations — budget R15,000-R40,000 per station; (b) conduct air monitoring to ensure respirable silica levels are below the Occupational Exposure Limit (OEL) of 0.1 mg/m³; (c) implement a health surveillance programme — baseline and annual medical examinations (chest X-ray, lung function test) for exposed workers — R800-R1,500 per worker per year; (d) provide respiratory protective equipment (RPE) as a last resort after engineering controls; (e) train workers on silica hazards; (f) maintain a health and safety file. Non-compliance fines: R50,000-R500,000 per violation. The inspector can issue a prohibition notice stopping work until compliance. Engage an occupational hygienist for an assessment and remediation plan — their report (R8,000-R15,000) demonstrates good faith and may reduce penalties.

#### Q: 173. I want to recycle my tile offcuts into aggregate for concrete production. Do I need a waste management licence for this, and are tile offcuts classified as general waste or hazardous waste?
**Answer:** Tile offcuts (ceramic and porcelain) are generally classified as General Waste (GW) under the Waste Classification and Management Regulations, not hazardous waste — provided they do not contain lead-based glazes or other contaminants. However, processing waste for recycling may trigger licensing requirements: (a) if you are merely separating and storing offcuts for collection by a recycler, no licence is needed; (b) if you are crushing/processing the tiles on-site to produce aggregate, you may need a Waste Management Licence under Category C (waste storage and treatment) or an exemption from the DEFF; (c) if annual waste processed exceeds specified thresholds, a licence is definitely required. Contact your provincial Department of Environment for guidance — many provinces have streamlined processes for low-risk waste recycling. Alternatively, partner with an existing licensed recycler who collects your offcuts and processes them at their facility — this avoids licensing requirements for you.

#### Q: 174. A customer returned tiles and claimed they were "environmentally unsafe" because the packaging stated they were "tested on animals." This is impossible for tiles. But the customer threatened to report me to the Green Scorpions ( environmental enforcement). How do I handle this?
**Answer:** This appears to be a confused or vexatious customer. Ceramic tiles are inorganic mineral products — no animal testing is involved in tile manufacturing. The "Green Scorpions" (the Environmental Management Inspectorate) deal with serious environmental crimes — they do not respond to consumer complaints about product labelling. Your response: (a) calmly explain that ceramic tiles are mineral-based and no animal testing is conducted; (b) offer to show the product's environmental certifications (if any) such as ISO 14001, Ecolabel, or LEED compliance; (c) if the customer persists, ask for their concern in writing — vexatious complainants rarely follow through; (d) do not offer a refund for a non-existent issue — it encourages frivolous complaints. If the customer reports you to any authority, cooperate fully — a quick explanation will resolve the matter. Consider whether this customer is genuinely confused or attempting to extort a refund through threats.

#### Q: 175. My landlord is demanding I remove all asbestos-containing material (ACMs) from the warehouse roof before my lease ends. The asbestos was there before I moved in. Who is responsible for removal and what does it cost?
**Answer:** Asbestos removal is regulated under the Asbestos Abatement Regulations 2020. Responsibility depends on your lease: (a) if the lease requires you to return the premises in the same condition as received (fair wear and tear excepted), you are not responsible for pre-existing asbestos; (b) if you disturbed the asbestos during your tenancy (e.g., drilled into roof sheets, damaged them), you may be liable for remediation; (c) the landlord as property owner has ultimate responsibility for asbestos management. Asbestos roof removal for a 1,000 m² warehouse: R250,000-R500,000 including licensed removal, disposal at a licensed hazardous waste facility, air monitoring, and clearance certificate. The landlord cannot force you to pay for pre-existing asbestos unless your lease specifically requires it. If there is a dispute, the Department of Employment and Labour can issue a compliance notice to the property owner (the landlord). Do not attempt DIY removal — it is a criminal offence.
## ONLINE MARKETPLACE SELLING

#### Q: 176. I want to sell tiles on the Builders Marketplace platform. They require me to commit to 48-hour dispatch and 14-day free returns. My tiles are bulky and returns cost R350 per box. How do I make this economically viable?
**Answer:** Marketplace selling of tiles is challenging due to logistics costs. Strategies: (a) limit marketplace offerings to your fastest-moving, highest-margin SKUs — do not list the full range; (b) build the return cost into your pricing — add R200-R300 per box to cover expected return rate (typically 8-15% for building materials due to customer ordering errors); (c) use detailed product descriptions, high-resolution images, and a "order samples first" option to reduce wrong-purchase returns; (d) negotiate with the marketplace for category-specific terms — Builders may accept 72-hour dispatch and customer-paid returns for bulky items; (e) offer "click and collect" from your warehouse as the primary fulfilment method, with delivery as a paid option; (f) set minimum order values (e.g., R2,000) to make the logistics cost bearable. Calculate your total cost of selling on the platform: commission (10-15%) + payment processing (2-3%) + packaging (R50/box) + delivery (R150-350 depending on distance) + returns allocation (8-15% of revenue) = total cost of 25-40%. Your marketplace pricing must reflect this.

#### Q: 177. A customer bought tiles from me on Takealot and gave a 1-star review stating "tiles arrived broken, merchant refused refund." The tiles were intact when dispatched — the customer dropped them during unloading. Can I have the review removed?
**Answer:** Takealot's review policy allows merchants to flag reviews for removal if they are: factually incorrect, contain abusive language, or violate platform rules. However, Takealot generally does not remove negative reviews unless they breach specific policies. Your options: (a) respond professionally to the review: "We are sorry to hear about the damage. Our dispatch records show the tiles were inspected and securely packaged. We have offered to arrange a damage assessment — please contact us directly"; (b) flag the review to Takealot as "disputed factual claim" — provide your dispatch photos and packaging records; (c) if the review is defamatory (makes objectively false statements that damage your reputation), consult a lawyer about a takedown letter — but this is expensive and slow; (d) proactively encourage satisfied customers to leave positive reviews to dilute the negative one. The most effective response is a professional, factual reply that demonstrates to future customers that you handle issues responsibly.

#### Q: 178. Builders Marketplace requires all product images to have a white background and no branding watermarks. My supplier's brand images have their logo embedded. If I remove the logo, am I infringing their copyright or trademark?
**Answer:** If the supplier has provided you with authorised product images for marketing purposes, removing their logo to comply with marketplace requirements is generally permissible — you are adapting the image for a specific sales channel, not claiming ownership of the brand. However: (a) check your distributorship agreement for image modification rights; (b) do not replace the supplier's logo with your own — that would be passing off; (c) if the logo is integral to the product design (e.g., embossed on the tile face), it cannot be removed as it represents the actual product. Best practice: request "marketplace-ready" images from the supplier without watermarks. Most suppliers have these for digital commerce. If they do not, take your own product photographs — this also differentiates your listing from competitors using the same supplier images.

#### Q: 179. I sold tiles through an online marketplace and the customer paid via credit card. Three months later, the customer initiated a chargeback claiming "item not as described." The tiles were exactly as described. How do I dispute the chargeback?
**Answer:** Chargebacks are a significant risk for online merchants. Your dispute process: (a) respond to the marketplace/payment processor within the specified timeframe (usually 7-10 days); (b) provide evidence: product description page screenshots, product specifications, photos of the actual tiles dispatched, delivery confirmation with signature, and any customer correspondence; (c) if you have a signed delivery note and the customer did not report issues within 7 days of delivery, argue they accepted the goods; (d) under CPA section 55, the customer must prove the goods are not reasonably suitable for the purpose — a vague "not as described" claim requires substantiation. Chargeback win rates for merchants are low (20-30%) because card schemes tend to favour cardholders. Prevention: (a) detailed product descriptions with measurements, material specifications, and care instructions; (b) require customer acknowledgement of product details before checkout; (c) photograph every order before dispatch; (d) use signed delivery confirmation. Build a chargeback reserve of 2-3% of online revenue.

#### Q: 180. An online marketplace customer ordered 50 boxes of tiles, received them, and then claimed they "never ordered them" — suggesting fraud on their account. The marketplace refunded them and debited my account. The tiles were not returned. What can I do?
**Answer:** This is "friendly fraud" — increasingly common in e-commerce. Your options: (a) dispute the refund through the marketplace's seller protection programme — provide delivery confirmation, GPS tracking, and signature; (b) if the marketplace debited your account without a valid return, this may breach their seller terms — escalate to their merchant support team and threaten to delist; (c) the customer has been unjustly enriched — send a formal letter of demand for the tile value plus costs; (d) if the value exceeds R20,000, consider litigation in the Magistrate's Court; (e) if the customer used a stolen credit card, the liability typically falls on the card issuer, not the merchant — but marketplaces often pass this risk to sellers. Prevention: (a) verify billing and delivery addresses match for high-value orders; (b) require ID verification for orders above a threshold; (c) use a fraud detection service (e.g., Stripe Radar, PayPal fraud filters); (d) photograph the delivery with the customer's premises visible as evidence. For marketplace sellers, the risk of fraud losses is a cost of doing business — factor it into your pricing.
## CROSS-CUTTING & EDGE CASES

#### Q: 181. A traditional council in rural Eastern Cape wants me to supply tiles for a community hall but insists on paying in instalments from community fundraising. There is no written contract. How do I protect myself?
**Answer:** Trading with traditional councils in former homeland areas requires special care: (a) the council may not have legal personality to contract — check if they are registered as a juristic person or if the Tribal Authority Act gives them contracting capacity; (b) demand a resolution from the council authorising the purchase and specifying payment terms; (c) require payment of at least 70% upfront before delivery — community projects are notorious for funding shortfalls; (d) obtain personal suretyships from senior councillors or the traditional leader (Induna) — this gives you recourse against individuals with assets; (e) register a notarial bond over the tiles until full payment (retention of title); (f) deliver in phases against payment — do not release the full order until fully paid. Document everything in writing, even if the council is uncomfortable with formal contracts — explain that your insurer and SARS require documentation. Be respectful of cultural protocols while protecting your business interests.

#### Q: 182. A customer wants to use swimming pool tiles (glazed ceramic) for their kitchen splashback. I warned them the tiles are not rated for food preparation areas due to the glaze composition. They insist and will sign a waiver. If something goes wrong, does the waiver protect me?
**Answer:** A signed waiver does not override the CPA. Under section 48, any term that purports to waive the consumer's rights under the Act is void. Even with a signed waiver, you remain liable if: (a) the tiles cause harm due to an unsafe characteristic; (b) you should have known the intended use was dangerous; (c) you failed to provide adequate safety information. Your best protection: (a) refuse the sale if you genuinely believe the product is unsafe for the intended use; (b) if you proceed, provide a written warning specifying the risk and recommending an appropriate alternative; (c) ensure the tiles carry the correct SANS classification and labelling; (d) maintain product liability insurance. A waiver may be considered in mitigation of damages (reducing the amount you pay) but will not eliminate liability. If the customer becomes insistent, consider whether the sale is worth the risk.

#### Q: 183. My business partner and I are splitting our tile merchant business. He is taking the installation division; I am keeping the supply division. How do we handle existing stock that is jointly owned and existing customer contracts?
**Answer:** This is a complex unbundling requiring: (a) a formal partnership dissolution agreement drafted by a commercial attorney; (b) stock valuation by an independent assessor — each party takes stock proportionate to their ownership or buys out the other's share; (c) customer contract allocation — assign contracts to the relevant division with customer consent (unilateral assignment without consent may breach the contracts); (d) supplier agreement renegotiation — suppliers may need to approve the split and enter new agreements with each entity; (e) BEE implications — if your combined BEE status was based on shared ownership, each new entity must be independently rated; (f) tax clearance and VAT registration for the new entity; (g) employee allocation — follow section 197 of the Labour Relations Act if the division is a transfer of a going concern (employees transfer with their terms and conditions). Budget R50,000-R150,000 for legal, accounting, and valuation costs. Do not attempt an informal split — it creates disputes for years.

#### Q: 184. A customer paid a R50,000 deposit for tiles, then died before delivery. Their executor is demanding the deposit back but the tiles were already ordered from my supplier (non-refundable). What is my legal position?
**Answer:** When a customer dies, their contract with you does not automatically terminate — it becomes an obligation of their estate. The executor can choose to: (a) honour the contract and take delivery (paying the balance); (b) cancel the contract, in which case standard cancellation terms apply. If your terms state deposits are non-refundable once orders are placed with the supplier, this is enforceable against the estate — provided the term is fair and was communicated. However, under the CPA, the estate may argue the term is unfair. Your practical approach: (a) offer to cancel and refund the deposit less your actual costs (supplier cancellation fee, administration); (b) if the tiles are generic and resellable, offer a full refund once you resell them; (c) if the tiles are custom-ordered and non-returnable, retain the deposit with a clear breakdown of your costs. Be empathetic in communication — the executor is dealing with bereavement — but protect your commercial position.

#### Q: 185. I want to offer a loyalty programme where contractors earn points redeemable for merchandise (tools, braai equipment) rather than discounts. Are there tax implications for me and the contractors?
**Answer:** Loyalty programmes have tax implications: (a) for you, the cost of loyalty rewards is tax-deductible as a marketing expense; (b) for the contractor (if a registered business), the value of rewards received may be considered taxable fringe benefits if they exceed R5,000 per annum — but this is rarely enforced for small business loyalty programmes; (c) if the loyalty points are linked to purchase volume, they are treated as a trade discount for VAT purposes — you must account for VAT on the full selling price, not the net price after points value; (d) if rewards are given as prizes in a competition/lottery, they may be subject to 15% LOTTO tax (unlikely to apply to standard loyalty programmes). Structuring: keep rewards below R5,000 per contractor per year; issue annual statements showing points earned and redeemed; record the cost as marketing expense. Consult your accountant on the specific VAT treatment — SARS views loyalty rewards as part of the overall transaction price for VAT purposes.

#### Q: 186. A competitor has registered a business name almost identical to mine ("Tile Master" vs my "Tile Masters") and is operating in the same city. They are confusing my customers. What legal action can I take?
**Answer:** You have multiple potential claims: (a) passing off — if you can prove goodwill in your name, confusion, and damage; (b) unlawful competition — if they are deliberately free-riding on your reputation; (c) if you have a registered trademark for "Tile Masters," trademark infringement (stronger claim than passing off); (d) if the name was registered at CIPC after yours, you can object to the registration under the Companies Act (objection within 12 months of your becoming aware). Immediate actions: (a) send a cease-and-desist letter from an attorney; (b) if they continue, apply for an interim interdict to stop them using the name — this is urgent and can be obtained within days if confusion is proven; (c) lodge a complaint with CIPC to deregister or amend their name; (d) simultaneously, strengthen your own branding to reduce confusion (distinctive logo, tagline, colour scheme). Trademark registration is critical — if you have not registered your trading name, do so immediately through a trademark attorney (R4,000-R8,000 per class).

#### Q: 187. My 18-year-old son works in the business during school holidays. I pay him a casual wage. A labour inspector says I must register him for UIF and deduct PAYE. Is this correct for holiday employment?
**Answer:** Under South African labour law: (a) any person working more than 24 hours per month must be registered for UIF — this applies to casual and holiday workers; (b) PAYE applies if the employee earns above the tax threshold (currently R91,250 per annum for individuals under 65) — casual holiday earnings typically do not exceed this; (c) if your son is a genuine employee (works under your direction, uses your equipment, paid a wage), employment law applies regardless of family relationship; (d) however, if he is genuinely assisting as a family member without a formal employment relationship, the inspector may be overreaching — but this is a grey area. Safest approach: register him for UIF (employer contribution only, 1% of wage) and do not deduct PAYE if he is below the threshold. The UIF registration cost is minimal and avoids penalties. Keep accurate timesheets and pay slips to demonstrate compliance.

#### Q: 188. A customer wants me to supply tiles for a prison renovation project. The Department of Correctional Services requires that all suppliers and their employees pass a security clearance. None of my staff have criminal records, but the clearance process takes 8 weeks. Can I charge the customer for the delay?
**Answer:** Government security clearances are outside your control and are a known requirement for correctional facility contracts. Your contract with the customer should specify: (a) delivery is subject to security clearance being obtained; (b) the timeline for clearance is an estimate based on the Department's processing time; (c) you are not liable for delays caused by the clearance process. If you did not include these terms in your quotation, you cannot retrospectively charge for delay — but you can negotiate an extension of time with the customer. For future government security work: (a) build clearance processing time into your project schedule; (b) apply for clearances as early as possible; (c) consider which staff members are likely to pass clearance and assign them to sensitive projects; (d) include a "security clearance contingency" in your pricing to cover the administrative cost and timeline risk.

#### Q: 189. I discovered that my warehouse supervisor has been running a competing tile supply business from my premises after hours, using my phones and computer. He has taken my customer list. Can I claim damages for theft of confidential information?
**Answer:** Yes — multiple claims: (a) breach of employment contract (fiduciary duty, conflict of interest); (b) theft of confidential information (your customer list is a trade secret); (c) unfair competition (using your resources for his business); (d) possibly computer-related fraud if he accessed your systems without authorisation. Your actions: (a) suspend him pending disciplinary investigation; (b) preserve digital evidence (emails, phone logs, system access records) before he can delete it; (c) commission a forensic IT audit to document the extent of data theft and resource misuse; (d) institute disciplinary proceedings for gross misconduct — dismissal is appropriate; (e) lay criminal charges for theft and fraud; (f) sue for damages — quantify the loss (diverted customers, misuse of resources, devaluation of customer list). An urgent interdict can stop him from using the stolen information while the case proceeds. Budget R50,000-R150,000 for legal and forensic costs.

#### Q: 190. A customer bought tiles from me and paid in cash. They now want a VAT invoice to claim input tax. But I recorded the sale as a cash transaction without their details. Can I issue a retrospective VAT invoice?
**Answer:** Yes, you can issue a tax invoice retrospectively provided: (a) the original transaction was a taxable supply; (b) you have a valid tax invoice containing all required particulars (your VAT number, customer's VAT number if over R5,000, date, description, amount, VAT charged or tax rate); (c) you include the transaction in the correct VAT period (or adjust in the current period if the original period is closed). If you did not charge VAT at the time (e.g., you treated it as a zero-rated or exempt sale in error), you must correct this. Retrospective tax invoices are permitted under SARS VAT rules — issue it now with the original transaction date and a note "retrospective tax invoice issued [date]." Ensure the customer's VAT number is on the invoice if the supply exceeds R5,000. Do not issue a VAT invoice for a sale where no VAT was actually charged — this constitutes tax fraud.

#### Q: 191. My supplier has been placed under business rescue. I have paid a R300,000 deposit for tiles that have not been delivered. The business rescue practitioner says I am an unsecured creditor and will get "cents in the rand." Can I claim my tiles specifically (they are in the supplier's warehouse) or am I stuck in the creditor queue?
**Answer:** Under business rescue (section 128 of the Companies Act), a moratorium applies to all claims against the company. However: (a) if your contract contained a valid retention of title clause and the tiles are identifiable (specific batch, your order number, segregated), you can claim your specific goods — they do not form part of the rescue practitioner's estate; (b) if you registered a security interest (notarial bond or PPSA security) over the stock, you are a secured creditor with priority; (c) if you have neither retention of title nor registered security, you are an unsecured creditor and must wait for the business rescue plan. Act immediately: (a) identify your specific stock in the warehouse (visit with the practitioner's permission); (b) provide your purchase order and proof of payment; (c) if stock is identifiable, demand its release — the practitioner cannot withhold your property. If the practitioner refuses, apply to court for an order compelling release of your property. Time is critical — stock may be sold to fund the rescue.

#### Q: 192. I want to retire and sell my tile merchant business. A competitor wants to buy my customer list and stock but not my premises or staff. What are my obligations to my employees under section 197 of the Labour Relations Act?
**Answer:** Section 197 applies when a business (or part of it) is transferred as a going concern. If the competitor is buying only stock and customer list (not the business itself), section 197 does not apply — the employees remain your responsibility. You must: (a) follow fair retrenchment procedures if you are closing the business (consultation, selection criteria, severance pay = 1 week per year of service, notice pay); (b) if the competitor is taking over your premises and continuing a similar business, they may be obliged to take on your employees on the same terms — consult a labour lawyer on whether this constitutes a transfer of a going concern; (c) inform employees of the sale process as soon as practicable — secrecy breeds distrust and potential litigation; (d) consider negotiating with the competitor to take key staff as a condition of sale — this may increase the sale price but reduces your retrenchment costs. The customer list is a valuable asset — ensure it is properly protected with confidentiality clauses in the sale agreement.

#### Q: 193. A customer claims the tiles I supplied contain radioactive material (they read about "radioactive granite" online) and are unsafe for their home. They want a full refund and compensation for "emotional distress." How do I respond?
**Answer:** Ceramic and porcelain tiles are made from clay, silica, and feldspar — materials with negligible radioactivity. "Radioactive tiles" is an internet myth unless the tiles contain specific mineral additives (rare in standard ceramics). Your response: (a) request the specific basis for their concern — what article, what brand, what test results?; (b) provide the tile's technical data sheet showing composition — standard ceramics contain no radioactive materials; (c) if the customer persists, offer an independent laboratory test for radioactivity (gamma spectroscopy, R5,000-R10,000) — at their cost if the test shows no radioactivity; (d) emotional distress compensation is not recoverable under the CPA for unfounded fears — actual harm must be proven; (e) do not refund without evidence — it validates a baseless claim and sets a precedent. If the tiles are porcelain (fired at 1,200°C+), they are chemically and radiologically inert. This is a customer education issue, not a product safety issue.

#### Q: 194. My biggest supplier has been acquired by a larger international group. They have sent a new distribution agreement that replaces my existing terms with unfavourable conditions (shorter payment terms, higher MOQ, no returns). Can they force me to sign?
**Answer:** No — your existing agreement remains valid until it expires or is lawfully terminated. The new owner inherits all existing contracts (cession of contracts applies on share sale, not asset sale). They cannot unilaterally impose new terms mid-contract. Your options: (a) refuse to sign the new agreement and continue on existing terms until expiry; (b) negotiate the new terms — the new owner may have flexibility they are not initially showing; (c) if they threaten to terminate your existing agreement, check the termination clause — most distributorship agreements require notice (3-12 months); (d) if they refuse to supply under existing terms, this is breach of contract — you can claim damages and apply for an urgent interdict to force supply while the dispute is resolved. However, consider the relationship: fighting a new owner rarely ends well. Assess whether the new terms are genuinely unworkable or merely less favourable, and whether alternative suppliers exist.

#### Q: 195. A heritage architect has specified reclaimed Victorian-era tiles for a renovation in the Bo-Kaap. I have sourced replicas from Portugal but the heritage authority insists on genuine period tiles. Can I challenge this as a restraint of trade?
**Answer:** Heritage authorities in the Western Cape (Heritage Western Cape) and Cape Town have significant power to regulate alterations to heritage-protected buildings and precincts. The Bo-Kaap is a Protected Area under the National Heritage Resources Act. If HWC has issued a heritage permit condition requiring genuine period materials, this is not a restraint of trade — it is a lawful exercise of heritage protection powers. Your options: (a) source genuine reclaimed Victorian tiles from salvage yards, demolition sites, or UK salvage specialists (expensive but authentic); (b) engage a heritage consultant to argue that high-quality replicas meet the conservation objective — some heritage officers accept replicas if they are visually and materially indistinguishable; (c) obtain a written motivation from the architect supporting the replica option; (d) if the heritage authority is unreasonable, appeal to the Provincial Heritage Resources Authority. Do not install replicas without heritage approval — the building owner can be ordered to remove non-compliant work at their cost.

#### Q: 196. I want to sponsor a local school rugby team's kit in exchange for branding on their jerseys and at the school. The school says I must provide a BEE certificate to sponsor them. Is this required for a private sponsorship?
**Answer:** No — a private sponsorship is a commercial transaction, not a government procurement process. The school cannot require a BEE certificate unless: (a) the sponsorship is funded by government money channelled through the school; (b) the school is a public school and the sponsorship agreement is subject to PFMA or provincial procurement rules (unusual for kit sponsorships); (c) the school has its own policy requiring BEE credentials for all suppliers. As a private business, you can sponsor whoever you choose without BEE documentation. However, for your own BEE scorecard, the sponsorship may qualify for Socio-Economic Development (SED) points if the school is a registered PBO with section 18A status — so it is in your interest to obtain a section 18A receipt regardless. If the school insists on BEE and it is a genuine condition, provide your BEE certificate if you have one, or explain that BEE is not applicable to private sponsorships.

#### Q: 197. A contractor paid me with an EFT that cleared. Two weeks later, his bank reversed the payment claiming "unauthorised transaction." I had already dispatched the tiles. The bank says I must resolve it with the customer. What is my legal position?
**Answer:** This is a difficult situation. When an EFT clears into your account, you are entitled to rely on that payment — the bank's subsequent reversal is between the bank and their customer (the contractor), not your problem. Your position: (a) the contractor authorised the payment (he gave you the instruction or made the transfer himself) — the "unauthorised" claim is likely a dispute between the contractor and his bank, not genuine fraud; (b) you are a bona fide recipient for value — you gave consideration (tiles) in exchange for the payment; (c) write to the bank stating you are a bona fide third party and the reversal has unjustly enriched their customer at your expense; (d) demand payment from the contractor — the reversed payment does not extinguish his debt; (e) if the contractor refuses, sue him for the outstanding amount — the fact that his bank reversed the payment is his problem, not yours. Prevention: for large payments, wait 7-10 days before dispatching goods if the customer relationship is new; for established customers, EFT reliance is standard business practice and the reversal risk is low.
